Here's something that might sound small but isn't: a book with your child's actual name in it, with pictures that look like your family, with a story that maps onto exactly what is happening in your house right now — that lands differently than any book from a shelf.

Not because the published books aren't good. Some of them are genuinely beautiful. But because a two-year-old hearing *their* name in a story about *their* mama being in bed, in *their* house, with *their* dog in the picture — that's a different kind of recognition entirely. It says: *this story is about you. You are seen.*

At Plubis, you can create a personalised picture book for your child in five minutes. Tell us what's happening, tell us your child's name and a few details about your family, and we'll build a story that speaks directly to them. You don't need to explain the diagnosis. You just need to describe the feelings that are real.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 2-year-old about a parent going away
metaDescription: Picture books for 2-year-olds going through a parent traveling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Your two-year-old doesn't know what "business trip" means. They don't know what "just three days" means either. What they know is that the person who usually tucks them in isn't here, and the house smells slightly wrong, and something is off and they can't name it.

That's a lot for a tiny person to carry.

## What's actually going on at this age

Two-year-olds don't have object permanence for people fully locked in yet. Out of sight is genuinely alarming — not manipulative, not dramatic, alarming. The absence of a parent at this age lands like a physical disruption. Their whole world is routine and sensory familiarity. When a key person vanishes from that world without a comprehensible explanation, the nervous system notices.

So books aren't going to "explain" the trip in a way they understand. What they do is give the feeling a face. A sad bunny whose mama went away and then came back. That narrative loop — gone, then back — is the thing. Not the reason. Not the logic. Just: they go, and then they come back. Repeated enough times, that loop becomes something a toddler can hold.

Board books only at this age. This is non-negotiable. The book will get wet, thrown, slept on. Get the board book version of everything.

And start reading before the parent leaves. Not the morning of. The week before. Low-stakes, bedtime, boring repetition. That's what makes it work.

## The books worth having

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Chester the raccoon doesn't want his mom to go. She presses a kiss into his palm so he carries her love wherever he is. The physical ritual is everything here — actually press a kiss into your kid's palm before the traveling parent leaves, and again every bedtime while they're gone. Two-year-olds can do the hand thing even when they're too upset to hear words. That's the whole genius of this book. The kiss is a physical object they can hold. Buy this one. Don't borrow it from the library — you'll want it permanently.

**"Llama Llama Misses Mama" by Anna Dewdney** — Technically about school drop-off, but "misses Mama" is "misses Mama" and a two-year-old doesn't make that distinction. The big warm illustrations, the bouncy rhyme, the feeling of missing someone and getting through the day — it's exactly right. Llama Llama's face when he misses his mama is the most specific sad-but-okay face in picture book history. Your kid will point at it and say "sad" and that is the entire developmental win.

**"Owl Babies" by Martin Waddell** — Three baby owls wake up in the night and their mother is gone. They wait. They worry. She comes back. That's the whole book. It sounds almost too simple but it's doing enormous work — the return is the point. The repeated line "I want my mommy" will make your kid either feel deeply seen or deeply upset, and both of those are fine. The illustrations are moody and beautiful. Read it slowly. This one is about sitting with the waiting, not rushing past it.

**"Don't Worry, Little Crab" by Chris Haughton** — Little Crab is nervous about the big ocean. Wise older crab helps. It's not directly about a parent leaving, but it's about a small creature being scared and being accompanied through the fear — which is exactly what your kid needs someone to model right now. Haughton's blocky, graphic illustrations are bold enough for a two-year-old to follow easily. Short. Reads in under five minutes. Good for the end of a hard day.

**"Wherever You Are: My Love Will Find You" by Nancy Tillman** — A parent addresses their child directly: wherever you go, my love will find you. No plot. No characters. Just the promise, page after page, in Tillman's gorgeous painterly style. Some kids find this incredibly soothing. Some find the abstract lack of narrative confusing at two. Try it — if they lean in, read it every night the traveling parent is away. If they tune out, shelve it for age three. But when it lands, it really lands.

**"Are You My Mother?" by P.D. Eastman** — Classic for a reason. A baby bird falls out of the nest and searches for its mother, asking everyone it meets. Spoiler: she comes back. The book handles the terror of separation by making it a quest, not a tragedy, and the reunion is satisfying in the way only children's book reunions are. Two-year-olds who are in separation-anxiety mode will watch this baby bird with intense focus. They know. They're working something out.

**"Goodnight Moon" by Margaret Wise Brown** — Not about separation, but worth including in the bedtime rotation when a parent is away. The familiar ritual of saying goodnight to every single thing in the room is grounding. Routine soothes. This book IS routine. The traveling parent can record themselves reading it on your phone before they leave — kid hears the parent's voice, follows the familiar words. That combination works better than almost anything else at two.

## How to read these without making it worse

Here's what doesn't work: sitting your two-year-old down for a Serious Reading About Feelings. They'll feel the weight of the ceremony before you open the cover.

What works: regular, low-key bedtime reading, starting five or six days before the parent leaves. Same books, same order, same voice. Let them interrupt. Let them point at the owls and say "sad owls" twelve times. Don't rush to the reassurance — let the feeling be there for a second before you say "but mama comes back."

While the parent is away, those same books become anchors. The child has heard the owl babies' story enough times that it's familiar territory. Familiar is safe. Familiar is the whole ballgame at two.

If the traveling parent can record a video or voice note reading one of the books, use it. Hearing the gone parent's voice say the words to the kissing hand book while looking at the pictures is powerful beyond what you'd expect.

## The small things that help alongside the books

A piece of the traveling parent's clothing in the crib. Not a safety hazard — a t-shirt bunched at the side. The smell is real comfort at this age in a way that words aren't.

A photo on the nightstand or taped to the wall at the child's height. Point at it in the morning. "Daddy is at his trip. He loves you. He's coming back on Saturday." Even if "Saturday" means nothing, the ritual of saying it does something.

A countdown that's physical, not verbal. Stickers on a calendar. One sticker per day until the parent comes home. Two-year-olds can't read calendars but they can understand "when all the stickers are here, Mama comes home" more than they can understand "in four days."

## When a book isn't enough

Some two-year-olds fall apart when a parent travels — not just sad but inconsolable, not sleeping, not eating, genuinely dysregulated for the whole trip. If that's happening every time a parent travels, and it's not improving with repetition and reassurance, it's worth mentioning to your pediatrician. Separation anxiety is normal at this age but there's a range, and severe disruption to sleep and eating is worth professional eyes. Books help. They're not the ceiling of what's available.

## Make a book made just for your child

Here's what none of these books can do: say your child's name. Say "Daddy is going to Boston" or "Mama has to fly to London." Put your actual kid at the center of the story about your actual situation. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — you tell us your child's name and what's happening, and we make them the main character. "Mia's daddy was going on a trip" hits a two-year-old differently than Chester the raccoon, because Mia is them. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 2-year-old about trying new foods
metaDescription: Picture books for 2-year-olds going through picky eating. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Your two-year-old ate literally everything six months ago. They were the baby who tried avocado and made a face of pure joy. You told people about it. You were smug, a little, at the mommy group. And now they eat four foods. Possibly three. And two of those have to be the right brand or you can throw dinner directly in the trash.

Welcome to the picky eating phase. You're not alone. You are not in fact a failure as a parent. This is so normal it has its own pediatric terminology (food neophobia: fear of new foods, peaks around age two to three, very common in humans). The toddler who ate everything at eight months and refuses everything at twenty-four months is on the standard arc.

Can books fix picky eating? No. Books cannot fix picky eating. The only things that actually help are time, repeated low-pressure exposure, not making it a battle, and more time. Books are a small piece of a longer project. But they're a useful piece, because they let the concept of "new food" live in a fun, safe context that isn't the dinner table.

## What works at this age

Two-year-olds respond to stories and characters, not logic. You cannot explain nutrition to a two-year-old. You can read them a book where a character tries a strawberry and doesn't die. That experience goes into a kind of internal reference library. Repeated enough times, it makes "trying a strawberry" seem like the kind of thing a person does.

Short books, bright illustrations, simple concepts. The food should look appealing in the pictures, not alarming. Board books win over paperbacks at this age for durability reasons you are already aware of.

And don't read these books AT dinner. Dinner is a pressure environment. Read them at bath time, at story time, on a random afternoon. The goal is to build a warm association with food concepts outside of the high-stakes eating context.

## The books

**"Green Eggs and Ham" by Dr. Seuss** — Unavoidable. Correct. A character who absolutely will not try a new food and then does and likes it. The structure is perfectly calibrated for two-year-olds because the refusal is so recognizable and funny, not shamed. Sam-I-Am is annoying and your kid will identify more with the refuser. That's fine. The ending still lands. Best on this list for kids in a pure refusal phase.

**"Eating the Alphabet" by Lois Ehlert** — Not a story. Fruits and vegetables from A to Z, cut-paper collage illustrations, brilliant colors. Two-year-olds love pointing at things. This is a pointing book. Not trying to change behavior — just making food visually interesting and normal. The pomegranate gets the same page as the apple. Exotic foods are treated as completely ordinary. That's quiet, useful work.

**"The Very Hungry Caterpillar" by Eric Carle** — You know this one. The caterpillar eats through an enormous variety of foods. The list is part of the book's DNA. Two-year-olds who've heard "he ate through one apple, but he was still hungry" a hundred times have absorbed a long list of foods as something normal and even desirable. Eric Carle's collage illustrations make everything look good. Genuinely may be the most effective picky eating book that was never marketed as a picky eating book.

**"Yoko" by Rosemary Wells** — Yoko brings sushi to school. The other kids make fun of it. Eventually one classmate tries it and loves it. This is a little complex for two — better at three or four — but if you're starting early, the message (your food is good, other people might learn to love it too) is worth beginning to plant. The illustrations are warm and the story doesn't require full comprehension to land.

**"I Will Never Not Ever Eat a Tomato" by Lauren Child** — Charlie convinces his sister that carrots are orange twiglets from Jupiter. Peas are green drops of ocean foam. The reframing works. This is slightly long for a two-year-old but if your kid has attention span, the concept — a new story about the same food makes the food different — is genuinely useful and very funny. Charming illustrations. Lola is obsessed with not eating things and this book clearly loves her for it.

**"My Very First Book of Food" by Eric Carle** — A simple board book where top half and bottom halves of the page can be mixed and matched. This is a food toy as much as a food book. Two-year-olds love mixing and matching. Engagement is high. Indirect food exposure is happening. No eating required.

## How to use these at home

The dinner table is not book time. Don't sit down at dinner with the picky eating book and read it while they refuse the peas. That will make things worse.

Read the book at a completely neutral moment. Talk about the pictures. "Look at all these colors." "The caterpillar really liked those plums, huh." That's it. You're not selling the vegetables. You're just making them familiar objects in the world.

Some parents keep a copy of The Very Hungry Caterpillar in the kitchen and flip through it occasionally while cooking. Zero pressure. Just occasional exposure. This seems absurd and also sort of works.

## The thing nobody says about picky eating at two

Your two-year-old's picky eating is almost certainly not going to last forever, and it is almost certainly not your fault. The developmental driver (food neophobia, needing control over something, textural sensitivity that changes with age) is bigger than any parenting strategy. Your job is to keep offering, stay calm, keep the dinner table non-combative, and wait.

If eating is causing significant anxiety for your child — not just "won't try new foods" but genuine distress around mealtime, gagging on textures, extreme restriction to only 5-10 foods with no flexibility — mention it to your pediatrician. Feeding therapy exists and works, and starting early is better.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's something Green Eggs and Ham can't do: name the specific food your kid refuses. Put your kid's name in the story. Make the character exactly like them — stubborn, funny, eventually brave. Plubis builds it in five minutes.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 2-year-old about potty training
metaDescription: Picture books for 2-year-olds going through potty training. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Your two-year-old was doing so well. Three days in a row. You texted your mom. You told daycare. You mentally calculated how much money you'd save on diapers. And then, this morning, they looked you dead in the eye and peed on the floor.

Welcome to potty training with a two-year-old. It's not regression — regression implies they had it locked in. At two, they have maybe thirty minutes of lock-in on anything. This is just how it goes.

Picture books help. Not because a two-year-old will read a book and suddenly understand bladder control. They won't. But sitting down together with a book that says "everyone uses the potty" normalizes the whole situation without making it A Big Stressful Deal. The vibe you want to cultivate is: this is fine, this is normal, the potty is just there when you need it. Books can carry that vibe better than another round of "do you need to go? Are you sure? What about now?"

## What actually works at this age

Two-year-olds don't do emotional processing through narrative. They process through repetition. Through faces. Through pointing at pictures and saying "that!" The books that work at this age are the ones with big, clear illustrations, minimal text, and a character doing something recognizable. If the character sits on the potty and looks comfortable, that image does something. It's not magic. But it stacks.

Board books only, please. A two-year-old in the middle of potty training is already operating at full chaos capacity. Paperback spines will be destroyed. Get the board book if there's a board book option.

Don't read these during a potty attempt. That's too much pressure and too many competing things. Read them at bedtime, or on the couch, or whenever you're just hanging out. Let the book be neutral ground. The potty is already loaded enough.

And honestly? The most useful thing about reading these books is that it gives you a chance to use the word "potty" in a low-stakes context about thirty times a day. That repetition is doing work even when your kid seems to be paying absolutely zero attention.

## Book picks

**"Potty" by Leslie Patricelli** — The gold standard for this age. Absolutely minimal text. A round little baby with big eyes thinks about whether to use the potty or go in the diaper. They choose the potty. Everyone celebrates. That's the whole book. Which is perfect, because that IS the whole job right now. The illustrations are warm and funny and the baby looks genuinely satisfied at the end. Two-year-olds respond to this face. Read it every night for a week. It will not get old to them, even if it gets very old to you.

**"Everyone Poops" by Taro Gomi** — This book has been doing its job since 1977 and it's not stopping. Gomi's whole argument is that this is a totally normal animal thing that every creature does, and somehow when you see an elephant pooping alongside a human pooping, the shame just... evaporates. The art is flat and matter-of-fact and funny. Two-year-olds find it genuinely hilarious. They will request it repeatedly. It will become your most-read book. You will dream about it.

**"No More Diapers for Ducky!" by Bernette Ford** — Ducky is sitting on a little potty while Piggy waits outside. Piggy eventually realizes maybe it's time to try too. This one is great because it shows the potty as just a thing animals do, not a dramatic achievement. The illustrations are soft and sweet. If your two-year-old has any stuffed animals (they do), you can do a little live reenactment with the stuffed animals using the potty, and that will likely do more developmental work than the book itself.

**"The Potty Train" by David Hochman and Ruth Kennison** — A train full of animals is heading somewhere, and every stop has someone new using the potty. The train metaphor is a gift if your kid is even slightly into trains, which — statistically — they probably are. Simon & Schuster published this one and it's properly illustrated and not patronizing. Read it in a conductor voice. It becomes an event.

**"Once Upon a Potty" by Alona Frankel** — One of the oldest potty books in existence. Published in 1980. Still works. The reason it's lasted is that it explains the physical situation — what a potty is, where it goes, what it does — without any drama. It just walks through the whole thing plainly. For a two-year-old who seems genuinely confused about what is even being asked of them, this is the book. There's a boy version and a girl version.

**"P Is for Potty!" by Naomi Kleinberg (Sesame Street)** — If your kid watches Sesame Street, this is a cheat code. Elmo is already trusted. Elmo on the potty is an endorsement. It's alphabet-themed, so there's some learning-ish structure, but mostly it's Elmo modeling relaxed potty behavior, which is exactly what you want your child to see.

**"Big Girl Panties" by Fran Manushkin** — Rhyming text, bright illustrations, a little girl who's excited about the transition. The book has genuine energy — not the forced enthusiasm of a corporate product, but actual playfulness. Good for kids who are motivated by the "big kid" idea. If your child has been expressing any interest in underwear, this one will land.

## Reading together without making it weird

Here's the trap: you read the book, the book talks about the potty, you immediately ask "should we try?" and now the book is associated with being put on the spot. Don't do this. Read the book, say "that baby used the potty! Cool." Full stop. Keep the book separate from the actual attempt.

Let them hold the book. Let them point at things. Ask what color the potty is, what face the character is making, what animal is in the corner. Two-year-olds love to be the person who knows things in a book. Give them that without making it about their own bathroom behavior.

If your child brings you the potty book themselves, that's useful information. They're thinking about it. Don't pounce. Just read it.

## A thing nobody tells you

Some two-year-olds are genuinely not ready and there is nothing, no book, no sticker chart, no special underwear, that will change that. The readiness window is wide and it doesn't mean anything about intelligence or development. If you've been at it for months and your kid seems completely uninterested and unstressed about the whole thing — not anxious, just genuinely indifferent — it might be worth backing off entirely for a few weeks. Books can help with motivation and normalization. They can't create readiness that isn't there. No shame in that.

## When a book isn't enough

If your two-year-old seems in actual distress around the potty — scared, crying, physically holding it until they're uncomfortable — that's worth bringing up with your pediatrician. There are real physiological things that can make this harder, and there are real emotional components that reading a board book won't address. A book is a conversation starter, not a solution.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what none of these books do: say your child's actual name. Show a character who looks like them. "Leo uses the potty" hits differently at two years old than "the baby uses the potty." Plubis makes custom picture books about real kids — you tell us your child's name and situation, and we generate an illustrated book in five minutes with them as the main character. It won't replace Taro Gomi. But it's one more piece of normalization, with their name on it. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 2-year-old about learning to share
metaDescription: Picture books for 2-year-olds going through sharing. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Let's just say it plainly: your 2-year-old does not want to share. Not even a little. Not the blue crayon, not the half-eaten cracker, not the toy they haven't touched in six months but are suddenly extremely possessive of the moment another child looks at it. "Mine" is not just a word to a 2-year-old. It is a philosophy. A whole worldview. A constitutional amendment.

And before you google "is my child a sociopath" — they are not. This is developmentally totally normal. Two-year-olds are in the thick of figuring out that they are a *person*, with their own stuff, their own preferences, their own will. The concept of "we can both enjoy this thing" is genuinely not something their brain is fully wired for yet. The prefrontal cortex — the part that handles sharing, impulse control, and seeing things from another person's perspective — is not done developing until age 25. Twenty-five. So in a way, demanding your 2-year-old share is like demanding your cat file a tax return. Technically you can try.

But here's the thing. Even if they can't fully grasp sharing yet, books plant seeds. The right story at the right moment does something a lecture never will — it sneaks the idea in sideways. Your kid isn't being told what to do. They're watching a character they love wrestle with the same impossible situation. And slowly, over many reads, something shifts.

These books are the ones worth reading. Over and over. (And yes, they will ask for over and over.)


## The books

### *I Can Share* by Karen Katz

Karen Katz is basically the patron saint of toddler board books, and this one earns its place. It's a lift-the-flap book, which immediately gives your 2-year-old something to *do* with their hands while you read — crucial when you have a creature with approximately 11 seconds of sitting-still capacity. The concept is kept genuinely simple: a child shares crayons, shares a snack, shares time with a baby sibling. Nothing complicated. No moral thunderclap at the end. Just "here is what sharing looks like" shown warmly, repeatedly, in bright and friendly pictures.

What I like about it is that it doesn't try to convince a 2-year-old that sharing is fun. It just shows sharing as a normal, everyday thing people do. That normalization matters more than any lesson at this age. The lift-the-flap element keeps restless fingers busy and honestly makes them more likely to sit through it. Recommended.


### *That's (Not) Mine* by Anna Kang, illustrated by Christopher Weyant

Two bears. One chair. Neither will give it up. And the conversation they have is one of the most accurate representations of toddler conflict logic I've ever seen in print. "It's mine." "No, it's mine." "I had it first." "I want it more." Sound familiar? It should. You've probably heard some version of this eleven times today already.

The genius of this book is that it doesn't resolve with one bear heroically handing over the chair. They can't agree. So they do something else entirely. Together. That's actually a profound thing to model — sometimes sharing doesn't happen, and the answer is finding a third option. It's funny, the illustrations are irresistible (Weyant's bears have enormous expressive eyes), and your 2-year-old will probably make you read it until the pages are soft as felt. The sequel, *You Are (Not) Small*, is also wonderful.


### *Llama Llama Time to Share* by Anna Dewdney

Anna Dewdney understood toddler feelings better than almost anyone, and she doesn't flinch here. When Nelly Gnu comes over for a playdate, Llama Llama does *not* want to share Giraffa — his most beloved stuffed animal. He really, really doesn't want to. The book honors that feeling instead of dismissing it. And then things go wrong the way they always go wrong (Giraffa ends up with a torn ear in a tug-of-war), and there's a genuine emotional moment where Llama Llama has to figure out what actually matters.

Dewdney writes in rhyme, and the rhythm is easy for tired parents reading at bedtime for the fourth time. The pictures are cozy and warm. And the emotional arc — wanting to protect your special thing, the panic when it's damaged, the eventual decision to share more carefully — is emotionally true for a 2-year-old in a way that a lot of "sharing is caring" books just aren't. It doesn't pretend sharing is painless. That honesty is what makes it work.


### *It's Mine!* by Leo Lionni

Leo Lionni is a legend, and this 1986 book is deceptively simple. Three frogs — Milton, Rupert, and Lydia — live on an island in a pond, and they fight constantly. Everything is "mine." The lily pads, the pond, the air. And then a storm comes. And suddenly they need each other.

For a 2-year-old you're not reading this for the fable structure (that's more of a 4-year-old payoff). You're reading it because the illustrations are stunning — Lionni's torn-paper collage style is tactile and visually captivating in a way that holds very young attention. And the emotional throughline — three creatures who feel better together than apart — lands even without full comprehension. Read it slowly. Let them look at the pictures. Ask "what's happening here?" You'll be surprised what they notice.


## How to actually use these books (without losing your mind)

Here's the reality of reading to a 2-year-old: they are not sitting passively absorbing your lesson. They're chewing the corner of the book, pointing at a dog in the background, and asking "why?" to every sentence. That's fine. That's good, actually.

A few things that help:

**Read it before the situation, not during.** Pulling out a book about sharing in the middle of a meltdown is not going to work. Nobody has ever calmed down a 2-year-old mid-tantrum with literature. Read these books at calm moments — before a playdate, at bedtime, on a lazy morning. The ideas sink in when their nervous system isn't already flooded.

**Don't explain the moral.** Seriously. Resist the urge to go "see, the llama should have shared, that's the lesson." Ask open questions instead. "How do you think Llama Llama feels right now?" That's it. Let them think. Their answer doesn't need to be right.

**Repeat, repeat, repeat.** The 47th reading of *That's (Not) Mine* is doing more work than the 1st. Repetition is how toddlers process. When they ask for it again, that's a good sign.

**Lower your expectations for immediate results.** Reading a sharing book will not make your 2-year-old share their crackers at snack time tomorrow. It is a long game. You are planting seeds. Some of them take a year to sprout. That's okay.


## The bonus section: what to say when the meltdown happens anyway

Even with the best books and the best intentions, there will be moments — at the playground, at a playdate, at your mother-in-law's house with an audience — where your child absolutely loses it over a toy. Here's what actually helps at 2:

Name the feeling without shaming the feeling. "You really love that truck. It's hard when someone else has it." Full stop. Don't launch into the lecture. Just name it.

Then, if you need to intervene, make the time concrete. "You can have it back in two minutes." And actually give it back in two minutes. Two-year-olds have no concept of "in a little while" but they understand "after this song" or "when the timer goes off." Use that.

And then move on. Don't make it A Whole Thing. The quicker you normalize and redirect, the quicker they recover. They're not bad. They're just 2.


## One more thing

If you've ever secretly thought "my kid is weirdly bad at sharing compared to other kids" — I promise you the other kids are just as bad. Their parents are just quieter about it. Every 2-year-old on the planet is doing some version of this. The ones who look like they're sharing fine on the playground? They're being coached. Or bribed. Or they just haven't seen a toy they really want yet.

Sharing is hard for adults too, when you think about it. We just have better words for "that's mine" — we call it "personal property" and we built entire legal systems around it.

So go easy on your 2-year-old. Buy them a book. Read it forty-seven times. And know that somewhere in there, quietly, something is clicking into place.


*Want to make a custom picture book starring your child — with their name, their favorite toy, and their own sharing moment? The first one is free.*

title: The best picture books to read to a 2-year-old about tantrums and meltdowns
metaDescription: Picture books for 2-year-olds going through tantrums. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
It happened on a Tuesday. The orange was peeled wrong. Not wrong in any way you could explain to another adult. Just wrong — in the way that only a two-year-old can identify, pointing at the orange with a kind of silent fury that precedes a sound you didn't know a small person could make.

That's where you are. Welcome.

Here's the thing about tantrums at two: they're not manipulation. They're not bad parenting. They're the predictable output of a nervous system that has way more feelings than tools to handle those feelings. The prefrontal cortex — the part of the brain that handles regulation, patience, and not screaming in a grocery store — is genuinely offline. Won't come online for years. The tantrum IS the child trying to handle something they can't yet handle. That's all it is.

Books don't stop tantrums. Nothing stops tantrums in real time except time. But books, read during calm moments, do something slower and more important: they give your kid a vocabulary for what's happening inside them, and that vocabulary — eventually, gradually — becomes a bridge between feeling and words.

## What actually helps at this age

Two-year-olds can't read parenting literature. They can look at a picture of a character who is very, very angry and point and say "mad." That's a win. That's a real win. Don't underestimate it.

Books for this age need to: show big feelings clearly (big faces, expressive illustrations), not solve the tantrum in a way that feels fake, and be short. Under ten pages if possible. They need to land before the attention span runs out.

Don't read these books during a meltdown. It won't work and it might make things worse. Read them at bath time, at breakfast, while everyone's calm. The seeds you plant at 7am show up at 3pm in completely non-obvious ways.

## The books

**"David Gets in Trouble" by David Shannon** — David does things he shouldn't. He makes excuses. He feels bad. He says sorry to his mom. It's almost embarrassingly simple as a narrative, but two-year-olds love David with a deep personal recognition. He does the thing, he pays the consequence, it ends in love. That arc is comforting and useful. The "no David" books as a series are excellent. This one specifically is good for the "I didn't mean to" phase that usually kicks in around two.

**"When Sophie Gets Angry — Really, Really Angry" by Molly Bang** — Sophie gets angry. The illustrations go red and orange. She runs outside, climbs a tree, watches the ocean, comes back feeling better. That's the plot. For a two-year-old, the red pages are the thing — they look how anger feels, and kids recognize it. "That's angry," you can say, and they'll nod with intense certainty. Caldecott Honor. Genuinely beautiful. Best on this list for representing what big anger actually looks like from the inside.

**"Grumpy Monkey" by Suzanne Lang** — Jim doesn't know why he's in a terrible mood. All his friends try to help and he hates their help. Eventually he just... has his bad mood. And then it passes. The lesson — that you don't always have to fix a feeling, sometimes you just have to have it — is more sophisticated than most adults give two-year-olds credit for absorbing. But they absorb it. Read this one in your best disgruntled-chimp voice. They'll lose it.

**"Calm-Down Time" by Elizabeth Verdick** — This is not a fancy book. It's a board book about deep breaths and squeezing a toy and asking for a hug. Very practical, almost clinical in its approach. But for two-year-olds, having a literal script — "when I feel like this, I can do this" — is genuinely useful. Pair it with something more emotionally resonant. Use this one to give them the tools, use Sophie for the feeling.

**"Llama Llama Mad at Mama" by Anna Dewdney** — Grocery store. Llama Llama hates it. Everything about it. Has a complete meltdown in the cereal aisle. Mama stays calm. They repair. If you have ever been the parent standing in the cereal aisle, this book is for you as much as for your kid. The illustrations are so specific — the expression on Llama Llama mid-tantrum is so accurate — that reading it together feels less like parenting and more like sharing a joke about the human condition. Except your kid doesn't know it's a joke yet. They just feel seen.

**"Sometimes I'm Bombaloo" by Rachel Vail** — Katie is a good kid who sometimes becomes Bombaloo. Bombaloo kicks. Bombaloo screams. Then mom helps her come back to Katie. The "Bombaloo" language is genius — it gives the out-of-control state a name that isn't the child's name. "Are you feeling Bombaloo?" is less accusatory than "why are you acting like this." Two-year-olds can work with this. The resolution is warm without being too easy.

## How to read these during regular life

You're building a library of references, not delivering lessons. When your kid is calm and reading about Sophie's red anger, you can say "I wonder if Sophie felt like you did this morning?" Keep it light. Keep it curious. Don't turn it into processing the tantrum. Just open the door a crack.

The single most useful thing you can do is name the feeling in the book out loud, matter-of-factly. "She looks SO angry. See her face?" You're teaching them the word. The word is the beginning of the whole project.

And when the tantrum happens in real life — and it will, many times, possibly today — you don't need to narrate it. Just be near them. After, much later, when everyone's calm, you can say "that was really hard" and leave it at that. Books are for before and after. Not during.

## When it feels like more than typical

Tantrums at two are normal. Long ones, frequent ones — still often normal. But if your child is having tantrums that consistently last over an hour, involve self-harm (head banging, biting themselves), or seem totally disconnected from any trigger, mention it to your pediatrician. Some sensory sensitivities, some processing differences, show up first as tantrum patterns that look a little different. You're not overreacting by asking.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what none of these books can do: call your child by their name. Put them in the story. Describe the exact thing that sets them off (the orange. it's always the orange). Plubis builds a custom picture book for your specific kid in five minutes. The child who gets overwhelmed and finds their way back — named after your actual child.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about bedtime
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through bedtime routines. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds have discovered the infinite negotiation. You say bedtime. They say *but what if* we do one more thing, have one more drink, check that the dog is okay, find the other sock, tell you something very important about a dream they had. They will do this indefinitely. They have no concept of time running out. You do, and it's making you insane.

The thing about three is it's the first age where they have actual arguments. Not just "no" (that's two) but structured, logical-ish reasoning. "I'm not tired" delivered with complete conviction at 9pm, while they're rubbing their eyes. "One more book" proposed so sincerely you almost believe they mean it. Except they always mean one more after that one too.

Books are still your best move. Not because they shortcut the negotiation — nothing does — but because a book gives bedtime a shape. It says: we are in the ritual. The ritual has an end. The end is sleep. Three-year-olds can start to understand that arc, even if they fight it.

## What's different at three

At two, books worked because of rhythm and repetition. At three, they work because of *story*. Your kid can now follow a character through a problem and a resolution. They care what happens. They have feelings about the characters. This is a superpower for bedtime books because it means you can read a story where someone *overcomes* not wanting to sleep — or where the world goes quiet — and it actually lands as more than background music.

Three-year-olds are also starting to develop fears. Monsters. The dark. Sounds at night. The terrifying moment when you're the last one awake and the house is quiet in a way that feels different from daytime quiet. Books that name those fears and sit with them — rather than dismissing them — are worth their weight. "Of course it feels scary. Let's see what our friend does when it feels scary."

They're also, crucially, old enough to understand a rule. "We read two books and then it's sleep" can be a real contract at three if you hold it consistently. Start with three if you need to, work down to two. The point is the limit. The limit is the whole thing.

## The books

**"Bedtime for Frances" by Russell Hoban** — Frances is a badger who cannot go to sleep. There is a tiger, a giant, sounds, a fuzzy thing on the curtain. Her parents handle her with this combination of warmth and firm exasperation that feels painfully realistic. Each excuse is met patiently once, then redirected. This is the classic book for the three-year-old who has elaborate reasons for being unable to sleep, because Frances has elaborate reasons too, and she works through them, and she goes to sleep. It's also actually funny — Hoban's deadpan parental dialogue is comedy gold. You will laugh. Read it out loud with good timing.

**"In the Night Kitchen" by Maurice Sendak** — Controversial in some circles (the naked baby) but developmentally brilliant. Mickey falls out of his clothes and into a dough-filled dream world where nothing works the way it should. It's trippy and funny and weird in a way that three-year-olds love and adults find disorienting, which is kind of the point. Dreams are weird. The night is its own country. Sendak understood that better than almost anyone writing for kids. If you want something that honors the strangeness of the night rather than trying to make it sterile and safe, this is it. Not a soothing book, exactly. More of a validating one.

**"The Going to Bed Book" by Sandra Boynton** — Still works beautifully at three, maybe more so because they get the joke now. Animals on a boat doing absurd bedtime exercise routines. Your three-year-old will want to do the exercises along with the animals. Let them. The physical action — jumping, rocking, settling — is actually a good transition ritual. Get it out of their body before you ask their body to be still.

**"Ten Minutes to Bedtime" by Peggy Rathmann** — A hamster army shows up for the ten-minute countdown to a little boy's bedtime. Total chaos, hilariously illustrated, and then everyone settles down and goes home and the boy goes to sleep. This one is funny in a maximalist way — lots to look at on every page. Three-year-olds lose their minds over the hamsters. The countdown structure gives them something to track ("two minutes to bedtime!") which is genuinely useful for kids who struggle with the transition. Peggy Rathmann is underrated. Wildly.

**"Goodnight, Gorilla" by Peggy Rathmann** — Completely different energy from Ten Minutes. Almost no words. A gorilla steals the zookeeper's keys and lets all the animals out to follow him home. The joke builds slowly through pictures alone. Your three-year-old will narrate it themselves — pointing at what the zookeeper's wife doesn't notice, delighting in the chaos unfolding behind her. Reading a wordless book at bedtime puts them in the narrator seat, which is calming in a different way. They have control of the story. That matters to a three-year-old.

**"Bear Snores On" by Karma Wilson** — Still strong at three because now they understand the joke: Bear is asleep and missing everything and somehow that's both funny and enviable. Your kid might want to be Bear. Let that be the thing — "Let's both snore like Bear" right after you close the book. It works more often than it has any right to.

**"Sleep Is for Everyone" by Paul Showers** — Part of the Let's-Read-and-Find-Out Science series. It explains why sleep is a biological need — for animals, for kids, for everyone. Three-year-olds respond well to *information* delivered as story because their big new thing is understanding *why*. Why do I have to sleep? Here's why: your brain is growing, your body is growing, even puppies and birds have to sleep. The factual answer satisfies something that "because I said so" doesn't. Not a classic in the storybook sense but profoundly practical. Pull it out when the negotiations have turned philosophical.

## How to actually read these

Here's something counterintuitive: don't make bedtime reading the most exciting thing in the day. You want it warm and cozy, not thrilling. If you're doing voices, keep them quiet voices. If you're asking questions, ask sleepy questions. "I wonder where Frances is going to sleep tonight" — said softly, not energetically. The energy you bring is the energy you get back.

Three-year-olds will interrupt constantly. "But why does Bear snore?" "What is that animal?" "Do badgers have beds?" Answer briefly, move on. Don't let the book become a discussion group. You can debrief tomorrow morning. Right now is wind-down time.

The two-books-and-done rule: establish it, hold it, and use the books as the signal that we're now in sleep territory. When you say "let's pick our two books for tonight" they should know, at a bone level, that this means the end is coming. Three-year-olds can learn that Pavlovian response. It takes about two weeks of consistency and then it runs on autopilot.

## Bonus: the bedtime negotiation

Three-year-olds are magnificent lawyers. They will argue that a picture book counts as a nap, that reading with a flashlight under the covers is actually a different activity, that it's only a little bit bedtime. Here are the only two things that actually work:

**Pre-commitment.** Before the routine starts, say out loud: "Tonight we'll do bath, two books, song, lights out. That's our deal." State it plainly when there's no pressure. Then when the negotiation starts, you're not making a new rule — you're enforcing a pre-agreed contract. Huge difference. Three-year-olds respect agreements more than decrees.

**Give them one real choice.** "Which two books do you want tonight?" That's it. That's the whole choice. Within the container, full control. Outside the container, none. This reduces the power-struggle feeling enormously. They picked the books. They had power. The bedtime still happened.

## When a book isn't enough

Some three-year-olds have nights where no book, no song, no ritual touches the anxiety underneath. If your kid has persistent night fears — monsters, darkness, being alone, sounds — and the fears are escalating rather than fading after a month or two, it's worth mentioning to your pediatrician. Sleep anxiety at three is common and usually manageable with some guidance from an early childhood specialist. Normalizing it at home helps, but sometimes it needs more than a book.

## Make a book made just for your child

Your three-year-old is old enough to be genuinely delighted that the main character has *their name*. "That's ME!" is a real thing they say, loudly, when you hand them a book about a child named exactly what they're named. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — you give us your child's name and situation, and we make them the hero of their own bedtime story. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about big feelings
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through big feelings. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a wild age for feelings. Wild in the specific way of: your kid now has opinions, vocabulary, and approximately no ability to manage either. They can tell you they're angry. They can say the word "unfair" with genuine conviction. They just cannot do anything useful with those feelings once they show up. It's like watching someone who just learned to name every part of a car engine but still drives it off the road every forty minutes.

Also, three is when the feelings get more textured. It's not just hungry-tired-frustrated anymore. There's jealousy (about the baby, about a toy, about a friend at preschool). There's embarrassment (they tried something and it didn't work). There's loneliness (they genuinely miss someone). These are not toddler emotions. These are human emotions, and they're showing up in a body and brain that doesn't have the hardware to handle them yet.

So. Books. Here's what I'd put in your hands.

## What a three-year-old is actually ready for

At three, the story matters now. Not a lot — they still care about faces and voices — but they can follow a character wanting something, encountering a problem, and finding a way through. That means books about feelings can do more than just name an emotion. They can model a response. They can show what it looks like when you're angry and then you calm down. Not just the calm-down, but the angry too.

Three-year-olds also identify strongly with characters who are like them. If your kid loves dogs, the dog in the book gets extra attention. If they have a little sibling, they'll track any sibling dynamics without being asked. Work with this. Pick books that have a character hook your kid will respond to.

And at this age: they start to have favorite books for real. Not just the one that's on top of the pile. They will request specific ones and they will be offended if you suggest a different one. Again — let it be boring and repeated. The one they want fifteen nights running is the one doing the emotional work.

## Books worth reading (with opinions attached)

**"Grumpy Monkey" by Suzanne Lang** — Still the best book on this list for a three-year-old in a bad mood with no reason why. Jim the chimp is grumpy. Everyone tells him to be less grumpy. He gets grumpier. The ending is that sometimes you're grumpy and that's that. This is the book for your kid after a hard day at preschool when nothing specific happened but everything felt bad. The joke lands at three in a way it almost doesn't at two. Three-year-olds recognize Jim's exasperation at people trying to fix him. They have feelings about that.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Not technically a feelings book but parents use it for separation anxiety, grief, loneliness — any emotion that comes from missing someone. The concept: love connects people like an invisible string, even when they're far away. Three-year-olds can hold this metaphor. You can touch your chest at bedtime and say "the string goes right here" and it will be a real thing in your kid's inner world for years. That's rare. Use it.

**"When Sophie Gets Angry — Really, Really Angry" by Molly Bang** — The color storytelling alone is worth buying this book. The pages go from warm yellows to full-red-orange storm when Sophie is at peak anger — then gradually back to calm blue-green as she settles. Three-year-olds who can't yet track every word still track the color. They feel the shift in the pages before they understand it. This is one of the few picture books that does genuine art-level emotional work. Don't rush through it. Let them sit in the red pages.

**"Big Life Journal for Little Kids" — no, wait, let me give you books, not journals. Moving on.**

**"Listening to My Body" by Gabi Garcia** — Where do feelings live in the body? This book asks that. Butterflies in the stomach for nervous. Chest puffed up for proud. Shoulders up by the ears for scared. Three-year-olds love this because they're starting to notice that feelings have a physical address. "Where do you feel the sad?" suddenly becomes a question they can answer. That's a co-regulation tool disguised as a picture book. Sneaky. Good.

**"In My Heart: A Book of Feelings" by Jo Witek** — Ten feelings, one per page, each with a short description of what the feeling feels like from the inside. "Happy" is like a big yellow balloon in your chest. "Sad" feels heavy like a wet sweater. The similes are genuinely beautiful and accessible to a three-year-old with a decent vocabulary. It's a die-cut book too — die-cut heart shape on each page — and three-year-olds lose their minds over die-cuts. Physical novelty plus emotional content. Great combo.

**"Jabari Jumps" by Gaia Cornwall** — Jabari is going to jump off the high diving board. He is completely ready. He just wants to wait one more minute. And another. The fear is handled with enormous warmth and zero mockery — Jabari's dad sits next to him and is just there while Jabari gets ready in his own time. This is the book for the kid who freezes in front of hard things. It says: you can take your time, and someone will wait with you. Bring tissues for yourself. Seriously.

**"Grump" by Janet Wong and Illustrated by John Wallace** — Harder to find but worth hunting. A kid who wakes up grumpy and can't shake it. Short and honest and funny in the tired-parent way rather than the cheerful-children's-book way. Good for mornings when your kid woke up wrong and you're both just surviving until noon.

**"Breathe Like a Bear" by Kira Willey** — A collection of very short breathing and mindfulness exercises in picture book form. Some people find this kind of book too prescriptive for toddlers. I disagree. At three, kids can DO the exercises if you do them together, and any physical anchor for a feeling is useful. Do the bear breath with them. Make it a game. A three-year-old who learns to take three big bear breaths has a tool that will serve them for decades. That's not nothing.

## How to use these without turning bedtime into therapy

Here's a thing parents do that is understandable but counterproductive: they wait until their kid is emotional and then bring out the feelings book like an intervention. "Remember when the bunny was angry? That's what you're doing right now." Don't do this. In the middle of a meltdown, your kid's nervous system is offline for abstract processing. The book works when things are calm. It builds vocabulary and models for LATER. Not for right now.

At calm times: read the book. Ask about the face. Let them narrate. Let them be silly about it — "the bunny looks like POOP" is still emotional engagement, even if it's not what you were going for.

When you're actually in a hard moment, the tools are physical. Get down on their level. Make eye contact if they'll let you. Do the bear breath together. Sit close without talking. The book gave you and your kid a shared language — use that language, but lightly. "Sounds like a big grumpy feeling is here" and then just... be there.

## A thing about three-year-olds that nobody warns you about

Three is also the age when feelings suddenly seem to contradict each other and your kid has no idea why they're crying about something they wanted. "But you WANTED the yellow cup!" "I KNOW!" This is the beginning of ambivalence, which is one of the most confusing human experiences. Books like Grumpy Monkey and Jabari Jumps are actually introducing that concept — sometimes the feeling doesn't make obvious sense, and you don't have to fix it, you just have to be with it. That's an extraordinarily sophisticated idea. But picture books can carry it without it feeling like a lecture. That's their superpower.

## When a book isn't enough

Some three-year-olds have a genuinely hard time with emotional regulation beyond what books and breathing exercises can address. If your child is having meltdowns that last significantly longer than twenty minutes regularly, seems unable to recover from disappointment, has aggressive behaviors that are escalating, or is showing anxiety that's affecting eating and sleeping — please mention it to your pediatrician. Early childhood mental health specialists exist and they work with kids this age. There's nothing unusual about needing that support. Getting it early is genuinely the smart call.

## Make a book made just for your child

All the books on this list are wonderful and none of them are about your kid. Your kid is named a specific thing. They have a specific thing that makes them feel Bombaloo. Plubis makes a custom picture book in five minutes — you put in their name, their situation, and we make them the main character of a full illustrated story. "Elias felt SO grumpy and didn't know why" is a different read than any book you'll find on a shelf. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about building confidence
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through confidence and self-esteem. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is the age where confidence gets personal.

At two, your kid was basically a little confidence id — bold and reckless and entirely unaware of other people's judgments. The second you handed them a spoon they wanted to use it themselves. On everything. Including the cat.

But three? Three is when the social brain switches on. They start noticing other kids. Comparing. They understand now that there are things you're "good at" and things you're "not good at," and they are starting to file themselves somewhere on that spectrum. It's the beginning of the inner monologue. And the inner monologue of a three-year-old is not always kind.

You'll see it. The three-year-old who draws a circle, looks at it, and says "it's bad" before you've even seen it. The one who refuses to sing the song at music class because "I don't know how." The one who plays fine alone but freezes completely when another kid approaches.

None of this is a crisis. But all of it is real, and it deserves to be taken seriously.

Books are not a solution. They're a conversation starter. A way to name things that are hard to name. Here are the ones worth reading.


## What three-year-old confidence actually looks like (and doesn't)

Before the list, let's be honest about something.

Confidence at three is *extremely* situation-specific. You may have a child who is a total social butterfly at the playground but completely undone by the idea of trying a new food. Or one who is fearless on any climbing structure but cries if they get a word wrong in a song.

This is not inconsistency. This is not "mixed signals." This is how confidence actually works — it's domain-specific, especially in early childhood. A kid isn't globally confident or globally not. They have patches.

The books that work best for this age don't say "be confident in all things always." The good ones zoom in. Gerald the giraffe was scared about *dancing specifically*. Vashti in *The Dot* was stuck on *art specifically*. The specificity is the point. It gives kids something to grab onto.


## The books

### *I Like Myself!* by Karen Beaumont, illustrated by David Catrow (2004)

I know this appears on the age-two list too. It belongs here just as much, maybe more.

At three, kids are starting to be aware of how they look to other people. The narrator of *I Like Myself!* gets delightfully out ahead of this: "I like my teeth, I like my toes. I like the freckles on my nose. I don't care what you think, you see — 'cause I'm the only me there'll ever be!"

It's not subtle. It's not trying to be. And three-year-olds don't need subtle. They need direct and loud and singable.

The Catrow illustrations are grotesquely charming — the little girl has this wild energy, giant hair, expressions that take up the whole page. She's not performing cuteness. She's just *being*, in big wide-open brushstrokes, and that is deeply appealing to a three-year-old who wants very badly to just *be* without someone correcting their table manners.

My opinion: read this one with volume. Not because it's a bedtime book. Because it's a morning book. Before drop-off. In the car. When the day hasn't yet required anything hard.


### *Giraffes Can't Dance* by Giles Andreae, illustrated by Guy Parker-Rees (1999, US 2001)

If you have not yet read this with your three-year-old, you should do it tonight.

The emotional journey here — embarrassment, retreat, unexpected discovery, triumph — is something a three-year-old can feel in their whole body. Gerald slumping away from the jungle dance hits different when you've watched your kid slink away from an activity because a bigger kid said they were doing it wrong.

What the book doesn't do, and I respect it enormously for this, is tell Gerald to just ignore the mean animals. Gerald is genuinely hurt. He leaves. He needs a minute. And then he finds his own song in the moonlight, alone, before anyone is watching. The confidence builds privately first. Then others see it.

This is actually a really accurate model. Confidence doesn't usually start in front of an audience. It starts in private. The cricket who tells Gerald "sometimes when you're different you just need a different song" is the best adult character in children's picture books because the cricket gives Gerald a reframe, not a pep talk.

At three, they won't fully analyze this. But they'll feel the warmth of it. They'll want Gerald to be okay. And when he dances, they will be very happy about it.


### *Beautiful Oops!* by Barney Saltzberg (2010)

This one is a different kind of confidence book, and it belongs in the conversation.

It's not about social confidence or being brave. It's about creative confidence — specifically, the idea that making mistakes is not the end of the story. Saltzberg built a book full of "oopses" that become things: a torn piece of paper becomes a hippo's mouth, a blob of paint becomes an elephant.

For three-year-olds, this lands because three-year-olds are making things constantly and three-year-olds are also noticing when their things don't look the way they wanted. The perfectionism is just beginning. The scribble that "isn't right." The playdough animal that "doesn't look like a dog."

*Beautiful Oops!* tells them something specific and useful: the mistake is not a failure of the thing. It's the start of a different thing. That's actually a skill — the ability to pivot creatively instead of collapsing in disappointment — and it turns out you can introduce it to a three-year-old through a board book with flaps and holes and folded pages.

It's interactive too, which matters at this age. Flaps. Pop-ups. They'll want to do it again. Fine by me.


### *I Am Enough* by Grace Byers, illustrated by Keturah A. Bobo (2018)

At three, this book starts to land in a more complete way than it does at two.

Three-year-olds have developed enough language to pick up on more of the rhythm, and Byers' text has a genuine lullaby quality that makes it feel safe. "I am the sun. I am the moon. I am the in-between." The imagery is cosmic and warm at the same time.

What *I Am Enough* does that the louder, more exuberant confidence books don't is offer something quieter: sufficiency. Not "I am amazing" or "I can do anything." Just: I am enough. I don't have to be more than what I am.

For a three-year-old who has started measuring themselves against other kids, this is actually useful medicine. Not a hype-up. A settling-down.

Read it slow. After a hard day, maybe. When your kid came home from preschool and someone said something that stuck. You don't have to point at the message. Just read it slowly, close together, and let the art do the work.


## The bonus pick: *The Most Magnificent Thing* by Ashley Spires (2014)

Okay, this one isn't typically listed under "confidence" — it's usually filed under perseverance, frustration tolerance, growth mindset. But I want to argue for it here.

A little girl decides to build the most magnificent thing. She builds it. It's wrong. She builds it again. Still wrong. Again. Wrong. She gets SO frustrated she quits. Her dog takes her for a walk. She comes back. She tries again. It works.

The reason this is a confidence book: it models what productive struggle looks like. The little girl doesn't give up because she believes she can't do it. She's temporarily overwhelmed. And when she comes back, she recognizes that some of her previous attempts were partially right, partially good, partially useful.

That is confidence. Not the untested kind. The kind that comes from having tried, having failed, having noticed what was actually good in the failure, and having tried again.

For three-year-olds building that inner voice — the one that says "I can try" or "I can't" — this book offers something more real than pure affirmation. It shows the whole arc.

Published in 2014 by Kids Can Press. It won a bunch of awards. Deservedly.


## Reading these with your actual three-year-old

Few notes from the trenches:

**Three-year-olds will sometimes tell you the character is wrong.** Gerald shouldn't feel sad. The little girl shouldn't quit. This is them processing. Let them argue with the book. Ask why. "Why do you think she should keep going?" You are watching their theory of resilience take shape in real time.

**They'll ask "is that real?"** Especially about emotional content. Is Gerald real? Did that really happen to him? You can say: "Not a real giraffe. But lots of kids have felt what Gerald felt. Have you ever felt that way?" Sometimes you'll get a response that surprises you.

**Don't save confidence books for crisis moments.** The best time to read about trying hard things is when things are currently easy. The book becomes a resource they already know when the hard thing arrives.

**Let them pick.** If they choose *Beautiful Oops!* fourteen nights in a row, that might be meaningful. Or it might just be they like the flaps. Both are fine.


## And here's what no book can do

No book — not even a perfect one — replaces the moment you catch your three-year-old trying something hard and say nothing. Just watch. Let them figure it out. And when they do, say "you did it" without overdoing it.

That moment is the actual thing. Books support it. They practice the emotional vocabulary. They give language to the fear. But the real confidence comes from doing and being witnessed without pressure.

You're already doing most of this. The books are just backup.


If you want to go one step further — a story that's *specifically* about your kid, their exact flavor of stuck, their actual name, their actual life — that's what custom storybooks do.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about going to the dentist
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through dentist visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is such a fascinating age for fear. Your kid is now smart enough to anticipate things but not yet experienced enough to know that most anticipated things turn out fine. So they exist in this constant low hum of pre-worry about everything new — haircuts, thunderstorms, the vacuum cleaner, and yes, the dentist.

Also: three-year-olds ask *why* constantly. "Why does the dentist look in my mouth?" "Why do I have to sit in the chair?" "Why does it smell like that?" "Why can't I eat candy instead?" You will answer these questions seventeen times before the appointment and then they'll ask them all again in the waiting room at full volume while everyone stares at you.

This is actually good news for book prep, though. Three-year-olds are old enough to genuinely engage with a story — they'll follow a narrative, they'll ask questions about the characters, they'll want to know what happens next. A well-chosen picture book can do real preparation work at this age in a way it couldn't at two.

The catch: the book has to be honest. Three-year-olds have finely tuned nonsense detectors. If a book tells them the dentist visit is going to be totally fun and amazing and nothing will feel weird, your kid will not believe it. And then when something *does* feel weird — the cold air from the little tool, the bright light, the taste of the fluoride — they'll feel lied to. That's worse than being scared in the first place.

## What actually works at three

Three-year-olds do best with books that acknowledge the feelings without catastrophizing them. "The dentist's chair feels a little different" is better than "going to the dentist is SO FUN!" One of those is true.

They also do well when the character in the book starts out nervous and ends up okay — not thrilled, not in love with the dentist, just *okay*. That's a realistic and achievable model for your kid to follow. Don't shoot for "they'll love it." Shoot for "they'll get through it and feel proud afterward."

Routine preview is huge at three. Tell the story of the appointment before it happens. "We'll park the car. We'll walk through the door. The waiting room has toys. Then they'll call your name..." Do this in the car on the way there if you can. Predictability genuinely reduces anxiety in three-year-olds — they don't have the coping skills for big surprises yet, so the more you can map out what's coming, the more manageable it feels.

And play pretend. Get a spoon, a flashlight, a kitchen timer. Play dentist with stuffed animals. Let your kid fix everyone's teeth. The more they feel like an expert on the process, the less frightening the real thing will be.

## The books worth having on your shelf

Some of these your kid will want to read every night for two weeks before the appointment. Budget for that emotionally.

**"Curious George Visits the Dentist" by H.A. Rey (adapted by various)** — George is nervous. George messes something up (obviously). The dentist is patient and kind. Everything is fine. This is the George formula and it works because George's anxiety is visible but not overwhelming, and his resolution is earned not magical. Three-year-olds who love George will actually ask to read this one repeatedly, which is exactly what you want.

**"The Berenstain Bears Visit the Dentist" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — Sister Bear's loose tooth is a brilliant detail because it gives three-year-olds something concrete to identify with. This is also one of the more realistic depictions of what actually happens in a dental office — the chair, the tools, the friendly hygienist. It's been around forever for good reason.

**"Lama Lama and the Bully Goat" by Anna Dewdney** — Not about dentists. But if your three-year-old is deep in a Llama Llama phase, literally any Anna Dewdney book before a stressful event is worth reading just for the emotional regulation modeling. Come back to me on this if your kid has ever had a meltdown and been soothed by Llama's red pajamas. You know what I mean.

**"Daniel Tiger Goes to the Dentist" by Various (based on Fred Rogers' character)** — I have complicated feelings about Daniel Tiger as a franchise but I cannot deny that the songs work. "Grown-ups come back" and "take a deep breath and count to four" are genuinely useful emotional tools for three-year-olds, and this book puts them in a dental context. If your kid watches the show, this is a no-brainer.

**"My Dentist Is Cool!" by Steve Metzger** — This one walks through a first dental visit step by step with a warmly illustrated kid protagonist. The dentist explains every tool before using it, which is actually good modeling for what to *ask* your pediatric dentist to do. Realistic, kind, not saccharine.

**"Brush, Brush, Brush!" by Alicia Padron** — This is less about the dentist appointment and more about the why behind brushing, but it's cheerful and short enough to read five times in a row without losing your mind. A good companion piece to the dentist-specific books.

**"Just Going to the Dentist" by Mercer Mayer** — Little Critter is a useful character because he's always slightly reluctant and always fine. The art is warm, the story is simple, and the ending is satisfying without being falsely triumphant. Three-year-olds read the emotion on Little Critter's face before they process the words, and that face goes from uncertain to relieved in a very readable arc.

**"Peppa Pig: Dentist Trip" by various** — Yes, it's a licensed tie-in. Yes, Peppa Pig is occasionally the most irritating character in children's media. But if your kid is in a Peppa phase right now — and statistically there's a decent chance they are — this book will be listened to. And it's accurate enough about what happens at the dentist that it counts as real prep.

## Getting the most out of reading together

Don't save the dental book for the night before. Start three or four days out. Read it at bedtime, keep it in the regular rotation, let it become familiar. Familiarity is the whole point.

When you read, slow down at the tool pages. Name the mirror. Name the little pick. Name the water squirter thing. (It's called a syringe, technically, but "water squirter" is fine.) Three-year-olds who know the names of the tools are genuinely less scared of them. Unknown things loom larger than named things.

Ask "what do you notice on this page?" instead of "how does that make you feel?" Three-year-olds often find direct emotional questions hard to answer but will happily point out details in illustrations — and in doing so, they process the content at their own pace.

If your kid asks "will it hurt?" don't reflexively say "no." Some things might feel a little weird or uncomfortable. Say: "Most of it will feel totally fine. If anything feels weird you can raise your hand and the dentist will stop." That's honest and it gives them an actual tool to use. Kids who know they have some control over the situation are less scared than kids who feel like they have none.

## The conversation worth having (that the book will open up)

Books are conversation starters. Use them. After reading, ask:

- "Which part would you want to know about first if YOU went to the dentist?"
- "What do you think the dentist's favorite part of their job is?"
- "What should we bring with us? Should [stuffed animal] come too?"

The third question is doing a lot of quiet work. It shifts from "something is happening to you" to "we're going on an adventure together and we get to plan for it." Small reframe, big difference.

And when you get home — regardless of how it went — make a big deal out of the fact that they did it. Not the performance of it, not whether they cried or didn't cry. Just: "you went to the dentist and you survived and that makes you incredibly capable." Three-year-olds are building their self-concept right now. Let them build one where they're the kind of person who gets through hard things.

## Make a book made for your child

The books above are good. But there's something none of them can do: put your kid's actual name in the story, use your kid's actual dentist's name if you know it, describe the specific waiting room your kid is about to walk into.

Plubis makes personalized picture books for exactly this. You give it the situation — dentist visit — your child's name, their age, and a few optional details. It generates a book where the protagonist is your three-year-old specifically. Not a bear. Not Elmo. Not Little Critter. Your kid, walking into their appointment, feeling their feelings, and coming out okay.

For kids who struggle to identify with generic characters, this isn't a small thing. Hearing your own name read aloud in a story does something to your brain. It makes the story feel real in a way that "Bear goes to the dentist" just can't.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about a parent being deployed
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through military deployment. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a hard age for deployment. Actually, there is no easy age for deployment — but three is its own specific kind of hard.

A 3-year-old is just starting to understand time. "Tomorrow" is real. "Next week" is murky. "Six months" is completely incomprehensible. So when you try to explain that Mommy or Daddy left and will be back in "a while," what your child hears is something closer to: the person I love most left and I don't know if that's forever.

They can't tell you that's what they're feeling. They show you instead. Tantrums that seem too big for the moment. Sudden fear of new things. Asking "Where's Daddy?" forty times a day in a voice that doesn't quite believe the answer. Needing to sleep with the lights on again.

Books won't fix the fear. But they can name it. And naming something — for a 3-year-old, for anyone — is the first step toward it being manageable.

## What to look for at this age

Three-year-olds are newly verbal and they use that language to try to make sense of their world. They ask "why" constantly. They notice inconsistencies. If you tell them Daddy is "helping people" and then he sounds sad on a video call, they notice. They're paying closer attention than you think.

The books that land at this age are the ones that:

**Name the feelings explicitly.** Not just "sad" but "missing" and "angry" and "confused." Three-year-olds are learning their emotional vocabulary in real time. A book that shows a character feeling the specific messy thing your child is feeling — and names it out loud — is a huge relief. Oh, this has a word.

**Show the deployment cycle.** Goodbye, then time passing, then reunion. Even if your child can't feel that arc cognitively, seeing it in a story builds a template: this has a shape. There's a middle and there's an end.

**Feature the at-home parent.** The at-home parent is sometimes invisible in these books, which is a real gap. The child needs to see the person who *is* there being competent, warm, and okay. Because part of what's scary isn't just the missing parent — it's watching the at-home parent struggle with the weight of everything.

**Don't lie.** Three-year-olds have excellent nonsense detectors. Books that are relentlessly, artificially cheerful about deployment feel wrong to them on some level they can't articulate. A little bit of honest sadness in the book gives the child permission to feel their own.

## Book picks for 3-year-olds

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Chester the raccoon doesn't want to leave his mother. She presses a kiss into his palm so he can carry it with him. The book isn't specifically about military deployment — it was written about starting school — but it's been adopted wholesale by military families because the central idea is so exactly right: love is portable. You can take it with you. You can press it against your cheek when you're scared in the night. The kissing hand ritual you do in real life before a deployment is worth more than the book itself, but the book gives you the language for it.

**"While You Are Away" by Eileen Spinelli** — Three children, three military parents in three different branches, three different ways of missing someone. What Spinelli does that most books don't is show the *parallel* reality: Daddy is eating and sleeping somewhere right now, probably thinking of you, probably looking at your picture. The child and the deployed parent are both living their days, both missing each other. For a 3-year-old who asks "what is Daddy doing right NOW," this book starts to answer that.

**"Night Catch" by Brenda Ehrmantraut** — Before deployment, a father and son establish a ritual: every night, they'll both look at the North Star at the same time and "play catch" across the distance. This is one of the most practically useful books on this list because it gives you a ritual to actually do. Tonight, after you close the book, go find the brightest star out the window. "Let's throw to Daddy." Three-year-olds are ritualistic by nature. Give them a deployment ritual and they'll hold onto it like a rope.

**"Lily Hates Goodbyes" by Jerilyn Marler** — Lily's daddy is deploying and Lily is not okay with this. She's angry. She's sad. She stalls and drags her feet and has very big feelings. Her mother doesn't dismiss these feelings or perform cheerfulness over them — she helps Lily find her way through them.
