This is one of the most honest deployment books available, and the anger angle is especially important at three, when tantrums are the primary coping mechanism and caregivers sometimes don't know whether the enormous feelings are about the deployment or just being three. Probably both. Usually both.

**"Hero Dad" by Melinda Hardin** — A child narrates how their deployed parent is like a superhero — the uniform, the missions, the courage. Written simply, one sentence per page, watercolor illustrations. The "superhero" framing isn't toxic positivity — it's actually how a lot of military kids genuinely feel about their parents. Pride and missing someone aren't opposites. They coexist. This book holds both at the same time, which is honest and useful.

**"Wherever You Are: My Love Will Find You" by Nancy Tillman** — Tillman writes with such directness about the unbreakable quality of parental love that it can knock the breath out of you a little on the first read. The promise in the title is the whole book: wherever you go, whatever distance separates you, the love travels. For a 3-year-old who is old enough to feel the weight of "far away" but not yet old enough to fully trust that absence isn't abandonment — this matters.

**"When Dad's at Sea" by Mindy Pelton** — Emily's Navy pilot father deploys for six months on an aircraft carrier. He makes her a paper chain before he leaves — one link for each day, to be removed each morning — so she can watch the deployment shorten over time. Pelton wrote this from her own life, and that real-life rootedness shows. It's specific and true in a way that fictional generalities aren't. The paper chain idea is one of the most concrete, child-friendly deployment tools I've ever encountered. Make one. Tear a link every morning. Let your 3-year-old see the pile of links grow.

## Reading together

At three, reading is a conversation, not a performance. Your child will interrupt. They'll ask questions mid-sentence. They'll say "read that part again" about a part you already read twice. Let them.

The interruptions are often where the real processing happens. "Why is Lily crying?" is an invitation. Not a disruption. Answer it. Then keep going.

If your child starts asking about the deployed parent during reading — "Is Daddy scared?" "Does Mama miss me?" — answer honestly and simply. "I think Daddy misses you very much. I bet he's thinking about you right now." You don't have to have certainty to give comfort. Warmth and presence fill in what certainty can't.

Keep a consistent reading time. Same chair if possible. Same pre-sleep routine. When everything is uncertain, the ritual of the routine is a form of safety.

## The hard nights — a conversation starter

There will be a night where the book doesn't help. Where your 3-year-old wants the deployed parent, specifically, not a story, not you, not any substitute — them. Their smell. Their voice. The particular way they do the goodnight hug.

On those nights, don't fight it. Don't try to redirect. Sit in the grief with them for a minute. "I miss Daddy too. It's okay to miss him. He's coming home."

Then, if you want to try a book, try "Night Catch." Go find the star together. Throw the ball into the sky.

Some nights that works. Some nights nothing works except sitting there together in the missing of someone, which is its own kind of love.

## A book made just for your child

General picture books are wonderful. They show your child that other families have been through this, that this is something that can be named and survived. But they can't show your child's own house, their own yard, their parent's actual face in uniform, the dog's actual name.

Plubis lets you make a custom picture book in about five minutes — with your child's name, your family's specifics, the deployed parent as a real character in the story. For a 3-year-old who is just beginning to separate "my story" from "everyone else's story," seeing themselves in a book is a different kind of recognition than any off-the-shelf title can provide.

The deployed parent can contribute. Can help write the words. Can be part of making the thing that gets read every night in their absence.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about parents getting divorced
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through divorce. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a tricky age for this.

A three-year-old is old enough to notice that something is wrong but not old enough to understand why. They're building their first real mental model of how the world works — and that model is built almost entirely on watching you. So when you're sad, or tense, or trying very hard to hold it together in the kitchen while explaining why Daddy has a new apartment, they feel all of it.

Three-year-olds also have a completely magical way of assuming things are their fault. You didn't do anything to cause this, buddy. They believe it anyway. It's just how their brains work at this stage — egocentric, not in a selfish way, but in a deeply literal *I am the center of everything so I must have caused this* way. Books that speak directly to that — gently, without making a big deal out of it — do real work.

The goal isn't to explain divorce. It's to make the child feel less alone in what they're feeling, and less responsible for what's happening.

## What actually helps at this age

You want books that:

- **Name feelings without catastrophizing them.** "Sad is okay. Confused is okay. You can feel a lot of things at once."
- **Show both parents loving the child.** Not as a fairy tale, but as a given.
- **Keep the child's daily world recognizable.** Favorite toys. Snacks. The dog. Bedtime.
- **Don't require a therapist's vocabulary to engage with.** Short sentences. Big feelings shown in art, not described in clinical language.

Avoid books that end with "and then everything was fine" if that's not honest. Your kid can feel dishonesty. You can too.

## Books I'd actually recommend

**"Two Homes" by Claire Masurel** — I know it's listed everywhere, but it's listed everywhere because it actually works. At three, kids can engage a little more with Alex's experience — the two bedrooms, the two gardens, the two sets of friends. Read it slowly. Let them study the pictures. Don't rush toward the last page.

**"It's Not Your Fault, KoKo Bear" by Vicki Lansky** — KoKo the bear is going through exactly what your child is going through. The writing is unusually direct — it says "it's not your fault" out loud, which matters, because your three-year-old has probably already decided that it is. There are also notes for parents at the bottom of each page, which I find genuinely useful rather than preachy. One of the best books for this specific situation.

**"My Family's Changing" by Pat Thomas** — This one is written almost like a conversation. It asks the child questions, acknowledges that divorce can feel confusing and scary, and validates that it's okay to miss the way things were. A little more text-heavy than some alternatives, but the warmth of the writing makes it work. Better for the slightly older or more verbal three-year-old.

**"Fred Stays With Me" by Nancy Coffelt** — Strictly speaking this is about a dog who goes back and forth between two homes. But the experience the child narrator describes — packing a bag, going between Mom's house and Dad's house, having the one constant of her dog — is pitch-perfect for a three-year-old navigating the same transitions. And because it's filtered through the dog, it doesn't feel as heavy to read aloud. Good for a child who's struggling with the back-and-forth specifically.

**"When My Parents Forgot How to Be Friends" by Jennifer Moore-Mallinos** — I like that the title frames it this way. Not that they stopped loving each other in some grand tragic sense, but that they forgot how to be friends. It's honest without being devastating. The child in the book still feels sad, still wishes things were different — and that's allowed. The book doesn't rush past it. Try your library for this one first.

## Reading together without making it a big thing

Here's the thing about three-year-olds and hard books: you don't need to have The Talk before reading. You don't need to set the scene or explain what's happening. Just read the book.

If they ask questions, answer them simply. "Yes, kind of like our family." "Yes, you have two homes too." "I know it feels strange."

If they go quiet and just stare at a page, that's okay. Let the quiet be there.

If they want to read it again tomorrow night, read it again. And the night after. Repetition is how three-year-olds process things. The tenth read is often when something clicks.

And if you cry a little reading it — honestly, that's okay too. You don't have to pretend it doesn't affect you. Kids can see you have feelings about hard things and still feel safe. What they need is for you to still be there. You are.

## A note on the hard conversations

At some point your three-year-old will say something that stops you cold. "Did you and Daddy stop loving each other?" "Is it because I was bad?" "Will you stop loving me too?"

There's no script that makes those questions easy. But a few things hold up: answer the question they actually asked, keep it short, and then hug them. "No, I will never stop loving you. Never." Say it like you mean it, because you do.

If the questions keep coming in ways that feel beyond what books or conversations are handling, a play therapist who works with young children can be genuinely helpful. Not as a last resort — just as another tool.

## A book that's just for them

One thing I hear from parents is that their child needed to see *their own* story. Not KoKo Bear's story. Not some illustrated family they can't quite connect with. Their name. Their house. Their dog.

Plubis makes personalized picture books for exactly these moments. You tell us the situation and your child's name, and we build a book around their world — one that names the feelings, keeps both parents in the picture, and ends somewhere safe.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about going to the doctor
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through doctor visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds have opinions. Loud ones. About everything.

They have opinions about which cup has the right amount of milk. About whether their socks are "scratchy." About the direction you took to the grocery store. So when you tell a 3-year-old you're going to the doctor, you should expect that opinion to arrive immediately and at volume.

Here's the thing, though: three is also the age when books start to really work as emotional prep tools, in a way they can't quite at two. Because 3-year-olds have enough language to process a story. They can tell you what the character is feeling. They can connect what happens to Froggy at the doctor to what might happen to them. They are doing genuine narrative reasoning — not at an adult level, obviously, but enough to matter.

This is the sweet spot. Read the right books a few days before, and you will have a slightly less chaotic checkup. I won't promise a perfect one. Nobody's promising that.

The other thing that's different at three: they're going to ask questions. Real, specific questions. "What is that silver thing?" (A stethoscope.) "Does it hurt when she does that?" (Probably not, but we'll say if it does.) "Why does the doctor need to look at my privates?" (That one you get to answer — have fun.) Books open these conversations. That's most of what they're for at this age.

## What's developmentally happening at 3

Three-year-olds are just starting to understand that other people have different feelings from their own — that the doctor might actually be trying to help, even if the help feels uncomfortable. That's abstract. Books build that bridge.

They also have fears that are more specific now. Not just "unknown scary place" but "that thing is going to go in my ear and I don't like it" or "last time I got a shot and I didn't know it was coming." These specific fears need specific addresses. A book that shows the sequence — here's the ear check, here's how it works, it takes five seconds — can defuse a specific fear in a way no amount of reassurance can.

They also love repetition. Read the same book five times before the appointment. That's not boring, it's preparing.

## Book picks

**"Froggy Goes to the Doctor" by Jonathan London, illustrated by Frank Remkiewicz** — This is the gold standard for 3-year-olds. Froggy is chaotic in the exact way 3-year-olds are chaotic — forgetting things, getting distracted, arriving in wrong clothing, doing everything backwards. And then the checkup happens: height, weight, eyes, ears, reflexes, the whole deal. There's a moment where Froggy accidentally shows everyone his underpants. Your kid will lose their mind with delight. The humor does real work here — it takes the tension out of the medical setting by making everything slightly absurd. And the final reveal (a gold star and a lollipop) lands like a victory. Published by Viking in 2002. Ages 3–6. One of the best.

**"The Berenstain Bears Go to the Doctor" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — This one has been around since 1981 and it's still doing the job. Brother Bear and Sister Bear go for their checkups. Papa is actually more nervous than the kids. There's a shot at the end — the book doesn't hide from it. That honesty is rare and worth something. Your 3-year-old will spot the shot coming on the page and you'll get a conversation out of it. "Does it hurt?" "For a second, yes. And then it's done." That's a better prep than a book that pretends shots don't happen. Ages 3–7, Random House.

**"Say Ahhh! Dora Goes to the Doctor" by Nickelodeon Publishing** — If your kid is in a Dora phase, this is a no-brainer. Dora goes for her annual checkup. The doctor measures her height and weight, listens to her heart, looks in her nose and throat — and Dora gets to pick a treat at the end. It's not literary. There's no great writing here. But Dora is a friend your kid already trusts, and that trust transfers. This is a pure familiarity play, and familiarity is a real tool. Published by Simon Spotlight, 2008. Ages 3+.

**"Bear Feels Sick" by Karma Wilson, illustrated by Jane Chapman** — This one is slightly different from the others — it's about being sick at home, not the doctor's office specifically. But it's relevant because it addresses the *why* of the doctor visit. Bear doesn't feel well. His friends take care of him. He rests and recovers. The rhyming text is rhythmic and actually quite beautiful, and Jane Chapman's illustrations are warm and soft in a way that reads as gentle and safe. Lots of 3-year-olds are scared of being sick, not just of the doctor. This book addresses that layer. Published by McElderry Books, 2007. Ages 3–7.

**"Doctor Maisy" by Lucy Cousins** — I know I recommended this for 2-year-olds too. It works for 3s just as well, probably better, because now they can participate in the role-play it inspires. After reading, they'll want to be the doctor. Give them a toy stethoscope if you have one. Let them check your heartbeat. Let them examine their stuffed animals. That play is direct emotional preparation for being the patient. Ages 2–4, Candlewick 2001. Don't skip it just because it's simple.

**"Going to the Doctor" by Anne Civardi, illustrated by Stephen Cartwright** — I want to make a case for this one specifically for 3-year-olds because the Usborne format — realistic illustrated children, clear sequential pages, the famous hidden yellow duck — is really well-matched to the age. Three-year-olds are developing observation skills rapidly. Finding the duck on every page turns the read-aloud into an activity, and the medical content slips in while they're busy hunting. It also shows a child getting a shot without making it traumatic. Stephen Cartwright's illustrations have been making kids comfortable since the 1970s for a reason. Widely available in current editions.

**"Daniel Visits the Doctor" by Becky Friedman** — The Daniel Tiger strategy — "Talking about it makes it less scary" — is genuinely a skill that 3-year-olds can learn and use. Not just for doctor visits. For haircuts, for new classrooms, for all of it. The fact that Daniel himself is nervous and then works through it is developmentally exactly right for this age. 3-year-olds are starting to understand that feelings can change. This book shows that in action. Simon Spotlight, 2014.

## Reading together

At three, you can actually do voices. Do the voices. Make Froggy sound confused. Make the Berenstain Bear doctor sound gentle and cheerful. Your 3-year-old will start correcting you when you get the voice wrong — that means they're deeply engaged. Let them correct you.

Pause on the medical tools. "What do you think that does?" They will have a theory. The theory will be wrong and hilarious. Go with it for a minute before you explain. The explanation lands better after they've had a chance to wonder.

Ask "how does [character] feel?" and then follow with "have you ever felt like that?" A 3-year-old who can't articulate their own nervousness will sometimes be able to say "yeah, like Froggy." That's enough. That shared language gives you something to reference later: "Remember how Froggy felt nervous and then it was okay?"

Don't do a marathon read. One book, fully engaged, beats three books with a kid who's drifting.

## When a book isn't enough

Sometimes the book reads well and the appointment still goes sideways. Here are a few things that help alongside books, specifically for 3s:

- Play doctor at home. Your kid checks your ears. You check their ears. Do this a lot in the week before the appointment.
- Name the specific fear. Don't be vague. "Are you worried about the shot?" gives them a target. Unnamed fears are worse than named ones.
- Let them bring something. A stuffed animal, a toy, a specific blanket. The comfort object coming along is not babying — it's scaffolding.
- Tell them about a time you went to the doctor. Keep it simple and non-scary. "I went last year and the doctor checked my eyes. It felt a little cold."

You don't have to have it all figured out. Honest uncertainty from a calm parent — "I'm not sure exactly which checks they'll do today, but I'll be right there" — is more reassuring than false certainty.

## Make a book made for your child

Every book above is wonderful. But none of them star a kid with your child's exact name, their exact worry, their exact stuffed bear named Gerald who absolutely must come to every appointment.

A personalized book is different. It's "you," not "Froggy." It says your name. It knows about Gerald. It puts your specific kid in the story.

At [Plubis](https://plubis.app), you answer a few quick questions about your child and we build a custom picture book about their doctor visit — one they can hold while they're waiting on the crinkly paper table. Five minutes to make. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about the first day of school
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through first day of school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a genuinely interesting age for this. Old enough to understand "you're going somewhere new" and young enough to have absolutely no coping mechanisms for it. They might surprise you — walk right in, never look back. Or they might wrap both arms around your leg and make a sound you didn't know they could make. You won't know until the day.

Books help. Not because they explain school (you can explain school all you want, three-year-olds don't believe abstract explanations) but because they make the feelings feel named and okay. There's something about a nervous mouse in a picture book that gives language to a nervous three-year-old in a way that "don't worry, you'll have fun" never does.

## What three-year-olds are actually ready for

At three, kids can follow a simple plot. Character wants something, faces a problem, things work out. They're starting to have real emotional vocabulary — "scared," "happy," "miss you" — so books that use those words directly land differently than they did at two.

They also start identifying with characters. A kid who loves dogs will care about a nervous dog starting school more than a nervous cat. A kid obsessed with trains probably wants the school-themed Thomas book, not the most emotionally sophisticated title. Worth knowing when you're picking.

Still recommend board books or chunky hardcovers for home use. Paperbacks are for age five and older in my experience — they get loved to destruction before then.

One more thing: three-year-olds will ask for the same book fifteen nights in a row and then never again. Don't fight it. The one they want to read fifteen times is the one doing the work.

## The picks

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Still the GOAT at this age. Maybe even more effective at three because your kid can understand the reason behind the ritual, not just copy the motion. "Mama's kiss will be there whenever you need it" is a sentence that makes sense to a three-year-old in a way it almost doesn't at two. Read it every night for a week before school starts. Actually do the kiss-in-the-palm thing at drop-off. Every single morning. The repetition is the point.

**"Wemberly Worried" by Kevin Henkes** — Wemberly worries about everything. Big things, small things, completely made-up things. She worries about school starting. She worries about the radiator. If your kid is an anxious worrier, this book sees them without making worry seem like a problem to be fixed — Wemberly isn't broken, she's just Wemberly. The ending, where she finds Jewel (another nervous kid), is perfect but not saccharine. Henkes is just better at this than almost anyone. The illustrations of Wemberly's worried mouse face are extraordinarily specific.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Not technically a school book, but parents use it for separations constantly and I understand why. The premise: love is like an invisible string connecting you to the people you love, even when they're far away. Three-year-olds LOVE this concept. You can point at your chest and say "the string goes right here" at drop-off. Takes two seconds. Works better than a five-minute goodbye speech. Works better than almost anything, honestly.

**"Pete the Cat: Rocking in My School Shoes" by Eric Litwin** — Pete goes to school and it's all fine and cool because Pete is unflappable. This is genuinely useful for kids who respond well to modeling calmness — if Pete isn't worried, maybe I don't need to be either. The song you can sing along to is a bonus. Fair warning: you will have it in your head for three weeks straight and you'll be humming it in the grocery store and a stranger will look at you.

**"Chrysanthemum" by Kevin Henkes** — A little advanced for three, but worth a try if your kid is verbal and emotionally tuned in. Chrysanthemum loves her name until school starts and classmates make fun of it. The feelings around not fitting in and then finding your people are timeless. If your kid is starting school anxious about being "different" in some way, this one is worth reading even if they don't catch every nuance. The illustrations alone carry the emotional arc — you can tell what's happening from the faces even if the words go over their heads.

**"Little School" by Beth Norling** — Much lesser-known. Short. Gentle. Shows the actual physical things at school — the coat hooks, the snack table, the rug for story time — which is quietly brilliant for kids who are scared of the unknown. Sometimes the fear isn't separation, it's "I don't know where anything is." This book answers the small practical questions. Where does my bag go? What does a teacher look like up close? Good for the kid who needs to mentally map new environments before they enter them.

**"Mouse's First Day of School" by Lauren Thompson** — A mouse hides in a backpack and comes to school as a stowaway. Everything is new and a little scary and then wonderful. Your three-year-old will probably want to pretend they're the mouse, which is a great game to play on the way to school. Role play is how three-year-olds process things. Let them be the mouse. You can be the backpack.

## How to use these without overdoing it

Don't read seven books in one sitting. That's a lot. Pick two, rotate, let them ask for the one they want. The one they keep asking for is the one that's meeting them where they are.

When they point at the worried face in Wemberly Worried, don't rush to say "but school is FUN!" Sit with it a second. "She's feeling worried. That makes sense. New places can feel scary." That's the whole job right there. You don't have to fix the feeling to acknowledge it.

You know that wobbly-chin moment at drop-off? Yeah. The book won't stop that. But it gives you a shared language. "Remember the invisible string?" can get you out of the parking lot. "Grown-ups come back, like Daniel Tiger" can get you through the classroom door. The scripts matter.

## A note on what not to do

Don't use the books to promise something you can't guarantee. "School will be SO fun, just like Llama Llama!" is setting you up for a very difficult conversation on day two when school is loud and confusing and their best friend from daycare isn't there. The books that work are the ones that say "this is hard AND you'll find your way." Not "this will be great."

## When a book isn't enough

If your three-year-old is still in genuine distress after a few weeks — not just the five-minute cry but not eating, not playing, clinging to teachers all day, coming home emotionally wrecked — that's worth talking to your pediatrician about. Ask the teacher too. They've seen a lot of transitions and they'll know if it's typical adjustment or something to look into. Books are a conversation starter, not a treatment plan. There's nothing wrong with needing more support.

## Make a book made just for your child

Custom picture books exist now and they're not the cheesy "insert name here" things from the mall. Plubis generates a full illustrated picture book about your actual child — their name, their situation, with them as the main character. "Mia was nervous about the first day of school" hits differently than "Wemberly was nervous about the first day of school." Takes five minutes to make. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about losing a grandparent
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a grandparent dying. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a strange age for grief. They understand more than two-year-olds. They have real questions. But their logic is still very literal — "if she died why can't we get her back" is a completely reasonable question from a three-year-old brain. They're not being difficult. They genuinely don't get permanence yet.

And you're exhausted. You're probably grieving yourself.

Some days you answer the same question four times before breakfast and you don't know how you're doing it. You're doing it because you love them. That's all there is.

Let's find you a book.

## What grief looks like at three

Three-year-olds are starting to understand that death is different from going away. But they might not get that it's forever — at least not right away. They'll ask where the person went. They'll ask to call them. They might seem totally fine for three days and then fall apart at the grocery store when they see something that reminds them.

That's normal. That's how grief works at three. It comes in waves. It leaks out sideways.

Watch for sleep disruption. Death makes their world feel unsafe. Bedtime routines matter more than ever right now. Watch for regression too — accidents, baby talk, wanting to be carried. This is their way of saying "I need to feel small and held right now." And watch for magical thinking: they might tell friends that their grandparent went on a trip, or decide that if they're really good the grandparent will come back. Don't correct it too harshly. Meet it gently.

You can't prepare for all of it. You can just stay available.

## Book picks for three-year-olds

**"Lifetimes" by Bryan Mellonie and Robert Ingpen** — Still the best starting point for this age. Begins with plants and animals — beginnings, endings, the living in between. Brings it around to people gently. The watercolor illustrations are calm and soft. Good for kids who need to ease in.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — "Can love go all the way to where Grandpa is?" Yes. That's the whole book, basically. It's simple and it's true. Three-year-olds will get it.

**"Goodbye Grandpa" by Steven Hartman** (check your library) — Specifically about a grandfather, which matters if your child had a grandfather die. Direct, warm, child-level.

**"Everett Anderson's Goodbye" by Lucille Clifton** — This one's a quiet masterpiece. Everett moves through the stages of grief after his father dies. Rich, poetic, honest. Some of it is for slightly older kids, but even at three, there are pages and lines that land. "I know... that love doesn't stop." Read it for yourself first.

**"The Goodbye Book" by Todd Parr** — If the child is struggling to name feelings, this book does that work. Big faces, simple words, full emotional permission. Hard to go wrong with Todd Parr when you're dealing with anything tender.

**"Always and Forever" by Alan Durant** — The community of forest animals grieves together, remembers together, and slowly finds joy again. It doesn't pretend grief disappears. Three-year-olds track the emotions in pictures well. This one has staying power — you'll re-read it.

"Lifetimes" is the one I'd buy first if you're ordering something tonight. "The Goodbye Book" is a good second. The rest: try your library.

## How to read these when you're also grieving

I want to say something that doesn't usually get said in these articles: you're allowed to be sad in front of your kid.

Not collapsed. Not unable to function. But real. If you tear up reading about a grandparent, that teaches your three-year-old something true: that sadness is allowed, that love leaves a mark, that people cry because they care.

You don't need a performance of okay-ness. You need to be honestly, gently present.

Read in short bursts. If attention wanders, close the book and pick it up later. Let them ask questions mid-page — stop reading, answer, come back. Revisit the same book many times. Kids process through repetition. Don't force it. If they push the book away tonight, try again in a few days.

And don't expect one book to do all the work. It won't. Books are conversation-starters, not therapists.

## The power of keeping rituals — a bonus section

This matters more than any book.

Whatever the grandparent did with your child — a game, a song, a food, a phrase, a thing they always said — keep it going. Say the person's name. "We're making Grandma's bread today." "This is the song Grandpa always sang to you."

At three, they're building the internal world that will carry this person forward. Help them build it by being specific. Not "Grandma was a good person" (too abstract) but "Grandma always smelled like rose powder and she was the only one who could make you stop crying as a baby."

Those specifics will matter forever.

## When a book isn't enough — conversation starters

"What do you remember about Grandpa?" — even at three they have memories. Let them say them. Listen without correcting.

"What do you miss about her?" — Let them answer. Even if the answer is "her cookies" or "her TV show." That's real.

"It's okay to feel sad. I feel sad too. We love him."

"He can't come back, but we can always think about him and love him."

Keep it short. Keep it true. Repeat it as many times as they need.

## Make a book made for your child

Every book on that list is about a bear or a rabbit or a fictional kid. None of them are about your child's grandparent — the specific person with the specific smell and the specific chair they always sat in.

Plubis makes a picture book about the real grief your child is carrying. You share a bit about your child and the grandparent they lost. We generate a book that speaks to *their* specific moment — in five minutes, downloadable as a PDF.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about the loss of a pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through losing a pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
My neighbor's daughter was three when their cat died. She kept setting a food bowl out for two weeks after. Not because she didn't understand. She understood more than they thought. She just wasn't ready for it to be over.

That's a three-year-old with grief. They get it — and then they don't — and then they really, really do, all at once, usually at 7pm when you're trying to get dinner on the table.

## What's different at three

Three-year-olds have language now. Real language. They'll say "I'm sad about Goldie" or "I want the dog to come back" and mean it completely. They can follow a story with emotional complexity. They can ask follow-up questions. And they will. The hard ones.

The good news: because they have words, you have more to work with. You can say "that's called missing someone" and they might actually hold onto that. You can talk about grief and have something close to a real conversation, not just a one-sided monologue into a small confused face.

The books that work best at three are ones that name the emotions clearly, show a character going through something messy and real, and don't wrap up too quickly. Three-year-olds have a surprisingly sharp detector for false cheerfulness. They'll reject books that feel fake.

## Books that actually land at this age

**"Goodbye Mousie" by Robie H. Harris, illustrated by Jan Ormerod**

Still the best starting point. The boy in this book is basically three: he gets angry at Mousie for dying, he wants to put Mousie's favorite things in the burial box, he cries, and then — slowly — he starts to feel a little better. Harris doesn't rush any of it. For a three-year-old who just lost a pet, reading this one is like having someone say "I know. I've been there too." The anger section especially is valuable. Three-year-olds need to know that being mad is allowed.

**"The Goodbye Book" by Todd Parr**

Todd Parr's fish has lost his companion and doesn't know what to do with himself. The book cycles through feeling sad, feeling mad, not wanting to eat, and finally — not "getting better" exactly, but being held through it. It's simple and a little loud (Parr's illustrations always feel like they're slightly yelling at you in a friendly way) and it's perfect for kids this age. Short enough to read twice in one sitting. Three-year-olds often ask for it again immediately.

**"I'll Always Love You" by Hans Wilhelm**

A classic for a reason. Elfie the dachshund grows up alongside a boy, ages while the boy ages, and one morning doesn't wake up. The boy's siblings want a new dog right away. He isn't ready. He keeps thinking of how he always told Elfie "I'll always love you" before bed — and his neighbor never said that to their dog, and now it's too late. It's a book about love and what we say out loud. Three-year-olds respond to it emotionally even if they can't articulate why. Bring a tissue. Seriously.

**"Dog Heaven" by Cynthia Rylant**

If your family had a dog, get this one. Rylant paints a heaven full of biscuits and running and children who need a dog to love — and somehow it manages to be genuinely comforting rather than dismissive. The illustrations glow. For a three-year-old who keeps asking "but where IS our dog now?", this gives them something warm to picture. Rylant also wrote **"Cat Heaven"** for cat families — same format, equally beautiful.

**"When a Pet Dies" by Fred Rogers**

The photographs date it but the wisdom doesn't. Rogers says directly: the pet really is dead, and that means it won't come back. That honesty matters for three-year-olds who are testing whether the grown-ups are telling them the real thing. He also says: it's okay to feel really sad, and it won't feel like this forever. Worth finding at your library.

**"The Invisible Leash" by Patrice Karst, illustrated by Joanne Lew-Vriethoff**

From the author of the beloved "Invisible String" — this companion book applies the same idea to pet loss. Even when a pet dies, an invisible leash connects your heart to theirs. It's slightly abstract for a two-year-old, but three-year-olds generally get it — and love it. A lot of parents report their kids start "feeling the leash" on their own after reading it. That's a good sign. It's a concept they'll carry for years.

## Reading it together without making it a lesson

Here's the thing — this can't be a curriculum. Three-year-olds bolt from anything that feels like a lecture. Just read the book. When they point at something, respond. When they say "like our cat," say "yes. Just like our cat."

If they go quiet and stare at a page, let them stare. Don't rush to turn it. Some pages do that to kids — they need a moment to be in them.

And if they start acting out the story with stuffed animals after — perfect. That's exactly what play is for at this age. Let it happen. It's not morbid. It's healthy.

## When a book isn't enough

Some three-year-olds will seem fine for two weeks and then suddenly fall apart over something unrelated — and it's actually still the pet. That's normal. You can bring the book back out. You can say "I think you're still sad about Biscuit." You don't need a therapist to say that. You just need to name it.

## Make a book made for your child

None of these books have your pet's name. None of them describe the specific spot on the couch your cat always chose, or the way your dog greeted your kid every single morning. A custom book holds that. At Plubis, you can make a picture book that's actually about your child and your specific pet — their name, their personality, what made them special. Five minutes to make. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about being more independent
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through independence and big-kid skills. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds are negotiators. They will explain, at length, why they are capable of doing the thing, why you're wrong to doubt them, and why the fact that they have been crying for six minutes proves how serious they are about doing it themselves.

They insist on pouring their own milk. You know how this ends.

But here's the thing — they're not wrong to insist. The pushing-for-independence at three is developmentally exactly where it should be. They're building the internal model of themselves as capable people. Every time they try something and succeed (even partially), that model gets a little stronger. And every time they're blocked from trying — even helpfully, even because breakfast is going to be on the ceiling otherwise — that model takes a small hit.

So you let them pour the milk. You keep a mop nearby. You read them books about characters figuring things out on their own.


## *Ladybug Girl* by Jacky Davis, illustrated by David Soman

Lulu puts on her ladybug costume and immediately becomes someone with a different set of capabilities and a different relationship to the backyard. Her older brothers won't let her play with them. Her dog Bingo is her only companion. So she goes outside and makes her own adventure, completely unsupervised by narrative adults.

This is a New York Times bestselling book, and it earned that. Because what it gets right is something most independence books don't bother with: Lulu doesn't need to be taught to be independent. She already is. Nobody has to encourage her or explain the value of self-reliance. She just decides what she wants to do and does it.

For three-year-olds who are already this way — and most of them are — this book is a mirror. For three-year-olds who are timider, it's permission. The illustrations are gorgeous. Bingo the basset hound is the best supporting character in children's literature.

Slight hot take: this is better than a lot of the "you can do it!" books precisely because it doesn't moralize. Lulu doesn't need a lesson. She needs an afternoon.


## *All by Myself* by Mercer Mayer

Yes, this book appears on the 2-year-old list too. That's intentional, because it genuinely works across the ages of 2-5 and the reasons shift each time.

At two, they like it because Little Critter is doing things. At three, they start to *evaluate* Little Critter's performance. They'll notice when he doesn't quite succeed. They'll laugh at the gap between his confidence and his execution. And this is new — this is three-year-old cognition kicking in, the ability to hold two things at once: "he thinks he can do it" and "but he can't quite do it yet."

Mercer Mayer has been writing about this small, disheveled, lovable creature since 1975. Little Critter is the patron saint of kids who are trying. Not kids who have succeeded — kids who are in the middle of it, kind of messy, completely earnest.

It's a quick read. It's funny. Your three-year-old will identify with it even when they're four.


## *Leo the Late Bloomer* by Robert Kraus, illustrated by Jose Aruego

Leo can't read. Leo can't write. Leo can't draw. Leo can barely talk. His father watches anxiously. His mother says: he'll do it when he's ready.

She's right.

This book is for every three-year-old who isn't there yet on something — and for every parent who is quietly worried about it. Leo's story is slow by design. He watches other animals do things he can't. He tries. He fails. Nothing happens. And then, in his own time, Leo blooms.

Here's what I love about it and what I think parents miss: Leo's mother isn't passive. She's not ignoring the situation. She's actively choosing not to intervene, because she trusts her kid's timeline. That's a genuinely hard thing for parents of three-year-olds to do, because three is the age where everyone starts talking about "readiness" and "milestones."

This book won't resolve your anxiety about your kid's development. Nothing will. But it will give you something to hold onto during the waiting, which is most of parenting.


## *Owl Babies* by Martin Waddell, illustrated by Patrick Benson

Still worth reading at three, even though it skews slightly younger. At three, children start to *understand* the story in a more sophisticated way — they're not just experiencing it, they're interpreting it.

A three-year-old watching the three owl babies handle their mother's absence will notice the difference between Sarah (who thinks things through), Percy (who spirals a bit), and Bill (who just keeps repeating that he wants his mommy). They'll pick a favourite. They'll probably be Bill, but they'll *say* they're Sarah.

The payoff is still the best ending in picture books for this age group. When the mother returns and the owls go wild, there's genuine joy on the page. And your three-year-old — who is simultaneously trying to be independent and still very, very attached to you — needs to see that the independence and the attachment can coexist.


## *I Can Do It Myself!* by Diane Adams

Emily Pearl is a toddler who won't stop insisting she can do things herself. "I can do it, I can do it" is her refrain. She carries a package all by herself to deliver to her grandmother. The package is big. She's small. She insists.

And then it gets dark. And she is scared. And she needs her mother.

What I like about this book is that it doesn't treat independence and needing help as opposites. Emily is both: fiercely capable and genuinely afraid, sometimes in the same afternoon. That's true. That's honest. And it's a useful thing for a three-year-old to see, because three is the age where they're starting to notice that wanting to be big and feeling scared can both be real at the same time.

This book doesn't have the name recognition of some of the others on this list. That's a mistake. It handles its subject with more nuance than most.


## What three-year-old independence actually demands from you

Three-year-olds want to do things themselves. But they also want you to watch. This is not contradictory — they need the audience. They need to know you've seen them do the thing, that you registered it, that it counted.

The research on this is pretty clear: kids develop independence faster when parents let them try and pay attention to the attempt, not just the result. The attempt matters. The trying-and-failing-and-trying-again matters. What doesn't help is either doing it for them (obviously) or not watching at all (less obvious).

So the move at three is: step back, stay present, watch the thing happen. Let them struggle for longer than feels comfortable. Be there when they look up. That look — the "did you see that?" look — is what they're actually after.

Books support this by showing characters who struggle and then succeed, or who struggle and don't succeed but keep going. Either ending works. The struggling part is the point.


## The books that didn't make this list and why

There are a lot of books about independence for three-year-olds that are basically just cheerleading: "You can do it! You're amazing! Believe in yourself!" They have good intentions. But your three-year-old doesn't need a pep talk. They have no shortage of confidence. What they need is a story that takes the attempt seriously — the mess of it, the failure of it, the trying again of it.

The books on this list do that. They're not pep talks. They're companions.

If your three-year-old loves any of these, Mercer Mayer has a whole Little Critter series. Jacky Davis and David Soman have multiple Ladybug Girl books. You're not running out anytime soon.


## One more option: make the book about them

Here's a thing that works especially well at three, when kids are starting to really understand narrative and character: a book where they are the main character. Not a book *like* them — a book *about* them. With their name. Their face. Their specific big-kid skill they've been working on.

Three-year-olds are at the exact age where seeing themselves as the hero of a story does something real. They return to it. They internalize it differently than they internalize a book about Lulu or Little Critter.

**The first one is free.**

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about a friend moving away
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a friend moving away. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds are in this strange in-between place where they understand way more than we think they do, and way less than they let on.

Your kid is starting to grasp that when someone moves away, they don't come back on Tuesday. That it's not like a grandparent visit. That "far away" is different from "at their house." But they can't fully hold that understanding yet — it slips. One moment they'll say "can we call Leo?" with total matter-of-factness, and the next they'll have a meltdown at the playground because Leo isn't there, even though they seemed perfectly fine about it at breakfast.

That whiplash is exhausting for everyone. And it's completely normal.

Books are useful right now not just for the emotional processing — though that matters — but because three-year-olds are starting to tell stories. They're starting to make sense of the world through narrative. A book about a friend moving away gives them a scaffold. A shape. "Oh, it's like in the book."

Here's what actually helps.

## What works at age 3

Three-year-olds can handle a little more story than two-year-olds. You can have an actual plot arc now — friend is here, friend leaves, this feels bad, here's what we do about it. They can follow that.

What they can't handle: vague or abstract reassurances. "Friendships last forever" means nothing to a three-year-old. "You can draw her a picture and we'll mail it" means everything.

The best books at this age show a child actively doing something with the sad feeling. Not just sitting in it (though that matters too) — but finding a tangible, concrete way to stay connected or to honor the friendship. Three-year-olds respond to *actions* they can imagine doing themselves.

Also: humor helps. Absurdity helps. A book that's mostly sad the whole way through will lose them. They need a moment that makes them smile, even if the smile and the sad are right next to each other on the same page.

## Book picks

**"Goodbye, Friend! Hello, Friend!" by Cori Doerrfeld** — Still on the list at age three, maybe even more appropriate here than at two. The story builds slowly through the seasons — Stella and Charlie say goodbye to leaves, to snow, to spring flowers — so by the time the hardest goodbye comes, you've been practicing together. The illustrations are soft and specific, with just enough detail to invite questions. "What's she looking at?" "Where do you think Charlie went?" A three-year-old can actually have that conversation now. The ending is bittersweet rather than tidy, which I think is exactly right.

**"In a Jar" by Deborah Marcero** — This one is quietly beautiful and a little bit magical, which is perfect for three. Llewellyn loves collecting things in jars — a firefly, some autumn light, the reflection of the moon on a pond. When his best friend moves away, he finds a way to still share what he's collected. The illustrations are luminous, watercolor-warm, with a slightly old-fashioned storybook feel. The central metaphor — that you can gather good things and pass them along even across distance — is both genuinely lovely and surprisingly accessible to a child who is three and a half and very literal-minded. Ask your kid: "What would you put in your jar for your friend?"

**"Llama Llama Misses Mama" by Anna Dewdney** — A classic that earns its place on this list again at three because the feelings are so well-rendered. Missing is missing, whether it's mama at school or a friend who moved. The arc of this book — the waves of sadness, the distraction, the eventual okay-ness — mirrors exactly what children go through. Three-year-olds who already know and love this series will find real comfort in familiar characters handling a familiar emotion.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Sometimes criticized for being a bit heavy-handed, and honestly, yeah, a little. But the central idea is beautiful and three is exactly the right age for it — old enough to hold the metaphor, young enough to genuinely believe it. A mother explains to her two children that everyone who loves each other is connected by an invisible string of love, no matter how far apart they are. You can take it literally ("feel your string right now — it goes all the way to Leo's house") and your three-year-old will press a hand to their chest and feel it. I've seen it happen. The magic is real at this age. Use it.

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Chester Raccoon's mother presses a kiss into the palm of his hand before he goes to school, so he carries her love wherever he goes. At three, this book opens up a whole ritual you can create: press a kiss into your kid's palm and tell them their friend put a kiss there too, before they left. Then your kid can press it to their cheek whenever they miss them. It sounds corny. It works. The book is warm and gentle, the illustrations soft and inviting. Chester's reluctance is real and relatable.

**"Oh No! Time to Go!" by Rebecca Doughty** — A fun, rhyming romp through different kinds of goodbyes — from the everyday (goodbye, breakfast!) to the bigger ones (goodbye, best friend who moved away). It's genuinely funny in places and three-year-olds who are in a rhyme phase will want it read again immediately. The tone is gently bittersweet rather than heavy. Good for nights when you need the sad to be a little lighter. Good for kids who clam up with serious books but open up when they're laughing.

**"A Kiss Goodbye" by Audrey Penn** — Chester Raccoon's family is leaving their beloved tree. Chester has to say goodbye to the woods, the stream, the tree itself. It's about a *place* goodbye as much as a people goodbye, which might resonate if your child's friend group was tied to a specific spot — a daycare, a backyard, a playground. The grief in this book is specific and physical, and Penn handles it gently. Your three-year-old might want to talk about places they miss too. Let them.

## How to read these together

Stop more. Ask more questions. Three is the age of infinite questions and you can work with that.

Pause on the page where the character looks sad. "How do you think she feels right now?" Your kid might say "sad." Or they might say "like when I didn't get the yellow cup." Both answers are correct and useful. Don't redirect — follow it.

Don't skip the sad parts to get to the hopeful ending. Sit in the middle of the book for a second. "This part is really hard, isn't it." That's not a question. It's permission.

If your kid acts out scenes from the book afterward — packing boxes, saying pretend goodbyes, waving to imaginary friends — that's beautiful. That's play-processing. Don't interfere with it. Participate if invited.

## When it keeps coming up

Three-year-olds will ask about the friend. A lot. Sometimes daily for a while, then suddenly not at all, then again out of nowhere three weeks later while you're at the grocery store.

"Where is Leo now?" Give them an honest, simple answer every time. "He lives in a different city now, really far away." Don't add a whole reassurance speech — that's for you, not them. Just answer the question.

A photo somewhere they can see it helps. Not a shrine — just a photo. Evidence that the friend was real and the friendship was real.

At three, a video call can be golden. Keep it short and unscripted. Let them show each other toys. That's enough.

## Bonus: the one question worth asking

Try this: "What's your favorite thing you and Leo ever did together?"

Then just listen. Don't fill the silence. Don't add your memory of it. Just listen.

Three-year-olds remember in fragments — a color, a sound, a specific silly moment. When they tell you about it, what they're doing is making the friendship permanent. They're saying: this happened. This was real. Nobody can take that.

That conversation, honestly, is worth more than any book. But the book gets you there.

## Make a book made for your child

Your kid's friendship isn't generic. It had specific inside jokes, specific rituals, a specific playground where something specific always happened. A published book can get close to the feeling, but it can't use Leo's name. It can't describe the exact game they made up.

At Plubis, you can make a custom picture book about this exact friendship, with your child's name and their friend's name on every page. For a three-year-old who is starting to understand that stories are about *real things*, hearing their own story read back to them is genuinely powerful.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about making new friends
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through making friends. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
My kid walked up to a child at the park and said, with complete confidence, "You are my best friend now." The other child stared at her for three full seconds, said "No," and went back to the slide.

My kid stood there for a moment like she was recalibrating. And then she followed the kid to the slide anyway.

Three-year-olds are incredible. They have approximately zero social strategy and somehow unlimited resilience. The rejection doesn't land the way it will in two years. They just... recalibrate. Try again. Walk up to the next person. The boldness is breathtaking and also slightly terrifying because you know the world is going to eventually teach them to be more careful, and you're watching the last months of the before.

But here's the thing about three: they're also starting to really want friendship. Not just parallel play, not just proximity. They want a BEST FRIEND. They will say those exact words. "She's my best friend" about someone they met eleven minutes ago. The longing is genuine and enormous and they don't yet have the vocabulary or the skills to do much with it besides wave it around like a flag.

That gap between the wanting and the knowing-how is where books live.

## What a three-year-old is actually working on socially

At three, kids are learning that other people have inner lives. It's called theory of mind and it's still mostly under construction at this age — fully solidifying around four. What this means practically: your kid is only beginning to understand that the other child at the sandbox might not want to be hugged. Might have their own plan. Might be nervous about strangers, same as your kid is sometimes nervous.

This is enormous developmental work and it requires patience and repetition and a LOT of reading books about characters with faces.

Three-year-olds are also learning to wait, take turns, tolerate frustration. All of which are foundational friend skills. All of which they are famously not good at yet. This is fine. This is supposed to take years. Don't panic when your kid grabs the toy.

What books can do at three: give characters to point at, name the feelings, model what someone does when they're nervous about walking up to a new person. The "Will I Have a Friend?" question — said out loud, in a book, by a character who feels it the same way your kid does — does something that your reassurance can't quite do on its own.

## The books

**"Will I Have a Friend?" by Miriam Cohen** — The title IS the whole thing. Jim is starting school and he has one question going through his head all morning: will I have a friend? Not "will I like school" or "will the teacher be nice." Just that. The pure, essential anxiety of not yet belonging to anyone. The ending is quiet and real — Jim finds someone, but it's a slow finding, not a dramatic click. Cohen does not wrap things up too quickly and I love her for it. This is the best book on this entire list for the specific fear of "what if nobody wants to be my friend."

**"A Weekend with Wendell" by Kevin Henkes** — Wendell comes to Sophie's house for the weekend and proceeds to dominate every game, change every rule, and generally be an exhausting guest in the way that only certain children can be. Sophie is frustrated the whole time. But something shifts by the end. It's not a neat lesson — it's more like: sometimes the annoying kid becomes the kid you understand, and that's weird, and also friendship. This book is GREAT for three-year-olds who have encountered a domineering playmate (which is basically all of them) because it names the frustration without making it a villain story.

**"Best Friends" by Miriam Cohen** — A companion of sorts to "Will I Have a Friend?" — same characters, same gentle honest world. Jim has a best friend now, but something happens to complicate that. Cohen keeps writing the full complicated picture of early friendship: the wanting, the losing, the coming back. Read both together if you can find them. Your library will likely have at least one.

**"Frog and Toad Are Friends" by Arnold Lobel** — I know it looks like a book for older kids, and technically it's an early reader chapter book, but three-year-olds who are ready to sit for stories longer than ten pages will absolutely be held by Frog and Toad. Each chapter is a small story about two friends doing ordinary things together — one of them impulsive and warm (Frog), one of them cautious and grumpy (Toad). The friendship is complicated and real and wonderful. The chapter where Toad writes Frog a letter just so Frog will have something to wait for — your three-year-old won't fully understand it and will absorb the whole thing anyway. This is a book worth buying, not just borrowing. We've read ours to pieces.

**"The Grouchy Ladybug" by Eric Carle** — Strictly this is about manners, not friendship. But watch how your three-year-old responds to the grouchy ladybug who challenges everyone to a fight and then, exhausted and humbled, comes home to share. The social emotional arc — arrogance, failure, softening, belonging — is something three-year-olds track intuitively even when they couldn't explain it. Plus Eric Carle's ladybug is a genuinely funny character to read aloud. Give her a voice. Go full grouchy. Your kid will collapse.

**"Chester's Way" by Kevin Henkes** — Chester and Wilson do everything the same way, in the same order, all the time. Lilly moves in. Lilly does things her own way. Chester and Wilson think this is wrong. Then Lilly saves them from bullies and everything shifts. It's a book about the friend you didn't expect, the one who disrupts your comfortable routine and turns out to be the exact person you needed. For three-year-olds, the entry point is usually the bullies scene — dramatic, relatable — and the friendship follows.

## Reading these without it feeling like a lesson

Three-year-olds can smell an agenda. If you sit down with "Will I Have a Friend?" and say "I got this because of what happened at playgroup today" — the book is already poisoned. They'll fight it.

Just bring the book. Read it like it's interesting. Point at Jim's face at the beginning and say "he looks worried. I wonder about what." That's all. The conversation that follows will surprise you. Three-year-olds have more to say about this stuff than you think — they just need to come to it sideways, not head-on.

Also: don't insist on the whole book in one sitting. If they want to stop halfway, stop. If they want to go back to one page eight times, go back. The page they keep returning to is usually the one doing the most work.

## The part where they tell you

My neighbor's kid, three and a half, told her mom that she had "no friends" at school one night out of nowhere at dinner. Not crying, not upset. Just said it. "I have no friends at school." And her mom just sat there for a second because what do you say to that?

The answer, it turned out, was: "That sounds lonely. What's it like at lunch?" And then the kid talked for ten minutes. She HAD friends — she just also felt something that needed a word, and "no friends" was the best word she had for it at three.

The books give better words. That's a lot of what they're doing. Not teaching social skills directly but expanding the vocabulary for what friendship feels like — the wanting of it, the uncertainty, the specific relief when you finally find your person.

## What not to say

Don't tell a three-year-old "just go say hi!" and then expect that to work. They know how to say hi. The saying-hi is not the problem. The problem is the after — what happens when the hi doesn't get a response, when the other kid keeps playing, when the invitation is ignored.

Also don't promise: "You'll make so many friends!" You don't know that. They won't believe it anyway, on some level. What you can say honestly: "I know it feels hard. I'm going to be here after and you can tell me everything."

That one you can actually keep.

## A book made just for your child

Here's what none of these books do: use your child's name. Show the specific playground where they always try to find a friend and sometimes do and sometimes don't. Put them in the center of the story instead of alongside it. Plubis builds custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. You put in your child's name and the situation and we build something with them as the main character — the one doing the brave thing of walking toward someone new. For a three-year-old who asks "will I have a friend" out of nowhere at dinner, hearing a book that says "yes, you will, and here's what it might look like" with their actual name in it — that's different. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about moving to a new home
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through moving house. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds are just old enough to understand that the move is happening. And just young enough to have absolutely no idea what to do with that information.

They can tell you "we're going to a new house" and then dissolve into hysterics because their frog cup isn't where it normally is. They're grieving something they can't name. They're excited in ways that tip suddenly into terror. Sometimes in the same ten minutes.

This is a genuinely hard age to move with. Not the hardest — that's probably four or five, when the social losses really land — but hard in its own specific way. The vocabulary is starting to arrive but the emotional regulation isn't there yet. They feel everything acutely and they're working with limited tools.

Books are one of the best tools available right now.

## What a three-year-old actually needs from a book about moving

At three, kids can follow a story with a beginning, middle, and end. They can track a character's emotional arc over several pages. They're starting to have real conversations about feelings — "I'm sad" and "I'm scared" are things they can say and mean and recognize in a face on a page.

That's a meaningful upgrade from two. A story that shows a character scared about moving, going through the move, and then finding something good in the new place — that arc can land in a real way at three. It's not just a feeling they absorb through osmosis; it's a story they can hold and return to.

But don't overload them. A book with too much plot, too many characters, or too much nuance will lose them and it'll just be noise at bedtime. Short. Warm. Emotionally honest. Resolution that isn't too rushed but isn't dragged out either.

Board books are still fine at three — especially for home copies. A well-constructed picture book with simple language works now too, in a way it didn't quite at two.

And one more thing: three-year-olds will tell you what a book means to them by asking for it again. The one they want every night is the one doing the emotional work. Trust that. Don't take it as repetitiveness — take it as engagement.

## The books I'd actually reach for

**"Goodbye House" by Frank Asch** — Still the first one I'd reach for at three, maybe even more so than at two, because now they can actually follow the ritual. Bear walks through every room in the empty house saying goodbye — the kitchen, the bedroom, the backyard — and says goodbye to the memories in each place. Then they drive to the new house. The ending is gentle and hopeful without being falsely cheerful. At three, your child can walk through the ritual with Bear in a way that mirrors what you might actually do on moving day. Say goodbye to their room. Say goodbye to the backyard. Do that closure thing that feels slightly odd but really helps. This book is what gives kids permission to do that. Read it before the move. Maybe do the actual goodbye walk-through inspired by it. That's a real gift.

**"The Leaving Morning" by Angela Johnson, illustrated by David Soman** — This is a gem and not enough people know it. Two siblings on the morning they leave their apartment — the smells of the last breakfast, leaving sticky kisses on the windows, watching the movers, the strange empty sound of a home that's already becoming the past. It's written in a child's voice and it's sensory and specific in the way that only good writing is. Angela Johnson writes childhood the way it actually feels, not the way adults think it feels. The illustrations are warm and slightly wistful. This one is for the child who needs to sit with the grief of leaving rather than skip past it. That's valid. That's important. Don't rush through the sad pages to get to the hopeful ending. Let them feel it.

**"The Berenstain Bears' Moving Day" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — By three they can follow the whole story, not just the pictures. Small Bear misses his friends in the valley. He's skeptical about the hill. Things get better but not instantly. The pacing is honest — they don't land at the new house and immediately go "this is great!" There's a period of adjustment shown, which is the actual truth of a move. The Berenstain Bears art style is comfortable for kids who've seen it before. And if they haven't, the rounded warm palette still reads as safe. This is the most likely book to be on someone's shelf already at this age; grab it from the library if you don't have it.

**"I'm Not Moving, Mama!" by Nancy White Carlstrom** — Little Mouse refuses to go. Hard stop. He lists every single thing he'll miss and his mama listens to all of it without arguing. At three this lands especially well because three-year-olds are experts at digging in. They see Little Mouse doing exactly what they want to do and they feel deeply seen. And then they see the mama not fighting it, just staying warm and present until the resistance gradually softens. That's a useful thing to watch happen, even in a picture book. Especially in a picture book. Try the library for this one — it's older so it's not on every shelf.

**"A Chair for My Mother" by Vera B. Williams** — This is about moving after a fire, not a typical house move, so the circumstances are more dramatic. But the core emotional experience — a family starting over in a new place, building a home with what they have, the community around them — is deeply resonant for any three-year-old going through transition. The illustrations are bold and bright, painted in this folk-art style that children love. The little girl at the center is resilient in an unsentimental way. And the jar of coins that the family saves up for the perfect chair is one of those images that stays with children. Best on this list for the kid who needs to see that you can make a home from the ground up, with love and time.

**"Who Will Be My Friends?" by Syd Hoff** — Freddie moves to a new neighborhood and doesn't know anyone. The whole book is him figuring out how to find friends — he tries one thing, then another, then something works. It's one of the very first picture books ever written about this exact experience and it holds up. Simple illustrations, simple sentences, completely direct. For the three-year-old who's less worried about the house and more worried about where the friends went, this is exactly the right book. Short enough to read twice if they ask. They might ask.

**"Franklin's New Friend" by Paulette Bourgeois** — Moose is new to the neighborhood, nervous and a little withdrawn. Franklin works up the courage to include him. What's useful here for a three-year-old who's moving is the flip: they get to see themselves as potentially the new Moose — and to see that the Franklins of the world exist. Kids who are kind. Kids who will say hello. The book plants a seed that the new neighborhood might have a Franklin in it, which is a more useful hope than "everything will be great."

## Reading them together without overthinking it

Three-year-olds are good at questions. "Why is he sad?" "Is that his old house?" "Will he see his friends again?" Let the questions come. Don't have every answer. "I'm not sure — what do you think?" is a completely legitimate response that turns the book into a real conversation.

If they want to stop before the end and just look at one page for a while, stop. Let them look. They're working something out. The page they linger on is usually the one that's meeting them where they are.

Read these in the weeks before the move, not just after. The before-reading plants the frame. The after-reading gives them something to connect to experience. Both matter.

Don't force the ritual if they're melting down. Sometimes the book needs to wait for a calmer moment. You know your kid.

## The goodbye ritual

This isn't really about the books, but it lives alongside them. If you can, do a slow walk through the house before you hand over the keys. Let them lead. Let them say goodbye to their room, the backyard, the spot on the carpet they always sat in for cartoons. Three-year-olds respond to rituals and ceremonies in a way that's surprising. The goodbye that has a shape — that has a beginning and an end — is easier to move past than the one that just stops.

"Goodbye House" by Frank Asch is worth rereading the night before specifically for this reason. It gives the ritual a script.

## When a book isn't enough

A move can trigger real regression at three — suddenly using the potty less reliably, sleep problems, a return to behaviors they'd moved past. That's not failure. That's a stressed three-year-old communicating the only way they have access to. If it's intense or prolonged past a few weeks, check in with your pediatrician. Books open the conversation. They don't close the wound.

## Make a book made just for your child

At three, your child is starting to understand "this story is about me." That changes everything. Plubis builds custom illustrated picture books around your child — their name, their move, their feelings. Five minutes to generate. A book where the character isn't Little Mouse or Small Bear but is actually called by your kid's name, living in a world that mirrors their own. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about learning a new language
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a new language. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds are in this magical, maddening window where language is exploding out of them — new words daily, complete sentences, questions about *everything* — and if your family is raising them with more than one language, this is the age where the code-switching starts to get interesting. They'll say a sentence that's half one language and half another and look completely unbothered. Which is correct. They should be unbothered. That's exactly what healthy multilingual development looks like.

But here's the thing nobody tells you: three is also the age where the social world starts to matter. They notice when their words don't match. They notice when grandma uses different sounds. They notice when the kids at the park don't understand what they just said. This is new, and it can feel strange to them, and some kids handle it with curiosity and some kids handle it with a little shame — which, if that happens, you want to catch early and reverse as fast as possible.

Books do something useful here. Not instruction, not vocabulary drills — just *representation*. A child who sees characters in books who navigate two languages, two worlds, two ways of being, without being treated as broken or confused, absorbs a message that matters. You can't tell a three-year-old "being bilingual is a gift." They'll stare at you. But you can show them a book where it just... is. Normal. Good. Even funny sometimes.

## What three-year-olds can get from these books

At three, they're following plots. Simple ones, but plots. They have opinions about characters — they'll tell you which character is nice and which one is "being mean." They feel things on behalf of the people in the books, which is new and kind of extraordinary to watch.

So for the language topic, the books that work at three are ones with a clear emotional throughline: a character who feels caught between two worlds, or proud of their two languages, or confused about why other people don't understand them — and then a resolution that doesn't feel fake. Your kid will know if the resolution feels fake. Three-year-olds have excellent emotional radar.

Also: bilingual text on the page still works well at three. Reading the same sentence in two languages is more interesting than it sounds, especially if one language is theirs. Watch their face when they suddenly understand the English sentence because they know the Spanish (or Hindi, or Tagalog, or whatever) version. That little click of recognition — that's real.

## Books worth reading right now

**"Alma and How She Got Her Name" by Juana Martinez-Neal** — This one keeps appearing on these lists and it should. Alma has a long name and wants to know where it comes from. Her father explains each name — each ancestor, each piece of family history. For a three-year-old, this is the age where they'll actually track the story. The intergenerational piece (great-great-grandmother, grandfather, names carrying meaning across time) will be partially over their heads, but the core feeling — that your name, your language, your family history is something to be proud of — lands clearly. The illustrations are soft and warm and feel like something a grandmother would paint.

**"Marisol McDonald Doesn't Match / Marisol McDonald no combina" by Monica Brown, illustrated by Sara Palacios** — Marisol doesn't match and she's fine with it. The bilingual format (English on top, Spanish below each page) is clean, and at three your kid can start to notice the pattern — same story, different sounds. Salacios's illustrations are genuinely joyful, almost bouncy. This is one of those books where the message isn't delivered as a lesson, it's just present. Marisol is who she is. She plays soccer in a tutu. She likes pirates and painting. The end.

**"Gracias / Thanks" by Pat Mora, illustrated by John Parra** — A boy thanks all the things in his life — the rain, the lizards, his family, the words his grandmother uses. It's a gratitude book but the cultural specificity is what makes it land: the desert setting, the Spanish words woven in, the grandmother figure. At three, kids love the list structure (it's almost a game — what comes next?) and the illustrations are warm and earthy. A calm book for a calm moment.

**"The Name Jar" by Yangsook Choi** — Unhei has just moved from Korea to the United States and her new classmates can't pronounce her Korean name. They suggest she pick an American name. She keeps her Korean name. This is such a specific, real experience for so many immigrant children, and it's handled with honesty and no sentimentality. At three, the plot is slightly long — but the core emotional question (should I hide part of myself to fit in?) is one that three-year-olds with complicated identities can feel even if they can't articulate it. A book worth rereading. At three, and again at six, and again at ten.

**"Waiting for Papá / Esperando a Papá" by Rene Colato Lainez, illustrated by Anthony Accardo** — Beto has moved to the US with his mother; his father is still in El Salvador waiting for documents to come through. While he waits, Beto learns English so he can teach it to his father when he arrives. This book is tender and hard and real in a way a lot of bilingual-themed books aren't. It doesn't sanitize the experience of family separation or immigration difficulty. If that's close to your family's story, this book might make you cry. That's okay. Your three-year-old will see that and understand that the story matters.
