**"My Diary from Here to There / Mi diario de aquí hasta allá" by Amada Irma Pérez, illustrated by Maya Christina Gonzalez** — A girl writes in her diary about moving from Mexico to the United States. Written in first person, bilingual format, real emotional honesty about fear and loss and hope. For a three-year-old who can sit through a longer story, this is genuinely beautiful. For kids navigating an actual move or transition right now, it's one of the most directly validating books I know. The diary format makes it feel personal in a way that third-person narratives don't.

**"Dreamers" by Yuyi Morales** — Yuyi Morales wrote this as a love letter to her own experience immigrating from Mexico with her infant son. She arrived speaking no English; the library became her classroom. The illustrations are lush and dreamy and a little overwhelming in the best possible way. At three, they'll mostly track the emotion and the art. The actual story of immigration and library visits and language acquisition will reveal more with each reread as they get older. One of the most beautiful picture books published in the last decade, full stop.

## The reading experience, practically speaking

Three-year-olds have opinions. They will tell you which book they want and it will be the same book for three weeks. If they keep choosing one of these over and over, let them. That repetition is doing something. They're processing.

When you're reading a bilingual book, try occasionally reading a sentence in the heritage language and pausing to ask "what does that mean?" Not as a quiz — just curiosity. "Gracias means thank you, right? What else says thank you in your language?" Make it a conversation, not a lesson.

And when they code-switch in conversation — mix languages mid-sentence — respond in one of the languages naturally rather than correcting them. The correction teaches them there's a wrong way to speak. The natural response teaches them both languages are real and used and valid.

## A note on language shame

Some kids at three start to show early signs of language shame — they refuse to speak the heritage language with you in public, they ask you to "talk normal" (meaning the majority language), they're embarrassed when a grandparent uses words their friends don't understand. This is worth taking seriously. Not panicking over, but taking seriously.

Books where characters are proud of their heritage language, where the emotional arc isn't "finally fitting in" but "being yourself," are doing quiet work against that shame. Make the home environment feel rich and warm in the heritage language. Don't let other adults make comments that frame bilingualism as a complication. And if the shame is coming from somewhere specific — a child at school, a comment from a teacher — address it directly.

## When a book isn't enough

If your three-year-old is in a language environment that's genuinely causing them stress — a new country, a new school where no one speaks their home language, sudden immersion — books are one small piece. Connection is the bigger piece. A trusted adult who speaks their language. Time to just be at home where the words make sense. Food that tastes familiar. The books matter but the feeling of "I belong somewhere" matters more.

## Make a book made for your child

Plubis makes custom picture books for the exact situation your child is in — with their name, their two languages, their story at the center. A book where Riya is the one who knows two ways to say "I love you" is a different thing than a book about a fictional character going through something similar. Five minutes to make. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about getting a new pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a new pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
## What nobody tells you: three-year-olds are weird about new pets

Not weird bad. Weird interesting. They're terrified and obsessed simultaneously, which is a state three-year-olds are basically always in, so really a new pet is just an extension of the existing energy.

Your three-year-old has been asking for a pet. Or hasn't asked at all and you decided to get one anyway (risky move, respect). Either way, the animal has arrived, and now your kid is doing one of the following:

A) Screaming with joy and attempting to physically absorb the pet into their body.

B) Standing six feet back with both hands pressed to their mouth, watching it like it might explode.

C) Screaming with joy, immediately touching it, getting scared by a sound it made, crying, then screaming with joy again within the same 90 seconds.

All of these are correct responses. Three is the age of massive emotional swings, of wanting something desperately and then finding the reality of it completely overwhelming. A new pet — real, warm, moving, smelling like an animal — is a LOT when you've only experienced animals in books and on screens.

This is exactly why books about new pets are useful right now. Not because a book will teach your three-year-old to calm down (it will not), but because it gives the big feelings a container. A story about another kid going through the exact same chaos of getting a new animal says: this is a thing that happens, it can be good AND scary, and it turns out okay.

So. Let's find the right books.

## The books (and strong opinions about them)

Three-year-olds need books that meet them where they are: capable of real narrative, starting to understand cause and effect, desperate to know what characters are feeling, and still completely attached to repetition and rhythm. The best books for this age do at least three of those four things.

**"Amos and Boris" by William Steig** — This one is for when the new pet isn't going as smoothly as planned. A mouse and a whale become the world's most unlikely best friends. Steig's language is rich without being inaccessible, and the theme — that love between very different creatures is real and worth the inconvenience — lands quietly but hard. Read it when your kid is being too rough with the new kitten and you want to say something without lecturing.

**"Dog Loves Books" by Louise Yates** — A dog who loves books opens a bookshop and waits for customers who share his passion. The meta quality (a dog in a book about loving books) is funny, but the real hook is the dog's deep earnestness. Three-year-olds find this hugely relatable because they also feel very strongly about things that matter to nobody else in the house. Perfect reading-companion book.

**"Harry the Dirty Dog" by Gene Zion** — Harry runs away to avoid his bath, has adventures, comes back so dirty his family doesn't recognize him, and has to find a way to prove who he is. It sounds simple. It is profoundly emotionally resonant for three-year-olds who are also figuring out identity, belonging, and the complicated feeling of wanting to escape and also desperately wanting to be home. Classic for a reason.

**"Lyle, Lyle, Crocodile" by Bernard Waber** — A crocodile lives in a Manhattan townhouse and is beloved by his family. The premise alone wins. But the real draw is how Lyle handles the neighbor's fear — patiently, gently, by just being consistently himself until he earns trust. A three-year-old getting a new pet that's shy or nervous will find something real here.

**"Leo the Late Bloomer" by Robert Kraus** — Yes, this is about a tiger who can't do things yet. No, it's not specifically about pets. But here's the thing: your kid is probably worried they're not doing the pet thing right. They're too loud. They forgot to be gentle. They startled it. Leo the Late Bloomer, in his own time, figures it out. So will your kid.

**"A Bear Called Paddington" by Michael Bond** — Technically a chapter book but many three-year-olds will sit through the early chapters if you read with good voices. The premise of an unexpected animal arriving and upending a household's life is... relevant. The Brown family did not plan to adopt a bear at Paddington Station. They did it anyway. Now they have a bear. This is the energy.

**"Owen" by Kevin Henkes** — Owen's beloved blanket is causing problems as he approaches school age. Not a pet book. But the theme of attachment to a creature-comfort you're being told to give up maps perfectly onto the complicated feelings of suddenly having to share your parents' attention with a new pet. Henkes is a genius. Read everything he's written.

**"Clifford the Big Red Dog" by Norman Bridwell** — Look, Clifford is not sophisticated literature. But when your kid just got a dog, reading about Emily Elizabeth and her enormous, loyal, chaotic, well-meaning disaster of a pet is going to feel very right. Sometimes a book's value is in the pure joy of recognition.

## What happens when you read these together

Don't perform it. That's the main thing. You're exhausted, the new pet probably kept you up last night, and reading to your three-year-old doesn't need to be a production. Just read in a normal voice. Let them interrupt. Let them point at things. Let them ask "why" fourteen times about the same sentence.

The interruptions are the best part. Your three-year-old is going to make connections you didn't set up. They're going to say "Lyle is scared like our cat!" or "Harry is dirty like our dog after the park!" and those unprompted parallels are the whole point of reading books together — not the narrative, but the thinking that happens around it.

Also: let them hold the book. At three, kids are starting to read pictures with real attention and agency. They'll notice things in illustrations you've scrolled past eighteen times. Let them lead the pace sometimes. If they want to stay on one page for four minutes staring at a dog's expression, stay there. That's not distractibility — that's actually deep engagement.

## The "I changed my mind about the pet" conversation

Some three-year-olds ask for a pet, get the pet, and immediately decide they don't want it. The dog is too big. The cat scratches. The fish is boring. They wanted a different kind of pet. They're three — buyer's remorse is developmentally normal, and you can't return the dog.

Books won't fix this but they can open a door. After reading "Harry the Dirty Dog," you might say: "Harry's family was worried when they couldn't find him, right? They really missed him. Do you think our dog would be sad if he had to leave?" You're not guilt-tripping your kid. You're just offering the animal's perspective as a real thing to consider.

Alternatively, some books are good conversation openers precisely because they're not about your exact situation. Reading "Leo the Late Bloomer" and then saying "what do you think you want to learn to do with our new cat?" gives your child a sense of direction — a skill to build rather than just a feeling to manage.

Sometimes the conversation you need isn't in the book. It's just: "I know the puppy is a lot sometimes. It's a lot for me too." Your honesty gives them permission to have complicated feelings about something they're supposed to be purely happy about.

## A section just for the chaotic part (because there will be a chaotic part)

You're going to read these books and feel good about it. Then your three-year-old is going to do something absurd — try to put sunglasses on the cat, drop food into the fish tank "to make it more delicious," drag the dog by its tail while narrating a story they've invented — and you're going to wonder if any of this book stuff is doing anything.

It is. Slowly. Invisibly. The language from the books shows up later, in unexpected moments. The empathy isn't switched on by a single reading. It's accumulated drop by drop.

You're doing fine. The pet is probably fine. Your three-year-old is spectacular, actually.

## Make a book made for your child

The best version of a new-pet book for your three-year-old? A book where your three-year-old IS the main character. Where the pet has your pet's actual name. Where the story mirrors what actually happened in your house — the excitement, the nervousness, the moment they finally got brave enough to pet the dog.

Three-year-olds at this age are starting to understand stories as things that are ABOUT THEM and THEIR WORLD. A custom book hits differently than any off-the-shelf option.

Plubis builds that book for you. Tell us about your kid, your pet, the situation — and we generate a custom illustrated story that puts your child at the center of their own animal adventure. It takes about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about starting at a new school
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a new school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a specific kind of hard. Two-year-olds don't fully understand what "everyone else already has a best friend" means. But three? Three is when they start noticing. They see the kids who run to each other at drop-off. They see the pairs. They don't have vocabulary for it yet but something in them goes *oh. I'm on the outside of something.*

And you're watching that happen. From across the room, at a school you didn't choose for them at the start of the year, hoping someone goes over and asks them to play.

Mid-year school switches are different from first-day-of-school. The first-day books assume everyone's equally lost. In the real mid-year situation, there's one new kid and about twenty kids who already know the rules, the routines, the teacher's nicknames, whose cubby is whose. Your three-year-old is walking into a social structure that already exists without them in it.

Books won't fix that. But they can give your kid a way to think about it — and more importantly, they can give you a way to talk about it. Which at three is half the battle.

## What three-year-olds can actually get from a book about this

At three, language explodes. Vocabulary doubles. The ability to ask "why" appears, endlessly, forever, until you fantasize about moving to a cabin. This is actually useful here, because you can read a book and then talk about it in a way you couldn't at two.

Three-year-olds understand:
- Feeling nervous (they may call it "scared in my tummy" or just "weird")
- Wanting a friend and not having one yet
- That new things are sometimes hard before they get okay
- That adults come back

Three-year-olds don't understand:
- Social strategy. Do not try to teach them how to make friends via book discussion. It will not work.
- Time. "It'll get easier" means nothing to someone for whom last Tuesday is ancient history.
- The concept that the other kids aren't deliberately excluding them. They feel excluded whether it's intentional or not.

So the best books at this age lean into the feeling and model a resolution. Not a lesson, a story. The character felt left out, and then something shifted. That's the shape of it.

## The books I actually recommend

**"Wemberly Worried" by Kevin Henkes** — This is the right book for this situation at this age. Wemberly worries about starting school, specifically about not having any friends. She brings her stuffed bunny Petal. On the first day there's another girl who also has a stuffed animal. That's it. That tiny moment of connection — another kid who also did the thing — is so real and so true to how three-year-old friendships actually start. Buy this one.

**"The Brand New Kid" by Katie Couric** — A boy named Lazlo starts mid-year at a new school and everyone ignores him until one girl decides to be brave and say hi. The mid-year specific detail here is meaningful — this isn't a first-day-of-school book. It's about what it feels like when the rhythms are already established and you're outside them. The illustrations are lovely. And the girl who reaches out, Ellie, is a good character to talk about — "what would it feel like if someone did that for you?"

**"The New Girl... And Me" by Jacqui Robbins** — Beautifully illustrated and told from the perspective of a child in the class watching a new girl arrive. Your kid is the new kid, but reading about it from the other side is secretly useful. It builds empathy — and it also normalizes that making friends starts with someone doing something small and brave. It plants a seed: *the other kids might be trying to figure out how to talk to me too.*

**"My New School: A First Look at Starting a New School" by Pat Thomas** — This is the direct, factual one. Pat Thomas writes books that treat children's emotions with respect and explain situations matter-of-factly. Less narrative-driven than the others but useful because it validates feelings directly and explicitly. If your kid is a "but WHY" kid who wants explanations, this one gives them.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — I want to be honest: this one is harder. A new girl named Maya arrives at school and the other kids, including the narrator, don't include her. Maya eventually moves away. The teacher then does a lesson about kindness and the narrator feels the weight of what she didn't do. It's not a happy ending. It's a real ending.

For a kid going through a new school: use this carefully. It's better as a tool after they're settled, to understand what new kids feel. But if your three-year-old is anxious about the switch right now, maybe wait a few months on this one. It might land wrong when they're already scared.

**"All Are Welcome" by Alexandra Penfold** — The joyful counterpoint to the harder books. Every child belongs, the classroom is warm, new kids are greeted. It's aspirational, but aspiration is allowed. Read it when you need to rebuild the hope a bit.

**"When You Are Brave" by Pat Zietlow Miller** — Not school-specific but brave-specific, and that's useful. Going somewhere new mid-year is brave. Your three-year-old is doing a brave thing. This book says brave looks like going anyway even when it feels big. That's a true thing.

## Reading together — making it a conversation

At three, reading a book about feelings opens a door. You don't have to walk through it loudly. Just prop it open.

After you read **"Wemberly Worried"**, you might say: "Wemberly really wanted a friend. Do you ever feel like that at your new school?" And then — this is the important part — you wait. You don't fill the silence with reassurance. You let them answer, or not answer, or stare at the page.

Three-year-olds often can't answer directly. They'll answer sideways. "Petal is Wemberly's friend." Okay. That's them thinking about it. You can go there: "Do you have anything you wish you could bring that feels like Petal?" Maybe there's something small they want in their bag.

Don't push toward resolution in the conversation. Just name what the characters feel and let your kid be in the same room as that feeling. That's enough.

One more thing: read the same book multiple nights in a row. I know parents get bored. Read it anyway. Your kid needs the repetition and you're doing this for them, not for you.

## When a book isn't enough — conversation starters for the hard nights

Some nights you close the book and your kid is still just sad. They're staring at the ceiling in their bed and they don't want to sleep and they say "I don't like my new school."

Don't fix it. Don't argue with it. Just sit with it first.

"I hear you. It's hard being new. The other kids already know each other." Let that be true. Let them feel you understand it.

Then, if they want to keep talking, you can try:
- "Was there anyone today you sat near at snack?"
- "Did anyone make you laugh today?"
- "What was the most interesting thing in the classroom?"

Not "did you make friends" — that question is too big and it shuts kids down. Smaller questions open things up.

If the sad has lasted more than two or three weeks without any sign of settling — if they're crying at drop-off every day and not eating lunch and coming home exhausted from the effort of being there — that's worth a conversation with their teacher. Not to complain. Just to ask: "What do the mornings look like? Is there anything you can do to help connect them?" Good teachers at this age actively help with this. They seat kids near each other strategically. They pair up activities. If your kid's teacher isn't doing that, it's worth a gentle nudge.

## Make a book made for your child

There's a version of this where the story is about your kid specifically. Their name on every page. Their actual school. A new friend who has your kid's name in the dedication. That's not a generic board book — that's a story about *them* going through *this*.

At three, hearing their own name in a story is basically magic. They light up. And that's when the message gets in — when they're lit up and leaning forward and completely there.

We make personalized picture books at Plubis. Five minutes to create one. Your kid's name, their details, their story.

The first one is free.

situationLabel: a new baby brother or sister
title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about a new baby brother or sister
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through new sibling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
You know that thing where a three-year-old insists they were never a baby? "I was always big." And then a real baby shows up and suddenly everything they thought they knew about their place in this family has to be renegotiated. Yeah.

## What three-year-olds can handle — and what they can't

Three is a genuinely interesting age for this transition. Old enough to understand "a baby is coming," not old enough to really grasp what that means until the baby is home and eating 40 minutes of every hour and someone is saying "shhh" approximately every six minutes. Three-year-olds have big feelings and limited tools to process them, which is why you'll get the full spectrum: the sweetest big-sibling moments you've ever seen, followed immediately by a request to put the baby "back in your tummy."

Books for this age can start to carry a real story — a character who feels jealous, does something about it, and finds their way to something like okay. The emotional vocabulary doesn't need to be complex. What it needs to be is honest. "She didn't like the baby sometimes" is more useful than "she loved her baby sister from the very first moment" because one of those is true for most three-year-olds and one of them isn't.

## Books that actually hold up

**"Peter's Chair" by Ezra Jack Keats**
This is the one. Keats wrote it in 1967 and it hasn't aged a day. Peter watches his baby sister get his old crib, his old cradle, his old high chair — all painted pink. He grabs his chair (the last thing that's still his) and runs away. Then something shifts. Quietly, without any speeches. This is the gold standard for new-sibling books at this age. The collage illustrations are beautiful. Get it.

**"The New Baby" by Mercer Mayer**
If your kid loves the Little Critter books, this is an easy sell. Little Critter deals with a new baby sister in the most relatable way possible: he tries to be helpful and sometimes fails, he feels left out and says so, and he still loves his family. Nothing fancy. Just true. The style is cozy and familiar and that's exactly what you want when everything else feels new.

**"A Baby Sister for Frances" by Russell Hoban**
Frances is a badger. A very grumpy, very funny badger who decides to run away — not far, just under the dining room table. She packs a bag, takes her favorite crackers, and listens to her parents talk about how much they miss her. This one is slightly longer and plays better when the baby is a few months old and the novelty has worn off. For the three-year-old who's past the initial adjustment and into the long slog of sharing everything forever.

**"I Used to Be the Baby" by Rob Frazier**
A rhyming board book about the shift from being the youngest to being the oldest. Simple, rhythmic, satisfying. The kind of book whose spine breaks open to the same page every time because you've read it so many nights in a row. Good for the weeks right before the baby arrives.

**"Starring Lorenzo, and Little Foot" by Elys Dolan**
Slightly newer, slightly harder to find. Lorenzo is the family pet (a dog) and Little Foot is the new baby. The story is told from the dog's perspective, which creates just enough distance that kids can laugh at it while also totally identifying with Lorenzo's feelings of displacement. Funny. Sharp. Good for three-year-olds who don't want to talk about the baby directly.

## Reading it together without it turning into a therapy session

Three-year-olds will interrupt. They will point at pictures and say "that's me" and then go on a tangent about a sandwich they had last Tuesday. Let them. The digression is the work.

Try reading during a calm moment — not right after a hard incident with the baby, but not too far from it either. The middle-distance works. An hour or two after your three-year-old had a meltdown because the baby got held longer: that's the sweet spot where they're regulated enough to engage but the feelings are still fresh enough to be real.

Ask one open question, maximum. "I wonder how Frances is feeling right now." Then wait. You might get nothing. You might get a wall of feelings. Both are fine. Don't push. If they say "she's mad" and then move on, that's a win. The book did its job.

And honestly? Don't feel like you have to read it the "right" way every time. Some nights you're just reading a book before bed. That's also useful. The book seeps in.

## What NOT to say (a quick list)

Avoid: "You're the big kid now, you have to be patient." This is true but unhelpful. It asks a three-year-old to parent themselves.

Avoid: "The baby loves you so much." The baby is a small bundle of needs and doesn't love anyone specifically yet. Three-year-olds know this.

Avoid: "Aren't you so lucky to have a baby sister?" Lucky is not what they're feeling. Naming the luck erases the feeling.

Better: "It's hard sometimes, huh. And we still love you exactly as much." Simple. True. Done.

## When a book isn't enough

Some three-year-olds have a genuinely hard time with this transition — not just the normal hard, but regression, real aggression, weeks of disrupted sleep. If it's been a month and the distress isn't easing, talking to your pediatrician is the right move. Books can be a great conversation starter, but they're not the whole answer for every kid.

## Make a book made for your child

Plubis lets you build a custom picture book with your child's actual name in the story — so it's about *them*, their new sibling, their feelings. Pick the situation, add their name, and it's ready in about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about being afraid of the dark
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through nightmares and the dark. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds and the dark. Fun combo.

Something flips around this age. Their imagination is now powerful enough to generate threats but not yet mature enough to evaluate whether those threats are real. So the shadow on the ceiling? Definitely a monster. The creak? Evidence. The gap under the door where no light comes in? Obviously where it lives.

You can't logic your way through this. Trust me, parents have tried. "There's no such thing as monsters" said into the face of a completely unconvinced three-year-old at 11pm is one of parenting's great humbling experiences.

Books can do something your reasoning can't: they can meet your kid inside the fear and walk them out the other side.

## what works at this age

Three is the age where story starts to do real work. Your kid can follow a character through a problem to a resolution. They can feel that arc. They can hold in their mind: "this character was scared, then something happened, then they were okay."

That's the formula you want. Not "darkness is actually nice" (though that works too). The deeper move is: "scary thing happened, I survived it, I'm actually okay." That's what builds resilience in the nervous system — not avoiding fear but completing the fear cycle. Story does that.

Also: humor. Three-year-olds respond brilliantly to monsters that are embarrassed, nightmares that are actually cowardly, darkness that turns out to be confused. Laughter is a biological circuit-breaker for anxiety. The right book can get them giggling about the exact thing that was keeping them up. After a giggle, the brain literally recategorizes the threat.

Read these before bed, not after a nightmare. Pre-loading is more effective than crisis intervention. You want the frame in place before the lights go out, not while they're already spiraling.

## book picks

**"There's a Nightmare in My Closet" by Mercer Mayer**

Published in 1968 and it's still in print for a reason. A boy decides he's had enough of the nightmare in his closet, arms himself, and waits for it. The nightmare comes out — and immediately starts crying. So the boy puts it to bed. Full stop. That reversal — the child comforting the monster — is one of the most psychologically useful moves in picture books. It hands control back to the kid. Your three-year-old is not the victim of the nightmare. They're the one who can put it to bed. Mayer said he drew on his own childhood fears when he made it. You can feel that. It's not cute or sanitized. It's honest about what the dark feels like and then it offers a genuine out.

**"Orion and the Dark" by Emma Yarlett**

Orion is terrified of the dark. The Dark (capital D, a big friendly shadow creature) shows up and takes him on a tour of everything the dark actually does: letting the stars come out, letting nocturnal animals live their lives, covering the world in something quiet. The illustrations are extraordinary — physical flaps, windows, textures. But the concept is the thing. Orion stops being a victim and starts being someone who understands. Three-year-olds are at exactly the right age to start building that understanding. Templar/Candlewick. Ages 3-6, solidly.

**"The Dark" by Lemony Snicket, illustrated by Jon Klassen**

Laszlo is afraid of the dark. The dark lives in his house. One night, the dark calls him into the basement. This sounds terrifying and it is, a little — which is why it works. Snicket doesn't pretend the dark is nothing. He takes the fear seriously and then walks through it. Klassen's illustrations are spare and stunning, lots of white space and shadow. Three-year-olds who like slightly spooky things — the ones who kind of enjoy being a little scared — love this one. For the extremely fear-sensitive three-year-old, maybe save it for four. But if your kid is the type who wants to watch the slightly scarier movie, they're ready.

**"Can't You Sleep, Little Bear?" by Martin Waddell, illustrated by Barbara Firth**

Still essential at three. But now your kid is old enough to really feel what Little Bear feels — the specific anxiety of not wanting Big Bear to leave, of needing one more lantern, one more check. The Kate Greenaway Medal winner holds up because it doesn't solve the problem with a trick. It solves it with presence. Big Bear just keeps coming back. That's the reassurance that matters at three: not that the dark is safe, but that you will keep coming back.

**"Scaredy Squirrel at Night" by Mélanie Watt**

Scaredy Squirrel refuses to sleep because he's compiled a list of everything that could happen in his dreams: ghosts, aliens, zombies, dragons — all indexed and planned for. The humor is perfect for three-year-olds who are starting to be able to see the absurdity in anxiety alongside the anxiety itself. Watt's style is deadpan and detailed and kids find it hilarious. There's also something quietly reassuring in the structure: Scaredy Squirrel has a plan for everything. And then he realizes sleep is actually fine. Your slightly anxious, highly organized three-year-old may recognize someone they know.

**"Darkness Slipped In" by Ella Burfoot**

Daisy welcomes Darkness as a friend who comes through her window to play. Still works beautifully at three, maybe even better than at two because your kid can now appreciate the personification — the idea that darkness is a character, not just an absence. The rhyming text has a lilt that's genuinely pleasurable to read aloud, and the hot-pink-on-black design is visually arresting in a way three-year-olds respond to. Good for a child who's recently got scared of something that didn't scare them before. It resets the frame.

**"Little Owl's Night" by Divya Srinivasan**

Still works at three. Even more so, actually — because now your kid can appreciate the nocturnal animal details. Little Owl visits frogs, raccoons, fireflies, moths. Night becomes a full world instead of just an absence of day. If your three-year-old asks questions (they do, endlessly), this book generates great ones: "What animals are awake at night?" "Where do fireflies go?" Curiosity is a great replacement for dread.

## reading together

Three-year-olds want to talk during books. Not after. During.

Let them. "Is that monster scary?" "Yes." "Does the boy look scared?" "Yeah." "What do you think happens?" Let them predict. Wrong predictions are fine. The point is engagement, and engagement is the thing that makes the fear processing work instead of just washing over them.

After you finish, ask one question: "Who was scared in that book?" They'll tell you. Then: "What happened?" They'll tell you the resolution. That retelling is the brain consolidating: scared → something happened → okay. You're helping them build the neural pathway that says fear doesn't last forever.

Don't push "are YOU scared of the dark?" right after. They'll get there if they want to. The book does the work sideways. Direct questions about their own fear can feel like a test. The book is safer.

If they want you to read it again immediately, read it again. Repetition at three is still deeply useful. Each time through they go a little deeper into the emotional processing.

## when a book isn't enough

3am. Your three-year-old is sitting upright in bed, eyes huge, saying "there's something in my room."

Here's the conversation that works better than most:

"Tell me what you saw." Not "there's nothing there" — not yet. Let them describe it. Nod like you're taking it seriously. "A shadow? Like a big one?" Yes. "Okay. Let's look at it together."

Get up. Go look. With them, not for them. Look at the shadow. Point out what makes it: the streetlight through the curtain, the pile of clothes on the chair. "See? That's your hoodie." Don't dismiss the fear — dismiss the threat. Big difference.

Then: "You know what? You noticed something and you figured out what it was. That was brave." Give them the narrative of courage. That's not fake. Looking at the scary thing IS courage.

A three-year-old who learns they can investigate and survive is building something that will serve them for years.

## make a book made for your child

Your three-year-old knows what they're scared of. They'll tell you. The thing in the corner. The noise in the hall. The dark part under the stairs.

What if there was a book where the main character was them, scared of exactly that thing, and they figured it out?

At Plubis you can build that in about five minutes. Their name, their fear, their bedroom, their stuffed animal sidekick. We write them a story that meets them exactly where they are.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about a parent being unwell
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a parent being sick. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is such a strange age to be in the middle of something like this.

At three, your child is old enough to notice almost everything. Old enough to ask "why" about things you can't fully answer. Old enough to pick up on the whispered phone calls, the visitors who come over and speak quietly to the other adults, the way the house smells and feels different now. But not old enough to understand what cancer is, or what chronic means, or why the person who used to throw them in the air now winces when they reach up to be carried.

That gap — between what they notice and what they can make sense of — is where the fear lives.

Three-year-olds fill in gaps with their own explanations, and those explanations are almost always worse than the truth. "Mama is sick because I was bad." "Daddy is in the hospital because I didn't listen." Children this age are egocentric in the developmental sense — not selfish, just wired to believe that the world revolves around them and that they are somehow responsible for what happens in it.

So when you read with a three-year-old about a parent being sick, one of the most important things you can do is help them understand, again and again and again: *this is not your fault.* You don't have to say it with those exact words. A good book says it in the story.

## What actually works at three

Three-year-olds can handle more than two-year-olds can. They have longer attention spans (sometimes), they can follow a simple narrative arc, and they can talk about feelings more directly. Ask a three-year-old "how are you feeling?" and you might actually get an answer.

But three is also the age of magical thinking. They believe in invisible things. They believe their wishes and fears have power over what happens in the real world. This is completely normal — and it means that books which name the fear directly ("I'm scared Mama won't come home") and then address it ("but Mama comes home") are genuinely helpful. They short-circuit the magical thinking by putting a real shape on the fear.

At three, routine is still everything. If you can make reading-about-this a ritual — same time, same spot, same warmth — you give your child a predictable moment in what may be a very unpredictable season.

Also: don't skip the pages that feel hard. Three-year-olds are braver than we give them credit for. The page where the character misses Daddy, or feels angry, or cries — your child needs to see that those feelings have a place in the story. That they don't break anything.

## Book picks

**"When Mommy Is Sick" by Ferne Sherkin-Langer, illustrated by Kay Life** — A girl watches her mother go in and out of hospital. The illness is unnamed. The feelings are named instead: the missing, the worry, the stretch of waiting, and the relief. At three, that's the right level of abstraction. Your child doesn't need to understand the medical details. They need to see a child who feels what they feel and comes out the other side still okay. This book does that quietly and well.

**"Cancer Hates Kisses" by Jessica Reid Sliwerski, illustrated by Mika Song** — The author was a new mother when she was diagnosed with breast cancer. She wrote this book because she couldn't find one that was honest and warm at the same time. The approach — cancer hates laughter, cancer hates hugs, love is stronger — is emotionally intelligent for this age. Three-year-olds understand the logic: the bad thing has a weakness, and that weakness is us. There's something quietly empowering about that framing.

**"You Are the Best Medicine" by Julie Aigner Clark** — I keep coming back to this book for the younger ages because it does something rare: it gives the child a role. Not a burden, not a job — just the reassurance that their presence, their silliness, their ordinary small love is actually doing something real. For a three-year-old who is watching helplessly while a parent is sick, that is a gift. You're not useless in this. You are, in the deepest possible way, already helping.

**"Mommy Has to Stay in Bed" by Annette Rivlin-Gutman** — Bed rest, chronic illness, injury, recovery — this book works for any situation where a parent's mobility or presence is suddenly limited. The rhyming text is easy to follow at three, and the emotional arc is honest: it's frustrating and confusing and hard, and we still find ways to be together. At three, children need to see both parts of that truth.

**"Someone I Love Is Sick: Helping Very Young Children Cope with Cancer in the Family" by Kathleen McCue** — This one works a little differently. It's designed as a customisable tool, intended to be used alongside conversation. You can add photos and real details from your family. For a three-year-old, having their name and their family reflected back to them in a book about exactly their situation can be incredibly grounding. Worth knowing about.

**"Nowhere Hair" by Sue Glader, illustrated by Edith Buenen** — A child asks why Mama has no hair, and Mama answers honestly: it's medicine that made the hair go, and the hair will come back. The premise is cancer-specific, but the emotional content is universal — confusion, the need for honesty, and love that doesn't change even when appearances do. The rhyming format keeps it accessible for three-year-olds, and the illustrations are cheerful without being dishonest.

## Reading together without falling apart

Some parents can't read these books without crying. That's okay. Let me say that clearly: it is okay to cry in front of your three-year-old while reading a book about a sick parent.

You don't have to pretend to be fine. Three-year-olds already know you're not fine. What they need is not a parent who is magically unaffected — they need a parent who can feel sad and still be there. Who can cry and then close the book and say "I love you" and do the next thing. That is the actual lesson: feelings are survivable.

If you're too sick to read on certain days, have someone else read it. A grandparent, a partner, a friend. The book still works. The conversation still happens.

And when your child asks questions you can't answer — "Will you die?" "Will you get better?" "Why can't you play?" — it's okay to say "I don't know" alongside whatever truth you can offer. Three-year-olds can tolerate "I don't know, but I'm trying to get better, and I love you no matter what." They can hold that. They're more capable than we think.

## When a book isn't enough — a conversation starter

If your three-year-old seems to be acting out, regressing (back to diapers, thumb-sucking, baby talk), unusually clingy or unusually withdrawn, or having nightmares regularly — the book is not sufficient. That's not what books are for.

Here's something simple you can try: after reading one of these books, sit with the closed book and say: "That little kid in the story felt scared when their mama was sick. Do you ever feel scared?" And then just wait. Don't fill the silence. Let them answer or not answer.

Three-year-olds often answer with action rather than words — they'll crawl into your lap, or suddenly go play, or burst into tears for no apparent reason. All of those are answers.

If you're worried, contact your child's paediatrician or ask whether there's a child life specialist at your treatment centre. These are real resources, and asking for help for your child is one of the best things you can do while you're going through something this big yourself.

## Make a book made just for your child

Generic books are a good start. But there's a version of this that's even more specific — a book with your child's name in it, with a character who is going through exactly what they're going through, in a house that looks something like yours.

At three, personalisation is powerful. When a child hears their name in a story, something shifts. The story stops being about some other family and starts being about them. "Oh, that's my worry. That's my house. That's how I feel."

At Plubis, you can create that book in five minutes. You tell us the situation, your child's name, a few details about your family, and we build a story that speaks directly to your child's experience. No diagnosis required. Just the real feeling.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about a parent going away
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through a parent traveling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is a weird in-between age for this. Old enough to understand "Daddy is going on a trip" in a sentence. Not old enough to actually understand what that means in days, in distance, in the specific weight of a week without the person who does the voices at bedtime. You get all the comprehension and none of the coping tools.

Also: three-year-olds have opinions now. Strong ones. "I don't WANT Mama to go" is not a negotiation opener — it's a complete emotional statement that deserves to land before you move to reassurance. The books that work best at this age are the ones that let the wanting-them-to-stay feeling be real first.

## What three-year-olds need from these books

At three, kids are starting to grasp sequences. "First this, then that" is something they can follow. So a book that shows the arc — parent leaves, child misses them, parent comes back — can do more than just name a feeling. It can model the shape of the whole experience. That's a lot, actually. The brain doing the work of "this has a before, a middle, and an after, and the after is okay" is developmental gold.

They're also starting to identify strongly with characters. If your kid loves bears, the bear-parent book will land harder than the bunny-parent book. Don't overthink this — you probably know what animal they'll track right now. Pick accordingly.

Repetition is still the mechanism. The book you read eight nights before the trip and twice on the night of departure and every other night while the parent is gone is the book doing the work. Not the one you heroically sit down and read the morning of at 7am before school.

## The books I'd actually recommend

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — I know this is on every list I've ever seen and I don't care. It's there because it works. At three, your kid can understand *why* Chester's mom presses the kiss into his palm — not just copy the gesture but grasp that the love goes with him. Make it a ritual before every departure and every bedtime while the parent is away. Press the kiss in. Three-year-olds take rituals deadly seriously. Use that.

**"Owl Babies" by Martin Waddell** — Three baby owls wake in the night to find their mother gone. Bill, the youngest, keeps saying "I want my mommy" — a line so specific and so plain that three-year-olds will repeat it back to you with feeling. The mother returns. Everything is okay. The genius of this book is the waiting — not a three-page wait but a real, felt, illustrated wait. Your kid will sit in that waiting with the owls in a way that teaches them that waiting has an end. That's not nothing. That might actually be the whole thing.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At three they can genuinely grasp this metaphor: love is like a string that connects you to the people you love, even when they're far away. Point to your chest. Point to theirs. Tell them the string goes to the airport, onto the plane, into whatever city. You can do this at drop-off or bedtime the whole time the parent is gone. "Can you feel the string?" is a surprisingly useful question at 2am when they wake up scared.

**"Wherever You Are: My Love Will Find You" by Nancy Tillman** — A parent's voice, directly to the child: wherever you go, whatever happens, my love finds you. No plot, just the promise, repeated across the most gorgeous spread illustrations. This one is best read by the parent before they leave — record it on video if you can, so the child can watch it while they're gone. The voice of the gone parent saying "I love you, I find you, I'm with you" is worth more than any description of the book.

**"Daddy's Going to Miss You" by various — actually, try "Are You My Mother?" by P.D. Eastman** — I was going to recommend a specific goodbye-Daddy book here but I've seen too many of them that are saccharine to the point of uselessness. Instead: "Are You My Mother?" is a classic that three-year-olds are fully ready for. Baby bird falls, searches, finds. The searching itself is something they recognize. And the return — the mother flying back, the nest restored — is deeply satisfying. Sometimes the older classics do more work than the more targeted newer books.

**"The Rabbit Listened" by Cori Doerrfeld** — Taylor's block tower falls down. Many animals come with suggestions — talk about it! roar about it! laugh about it! Only the rabbit just sits and listens. This book isn't about parent travel specifically but it's about what it feels like when something hard happens and you're waiting for someone to just be with you instead of fixing it. For a three-year-old navigating a parent's absence, this one works beautifully as a model of what the staying parent can offer: presence, not just reassurance. Also: gorgeous. One of the best picture books of the last decade.

**"When You Are Brave" by Pat Zietlow Miller** — A child moves through difficult things — the first day somewhere new, being far from someone you love — and the refrain is about what being brave actually feels like: not the absence of fear but doing the hard thing anyway. Short, beautiful, directly applicable. For the three-year-old who's been brave enough to let the parent go and is now just waiting, this gives a name to what they've already done.

## Reading these well — and the one thing to skip

Don't read these as a lecture. Don't pause after every page and say "do YOU feel like that?" Most three-year-olds will shut down under direct questioning. What works is reading it calmly, slowly, and then narrating your own feelings out loud. "The owl babies really miss their mama. I think that makes sense. I miss people I love when they're far away too." That's it. That's the model. They're watching you have feelings about a book character — that's the rehearsal they need.

Don't promise the traveling parent will call every night and then have the call not happen. Three-year-olds have excellent memories for broken promises and zero forgiveness in the immediate term. Under-promise the calls. Over-deliver on the actual ritual — the kissing hand, the invisible string, the physical piece of clothing in the bed.

The one thing to skip: books that make the traveling parent a hero adventurer having a great time. I've seen a few of these and they land wrong for most three-year-olds — they want the parent home, not to be told the parent is off having adventures. Save that framing for when they're older and can be proud of what the parent is doing.

## What helps between the readings

A photo book — even just printed 4x6 photos in a cheap little album — that the child can hold and carry. Photos of the traveling parent with the child together. Not fancy. Not expensive. Just images they can bring to daycare and show the teacher. Three-year-olds process emotions through showing and telling. Let them tell everyone their daddy is on a trip and show them the picture. That's them working it out.

A simple paper chain countdown. One link per day. Rip one off each morning. When the chain is gone, the parent comes home. This is the kind of physical, body-level understanding that verbal reassurance can't replace at three.

## When a book isn't enough

If your three-year-old is falling apart every time a parent travels — not sleeping, regressing in toilet training, refusing to eat, having meltdowns far beyond their usual baseline — it's worth a conversation with your pediatrician. Separation anxiety is developmentally normal at three. Significant functional disruption for more than a few days warrants professional attention. The books are there to help; they're not the only tool available and there's nothing wrong with needing more support.

## Make a book made just for your child

Your three-year-old is at the exact age where hearing their own name in a story is magic. Not a llama, not an owl, not Chester the raccoon — them. "Arjun's mama went on a trip" is a different sentence in a different way than any of the books above. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes with your child as the main character. You pick the situation, tell us their name, and we generate the whole thing. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about trying new foods
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through picky eating. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three is peak picky eating for a lot of kids. Not all kids — some skate through this age as adventurous eaters and you should keep that information to yourself if your friends are in the thick of it — but for a significant portion of three-year-olds, the food world has contracted to a very small and specific set of acceptable items.

Here's what I mean by specific. Not just "pasta" — the right shape pasta, with the right sauce, in the right bowl, not touching anything else. Not just "crackers" — the specific crackers, which must not be broken, and the package must be the right color, and if you bought the generic brand this week, dinner is cancelled.

This is not being difficult. This is a developmental phase. Three-year-olds are asserting control over their environment (they can't control much), they're sensitive to sensory differences in a way adults forget about, and their immune systems are actually calibrated to be suspicious of unfamiliar foods. This is biology. It's annoying biology, but it's biology.

Books help with this in a sideways way. Not by making the broccoli suddenly appealing. By making the category of "trying something new" feel like something brave characters do.

## What works at three

Three-year-olds track a narrative. They remember stories. They form opinions about characters. They can now be told "remember in the book when Sam finally tried the green eggs?" and have that land as a real reference.

So at three, books can start to do more than just normalize food visually — they can actually build toward a moment. "I wonder if you could be brave like Sam." This works approximately 20% of the time in real life, but that 20% is worth building toward.

Still: neutral reading environment. Not at dinner. Not as a lesson. Just as a story.

## The books

**"Green Eggs and Ham" by Dr. Seuss** — Still the standard at three. They can now follow the full arc and feel the resolution — Sam tried it. Sam liked it. The refuser's absoluteness was funny but also ultimately wrong. Three-year-olds get this. They also know they're the refuser, not Sam, which makes the ending interesting. Read it without comment. Let it sit.

**"I Will Never Not Ever Eat a Tomato" by Lauren Child** — At three this works beautifully. The joke — pretending the carrot is an "orange twiglet from Jupiter" — is now fully funny to a three-year-old. The concept that a story about food can change how the food feels is one that some three-year-olds actually apply. Not most. But some. Worth trying.

**"Dragons Love Tacos" by Adam Rubin** — Dragons love tacos but HATE spicy salsa. The spicy salsa disasters are epic and extremely funny. This book isn't about teaching a kid to eat new things. It's about making food wildly fun and ridiculous, which decreases the tension around food generally, which is the actual prerequisite for trying anything new. One of the best picture books of the last decade, not just for picky eating. Read it for the joy.

**"Yoko" by Rosemary Wells** — Yoko's sushi is laughed at and eventually loved. For three-year-olds from families with specific cultural foods that they feel self-conscious about — or for any kid navigating being different — this is the book. The emotional arc is clear and the ending is warm without being saccharine. Rosemary Wells's illustrations are old-fashioned in a comforting way.

**"What's in Your Lunchbox?" by various illustrators (DK)** — More of a reference book than a story, but three-year-olds love it. Foods from different countries presented matter-of-factly. Not "try this exotic thing" — just: here is what lunches look like in different places, and they're all normal. Quiet normalization work. Good for browsing, not for reading front to back.

**"The Ugly Vegetables" by Grace Lin** — A girl thinks her mom's Chinese vegetable garden is ugly compared to the neighbors' flower gardens. Then the vegetables become a soup that brings the whole street over. Best on this list for kids whose picky eating is partly about cultural food feeling weird or wrong. The ending makes the "different" food the best food. The illustrations are gorgeous.

**"Rah, Rah, Radishes!" by April Pulley Sayre** — Photo book. Real vegetables, huge close-up photos, joyful text. At three, kids who are very visual and respond to real images (rather than illustrations) often engage with this differently than with storybooks. The radish looks magnificent. The kale looks interesting. Not trying to make them eat it — just making it visually real and beautiful.

## What to do with these books

Reading a picky eating book together is not permission to then bring up the uneaten vegetables. Let the book be the book.

But over time — weeks of reading, re-reading, leaving the books around — you can build a reference library. "You know how Sam tried it even though he really didn't want to? Could we try something like that?" Once. No follow-up. No pressure. Plant, walk away.

The most effective dinner table strategy for picky eating is this: offer a tiny amount of the new food alongside the accepted foods, say absolutely nothing about it, don't even look at whether they try it, and repeat consistently over many weeks. The books support this by building the concept outside the pressure zone.

## When picky eating needs more than patience

Most three-year-old picky eating resolves with time. But if your child is eating fewer than ten foods total, has escalating anxiety around mealtime, gags consistently on textures they used to tolerate, or is losing weight — those are reasons to talk to your pediatrician. A feeding specialist or occupational therapist can help with sensory-based feeding challenges in ways that no book or strategy will.

## Make a book made for your child

The nice thing about a custom book is that it can name the actual food. Not Sam's green eggs. Your kid's broccoli. Or oranges. Or whatever the thing is this week. Plubis builds it in five minutes with your child's name, their situation, and the right tone — brave but not preachy.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about potty training
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through potty training. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
You did it. Your three-year-old was trained. Past tense. They were going to the bathroom independently like a tiny civilized human. You stopped packing a full change of clothes. You made it through a two-hour grocery run without a single incident. You felt like you'd won something.

And now there's a new baby. Or they started preschool. Or you moved. Or nothing happened at all and they just... stopped. Completely and mysteriously regressed, like potty training was an app they uninstalled.

This is so normal it has a name. Potty regression. Three-year-olds do it constantly, and the reason is almost always stress or change, even small stress, even changes that seemed like no big deal to you. The nervous system of a three-year-old is still routing most things through "go back to what felt safe," and what felt safe was diapers. That's it. That's the whole explanation.

Books can help here in a way they couldn't at two. A three-year-old can actually follow a story. They can identify with a character who has an accident and feel less alone in it. They can hear "everyone has accidents sometimes" from a fictional bunny and take it in differently than when you say it, because you saying it feels like damage control and a book just says it as a fact.

## What works at this age

Three-year-olds are in the business of stories. They want a character, a problem, a resolution. They want to know that someone else went through the thing and came out okay. The books that work are the ones where the character has an accident, or struggles, or doesn't want to use the potty, and then finds their way — not because they were lectured, but because something shifted.

What doesn't work: books that are preachy. Books where an adult explains to a child why the potty is better and the child immediately agrees. Three-year-olds clock condescension. They've been clocking it since approximately eighteen months. If the book feels like a parenting technique disguised as a story, they will disengage immediately and ask for something about dinosaurs.

Also: this is not the time for sticker charts or rewards attached to the books. Keep the books separate from the behavior management system. The book is just a book. It's not a bribe and it's not a lesson. It's just something you do together at bedtime.

## The picks

**"Everyone Poops" by Taro Gomi** — Still works. Still always works. By three they can appreciate the deadpan humor in a way they couldn't at two. An elephant's poop is big because the elephant is big. A mouse's poop is small because the mouse is small. It's all fine. It's all just nature. If your kid is embarrassed about accidents, this book is gently relentless in its message that there is nothing to be embarrassed about.

**"Potty" by Leslie Patricelli** — Yes, it's a board book. Yes, your three-year-old will find it a little babyish, and that's fine — a slightly babyish book about potty training can feel less threatening than one that's aimed right at their level. Sometimes kids who are regressing need to visit the topic at a developmental step below where they are. There's no shame in that. Let them feel like the expert reading the baby book for a minute.

**"I Have to Go!" by Robert Munsch** — Munsch is genuinely funny and three-year-olds know it. Andrew keeps insisting he doesn't need to go, and then very urgently needs to go at the worst possible moment. Your kid will find this hilarious because they recognize the feeling. The humor is the point — it gives them distance from their own experience to laugh at someone else's version. There's something therapeutic about that. Munsch always gets this right.

**"Time to Pee!" by Mo Willems** — Mo Willems knows how to talk to children without ever talking down to them. This book uses encouraging mice as a pep squad, which sounds weird and is actually extremely charming. The book is aimed at kids who understand what the potty is but have resistance about actually using it — which is exactly the regression situation. The stickers in the back are optional but your kid will immediately want them, so be prepared for that conversation.

**"The Potty Train" by David Hochman and Ruth Kennison** — At three they can actually enjoy the train metaphor in full. All aboard. Every animal is getting on. There's momentum and fun and nobody on the Potty Train is anxious about anything. If your kid's regression is anxiety-driven, this book's breezy energy is genuinely useful. It says: this doesn't have to be hard. It just kind of... goes.

**"Once Upon a Potty" by Alona Frankel** — At three, they might find this one a little young, or they might love revisiting it. Either reaction is fine. For kids whose regression feels tied to wanting to be babied — new sibling, new preschool, feeling small — going back to a book they once "mastered" can feel comforting. There's also something subtly validating about a book that says "here's what a potty is, here's how you use it" without any judgment. No expectations. Just information.

**"Big Girl Panties" by Fran Manushkin** — The rhyme and the energy in this one is genuinely motivating for kids who respond to excitement. If your kid has gone into a regression where they're flat about the whole thing — not anxious, just indifferent — sometimes a book with real enthusiasm about underwear is exactly the reset they need.

## The conversation during the book

With three-year-olds, the book is a conversation opener, not just a solo story. When the character has an accident, you can say "oh, that happened to him too. Accidents happen." When the character uses the potty, you can say "he did it. That must have felt good." You don't have to connect it explicitly to your kid — they're making the connection internally even if they don't say so.

If they bring it up themselves — "I had an accident today" while you're reading about a character who had an accident — just say "yeah, that's what happens sometimes. It's okay." Don't turn it into a coaching moment right there. Let it just be acknowledged. That's the whole gift of the book: acknowledgment without pressure.

## The new baby situation specifically

Here's the thing about potty regression after a new sibling: your three-year-old is watching you pour enormous amounts of attention into a creature who is in diapers. The math is not lost on them. Diapers = attention. Using the potty = doing the independent thing that gets less fuss.

So sometimes reading a potty book together is actually doing double work — it's a point of connection between you and them, and it normalizes both the regression and the path back out of it. If your kid is going through new-baby regression, pair the potty books with extra undivided time that has nothing to do with bathroom behavior. The regression is almost never actually about the bathroom.

## When a book isn't enough

If regression continues for more than four to six weeks without any movement, or if your child seems ashamed or distressed rather than just reverting, it's worth talking to your pediatrician. Some kids have physical reasons for accidents — constipation causing pressure that makes bladder control harder, for example — and no amount of good bedtime reading solves a physical problem. Get the body side checked before assuming it's purely behavioral.

## Make a book made for your child

Custom books work differently at three than at two. A three-year-old hears their name in a story and lights up. They know it's about them. "Maya sometimes had accidents, and that was okay, because everyone does sometimes" is a different sentence when Maya is your kid's actual name. Plubis builds illustrated picture books with your child's name and situation in five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about learning to share
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through sharing. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Something interesting happens around age 3. Your child is starting to actually *want* friends. They notice other kids. They want to play with them, alongside them, in increasingly complicated ways. And yet — the sharing thing? Still a complete disaster. Maybe worse than at 2, because now there are higher stakes. There are playdates. There are birthday parties. There are situations where you, the parent, are watching and quietly sweating while your child looks another child dead in the eyes and says "No. Mine."

Age 3 is this strange in-between zone where kids intellectually begin to grasp that other people have feelings, but that knowledge doesn't reliably make it into their behavior under pressure. They know, in an abstract sense, that grabbing is not cool. They know it when it happens to *them*. They are very clear on how it feels when someone takes their truck. What they can't quite do yet is run that empathy in reverse, in real time, while also managing their own desire to have the yellow crayon right now.

This is not a character flaw. This is a brain that's still being built.

Books don't fix this overnight. But the right books show your 3-year-old characters they can relate to — characters who mess up the sharing thing, feel bad about it, try again. That's the model they need. Not a lecture. A story.


## What makes a good sharing book for a 3-year-old

At 3, kids can handle a bit more plot than at 2. They can follow a character through a small emotional arc. They can pick up on facial expressions in illustrations and start to read what a character is feeling. So the best books for this age don't just show sharing — they show the internal conflict *before* the sharing, the moment of decision, and what happens after.

They also need to be funny. Or at least interesting enough to compete with literally everything else a 3-year-old could be doing instead. Keep that bar in mind.


## The books

### *Should I Share My Ice Cream?* by Mo Willems

This is, without question, one of the funniest picture books about sharing ever made. Gerald the elephant has an ice cream cone. He loves ice cream. He also loves Piggie. And so begins a beautifully illustrated internal meltdown as Gerald debates whether to share his ice cream with his best friend.

The joke — which is perfectly paced, perfectly timed, and has made me laugh out loud more than I'd like to admit — is that Gerald spends so long agonizing over the decision that the ice cream melts. It's gone. And then Piggie shows up with two ice cream cones.

What this book does brilliantly is model the actual experience of wanting to share but struggling with it. Gerald isn't selfish. He's genuinely wrestling with something hard. And the resolution isn't "Gerald heroically gives up his ice cream." It's something warmer and funnier than that. Mo Willems understands 3-year-olds at a cellular level. The Elephant and Piggie series as a whole is worth owning, but if you're buying one book specifically about sharing, start here.


### *The Rainbow Fish* by Marcus Pfister

Okay, I'm going to give you a mild warning before I recommend this one: there is a real and ongoing debate among parents and educators about what this book is actually teaching. The Rainbow Fish — beautiful, glittery, beloved — learns to share his special scales with other fish and finds friendship. On the surface: lovely. Some critics argue the message is "give away what makes you special to be liked" which is, admittedly, a weird lesson.

But here's why I still recommend it for 3-year-olds: your 3-year-old is not going to be parsing the subtext about identity and belonging. They're going to be completely transfixed by the shiny foil scales on every spread. And what they'll absorb is: the fish was alone, then he shared, and then he had friends and felt good. That's actually a meaningful and accurate thing to show a child who is starting to figure out that friendships require giving as well as getting.

Read it. Enjoy the sparkly pages. Maybe save the philosophical discussion for when they're twelve.


### *Llama Llama Time to Share* by Anna Dewdney

If you skipped the 2-year-old article and landed here, welcome — this book belongs at 3 even more than at 2. At 3, kids can fully follow the emotional arc: Llama Llama has a beloved stuffed Giraffa, a new neighbor shows up, the playdate goes sideways, something gets damaged, and there's a real emotional reckoning.

What makes this land so well at 3 is that the book doesn't pretend the stuffed animal doesn't matter. It matters enormously to Llama Llama. Dewdney doesn't dismiss that. She just walks the character — and your child — through what happens when you love something too tightly and what you might decide to do differently.

The rhyme scheme is lovely. The illustrations are warm. And there's a particular page where Llama Llama's face crumples when Giraffa's ear tears that your 3-year-old will immediately recognize as a real feeling. That recognition is the whole point.


### *Enemy Pie* by Derek Munson, illustrated by Tara Calahan King

Technically this one skews slightly older — maybe 4 to 5 is the sweet spot — but I've seen 3-year-olds totally engaged with it when read with a bit of parent animation. The premise: a boy has an enemy (Jeremy Ross, who moved in down the street and immediately ruined summer). His dad offers to make Enemy Pie, a mysterious concoction that will get rid of enemies for good. The catch? The boy has to spend an entire day with his enemy first.

What follows is genuinely surprising — to the boy and, often, to the child listening. Because Jeremy Ross turns out to be... actually kind of fun? And suddenly the enemy isn't an enemy anymore. And the dad's enemy pie was never what the boy thought it was.

This is a book about sharing yourself — your time, your afternoon, your willingness to be wrong about someone. For a 3-year-old who is beginning to have social dynamics and occasional conflicts with other kids, it plants something real. Don't over-explain it afterward. The story does the work.


## A word about the "forced sharing" trap

Here's something I feel strongly about and want to say clearly: forcing a 3-year-old to share in the moment — yanking an object out of their hands and giving it to another child while saying "we share!" — is almost always counterproductive. Not because it's mean, but because it doesn't teach sharing. It teaches powerlessness. It teaches that an adult will take your things when another person wants them.

What actually works better at 3 is narrating turns: "You've had the truck for five minutes. When the timer goes off, it'll be Mia's turn." It models the concept without humiliation. And it shows your child that their needs matter too — that sharing doesn't mean they lose.

Books help enormously here because they let children watch characters navigate this without the emotional charge of their own stuff being at stake. Reading *Should I Share My Ice Cream?* at bedtime is a safe, low-stakes moment to absorb the concept. The playdate situation tomorrow is high-stakes. The book does the prep work.


## The honest thing nobody tells you

Three-year-olds are exhausting in a very specific way. They're old enough to argue but not old enough to be reasonable. They understand more than you expect and less than you hope. And they have Strong Opinions — about which cup, which chair, which part of the banana has the right curve — that seem absurd from the outside but are completely real to them.

Sharing fits right into this. The toy they're refusing to share might be one they've ignored for weeks. It doesn't matter. In this moment, it's the most important object in the known universe, and they will defend it accordingly.

This isn't a phase that passes with one good talk. It passes with about two years of patience, modeling, and a lot of books read at bedtime when everyone is calm and the stakes are low. You're doing the work. Keep going.


## One thing that actually helps at 3 that parents don't try enough

Ask your child to be the one who teaches you about sharing. Seriously. "Can you show me how to share this?" or "What do you think Gerald should do with the ice cream?" When kids explain a concept, they own it in a way they don't when they're receiving it. It's the same reason teaching something to someone else is the fastest way to learn it yourself.

After you read these books, ask them what they would do. Don't correct their answer. Just listen. The conversation that follows is usually more interesting than the book.


*Want a custom picture book with your child's name and face — set in their world, with their favorite things, about a sharing moment they actually had? The first one is free.*

title: The best picture books to read to a 3-year-old about tantrums and meltdowns
metaDescription: Picture books for 3-year-olds going through tantrums. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Three-year-olds have all the feelings of a two-year-old plus the language skills to tell you exactly how wronged they feel. This is both progress and a new problem.

At two, the meltdown was wordless — a pure physical event. At three, there are words. "It's NOT FAIR" (delivered with the conviction of someone who has studied international human rights law). "YOU NEVER let me." "I HATE going to bed" (absolutely untrue, they're exhausted, but they mean it in this moment). The vocabulary didn't fix the tantrums. It just added a narrative layer on top of them.

Here's the thing. The tantrum at three is the same neurological event as the tantrum at two — a nervous system overwhelmed, a prefrontal cortex that is genuinely not yet capable of regulation. But three is also the age when books can start doing something more. Not just naming feelings. Actually tracking a character through a feeling arc. Beginning, peak, resolution. Three-year-olds can follow that. And that arc — I felt a big thing, I got through it, I'm okay — is the most important story you can tell.

## What works at three

Three-year-olds can handle a bit more narrative than two-year-olds. They're tracking characters across several pages, noticing cause-and-effect, forming opinions about who did the right thing and who didn't. So the books that work at three have a bit more story in them.

They still need strong visual emotion cues — big faces, expressive illustrations. But now you can also ask "why do you think she did that?" and get an answer. That question is worth asking, because it pulls your kid into a collaborative meaning-making process, which is what builds emotional intelligence faster than anything else.

Short is still good. Under fifteen pages is ideal. But you have more room to work with than you did six months ago.

## The books

**"Grumpy Monkey" by Suzanne Lang** — I'd put this on every age's list. Jim is in a terrible mood and cannot explain it. His friends keep trying to fix it. He keeps hating their attempts. Eventually he just has his bad mood and it passes on its own. This is the book that teaches "not every feeling needs a solution" — a concept three-year-olds are developmentally ready to start grasping. Read it slowly. Do the grumpy voice. Ask "do you ever feel like Jim?" and let them answer.

**"When Sophie Gets Angry" by Molly Bang** — Still excellent at three. Now you can talk about what Sophie did when she got angry — ran outside, climbed the tree, watched the ocean. "What do YOU do when you feel really angry?" is a real question at three. They might say they don't know. That's okay. You planted the question. It'll come up again.

**"Llama Llama Mad at Mama" by Anna Dewdney** — The grocery store meltdown. The calm mama who holds on through it. The repair at the end. Three-year-olds can now follow the full arc and understand that Llama Llama's anger was real AND the repair was real AND they coexist. That's not obvious. It's worth repeating.

**"The Pigeon Has Feelings, Too!" by Mo Willems** — The pigeon is having a day. The pigeon expresses several emotions very loudly. Mo Willems writes the pigeon books with this weird genius for the exact emotional pitch of a preschooler — self-righteous, dramatic, briefly self-aware, then immediately back to self-righteous. Three-year-olds love the Pigeon with an almost personal intensity. Reading this together is a lot of pointing and giggling and "I know how Pigeon feels." Which is exactly what you want.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Not a tantrum book exactly, but best on this list for the three-year-old whose meltdowns are driven by separation anxiety (common at this age).
