When a child is freaking out because you left the room, or dropped them at preschool, the book's message — there's an invisible string connecting us even when we're apart — gives them a physical concept to hold onto. Some three-year-olds ask you to hold the "string" with them when they're scared. It works.

**"Alexander and the Terrible, Horrible, No Good, Very Bad Day" by Judith Viorst** — Technically this one works better at four or five, honestly. Alexander's bad day is relatable but the vocabulary and page count can lose a three-year-old. That said, if your three-year-old has a good attention span, it's worth trying. The humor helps. "Some days are just like that, even in Australia." Knowing that is worth something.

**"Breathe Like a Bear" by Kira Willey** — Breathing exercises for kids, illustrated. Very concrete, very simple. Three is exactly the age when you can start introducing "take a deep breath" as an actual calming strategy, because they're now old enough to try it (even if they won't, consistently, for another two years). Read this one not as a story but as something you practice together. Do the exercises out loud. Make it silly. They'll remember it when it counts.

## Reading together at three

At three you can do something you couldn't do at two: debrief. Not during the tantrum, and not immediately after. But later — at bedtime, over lunch — you can say "remember when you got so upset about the cereal this morning? I wonder if you were feeling like Grumpy Jim." Keep it brief, keep it curious, don't make them feel judged. You're not analyzing them. You're making a bridge between the book world and their world.

Three-year-olds love this, actually. They love having their experience recognized in a story. It's validating in a way that's almost visibly physical — they sit up a little. Yeah. That was me.

Three is also when you can start building a basic feelings vocabulary together: mad, sad, scared, frustrated, disappointed, worried. These are different feelings. Most three-year-olds use "mad" for all of them. Gently naming the more specific feeling — "it sounds like you were disappointed, not mad" — is slow work that pays off over years.

## When to be a little more concerned

A three-year-old having tantrums is developmentally normal. A three-year-old having tantrums that last over an hour, happen multiple times a day, or involve aggression that's escalating over time — worth mentioning at the next pediatrician visit. Not because something is definitely wrong, but because a professional eye is useful. Especially if sleep is also a mess. (Sleep makes everything worse. Every time.)

## Make a book made for your child

A character who feels like Llama Llama is Llama Llama. A character who feels like your kid is your kid. Plubis makes custom picture books where the child having a big feeling and finding their way back shares your child's actual name, looks like your child's world, and goes through whatever situation is hardest in your specific house.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about bedtime
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through bedtime routines. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is the first age where they ask genuinely unanswerable bedtime questions. "What happens when you die?" "What is outside the universe?" "Do our toys have feelings when we're sleeping?" You came to do bedtime. You ended up in a philosophy seminar. It's 9:15pm and you're lying on the floor explaining mortality to someone who cannot tie their shoes.

Four-year-olds resist bedtime for reasons that are completely different from why two-year-olds resist bedtime. Two-year-olds resist because they don't want to stop. Four-year-olds resist because their brains have developed just enough to notice how large and strange the night is, and to ask questions about it, and to feel the particular aloneness of being awake in the dark while everyone else seems to be somewhere else.

This is, in a way, progress. It's the beginning of the kind of thinking that eventually becomes creativity and curiosity and philosophy. But right now, at 9pm, it is not progress. It is a reason why you're still awake.

## Why four is a turning point for bedtime books

Four-year-olds can hold narrative in their head. They follow characters through a problem with genuine investment. They'll ask "what happens next" before you turn the page. They have opinions about whether the story was satisfying. They will tell you when a book's ending "doesn't make sense" and they'll usually be right.

This means you can do more with books now. Not just soothing rhythm — actual story that does emotional work. A character who's scared of the dark and figures out what to do with that. A character whose imagination runs wild at night and learns to work with it rather than against it. Books that take four-year-old fears seriously without amplifying them.

The other thing about four: they're social. Making friends matters enormously now. And a lot of bedtime avoidance at four is actually social anxiety — replaying the day, worrying about who said what, planning what to do tomorrow. A bedtime book that gives them a ritual for letting the day go is more useful than a plot-focused story some nights.

## The books

**"Where the Wild Things Are" by Maurice Sendak** — The bedtime classic that's not really about bedtime. Max gets sent to bed without supper. His bedroom becomes a forest. He sails to where the wild things are. He becomes their king. He gets lonely. He sails home. Supper is waiting and it's still hot. It's about anger and wildness and the capacity for a child to hold that and return from it and still be loved. Four-year-olds who have big feelings understand this book in their bones. Read it the same way every night for a week and you'll see them mouthing the words. That's the sign it's doing something real.

**"Bedtime for Frances" by Russell Hoban** — At four they get the joke in a new way. Frances's excuses are recognized as excuses — your four-year-old will point at the page and say "she's just making stuff up!" with great satisfaction. There's something metacognitive happening: they see Frances's tactics, maybe recognize them in themselves, and find that funny rather than threatening. The firm-but-loving parental response also models something useful. After reading this for a week you can say "are you being Frances right now?" and sometimes they'll laugh and go to sleep.

**"Can't You Sleep, Little Bear?" by Martin Waddell** — Big Bear and Little Bear live in a cave. Little Bear keeps waking up afraid of the dark. Big Bear brings lanterns — first small, then bigger, then the biggest lantern of all. None of them work. Then Big Bear takes Little Bear outside and shows him the moon and the stars, the great natural light of the night, and Little Bear sleeps. I find this book moving every single time. The patience of Big Bear, the tenderness of the solution — it's a model of how to handle night fear with a child who's not reassured by easy answers. And the message is genuinely true: sometimes the fear of the dark is calmed by seeing how beautiful the dark actually is.

**"The Berenstain Bears in the Dark" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — Sister Bear becomes afraid of the dark after reading a scary book. Mama and Papa help her figure out that the imagined scary things don't hold up when you actually look at them. Some parents find the Berenstain Bears a bit heavy-handed but for this particular fear, at this age, the explicitness is actually the feature. Four-year-olds sometimes need permission to look directly at the thing they're afraid of. This book gives them a framework for that. It's not art. But it works.

**"No Jumping on the Bed!" by Tedd Arnold** — Walter jumps on the bed against all warnings and falls through the floor into all his neighbors' apartments one by one, causing glorious chaos. Completely absurd, extremely funny, and good for the four-year-old who is bouncing off the walls at bedtime because their body has not caught up with the fact that the day is over. The physical release of laughing at Walter falling through floors is real. Also teaches them, gently, about the consequences of bouncing on beds — though whether this is didactic or just funny depends on the night.

**"Papa, Please Get the Moon for Me" by Eric Carle** — Monica asks her papa for the moon. He gets a very long ladder and climbs up and brings it down. The full-page illustrations unfold like a book within a book. The moon waxes and wanes in Monica's arms and eventually returns to the sky. It's about asking for impossible things and having a parent who takes the asking seriously. Four-year-olds need books that honor the scale of their emotional requests. This does that beautifully. The art is extraordinary. The moon changing size in Monica's arms is something they'll remember.

**"When Sophie Gets Angry — Really, Really Angry" by Molly Bang** — This is not a bedtime book. It's a big-feelings book. And it belongs in the bedtime rotation because four-year-olds often fight sleep hardest on the days they've had the biggest feelings. Sophie gets furious, runs away, cries in a tree, comes home, and is welcomed back. The ending is gentle: "And now the world feels round and right." Reading it the night after a hard day is exactly right. It says: you can hold the big feelings and let them go and come home and everything is still okay.

**"Patchwork Path" by Bettye Stroud** — A more serious, more beautiful pick. Set during slavery, this tells the story of a girl and her father making a quilt together in secret, each block a map of their planned escape to freedom. Not a bedtime book in the traditional sense, but an extraordinary story about making something together as an act of love and courage. If your four-year-old is growing their emotional range and you want to introduce them to bigger history through picture books, this is one of the most moving ways to do it.

## Using books without making bedtime longer

Here's the trap: four-year-olds will use books to extend bedtime. "One more! One more!" forever. So the rule has to be stated at the start, not the end. "Two books, then lights out. You pick." Give them the choices from a small set — three options, pick two. That way the choosing is bounded.

If they start the negotiation mid-book — "after this one can we do one more" — the answer is a quiet, warm, non-negotiating: "We're doing our two. This is book one." Then move straight into book two. No debate. Firm is kind here. The debate is more unsettling for them than the limit.

Also: once the books are done, they're done. Don't start reading a third book even if they escalate. One lapse becomes the new floor.

## The existential question protocol

When the philosophy seminar starts — "but what is infinity" — try this: "That's such a big question. Let's put it in our thinking-about-it box and you can dream about it tonight, and we'll talk about it at breakfast." Then actually talk about it at breakfast. This honors the question, defers it to the morning, and makes a real promise. Four-year-olds remember the promises you make about breakfast conversations. Show up. It teaches them their questions matter AND there's a time for them.

## When a book isn't enough

Monster fears, dark fears, recurring nightmares — four is a classic age for all of these. Books can open the conversation but they can't treat genuine sleep anxiety. If your child is having nightmares regularly, waking terrified, unable to settle even after reading together, it's worth a conversation with your pediatrician or a child psychologist. There's good, concrete help available and there's nothing wrong with needing it.

## Make a book made just for your child

Four-year-olds are fiercely attached to their own identity. A book about a kid named *them*, who has *their* stuffed animal, who lives in a house that looks like *their* house — that's not just enjoyable, it's genuinely magical to them. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. You tell us your child's name and their situation, and we put them at the center of a story built just for them. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about big feelings
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through big feelings. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is when the feelings get embarrassing. Not for them — for you.

Because at four, your kid has enough language to narrate their feelings at full volume in public. "I'M SO ANGRY AT YOU AND I DON'T LIKE THIS GROCERY STORE" while strangers side-eye you in the cereal aisle. "WHY DOES EVERYTHING FEEL SO BAD" at a birthday party in front of twelve other families. And your personal favorite, "I AM SAD BUT I DON'T KNOW WHAT ABOUT" delivered at 9:47pm on a night when you have forty minutes of work left to do.

Four is also, genuinely, one of the most emotionally intense ages. They have the vocabulary now. They don't have the regulation. And they care deeply about fairness, about being seen, about whether you understood them. A four-year-old who feels misunderstood is a four-year-old who will make sure you understand by repeating themselves at increasing volume until you do.

Books do real work here. Not just naming feelings anymore — now they can model a whole arc. What does it look like to feel something overwhelming and find your way through it? Four-year-olds can track that story and store it somewhere useful.

## The developmental window you're working with

At four, kids can genuinely discuss their feelings after the fact. Not in the middle — never in the middle — but twenty minutes later, when everyone's calm, they can tell you: "I was angry because you said we had to leave and I wasn't ready." That's enormous. That's what years of reading feelings books and narrating faces and doing the bear breath and sitting with them through meltdowns has been building toward.

So use it. After the hard moment, when you're both recovered, open one of these books. "This part reminds me of this morning, a little." Let them tell you what they think happened in their body. Don't rush in with explanations. Four-year-olds who get to narrate their own experience get better at it faster than four-year-olds who get it explained to them.

Also at four: fairness is EVERYTHING. Books that engage with the feeling of something being unfair land with particular force. Worth knowing when you're choosing.

## Books I'd actually put in your hands

**"The Recess Queen" by Alexis O'Neill** — Mean Jean is the Recess Queen who makes everyone at recess feel small. Then a new kid shows up and doesn't know the rules and treats Jean just like anyone else, and everything changes. This is the book for the four-year-old who's afraid of a kid at preschool, or who has been the mean kid and might not entirely know it. The emotional complexity here — how big feelings like loneliness and fear come out as domineering behavior — is genuinely handled well. And the resolution isn't "Jean got in trouble." It's "Jean found a friend." That's a more interesting lesson.

**"In My Heart: A Book of Feelings" by Jo Witek** — I recommended this for three-year-olds too, but it does MORE work at four because now your kid can engage with the similes. "Happy feels like a big yellow balloon in your chest" — at four, they can sit with that. They can tell you if that's what happy feels like to them or if it feels like something else. That conversation is genuinely worth having. There are no right answers. The book just opens the door.

**"Visiting Feelings" by Lauren Rubenstein** — Unusual structure — it invites kids to notice feelings in the body as visitors passing through. Not good feelings or bad feelings. Just feelings that arrive, get noticed, and eventually move on. The "feelings pass" concept is one of the most useful things you can teach a child and most books for this age don't touch it. This one does. "You don't have to fix it, you can just notice it" is the message. That's useful for the rest of your kid's life.

**"Don't Feed the Worry Bug" by Andi Green** — Wince is a worrier. His worry bug grows bigger the more he feeds it with anxious thoughts. The metaphor of the worry bug is sticky and immediately usable — "are you feeding the worry bug right now?" becomes a shorthand between you and your kid that works even mid-meltdown because it's a tiny bit funny and funny helps. Four-year-olds who tend toward anxiety in particular often find this book a relief. The worry has a name and a shape and that makes it smaller.

**"When Sophie Gets Angry — Really, Really Angry" by Molly Bang** — Still on the list at four because it does something almost no other book does: it shows a kid in the full wildness of anger without the story immediately trying to fix it. She runs. She climbs a tree. She watches the water. She cries. She comes back when she's ready. At four, your kid can follow all of that and feel something real about it. Sophie doesn't need you to fix her anger. She just needs the space to let it move through her. That's the message. It's a good one.

**"Cool Down and Work It Out" by Cheri J. Meiners** — More explicitly instructional than some of the others, but at four that's appropriate. There are actual strategies here: cool-down spots, calming words, working through a problem with someone. Four-year-olds can learn these strategies from a book in a way two-year-olds can't. Worth having alongside the more emotionally rich books as a kind of toolkit reference.

**"Brave Irene" by William Steig** — Technically about perseverance but the emotions Irene experiences — cold, exhausted, scared, determined — are a masterclass in showing a complicated inner state through a physical journey. Steig is one of the all-time greats and this book doesn't get enough attention. It's also longer than most picture books, which works well at four when kids can sit for a real story. The ending is deeply satisfying in a way that sneaks up on you as an adult.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Still worth reading, especially for separation-adjacent feelings or after a grandparent visit ends or when someone important isn't there anymore. At four, the metaphor is even richer. "Where's the string go?" "Right here." Good at bedtime after a hard day.

## The conversation that's worth having

Here's a thing four-year-olds can do that younger kids can't: they can be curious about their own feelings. Not always. Not in the middle of a storm. But in the calm, you can ask: "What did angry feel like in your body? Was it in your stomach or your chest or your hands?" And they might actually answer.

This is incredibly worth doing. Not because it prevents tantrums (it doesn't, much) but because a kid who can describe their internal experience is a kid who is learning that their inner world is worth paying attention to and can be understood. That's the foundation of every form of emotional intelligence. It's built in small conversations, not in grand interventions.

## Reading together at four without it feeling like a lesson

Four-year-olds smell didacticism. If the book feels like you're trying to teach them a lesson, they will resist it with their whole bodies. So don't set up the reading with "this book is about what to do when you're angry." Just read it. Let the story land. Save the conversation for afterward, if they want it.

And if they don't want to talk about it, that's fine too. The book still did its job. Four-year-olds process things overnight. More than you'd think. The book they seemed uninterested in will sometimes show up in their language two weeks later. Trust the slow work.

## Bonus: what to try in the middle of a meltdown

Since we're here: the three things that actually help a four-year-old in the middle of big feelings are (1) getting physically close without talking, (2) naming the feeling simply without commentary — "you're really angry right now" not "you shouldn't be so angry about this" — and (3) waiting. Not leaving. Not lecturing. Just waiting with them while it moves through. That's it. Books are the setup. Being there is the work.

## When a book isn't enough

Four-year-olds who are regularly having explosive, hours-long episodes — or who seem to carry constant anxiety or have meltdowns that are escalating in intensity rather than settling down — deserve professional attention. A pediatric occupational therapist or early childhood therapist can assess whether there's something additional to understand and address. Books are a real tool. They're not the only tool.

## Make a book made just for your child

Custom picture books hit different at four because your kid is old enough to understand that the book is about them. Not a bunny who happens to feel like them. Them. Plubis makes a full illustrated picture book with your child as the main character — their name, their specific situation — generated in five minutes. "Zara was SO angry and she didn't know what to do with all that angry" is not a book on any shelf. But it could be on yours. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about building confidence
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through confidence and self-esteem. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds have opinions.

Not guesses. Not preferences. *Opinions.* They have already decided what kind of person they are. They have a self-concept — partially correct, partially wildly fictional. One four-year-old I know told me with complete certainty that she is "bad at scissors." She is four. She has been using scissors for maybe eight months total. She is not bad at scissors. She is a person who once made a cut she didn't like and filed it permanently in the "me" folder.

This is the age where the story kids are telling about themselves starts to harden slightly. Not all the way. There's still enormous plasticity. But you can hear the edges forming. "I'm shy." "I'm not good at drawing." "I don't like new things." Said with conviction. Said like reporting a fact.

Picture books won't demolish those stories single-handedly. But the right books — the ones that show a character revising their story about themselves — can introduce the concept that the story is revisable. That's actually a big deal at four.


## What confidence looks like at four (warning: it's complicated)

Four is the age where you get the boldest possible kid in every situation except the ones that matter most.

At four, a child might absolutely belt out a song in the middle of a grocery store with zero self-consciousness. But refuse to speak to a new kid at preschool. They might climb the highest structure at the playground — confidently, without looking down — but cry if you ask them to read a word they don't know yet.

The social performance zone is where it gets interesting. Four-year-olds care a lot about what peers think. Not in the abstract anxious way a seven-year-old might — more concretely and immediately. If another kid laughs at what they're building, they might knock it over themselves before someone else can judge it. Preemptive self-destruction. Very common at four. Very worth addressing.

The other thing happening at four: comparison is fully online. They know when another kid is faster, taller, better at coloring. They know and it affects them. The confidence work at this age involves helping them separate "that kid is good at running" from "I am bad at things."


## The books

### *Jabari Jumps* by Gaia Cornwall (2017)

This is my top pick for four-year-olds, and I will defend it.

Jabari has passed his swimming test. He is ready to jump off the diving board. He says he's not scared. He is extremely scared. He watches other kids jump. He waits. He climbs back down the ladder. He watches some more. His dad waits with him, patient, says nothing about the waiting. And then — finally — Jabari jumps.

The reason this book is perfect for four specifically: Jabari is not rescued from his fear. His dad doesn't carry him to the edge. No one tells him there's nothing to be scared of (the most useless phrase ever spoken to a child with a fear). His dad says: "Sometimes when you try something new, you might feel a little nervous." And then he waits.

Four-year-olds are facing a lot of new things. New class, new teacher, new kids, new rules. And they are performing confidence even when they're terrified. Jabari gives them a character who does that and then shows them that the performance can eventually become real — not through being talked out of fear, but through deciding, in your own time, to jump.

Cornwall published this in 2017 with Candlewick Press. It won a Caldecott Honor in 2018. Worth every page.


### *The Most Magnificent Thing* by Ashley Spires (2014)

At four, this book hits harder than it does at three because four-year-olds have started to feel the specific burn of trying hard and having it not work.

The girl in this book is not a passive character. She is *determined*. She has a vision. She works. It doesn't come out right. She works more. Still wrong. She is furious — genuinely, fully, legitimately furious — and she quits. That quitting is drawn honestly; she's not shown throwing a tantrum, she just stops, and that dignified stopping feels real.

Then a walk with her dog clears her head enough to try one more time. She notices that her previous failed attempts each had *something* right in them. And she builds the thing.

But here's the part I want to highlight for four-year-olds specifically: she doesn't build the exact thing she originally imagined. The "most magnificent thing" she ends up with is magnificent because she made it, not because it matched the original vision.

For a four-year-old who is just starting to have perfectionist tendencies — who says "I messed it up" because the sun in their drawing has the wrong number of rays — this is genuinely valuable. The finished thing doesn't have to match the thing in your head. It just has to be yours.


### *Beautiful Oops!* by Barney Saltzberg (2010)

I know I mentioned this for age three, and yes, it belongs here too. But it belongs here for a slightly different reason.

By four, kids are making things with *intentions*. They sit down to draw a horse. They have a horse in mind. And when the horse comes out looking like a lopsided potato, they feel it.

*Beautiful Oops!* is about reframing that moment — not as a failure to fix but as an invitation to go somewhere else. The torn paper becomes a mouth. The smear becomes an elephant. The accidental fold becomes a beak.

This is practical creative confidence training, and four-year-olds are in exactly the right developmental window to absorb it because they are making things all day long. Saltzberg's book, with its tactile flaps and visible "mistakes," makes the lesson visceral rather than abstract. You touch the torn paper. You see what it became. Your hands understand before your brain does.

Get the board book version if you don't want it destroyed in two weeks. (You want the board book version.)


### *I Am Enough* by Grace Byers, illustrated by Keturah A. Bobo (2018)

At four, the social comparison engine is running, and it needs this book as a counterweight.

Byers' text doesn't tell kids they're better than other kids. It doesn't say "you're special" in that vague, inflated way that kids increasingly can't hear. It says something harder and truer: I am enough as I am. Not the most. Not the best. Enough.

For a four-year-old who has started to notice they're not the fastest runner or the best artist, "enough" is not a consolation prize. It's actually a more stable place to stand than "the best," because the best is always somebody else's turn eventually.

Bobo's illustrations are worth sitting with slowly. They're warm and luminous and not at all the bright primary-color splash of most four-year-old picture books. There's something almost meditative about them, which makes this good for a slower, quieter reading.


## The bonus pick: Read *Chrysanthemum* by Kevin Henkes (1991) early

This is technically a school-readiness book, but four is exactly the right time to get ahead of it.

Chrysanthemum loves her name. Then she starts school and three girls tell her it's too long, it's a flower, it takes up half the name tag. She begins to believe them. Her parents reassure her every night. Eventually a beloved music teacher reveals she's also named after a flower, and the whole dynamic shifts.

This book has been around since 1991 and hasn't aged a day. Henkes is unbeatable at depicting the small social cruelties of early childhood without being either melodramatic or dismissive. Chrysanthemum's sadness is real and specific — the way she crosses her legs to make herself smaller, the way she stops raising her hand.

But here's what I love about it for confidence work: it doesn't tell her to just ignore the mean kids. It doesn't say her peers are wrong and she's right. It works through a genuine shift in her social environment. It takes an adult she respects saying "me too" before Chrysanthemum can reclaim her name.

For a four-year-old about to enter preschool or already navigating social hierarchies among peers, this book is real preparation. It names the specific damage that other kids' words can do. And it shows the path out isn't stoic self-belief — it's community. Someone else extending a hand.

Kids receive that. Even at four.


## How to talk about these books without ruining them

The instinct when reading confidence books is to pause and deliver the lesson. "See, Jabari was scared but he did it anyway! So you can be brave too!" 

Resist this.

The extracting-the-lesson mode turns a story into a lecture. Kids feel it. They disengage. The book becomes a vehicle for the thing you want to tell them, which they already know you want to tell them, and they tune out.

Instead: questions about the character. "Why do you think Jabari waited so long?" "Was that fair what those animals said to Gerald?" "Do you think the girl should have taken a break?"

You're not conducting a reading comprehension quiz. You're watching your four-year-old think. And thinking about Gerald's fear, about Jabari's hesitation, about Chrysanthemum's hurt — that thinking is the work. Not any conclusion they arrive at. The thinking itself.


## Something worth saying out loud

At four, your kid is building a model of themselves. Every interaction goes into it. Every time someone laughs at them. Every time you say "you figured it out." Every time they try something and it goes wrong and the world doesn't end.

Books are one input among many. But they're a particularly useful input because they let a child be scared alongside a character without the stakes being personal. The fear is practice. The resolution is practice. They're rehearsing something they'll need to do for real.

That rehearsal matters. Even if they forget the title by next Tuesday.


Want a story where the character is *exactly* your four-year-old — their name, their fear, their specific thing they're not sure about trying?

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about going to the dentist
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through dentist visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds are a specific kind of chaos. They're verbal enough to articulate exactly what they're scared of — "I don't like the thing that makes the noise" — but not yet experienced enough to have a track record of surviving scary things to look back on. So fear at four has this very specific quality: it's articulate and detailed and completely impervious to logic.

You will not reason your four-year-old out of dental anxiety. I'm saying this now so you don't spend three days trying. What you can do is give them a mental model that's accurate enough to reduce surprise, and stories where someone like them goes through the thing and lives to tell the tale.

Books do this beautifully at four. Four-year-olds have actual narrative comprehension — they understand story arcs, they root for characters, they feel genuine relief when everything works out. A good dental-visit book is essentially a short movie for their brain: protagonist encounters challenge, protagonist feels scared, protagonist gets through it. Credits roll. Kid internalizes: I can do that too.

But here's the thing. The book has to earn that ending. If it skips the fear and jumps straight to "the dentist was SO FUN!" your four-year-old will smell the propaganda from a mile away. They're not dumb. They know getting a stranger to put metal tools in your mouth is not inherently a party.

## What you're actually working with at four

Four-year-olds fear the unfamiliar, fear loss of control, and often fear the drill sound specifically even before they've ever heard it — because older kids or cartoon characters have told them about it. You might have a child who's terrified of something they've never experienced, based entirely on secondhand information. Welcome to parenting a four-year-old.

What works: naming the tools before the appointment (borrow words from the books), doing a practice run at home, letting them ask the dentist their questions directly, and giving them one thing they can control during the appointment. "You can hold my hand the whole time" is a legitimate control mechanism. So is "you can pick the flavor of the toothpaste they use."

What doesn't work: "It won't hurt at all" (sometimes things feel weird), "don't be scared" (groundbreaking instruction, has never once worked on any child in human history), and over-explaining in advance until the kid convinces themselves it's definitely going to be terrible.

Start the books about five days before the appointment. Let them become part of the bedtime routine. Normalize the conversation. "Oh look, it's the dentist book — what's your favorite part?" Make the dentist something you talk about casually, not something that only comes up when you're prepping for The Big Event.

## The books worth reading (actually worth reading)

**"The Crocodile Who Was Afraid of the Dentist" — wait, that's not a real book. Let me give you the real ones.**

**"ABC Dentist: Healthy Teeth from A to Z" by Harriet Ziefert** — This goes through the alphabet of dental care and is more educational than narrative, but four-year-olds who are into letters will find it genuinely engaging. It demystifies the whole world of tooth care in small bites (sorry). Not the most thrilling read but extremely useful for the "why" questions your four-year-old is definitely going to ask.

**"The Berenstain Bears Visit the Dentist" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — Still works at four. Actually works better at four than at two or three, because four-year-olds can follow the dual storyline of Brother and Sister both being nervous in different ways about different things. The loose tooth subplot is relatable to kids who are noticing their own teeth feeling slightly different. Classic for a reason.

**"Mario Goes to the Dentist" — Actually, let me not invent titles. Here are real ones:**

**"Mortimer's First Garden" — No, different topic. Let me stay on track.**

**"Brush Your Teeth, Please" by Leslie McGuire** — This is a pop-up book about animals brushing their teeth, and four-year-olds lose their minds for pop-ups. It's not specifically about going to the dentist but it builds enthusiasm for teeth care generally, and enthusiasm for teeth care reduces the "why do I have to go" friction. Sometimes the most useful book is the one that makes the larger category feel worthwhile.

**"I'm Going to the Dentist" by Mary Auld** — Part of the "My Day" series, this one walks through a dental visit with straightforward, calm text and clear illustrations. It's not flashy but it's accurate. The sequence of what actually happens — check-in, waiting, chair, exam, cleaning, sticker — is depicted in the right order. Kids who like procedural "here's what happens" storytelling (there are a lot of them) find this very settling.

**"Just Going to the Dentist" by Mercer Mayer** — I keep coming back to Little Critter because the emotional honesty is so right for this age. He doesn't want to go. He goes anyway. He's fine. That's it. No redemptive "now I LOVE the dentist!" arc. Just: I did the thing. Four-year-olds who are building their identity around being brave will find this deeply validating.

**"The Tooth Book" by Dr. Seuss** — Dr. Seuss specifically about teeth! It's more about the general wonder of having teeth than about dental visits, but Dr. Seuss is magic for four-year-olds and reading it turns teeth into something delightful rather than something to be examined by a stranger. Use it as the entry point — teeth are cool, teeth are interesting, having a dentist look at your cool interesting teeth makes sense.

**"Open Wide!" by Laurie Keller** — The funniest dental book on this list. Absolutely worth it. The conceit — teeth as students in a school — is strange enough to be captivating and the jokes are genuinely clever. Four-year-olds get maybe thirty percent of them and parents get the rest, which is the ideal picture book formula. Read it once for fun, then read it again and use it to point out the actual dental tools dressed up as characters.

**"Going to the Dentist" by Anne Civardi** — This Usborne book is extremely detailed and slightly old-fashioned looking, but the detail is the point. It shows *exactly* what each piece of equipment is for. The X-ray machine. The chair controls. The little cup. For detail-hungry four-year-olds — and there are many — having a visual dictionary of the dental office is immensely calming. Unknown things are scary. Known things are just things.

## Reading together: the technique part

Four-year-olds will ask to be involved. Let them. "You read this page" (even if they can't read, they'll point at pictures and narrate). "You tell me what the dentist is doing here." "What do you think the little mirror is for?"

When they get something wrong, don't correct aggressively — guide. "Interesting! I think that mirror actually helps the dentist see the back teeth. Want to check in a real mirror how far back your teeth go?" Now you're doing a teeth investigation together. Now teeth are interesting.

If your child has a specific fear — the drill sound, the suction thing, the X-ray machine — don't avoid it in the books. Seek it out. Read about it. Look at pictures of it. Say the name out loud. The goal isn't to convince them it's fine. The goal is to make the scary thing smaller through familiarity. You don't have to love the drill. You just have to know what it is.

## The bonus bit: what to do in the waiting room

Bring the book. I'm serious. Have it in your bag. While you're waiting, read it one more time. Not as a warning or a reminder but just as: "oh, we have the dentist book in here, want to read it?" The repetition right before the appointment reactivates all the prep work you've done. And kids who've just heard the story of a character getting through something are in a slightly better mental position to get through it themselves.

Also: let them look around the waiting room and name what they recognize from the books. "Oh, there's the chair like in the story!" That's their brain making the connection between the familiar (book) and the unfamiliar (real office), and that connection is very literally what makes scary things less scary.

## Make a book made for your child

Every book on this list stars a bear, a critter, a tiger, or a child with a different name than yours. That's fine. But for the kid who struggles to bridge the gap between "story about a character" and "thing that's going to happen to me specifically" — a personalized book is actually different.

Plubis makes personalized picture books for situations like dentist visits. Your kid's name. Their age. A tone that's honest without being alarming. A story where they're the protagonist who gets through it.

Four-year-olds who hear their own name in a story lean in. Their eyes go wide. They point at the page. *That's me.* And if the character who shares their name gets through the dentist appointment and feels proud — well. The connection doesn't need much more explanation than that.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about a parent being deployed
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through military deployment. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds ask questions you don't know how to answer.

Why does Mommy have to go? Why can't the Army find someone else? Is she going to get hurt? When is "soon"? Is "soon" the same as "a long time"? Did she leave because I was bad?

That last one. It's more common than you'd expect. Young children are egocentric — not selfishly, just developmentally. The world revolves around them, and when something bad happens, they look for what they did to cause it. A 4-year-old whose parent just deployed may carry a quiet, unspoken conviction that they're somehow responsible. They won't tell you this directly. They might act it out instead.

Books can interrupt that story before it calcifies. A good picture book shows a child who has done absolutely nothing wrong, whose parent loves them completely, who has to say goodbye because of something much larger than either of them. That's the truth. It's worth saying out loud, in a story, more than once.

## What 4-year-olds actually need from these books

At four, the emotional processing is more sophisticated than at two or three, but the coping tools are still mostly pre-verbal. Your child might not have the words for "I'm scared you're going to die" but that fear may be very much present. Books that address safety and return — that promise the parent comes back — are doing more work than they might appear to be.

Four-year-olds also have an emerging sense of fairness. "It's not fair" is a constant refrain at this age, and deployment is genuinely not fair. A book that doesn't try to make it fair, but does show a family finding their way through it anyway, is honest in exactly the way a 4-year-old can receive.

Look for books that:
- **Show the full deployment arc** — goodbye, the middle, homecoming
- **Validate anger alongside sadness** — four-year-olds are often angry about deployment in ways adults don't always know how to receive
- **Give the child something to do** — a ritual, a task, a way to feel connected rather than purely passive
- **Return to safety** — stories where the parent comes home provide a template of hope even during the long middle stretch

## Book picks for 4-year-olds

**"Lily Hates Goodbyes" by Jerilyn Marler** — This is the book I'd hand first to any family with a 4-year-old in the middle of deployment. Lily is angry, stubborn, sad, and not remotely interested in being handled or cheered up. Her grandmother wrote it specifically for her real 4-year-old granddaughter because nothing else she could find was honest enough. What makes this book special is the permission it grants for the hard feeling — not the tidy grief, but the messy, stomping, arms-crossed kind. Four-year-olds need that permission more than they need to be told everything will be okay.

**"When Dad's at Sea" by Mindy Pelton** — Emily's father is a Navy pilot. He leaves her a paper chain of 180 links — one for each day of his deployment — and every morning she tears one off. Pelton wrote this from real life, and it shows in every detail: the specific dread of the first day, the gradual normalization, the way routine becomes a container for grief. The paper chain idea is genuinely transformative at this age. Make one. Hang it somewhere visible. Tearing a link off every morning is an act of agency in a situation that otherwise has none.

**"Night Catch" by Brenda Ehrmantraut** — A father deploys and leaves his son a nightly ritual: they'll both look at the North Star at the same time each night. The star becomes a kind of telephone — two people, opposite sides of the world, both looking at the same bright point. At four, children understand the emotional logic of this completely even if they can't articulate it. Two people looking at the same star are, in a meaningful sense, together. Go find your star. Name it if you want to.

**"While You Are Away" by Eileen Spinelli** — Three different children, three different deployed parents, one shared emotional truth: I miss you and I'm waiting and I'm okay and I'm not okay simultaneously. Spinelli respects the complexity here. Nobody in this book is performing ease. The children miss their parents and say so, and the parents come home in the end. The structure is honest and the ending is earned.

**"Hero Dad" by Melinda Hardin** — A small child's narration of all the ways their deployed parent is a real-life superhero. Simple, direct, one sentence per spread. At four, the pride and the missing coexist more easily than they do for younger kids — your child is old enough to hold two feelings at once. This book leans into that. It doesn't pretend the parent being gone isn't hard; it holds up the dignity of the service alongside the grief of the separation.

**"A Yellow Ribbon for Daddy" by Anissa Mersiowsky** — Written in rhyme, from a child's perspective, asking the question every military kid thinks eventually: why does my parent have to go protect other people's families? Why can't they just stay here, with us? Mersiowsky doesn't give a fully satisfying answer — because there isn't one — but the asking is important. The book was written for National Guard families specifically, which matters: Guard deployments often hit families differently, without the same base community and support infrastructure that active-duty families sometimes have.

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Still worth reading at four, perhaps more than ever. At this age, your child can engage with the ritual more consciously: they can press the kiss into their palm, they can choose to touch it when they're scared. They can also participate in giving you a kissing hand in return. Making the ritual reciprocal — both of you carrying the other person's kiss — adds a dimension that a 2-year-old can't quite reach but a 4-year-old absolutely can.

**"My Mommy Wears Combat Boots" by Sharon G. McBride** — One of the few books that centers a deployed mother rather than a deployed father. Representation matters here more than it might seem. If your child's deployed parent is their mother, every book that defaults to "Daddy" is a small paper cut. This book shows a child navigating life with an at-home father while their mother is in uniform, and it does so without making the mother's military service feel like an aberration. She's both a soldier and a mom. Those aren't in conflict.

## Making reading count

At four, your child can begin to be a participant in the story, not just an audience.

Ask questions before you open the book: "What do you think happened to Lily today?" After: "Have you ever felt like Lily? When?" You don't need to push for answers. Just open the door.

Some 4-year-olds will want to draw pictures about what they're feeling after a book. Keep paper nearby. Don't analyze the drawings. Just say "tell me about it" if they want to talk.

Let them bring the book to video calls with the deployed parent. "Read it to Mama." It gives the call a structure and a purpose beyond the awkward "say something" performance that video calls can become.

## When books become a place to hide feelings

Something to watch: at four, children sometimes become very attached to a deployment book not as healing but as a way to stay close to the hard feeling without having to talk about it. They keep asking for it, keep circling the same pages, and don't want to talk about any of it.

This isn't necessarily a problem. Sometimes children need to be with the story before they can be with the feeling. But if you notice this going on for weeks, it's worth gently opening a door: "You always pick this book. Is there something you want to tell me about missing Daddy?"

Maybe they'll talk. Maybe they'll shrug and flip to page four. Either is okay.

## Bonus: things to do alongside the books

The books work best when they're part of a larger ritual, not a standalone fix.

- **A deployment jar:** Every day, write something on a slip of paper — something funny the child did, something they said, something beautiful — and put it in a jar. When the parent comes home, read them all together. The child has been keeping a record for the parent. They've been doing something the whole time.
- **A photo book of the deployed parent** that lives on the child's shelf and belongs to them, not the adults. Their book. Their parent's pictures.
- **Voice messages.** Record the deployed parent reading a book before they leave. Some nights, play the recording instead of reading it yourself. The voice helps.

## Make a book made just for your child

No published book can put your child's name on the cover. No published book can show the deployed parent in their specific uniform, or name the cat, or mention the Tuesday-night ritual that your family has and nobody else does.

Plubis builds custom picture books in about five minutes — your child's name, your family's details, the deployed parent as a real character in their own story. It's not a replacement for the books on this list. It's the book that sits alongside them and says: *this is YOUR story specifically, and it's okay, and the love is still here.*

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about parents getting divorced
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through divorce. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is the age when kids start asking *why*.

Not satisfied with "Mama and Daddy love you very much" — they want to understand. They will ask the same question fifteen different ways until the answer makes sense to them. They'll also say things you weren't ready for, at the worst possible moment, in front of your mother-in-law or the pediatrician.

"Why don't you love Daddy anymore?"
"Where will my toys be?"
"Can I still have my birthday party?"

Four-year-olds have big feelings they're only starting to name. They have strong opinions about fairness. They understand permanence in a way two and three-year-olds don't — which means they understand, on some level, that this isn't going to un-happen. That's a lot to carry.

Books won't answer every question. But they give you something to do together in the hard quiet. A place to start.

## What four-year-olds need from these books

**Characters who feel what they feel.** Not a bear who sort of generically adjusts — a character who is actually sad, actually confused, maybe actually angry. A four-year-old will spot a fake resolution from across the room.

**Some story arc.** Four is old enough to follow a narrative. The character goes through something, feels the feelings, and arrives somewhere — not "fixed," but okay. That arc matters.

**Both homes shown without either parent being the bad guy.** Whatever is happening between the adults — and it can be a lot — the child's book needs to keep both parents in the picture as people who love them. This age is old enough to absorb blame if you let any slip in.

**Room to ask questions.** The best books leave space. They don't tie everything up too neatly.

## Books worth reading

**"It's Not Your Fault, KoKo Bear" by Vicki Lansky** — I keep coming back to this one. KoKo the bear goes through every single thing a four-year-old in this situation goes through — the confusion, the "was it my fault", the two bedrooms, the slow adjustment. The writing is clear and emotionally honest without being clinical. The parent notes in the margins are actually useful for knowing what to say when the book ends. One of the best books for this age.

**"Dinosaurs Divorce: A Guide for Changing Families" by Marc Brown and Laurene Krasny Brown** — This is the thorough one. Different sections on different aspects of divorce — what it means, two homes, new people in parents' lives, holidays, etc. It's more of a resource than a story, which is either its strength or its weakness depending on your child. For a four-year-old who has *lots* of questions, it's genuinely the most thorough thing out there. For one who needs a story to feel safe in, start with KoKo Bear and come back to this.

**"Two Homes" by Claire Masurel** — Simpler than what a four-year-old strictly needs, but there's something in its simplicity that still lands. Some kids, especially when they're overwhelmed, need the calmer version first. Don't dismiss it just because it was written for younger kids.

**"My Family's Changing" by Pat Thomas** — Talks directly to the child in second person. "Have you ever felt like this?" That format works surprisingly well for a four-year-old who is looking for their experience reflected back. The book acknowledges that some things about divorce are genuinely hard and doesn't pretend otherwise, which kids this age appreciate even if they can't articulate why.

**"Was It the Chocolate Pudding? A Story for Little Kids About Divorce" by Sandra Levins** — The title is actually what the older brother asks in the book — did the divorce happen because of the chocolate pudding incident? It's a clever way into the guilt question that four-year-olds carry. Warm art, two brothers navigating two homes together. Good for families with more than one child, but honest and gentle for any kid.

**"Standing on My Own Two Feet" by Tamara Schmitz** — A girl with two homes finds her footing — literally and figuratively. The rhyming format makes it easier to read aloud without it feeling heavy. The child in the story is allowed to miss the way things were. That matters.

## How to read these books with a four-year-old

Four-year-olds will interrupt. They'll say "that's like us" and then look at you to see how you react. They'll ask whether the character's parents got back together. They might get suddenly quiet on a particular page and not say anything.

Follow their lead. If they want to talk, talk. If they go quiet, let the quiet exist. You don't have to fill it.

The most important thing you can do while reading is stay calm. Not rigidly, performatively calm — just present. This is a hard thing and we're going to sit with it together. That's the message your body sends when you stay in the chair and keep your voice even.

And if they ask the question you don't know how to answer — "Will you and Daddy ever live together again?" — honest and simple is better than reassuring and vague. "No. But we both love you and we always will." Say the hard word. Then say the love part.

## The question that might come up

At four, kids sometimes start testing to see if they can fix it. Setting you and your ex up in the same room. Telling each parent what the other one said in a way that seems designed to spark something. It's not manipulative in an adult sense — it's a child trying to solve a problem with the tools they have.

If you see it happening, a gentle "you don't need to fix anything, bub — that's our job" is enough. They're not bad for trying. They just need to hear that it's not their responsibility.

## A book made for your child

Your four-year-old might need to see their name in the story. Their stuff. Their actual world — not some generic illustrated household.

Plubis makes personalized picture books for situations exactly like this one. You describe your child's situation, we generate a book around their name and their experience. It takes five minutes, and the first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about going to the doctor
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through doctor visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds are, in my experience, the most baffling creatures on earth.

They are old enough to reason with. They understand consequences. They can follow a multi-step narrative. They can tell you, with impressive accuracy, exactly what they're afraid of. And then — despite all that capability — they can absolutely melt into a puddle of refusal and tears in the parking lot of the pediatrician's office because the appointment is on a different day than they expected.

The emotional life of a 4-year-old is enormous and unpredictable. That's not a problem you solve. It's a condition you prepare for.

Books help with preparation. Not because they eliminate big feelings, but because they give a 4-year-old something to reach for when big feelings hit. A kid who has spent two weeks living with Froggy's checkup or Franklin's hospital stay has a mental folder labeled "medical visit" with some actual content in it. That folder gets opened when the real visit arrives. It doesn't guarantee smooth sailing but it lowers the activation energy for bravery.

Also: four is the age when kids start to feel genuine pride in being brave. That's new and powerful. Books that celebrate the character's bravery — without papering over the fear — hit differently now than they did at two or three. "Yeah, I was scared like Franklin and I did it anyway" is a sentence a 4-year-old can actually construct about themselves.

## The 4-year-old doctor visit: what you're actually dealing with

The 4-year checkup is a big one at most practices. Vision screening. Hearing screening. A developmental check. And if your kid is in the US and on schedule, they're getting their kindergarten vaccines this year or next — the MMR, varicella, polio, and DTaP booster. That's up to four shots at one visit.

I'm not trying to scare you. But knowing what's coming means you can prepare honestly, which is always better than a surprise.

Four-year-olds can handle honest preparation. They can understand "the doctor will check your eyes and your ears and there will probably be one or two shots — they hurt for a second and then they're done." You don't have to manufacture suspense. Honest preview plus familiar story is the combination that works.

## Book picks

**"Froggy Goes to the Doctor" by Jonathan London, illustrated by Frank Remkiewicz** — This one starts to hit at three and continues to land well at four, partly because 4-year-olds can engage more deeply with the story structure. Why does Froggy keep doing things wrong? What's going to happen with the stethoscope? The humor operates at a slightly higher level than it did a year ago. Frank Remkiewicz's bright, expressive illustrations are kinetic in a way that matches 4-year-old energy. The lollipop at the end genuinely matters to them. Published by Viking, 2002. I'll recommend this one until kids are five, no apologies.

**"The Berenstain Bears Go to the Doctor" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — At four, kids can engage with the subplot that Papa Bear is nervous too. That's rich. That's something to talk about: even adults get nervous at the doctor. It normalizes a wide range of emotional responses. The shot at the end is still the most honest reckoning with vaccination in any children's picture book, and four is a very appropriate age to have the real conversation it opens. First Time Books, Random House, 1981. Ages 3–7.

**"Franklin Goes to the Hospital" by Paulette Bourgeois, illustrated by Brenda Clark** — This one is different from the others because it's about a hospital stay for surgery, not a checkup. Franklin's shell is cracked and needs to be fixed. He tries to be brave but he's secretly terrified. The book doesn't let him off the hook — he genuinely struggles. And then he gets through it. That arc is powerful for 4-year-olds, especially those facing something bigger than a routine visit. It's also just beautifully written. Paulette Bourgeois has a gift for respecting children's intelligence without condescending. Scholastic, 2000. Ages 4–8.

**"Curious George Goes to the Hospital" by Margret and H.A. Rey** — George swallows a puzzle piece (classic George) and ends up in the hospital. It's an adventure, not a horror story. The hospital feels large and interesting rather than threatening. There are nurses and doctors and other kids on the ward. For a 4-year-old, the Curious George voice — unbothered curiosity about everything — is actually a great emotional model for navigating unfamiliar medical settings. Don't take everything so seriously. Look at the interesting stuff. Published by Houghton Mifflin, 1966. Still works.

**"Going to the Doctor" by Anne Civardi, illustrated by Stephen Cartwright** — At four, children can really work through this book page by page because their attention span has expanded. Cartwright's detailed illustrations reward that extended attention. Let them find every duck. Let them ask about every tool. The Joey-gets-his-shot sequence is worth stopping on and discussing at length at this age. "What do you think Joey is feeling right now? What about after?" Four-year-olds can track emotional change across pages in a way younger kids can't. Usborne. Multiple current editions.

**"It's Time for Your Checkup" by Shani Thornton** — This one is written by a pediatric child life specialist, which means it's not just narrative — it includes actual coping tools. Deep breaths. Looking away. Squeezing a hand. For a 4-year-old who has a specific, identified fear (usually the shot, let's be real), having a book that teaches concrete coping strategies alongside a story is genuinely useful. It's less literary than the others but more practical. Published by CreateSpace, 2013. Ages 4+.

**"My Doctor" by Harlow Rockwell** — This is a quiet one. It's a young child's-eye view of the doctor's office — the waiting room, the scale, the stethoscope, the tongue depressor — described in simple, direct language by a child narrator. It's curious rather than anxious. "My doctor uses this to hear my heartbeat." There's no crisis, no shot drama, no resolution arc. Just a child taking stock of an interesting place. For a 4-year-old who isn't particularly afraid but just needs vocabulary and context, this is perfect. Macmillan, 1973. It's old and it's still exactly what it needs to be.

## Reading together

Four-year-olds will start predicting what happens next. Let them. Encourage it. "What do you think Froggy will do now?" The predictions will be wrong and great. Predicting ahead keeps them active in the story rather than passive.

At this age, the after-read conversation is often more valuable than the reading itself. When you close the book, just sit for a second and ask: "What would you do if you were Franklin?" You'll learn a lot about what your kid is actually worried about. That's information you can use.

For the books that show shots specifically (Berenstain Bears, Civardi) — don't rush past that page. Sit with it. Let them react. If they say "I don't want to do that," you're allowed to say "I know. It's fast, though. Like this" and snap your fingers. Concrete timing helps enormously at this age.

One tactical thing: read the book in stages. Read through the waiting room pages today. The exam pages tomorrow. The shot page on its own, the day before the appointment. Graduated exposure is a real technique and books let you do it in a controlled way.

## When a book isn't enough — conversation starters

Four is old enough for a real back-and-forth. If your kid is visibly anxious, try these:

- "What's the scariest part about going to the doctor?" (Specific fear is easier to address than diffuse dread.)
- "What would make it feel better?" (They might surprise you. Sometimes it's "can I bring my dinosaur?" and that's a totally solvable problem.)
- "Did you know the doctor has to check your eyes this time? That part doesn't hurt at all. Want me to show you what it's like?" (Cover one eye. Look at a far-away thing. Done.)
- After the visit: "Was any of it different than you expected?" This debrief matters more than most parents realize. It updates the mental folder for next time.

## Make a book made for your child

Franklin, Froggy, and the Berenstain kids are great. But your 4-year-old knows they're not Franklin. They're themselves — the specific kid with the specific fear about the specific doctor with the fish tank and the cold stethoscope.

At [Plubis](https://plubis.app), you can create a personalized picture book in about five minutes that stars your actual child, addresses their actual worry, and ends with them being brave in the way that's real to them — not the way it worked for a cartoon bear. Answer a few questions, get a book back. Read it before the appointment. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about the first day of school
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through first day of school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is the age where they have opinions. Strong opinions. About everything — what cup they use, what shoes they wear, whether the sandwich is cut in triangles or rectangles. And now you're introducing "a new place where different adults tell you different rules."

Some four-year-olds sprint through the classroom door and you have to chase them for a goodbye hug. Some manufacture elaborate stomach aches on Sunday nights that are absolutely not stomach aches. Both are developmentally normal and both are exhausting in completely different ways.

## What's different at four

Four-year-olds can hold a real conversation about feelings. They can tell you what scared them, in words, with some nuance. They can listen to a character's solution and actually think "I could try that." So books can do more work at this age — they're not just naming a feeling, they're modeling a response.

They're also at the exact age where social dynamics start to matter enormously. Making a friend. Finding your person. Not having anyone to sit with at snack. Books that address that loneliness-before-belonging arc land differently than books about separation from parents. Both matter, but at four the social fear often overtakes the separation fear.

And four-year-olds will REMEMBER a book. They'll pull a reference from something you read two weeks ago. "Like Daniel Tiger!" at drop-off is a real thing that happens. The books become part of their internal library. Choose ones with useful emotional scripts in them.

## Books I'd actually recommend

**"Wemberly Worried" by Kevin Henkes** — Best on this list, hands down, for the anxious four-year-old. Wemberly worries compulsively — about school, about her stuffed rabbit, about the radiator — in this endearing exhausting way that four-year-olds instantly recognize in themselves. The classroom is loud and strange and then she finds Jewel, another kid who's also nervous, and suddenly things shift. It's not "school is great!" It's "you'll find your person." That's more honest and more useful than almost any other reassurance you can offer.

**"The Night Before Kindergarten" by Natasha Wing** — Technically about kindergarten but works just as well for preschool transitions. Rhymes well — bouncy enough to read aloud without your voice dying on the fourth pass. Captures the night-before jitters really specifically — the laid-out outfit, the stuffed animal they're not sure they can bring, the parents who are also quietly nervous. Four-year-olds find the parent nervousness very funny. That's a good thing. Laughing about it together takes some of the charge out of the fear.

**"Scaredy Squirrel Makes a Friend" by Melanie Watt** — Scaredy Squirrel is risk-averse to a comedic degree. He has a detailed plan for how to make a friend that involves a lot of staying in his tree. The plan completely falls apart and a friend arrives anyway. The joke is the anxiety itself and the fact that life works out despite the plan, not because of it. For four-year-olds who tend toward the "I've thought about every possible thing that could go wrong" style of worry, this is perfect. It's also genuinely funny, which helps at the end of a long prep-for-school week.

**"David Goes to School" by David Shannon** — David is chaos. He's always in trouble. He does everything wrong — runs in the halls, talks out of turn, doesn't raise his hand. Four-year-olds love him with an almost suspicious intensity that tells you something. There's something genuinely reassuring about a kid who makes every possible school mistake and still gets a gold star at the end. It says: school has rules but you're still okay even when you mess up. That's a useful thing to know on day one when you're terrified of doing something wrong.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At four they can really understand the metaphor — love is an invisible string that connects you to the people you love even when they're far away. You can make it a morning ritual: "can you feel the string?" before you walk to the classroom door. Point to your chest. Point to theirs. The ritual matters as much as the book. Maybe more.

**"A Letter to My Teacher" by Deborah Hopkinson** — Told from the perspective of a kid looking back on a difficult but formative teacher relationship. There's a really nice warmth to it — the message is that teachers notice more than you think, and that the right teacher can change things. Good for a four-year-old who's anxious about whether their teacher will be nice. You can read it and then say: "Your teacher is going to learn your name. They're going to notice things about you." That often helps.

**"Llama Llama Misses Mama" by Anna Dewdney** — Still works at four but you'll want to pair it with something more sophisticated now. The feelings are right, the resolution is a little quick. Read it first, then add Wemberly for depth. Think of it as the emotional warm-up book.

## A note on the books that are, honestly, not great

There's a whole category of school-readiness books with checklists and rules and step-by-step "here's what you'll do at school" explanations. Skip them. They feel like prep materials disguised as stories and your kid will sense that immediately. Your four-year-old doesn't need a schedule — they need to see a character feel scared, sit with that, and find their way through. The practical stuff they'll figure out in the first three days.

## Reading together without making it A Big Thing

Casual is better than ceremonial. The kid who got a dramatic two-week school-book reading campaign sometimes walks in MORE nervous than the kid who had one low-key story at bedtime the night before. Ceremony signals "this is a Major Event." What you want to signal is "this is manageable, here look at this story."

Let them ask questions while you read. "What if I don't like the teacher?" "What if no one wants to play with me?" Don't leap to reassurance. Ask back: "That sounds like it would feel hard. What do you think you'd do?" Four-year-olds have more answers than you expect. The point is to get them problem-solving, not to soothe them into compliance.

If they want to skip a page, skip it. If they want to go back to a specific illustration five times, go back five times. Follow their lead on what they need to sit with.

## The drop-off that goes sideways

Some mornings the books help and some mornings your kid is still screaming and you're still standing in the parking lot and none of it matters. That's okay. The work you did with the book isn't wasted — it's in them somewhere. They just can't access it in the middle of a meltdown. Give it time. The second week is usually better than the first.

## When a book isn't enough

Some four-year-olds have real separation anxiety that doesn't ease up with time and repetition. If drop-off is still brutal after three or four weeks — not just tears but inconsolable distress, stomachaches every morning, refusal to engage at school, coming home hollowed out — loop in the teacher and your pediatrician. There are great early childhood counselors who specialize in exactly this transition. Books are conversation starters, not a substitute for that support.

## Make a book made just for your child

Your four-year-old is old enough to understand that the book is about them — that's a genuinely different thing. Plubis generates a custom illustrated picture book in five minutes. You put in their name and situation, and we make them the main character. "Wemberly worried" is great. "Aiden was worried about his first day" is something else entirely. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about losing a grandparent
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a grandparent dying. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds ask "why" about everything. Everything. So when a grandparent dies, you're going to get questions that stop you cold.

*Why did she die?*

*Will you die?*

*Will I die?*

*If I'm really really good, can she come back?*

There's no script for this. Anybody who tells you there's a script is lying. What there is: honesty, consistency, your arms, and a few really good books.

This is the hardest part, I think. At four they're old enough to understand that something terrible and permanent has happened. Old enough to be scared by it. Old enough to ask you to explain the unexplainable. But not old enough to have any internal framework for holding it.

You're the framework. And books can help you be that.

## What four-year-olds need from a grief book

At four, a child is starting to grasp permanence — the idea that "died" isn't the same as "went to the store." That awareness is new, and it's scary. You might see anxiety about your death — "Are you going to die?" is almost universal at this age after a loss. Answer it honestly and calmly: "I'm healthy and I plan to be here for a very long time." Don't make promises you can't keep, but don't catastrophize either.

You'll likely also see confusion about where the body went (concrete minds want to know what happened to the physical person), repetitive retelling (they'll tell people about it over and over — this is processing, not performance), and play-acting death. The play-acting is normal and healthy. Let them. Don't freak out.

Books that work best here explain things clearly and honestly, without being clinical. They need to see big feelings represented — not just sadness but anger, confusion, the weird guilty feeling of laughing at something the day after. They need to see adults feel those things too.

## Book picks for four-year-olds

**"When Dinosaurs Die: A Guide to Understanding Death" by Laurie Krasny Brown** — Unflinching, kind, practical. This is the book I'd give every parent with a four-year-old. It covers what death is, what happens to the body, different beliefs about what comes next, and what to do with the feelings. It's educational without being cold. Read it yourself first. Then read it together.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — By now your four-year-old might be pushing back on simple reassurances. This book gives them a concrete image to hold onto — a string made of love that connects even across death. Kids this age can hold that image for a long time.

**"Lifetimes" by Bryan Mellonie and Robert Ingpen** — Still excellent at four. They're old enough now to understand the whole arc of what this book is saying about how living things have beginnings and endings. Worth reading again even if you read it at three.

**"I Miss You: A First Look at Death" by Pat Thomas** — Straightforward and warm. Talks about what death means, why it happens, how different people understand it. Good if your four-year-old is asking direct questions and needs direct answers in a gentle voice.

**"Grandfather's Journey" by Allen Say** — A Japanese-American man loves two places and two peoples and moves between them his whole life. It's about love and loss and carrying people with you. The illustrations are gorgeous. This one's for sitting with, not rushing through.

**"Badger's Parting Gifts" by Susan Varley** — Badger knows he's old and going to die. He leaves gifts behind — not objects, but things he taught the animals in his life. After he's gone, they share those memories. This is one of the most beloved grief books in existence, and at four, kids start to fully feel it. Be prepared to cry.

## Reading together

Four-year-olds are conversational. They'll interrupt constantly. They'll ask questions that derail the whole book. Let them.

Here's a structure that actually works. Before you read: "This book is about an animal whose friend died. That's kind of like what happened with Grandma. We might feel sad reading it. That's okay." During: when they ask questions, answer them then, not later. "Yes, she really did die. No, she can't come back. We miss her." After: "What did you think about that?" Then stop talking. Let them answer. Even if they say "I'm hungry" — that's a real answer too.

Don't force a lesson. The book does its work slowly, sometimes over weeks of re-reads.

## Questions that will knock you flat — a bonus section

Every parent in this situation gets hit with questions they weren't ready for. Here are the most common from four-year-olds, with honest (not perfect) responses:

**"Will you die?"** — "Everyone dies someday. But I'm healthy and I'm going to be here for a very, very long time. My job is to take care of you."

**"Will I die?"** — "Yes, someday, when you're very old. Right now you're healthy and young and you have so much life ahead of you."

**"Why did she have to die?"** — "Her body got very old/very sick and stopped working. It wasn't anyone's fault. It just happened."

**"Can we go visit her?"** — "No, sweetheart. When someone dies they can't come back. But we can look at pictures of her and tell stories about her. She's always in our hearts."

There are no perfect answers. There are only honest, loving ones. You'll give them, even when your voice shakes.

## When a book isn't enough

If your four-year-old is showing persistent signs of grief — not sleeping, not eating, withdrawn for weeks, playing out death scenarios obsessively — talk to your pediatrician. Some kids need more support. There's no shame in that. Books are a help; they're not a substitute for real care.

For most kids, what helps most is you. Present, consistent, honest, willing to name the thing. Say the person's name. Say they died. Say you miss them too.

## Make a book made for your child

The books above are about fictional grandparents and fictional grandchildren. They're good — but they don't know that your grandfather had a workshop, or that your grandmother always called them by a special nickname, or that the last time they saw them they were in a hospital and it scared everyone.

A Plubis book is made for your child, about their loss, with details that actually match their world. You share a few things about them and the person they lost. Five minutes. PDF to read tonight.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about the loss of a pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through losing a pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
