Four-year-olds ask why. A lot. And when a pet dies, "why" gets complicated fast — why did it happen, why can't it come back, why did we have to have a pet if it was going to die anyway.

This age is genuinely hard for pet loss because they're right in between: old enough to understand permanence but young enough that permanence still feels like a personal betrayal. Like you did this to them.

The books in this list meet them there — honestly, without flinching, and with some warmth to hold onto.

## What four-year-olds are doing with grief

At four, they have big questions *and* big feelings. They can sustain a longer story. They'll remember the book you read the night after the pet died and ask for it again two weeks later. They can talk about "missing" in ways that feel real — not just "where did it go" but "I wish it were here."

They're also starting to understand that death happens to everything. Which is terrifying. So some books at this age will bring up questions about grandparents, about you. That's not derailing. That's their brain doing the right thing — connecting dots. Sit with the questions. You don't have to answer all of them definitively.

## The actual books

**"Goodbye Mousie" by Robie H. Harris, illustrated by Jan Ormerod**

Yes, it's still on the list at four. The emotional accuracy of this book ages up well. A four-year-old will notice things a two-year-old missed — the way the boy's dad doesn't try to fix his feelings, the careful way they prepare the burial box together. Harris got something exactly right: that grief has stages even in a four-year-old, and they don't move through them in order. Get it from the library if you don't own it yet.

**"The Tenth Good Thing About Barney" by Judith Viorst, illustrated by Erik Blegvad**

This is the one for four-year-olds. A boy's cat Barney dies, and his mother suggests he think of ten good things about Barney to say at the funeral. He can only think of nine. Later, his father explains that Barney is in the ground now, helping things grow — and suddenly the boy has his tenth thing. It's a secular, honest, genuinely wise book about how grief and love and memory work together. Published in 1971 and still the best-reasoned book on the topic for this age. Four-year-olds can fully grasp the idea that Barney "helps flowers grow" — and it gives them something to hold onto that isn't only sad.

**"The Rough Patch" by Brian Lies**

A Caldecott Honor book and the most emotionally sophisticated picture book on this list. Evan and his dog do everything together, especially tending their prize garden. When the dog dies, Evan destroys the whole garden in his grief. Then a pumpkin vine sneaks in uninvited, and slowly — very slowly — he lets it grow. It's a book about what happens when grief turns to anger and then, eventually, to something that resembles hope. The illustrations carry all of it. This is the book for a four-year-old who isn't talking about how they feel — it shows the feeling instead of naming it, which can unlock something.

**"I'll Always Love You" by Hans Wilhelm**

Still worth reading at four, maybe even more so. The boy's regret — that he always said "I'll always love you" but his siblings never did — lands differently at this age. Four-year-olds understand the feeling of wishing you'd said something. This book is a gentle reminder, without being preachy, that the love we say out loud matters. Short. Real. Stays with you.

**"Henry and Bea" by Jessixa Bagley**

Slightly underrated, this one. Henry goes quiet after his cat dies and Bea — his best friend — doesn't push him, doesn't try to fix it, just shows up and sits with him. It's a book about being a good friend to someone who's grieving. For a four-year-old who may have watched a sibling or a friend lose a pet, this is perfect. The illustrations are soft and quiet in exactly the right way.

**"The Invisible Leash" by Patrice Karst, illustrated by Joanne Lew-Vriethoff**

The concept — an invisible leash connecting your heart to your pet's forever — is exactly the kind of thing a four-year-old can hold in their mind and return to. Some kids start "using" the leash on their own after reading it. If your child is concrete and literal, it might frustrate them (where is it, show me). But for imaginative four-year-olds, it's a real comfort in the form of an idea.

## Actually reading it without tears becoming a disaster

Some of these books will make you cry. Especially "The Rough Patch" and "I'll Always Love You." Let that happen. Four-year-olds whose parents cry at sad books learn something important: that sadness is okay, that books matter, and that grown-ups feel things too.

If they ask "why are you crying?" you can say "because this part is sad and I loved our cat too." That's a complete answer.

You don't need to debrief after every reading. Sometimes you just close the book and sit together for a minute. That's enough.

## A bonus section: when they ask about you

Four-year-olds will sometimes use the pet's death as an entry point to ask: "are YOU going to die?" or "am I going to die?" This is developmentally normal. They're not being morbid — they're being logical.

You don't have to give a theology lecture here. "Yes, someday, but not for a very, very long time" is honest and not scary. And then: "right now I'm here, and I'm not going anywhere." That's what they actually need to hear.

## When a book isn't enough

If your four-year-old is really struggling — not sleeping, not eating, very withdrawn for more than a week or two — name it directly: "I think you're still really sad about our dog. That makes sense. I'm sad too." You don't need a professional to say that. But if you're worried, trust your gut and check in with their pediatrician.

## Make a book made for your child

These books are excellent but they're about fictional pets. Your child lost a specific animal with a specific name, specific habits, a specific face. A book that's actually about *them* and *their pet* hits differently. At Plubis, you can make that book in about five minutes — personalized to your child's name, your pet's name, and the specific feelings you're navigating together. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about being more independent
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through independence and big-kid skills. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
At four, the independence stops being just a reflex and starts becoming an identity.

They don't just want to do things themselves — they want to *be* the kind of person who does things themselves. They'll tell other kids what they can do. They'll announce their abilities unprompted. They'll decline help with the exact tone of voice they heard you use on the phone when you're trying to sound competent.

It's equal parts pride and chaos. You're proud of them for becoming a person. You're also standing in the kitchen watching them try to operate the can opener and calculating the nearest urgent care.

Four is also the year when social comparison kicks in. They start noticing what other kids can do. They start wanting to keep up, or pull ahead, or understand why Mateo at preschool can already tie his shoes. The independence thing gets tangled up with the self-esteem thing and the friendship thing all at once.

Good books for four-year-olds hold all of that.


## *Ladybug Girl* by Jacky Davis, illustrated by David Soman

Still the gold standard for this age. When Lulu puts on her ladybug costume and goes outside to play alone because her brothers excluded her, she doesn't mope. She builds. She climbs. She explores. She invents a whole game out of the materials available.

Four-year-olds understand this now in a way they didn't at three. They've felt exclusion. They've had days where the group didn't include them and they had to figure out what to do with that. Lulu's solution — make your own adventure — is not naively optimistic. It's practical. It's what actually works.

The book has been a New York Times bestseller across the whole series for good reason. It treats children as competent without pretending competence is effortless. Lulu has to work a little at her afternoon. It's not automatically fun. She makes it fun.

Read it when your four-year-old has had a hard social day. It won't fix the feelings, but it'll give them something to do with them.


## *Leo the Late Bloomer* by Robert Kraus, illustrated by Jose Aruego

At four, this book hits differently than it did at three. At three, kids experience it. At four, they start to *relate* to it, because four is when the gap between what they want to be able to do and what they can actually do starts to sting a little.

Leo watches the other animals do things he can't. He tries. Nothing. He tries again. Nothing. His dad hovers anxiously. His mother just waits. And then, one day, Leo blooms — and the book makes it clear this was always going to happen, on Leo's schedule, not his father's.

For four-year-olds who are working hard at a skill and not quite getting there — reading, riding a bike, swimming without floaties, whatever it is — this book says: *your timeline is valid*. That's not a small thing. Kids this age already pick up on adult anxiety about whether they're "on track." Leo says that "on track" is a made-up concept.

Robert Kraus and Jose Aruego's collaboration produced one of the most quietly powerful books for this age group. It doesn't shout its message. It just shows it.


## *Amazing Grace* by Mary Hoffman, illustrated by Caroline Binch

Grace loves stories. She acts them all out — Joan of Arc, Anansi the Spider, Mowgli, every hero from every tale she knows. When her class decides to do Peter Pan and she wants to play the lead, she's told she can't because Peter Pan is neither Black nor a girl.

Grace's grandmother takes her to see a ballet performed by a Black ballerina. Grace goes to the audition. She gets the part.

This is one of those picture books that's technically about race and gender and also fundamentally about the right to decide what you're capable of. Four-year-olds are starting to hear limiting messages from the world — not always from parents, but from other kids, from media, from the casual assumptions that float around playgroups and classrooms. Amazing Grace is a direct refutation of those messages.

Mary Hoffman published this in 1991 and it became a New York Times bestseller and a Reading Rainbow selection. Over a million copies sold. It holds up completely, because the problem it's addressing hasn't gone away.

For any four-year-old who has been told — even implicitly — that a particular role isn't for them: read this one.


## *The Dot* by Peter H. Reynolds

Vashti stares at a blank page in art class. She says she can't draw. Her teacher says: make a mark and see where it takes you. Vashti makes a dot. The dot turns into something. The something becomes art. And then Vashti teaches another kid the same thing.

The Dot is about starting. Not about being good, not about talent, not about finishing — just about the act of beginning. For four-year-olds who have started to say "I can't" before they've tried (and oh, they have, at four this starts), this book gently picks apart the logic of that.

Peter Reynolds published this in 2003 and it's been a classroom staple ever since. The illustrations are loose and free and intentionally a little unpolished in places. The art style *demonstrates* the thesis. That's smart book-making.

Something worth saying: this book works best when you've got a kid who is starting to fear failure. Not the kid who throws themselves at everything — that kid doesn't need this yet. But the kid who hesitates, who says "it's too hard" before starting, who crumples the paper rather than risk a bad drawing? Give them this. Let them sit with it.


## *Froggy Gets Dressed* by Jonathan London, illustrated by Frank Remkiewicz

Four-year-olds who loved this at two or three will love it for completely different reasons now. At four, they're in on the joke in a new way. They know how to get dressed. They know Froggy should know how to get dressed. The comedy of Froggy failing to get dressed is now sophisticated comedy — they're laughing *at* Froggy, not with him, and there's something delicious about that.

Also: they can read the FLOP FLOP FLOP parts aloud. They can shout "FRRROOGGYY!" They're part of the performance.

This is the underrated joy of rereading books across ages. The book hasn't changed. The child has. Froggy Gets Dressed at four is genuinely different from Froggy Gets Dressed at two, even though it's the same pages.

The New York Public Library's 100 Picture Books Everyone Should Know list includes this for a reason. It's built for rereading.


## Why four is both the best and most exhausting age for this

Here's something nobody warns you about: four-year-old independence is louder than two-year-old independence. At two, they yell. At four, they *explain*. They have reasons. They have counter-arguments. They have questions about why the rule exists and whether it's fair and what would happen if they just did it anyway.

This is cognitively impressive and completely exhausting.

But it's also the foundation of something important. The four-year-old who argues with every boundary is practicing the skill of advocating for themselves. The one who insists on trying the hard thing is building a tolerance for effort. The one who wants to know *why* is developing critical thinking.

None of this helps when it's 7:30pm and they won't let you put their shoes on. I know. But it's still true.

Books give four-year-olds a place to see independence play out without stakes. They can watch Grace audition and Leo bloom and Vashti make her dot from the safe distance of your lap. They can process what it looks like to try, to be uncertain, to push through.

And sometimes, the next morning, they do the hard thing because they saw it on the page.


## Make it personal

Four is a great age for personalised books, because four-year-olds are old enough to follow a real story but young enough to still believe wholeheartedly in the magic of seeing their own name in print. A book about a character who looks like them, has their name, and is working on the exact skill they're working on right now?

They'll ask for it every night for two weeks. Ask me how I know.

**The first one is free.**

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about a friend moving away
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a friend moving away. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is the age when it actually hits.

At two or three, a friend moving away is mostly confusing. At four, your kid knows. They know their friend is gone and not coming back to preschool on Monday, and that the house on the corner has different kids in it now, and that none of this is okay. They might not have the vocabulary for it yet. But they have the feeling, and it's a big one.

Four-year-olds are also starting to understand fairness. And this isn't fair. Why did they have to move? Why can't we go visit? Why do people have to go away? These are genuinely hard philosophical questions and your four-year-old is going to ask all of them, possibly in the same breath as asking for a snack.

The good news: four is also the golden age of books. They can follow longer stories. They care about characters. They cry at sad parts (which is healthy). They remember stories for months. A good book read at the right moment can land so deep it becomes a reference point they'll carry for years.

So let's find the right books.

## What works at age 4

Four-year-olds want the story to be honest. They can tell when you're sugarcoating. A book that rushes to "but then she made a new friend and everything was fine" will get a four-year-old's side-eye, because they know that's not quite how it works.

They want: a character who really misses their friend. Real sadness on the page. And then — not a fix, but a path forward. Something the character *does* with the feeling.

They can also handle a little complexity now. A book where the character is both sad AND happy in the same moment. A story where two feelings exist at once. That's developmentally real for a four-year-old and it's worth looking for books that honor that.

Length: they can sit with longer texts now, but still — pictures carry the emotional weight. If the illustration is flat, the book won't land no matter how good the words are.

## Book picks

**"Evelyn Del Rey Is Moving Away" by Meg Medina, illustrated by Sonia Sánchez** — This is the one. If you're only going to read one book on this list, read this one. Daniela and Evelyn are best friends who live across the street from each other, mirroring each other's lives almost perfectly. On Evelyn's last day, they play together among the moving boxes in the empty apartment. They're sad. They're also just playing, because that's what kids do — hold sadness and ordinary life at the same time. Meg Medina won the Newbery Medal for a reason. The writing is spare and exact. Sonia Sánchez's illustrations are warm and specific and full of light. This book doesn't lie. It doesn't rush. It ends with both girls crying and also both girls knowing they'll be okay. Absolutely perfect for four.

**"Paper Planes" by Jim Helmore, illustrated by Richard Jones** — Mia and Ben are neighbors and best friends who love making and flying paper planes. When Ben has to move, they exchange planes and tearful goodbyes. But then the planes keep flying back and forth through the mail, carrying little notes. It's a book about staying connected through something physical and handmade, which four-year-olds find deeply satisfying. The illustrations by Richard Jones have this gorgeous atmospheric quality — big skies, golden light, the specific loneliness of looking out a window. After reading this, your kid will almost certainly want to make paper planes. Do it. Make them. Fly them.

**"In a Jar" by Deborah Marcero** — Llewellyn collects beautiful things in a jar — the first firefly of summer, a bit of beach, the color of the sky at sunset — and shares them with his best friend. When the friend moves, the jar becomes a way to send love across distance. At four, kids are ready to appreciate the strangeness and loveliness of this central idea. The book is more poetic than plot-driven, which either works for your kid or doesn't. If they like detailed illustrations and imaginative play, they'll love this. If they need more story, pair it with something else on the list.

**"Goodbye, Friend! Hello, Friend!" by Cori Doerrfeld** — Stays on the list at four because the emotional scaffolding is so good. Practicing small goodbyes before the big one is genuinely how children build emotional resilience. Four-year-olds can engage more deeply with the themes here — you can pause and really talk about what the characters are feeling, and they'll tell you real things back.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At four, the metaphor clicks in a new way. Four-year-olds can hold an abstract concept if it's tied to something physical, and "a string you can feel with your heart" is exactly that. Great for reading right after a friend has moved, especially if your child is struggling with the out-of-sight, out-of-existence confusion that comes at this age. After you read it, you can ask: "Can you feel the string right now?" and a four-year-old will often reach out their hand. That moment is worth more than I can say.

**"My Best Friend Moved Away" by Nancy Carlson** — Shorter and more direct than some others on this list. A child is best friends with the kid next door. That kid moves. It's sad. There are new kids now, and maybe — eventually — a new friend. Carlson doesn't rush the sadness, but she does let the ending be hopeful without being dishonest about it. The illustrations are bright and characterful. Good for a four-year-old who wants the story to be very literal and close to their actual experience.

**"Oh No! Time to Go!" by Rebecca Doughty** — The rhyme and humor make this one stand out. At four, a child who's been very sad about a friend leaving sometimes needs a book that's a little lighter — that treats goodbye as a universal human experience rather than a catastrophe. This book does that without minimizing real grief. It rhymes its way through goodbyes big and small and lands on something genuinely warm. Good for rereading, good for kids who don't want to sit still through something heavy.

**"A Chair for My Mother" by Vera B. Williams** — A slightly different angle, but worth including. A little girl and her family save coins to buy a big comfortable chair after losing everything in a fire. It's about community, about love persisting through loss, about neighbors and the ways we hold each other up. It doesn't map directly to a friend moving away, but the emotional truth of it — love after loss, the persistence of warmth — is exactly what a four-year-old processing any kind of loss benefits from hearing. It's also beautifully illustrated. Try your library.

## Reading together at four

Four is when you can actually have the conversation the book opens up.

Read it once through without stopping. Then go back to the part that hit them. You'll know which part — they went quiet, or they got fidgety in that specific way that means something landed.

"What do you think she was feeling right there?"

Let them answer. Then — and this is important — share your own experience. "I remember when my friend moved away when I was little. I felt really sad and also kind of angry." Four-year-olds are hungry for evidence that adults feel things too. It normalizes the emotion and it builds trust.

Don't be afraid of silence after a sad page. Sit in it. You don't have to narrate or explain. Just be there.

## When the grief shows up sideways

At four, grief doesn't always look like crying. It looks like:

— Aggression at preschool ("I don't want to be anyone's friend")
— Sudden regression (bedwetting, thumb-sucking, wanting to be carried)
— Obsessive asking ("Is Leo really really really far away?")
— Refusing to talk about it at all

All of these are normal. All of these are the four-year-old's nervous system doing its work.

Books help most when you read them calmly, consistently, without pressure. Not as a therapy session. Just as a bedtime story that happens to be about this exact thing your kid is going through.

If they want to talk after the book, great. If they just want to turn the light off, also great.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's the thing about picture books at age four: they're starting to understand that stories are written by someone, for someone. They'll sometimes say "this book is about me" when a character has their hair color or likes their favorite food.

Imagine a book that's actually, literally about them. Their name. Their friend's name. The exact park. The exact goodbye. Written in their voice, for their heart.

That's what Plubis makes. A custom picture book about your child's real friendship, built in five minutes. Printed and shipped, or readable right now.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about making new friends
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through making friends. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is the age when friendship becomes a whole entire thing. Not just proximity, not just "she was there and I was also there." Four-year-olds have best friends. They have enemies. They make elaborate alliances and destroy them inside the same afternoon. The social world at four is somehow already almost as complicated as it's going to get, just in miniature, played out with toy dinosaurs on a classroom rug.

And watching it is a lot.

You watch your kid approach a group and try to get in. You watch the little recalibrations when the group keeps playing without acknowledging them — the slow drift away, the "I didn't want to play that anyway" recovery that is so clearly not how they actually feel. You watch the cruelty that children this age don't even know is cruelty: "we don't want to play with you because we're playing fairies and you can't be a fairy." Said with complete matter-of-factness. No malice. Just social sorting, four-year-old style.

It's a lot for them. And because you love them, it's a lot for you.

Books can't fix any of this. But they can sit with it. They can name it. They can show your kid a character who went through something similar and came out the other side still intact. That's not nothing. That's actually kind of everything.

## What's happening socially at four that you need to know

Four is when exclusion becomes intentional — or at least starts to feel intentional. Kids are beginning to understand in-groups and out-groups. They're forming close pairs ("she's MY best friend, not yours") that feel like everything, and then collapsing those pairs, and then reforming them. It's exhausting to witness and also developmentally totally expected.

They're also beginning to understand that other people have feelings that are different from their own, and that their actions cause those feelings. This is huge. It's the foundation of empathy. But it's still rickety at four — they understand it in theory more than they live it in the moment, especially when they really want the toy.

Four-year-olds can also start to internalize social anxiety in ways they couldn't before. The kid who was bold and oblivious at two and three now might hover at the edge of a group, waiting, uncertain. Watching to see if it's safe. That is not a regression — that is the beginning of real social intelligence. But it can look like shyness or withdrawal and you might feel worried about it. The books that speak to that specific experience — the hovering, the uncertainty, the "do I belong here" question — are worth knowing.

## The books worth reading this year

**"Strictly No Elephants" by Lisa Mantchev** — A boy has a pet elephant. The local pet club says: no elephants. The theme of being excluded for something you can't change, finding the kid who's also excluded for something different, starting something new together — it's pitch perfect for four-year-olds. The illustration of the boy's face when he reads the "no elephants" sign is one of the most accurately drawn moments of social exclusion I've seen in a picture book. The ending is hopeful without being dishonest. This is in my top three on this whole list.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — A boy has an enemy. His dad has a recipe. The recipe requires spending a whole day with the enemy. The enemy turns out to be — you already know where this is going, but the path there is genuinely funny and the emotional insight is real. For four-year-olds who have already decided there's someone they don't like (or who are afraid of becoming someone else's enemy), this is the exact book. Also great for after a conflict — "remember Enemy Pie" as shorthand is useful for years after the first reading.

**"Scaredy Squirrel Makes a Friend" by Melanie Watt** — Scaredy Squirrel has decided the safest thing is to have an imaginary friend because imaginary friends don't have germs or bad intentions. His plan for making a real friend is extremely elaborate and involves a lot of staying in his tree. The plan fails completely. A dog shows up. The friendship happens in spite of the plan, not because of it. For the four-year-old who overthinks social situations — who stands at the edge of a group calculating risks instead of just going in — this book sees them and makes them laugh at themselves gently. That's a gift.

**"Chester's Way" by Kevin Henkes** — Chester and Wilson have been doing everything the same way their whole lives. They have a system. Lilly moves in next door and has absolutely no interest in their system. She does everything her own way, loudly. Chester and Wilson think this is wrong. They keep deciding she's too different. And then she saves them from some kids who are being terrible, and suddenly the different thing becomes the point. For the four-year-old who's drawn to kids who are "like them" and nervous about kids who feel strange or unpredictable, this one is the one.

**"Frog and Toad Are Friends" by Arnold Lobel** — At four they can really start to sit with Frog and Toad as a read-aloud chapter book. The friendship between Frog (enthusiastic, warm, slightly oblivious) and Toad (cautious, grumpy, secretly devoted) is one of the best friendships in all of children's literature. The story where Toad is embarrassed about his swimsuit and Frog just sits next to him anyway — that's friendship. That's the whole thing. Real friendship contains someone who sees you looking ridiculous and stays. Read this one slowly, one chapter at a time, over several nights. Savor it.

**"A Weekend with Wendell" by Kevin Henkes** — Wendell takes over Sophie's house for the weekend. He changes all the game rules. He takes all the good roles. He is a LOT. Sophie is quietly furious throughout. But something small shifts by the end. It's not a redemption arc exactly — it's more like: complicated people sometimes become friends if you hold out long enough and something happens that's bigger than the annoyance. Four-year-olds who have a Wendell in their life (there's always a Wendell) will recognize this immediately.

**"Let's Be Enemies" by Janice May Udry** — A short, funny, surprisingly old book (1961). John decides his best friend James is his enemy because James always bosses him around. He marches over to tell James this. James just asks if he wants some pretzels. They go play. The simplicity of it is the point — the way that four-year-old enmity and friendship can toggle in the same afternoon, sometimes mid-snack. Read it when there's been a fight. Read it after the reconciliation too.

## The conversation you'll have after

Four-year-olds will TALK about these books. Especially after Scaredy Squirrel, after Enemy Pie. They have opinions. "I would NOT want to spend a whole day with Jeremy." "Squirrel should just go down from the tree." "It's MEAN that the pet club said no elephants."

Let them argue with the books. Ask about their reasoning. "What would you have done instead?" You will learn more about what your kid is actually experiencing socially from this conversation than from asking "how was school?" directly. The question about a fictional character is somehow safer than the question about their own day. Use it.

And don't rush to deliver the moral. Four-year-olds hate being told what a story is about. They know what it's about. They were there.

## The moment at the birthday party

There's a specific birthday party situation that happens at four. Your kid knows some of the kids but not all of them. There's a corner of the room where two kids are clearly already best friends and they've made a little universe. Your kid stands near the universe and tries to enter it and can't quite get the angle right. And you're across the room with a paper plate of cake watching and your heart is just doing something.

You can't fix that moment. But you can go home and read them Enemy Pie and let them feel that the thing they're trying to figure out is a thing that characters in stories try to figure out too. That it's not a failing. It's just friendship being hard, which it always is, which is actually what makes it worth something when you find it.

## A bonus section: the friends they already have that they're taking for granted

Here's a thing worth noting: four-year-olds are often so focused on the friend they want and don't have that they're not really seeing the friend who's already there. Ask them about that kid. The one they always play with but never specifically mention as their best friend because it's just obvious. That kid matters. So does your kid to that kid.

Frog and Toad is good for this. It's a book about the friendship you already have and how much it contains.

## A book made just for your child

The books above are wonderful but they're about squirrels and frogs and boys with enemy problems. None of them are about your specific four-year-old, the one who stands at the edge of birthday party universes and tries to find the angle in. Plubis builds custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — your child's name, their situation, them as the main character. A four-year-old who sees their own name in a story about finding their person takes something from that book that Chester and Scaredy Squirrel can't quite reach. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about moving to a new home
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through moving house. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds are the move's loudest critics.

They have strong opinions and the vocabulary to express them at full volume. "I don't want to go." "I LOVE this house." "What about my friends?" "But WHERE will I sleep?" "But who will be my friend NOW?" Sometimes all in one afternoon, without stopping for breath.

And here's the thing — they're right to feel all of that. At four, friendships are becoming genuinely important. The kid next door who they've been playing with since they could walk is a real relationship. The sense of place — their bedroom, their backyard, the route to the park — is real and particular and theirs. Moving takes real things away. The books that work at this age are honest about that, not dismissive.

## What's different at four

Four-year-olds can sit with a story. They can follow a plot that unfolds over pages, track a character's emotional state across a whole arc, and remember what the character wanted at the beginning when the ending comes. That's a big cognitive leap from two or three.

Which means books can do a lot more work here. A book that says "this is hard AND you'll find your way" can actually land at four, not just wash over them. They'll process it. They'll bring lines from the book up days later, in contexts that surprise you.

They're also at the age where they start engaging with books as arguments. "That's not fair." "I wouldn't do that." "He should be mad." Let that happen. Those are real conversations. Disagreeing with a character is a sign of engagement, not disrespect.

Paperback picture books are fine now. Their hands are more careful most of the time. A book that gets well-loved — the spine showing the cracks of a hundred opens — is a good sign.

## The books I'd put in front of a four-year-old

**"Alexander, Who's Not (Do You Hear Me? I Mean It!) Going to Move" by Judith Viorst** — Best on this list. No contest. Alexander is furious about moving. He lists every single thing he'll miss — his friends, his house, his dentist who doesn't hurt him, the tree he can climb. He refuses to go. He is categorically not going. The whole book is his resistance, loud and elaborate and hilariously specific, and it's never once dismissed or minimized. He just goes. Because that's what happens. The anger doesn't get fixed; it just gets held by the story, acknowledged completely, and then life continues. For a four-year-old who is in full resistance mode, this book is the most seen I've ever seen a child feel from a picture book. Read it multiple times before the move. Read it once on the day of. Your kid may want to read it WITH you so that you're both acknowledging together that this is real and it's not fair. Let that be the point.

**"The Leaving Morning" by Angela Johnson, illustrated by David Soman** — Slower than Alexander. More sensory and quiet. Two siblings spend their last morning in their apartment — sticky kisses on the windows, the smell of the last breakfast, watching the movers carry out the furniture. This is a book about grief that's also strangely beautiful. It doesn't tell you to feel a certain way. It just shows the feeling with extraordinary specificity — the way an empty apartment sounds different than a full one, the way you remember the warmth of a place right before you leave it. For the four-year-old who tends toward the quiet, sad, absorbing end of big emotions, this is the right book. Not for the kid who needs to yell — give them Alexander for that. This one is for the kid who goes still and watches everything.

**"Goodbye House" by Frank Asch** — I keep recommending this across every age because it keeps being right. The ritual of saying goodbye to every room — explicitly, out loud, as a family — is genuinely helpful for kids who need closure. At four they can lead the ritual themselves. Inspired by this book, walk your four-year-old through your actual house on moving day and say goodbye to the kitchen, the backyard, their room. It sounds odd. It works. It gives the leaving a shape, which is what children need more than explanations.

**"Ira Says Goodbye" by Bernard Waber** — Ira's best friend Reggie is moving away. This is moving from the perspective of the one left behind, not the one going — which is a valid and important read for a four-year-old who has had a friend move, or who worries about what they're leaving for their own friends. The friendship between Ira and Reggie is specific and warm and believable. The sadness is real. The ending doesn't promise the friendship stays exactly the same — it promises something, which is more honest. If your child is both moving AND has a best friend who's staying behind, this book handles that specific grief directly.

**"Mitchell Is Moving" by Marjorie Weinman Sharmat** — Mitchell the dinosaur decides to move away even though his best friend Margo wants him to stay. He goes. He misses her. She misses him. There's something very honest about a book that says both things can be true — moving can be the right choice AND it's still a real loss. Not a polished emotional resolution. More of a "yeah, this is bittersweet and you're allowed to feel both." Worth checking your library for — it's older so it's not on every shelf but it's genuinely good.

**"My Very Own Room" by Amada Irma Perez** — A girl in a big, crowded family wants a room of her own. Through a move and some creative family problem-solving, she finally gets one. This is the book for the four-year-old who's moving somewhere with something to look forward to — a new room, a backyard, a space that's theirs. Not every move is pure loss. Some things actually get better. This book celebrates that without being falsely cheerful — the desire for the room is real and earned, and getting it is genuinely satisfying. If your child has something specific to look forward to in the new place, a book that names that hope honestly is worth having.

**"A Chair for My Mother" by Vera B. Williams** — After a fire, a family starts over in a new apartment. They save their coins in a jar to buy the perfect chair. This is the book about resilience that isn't preachy about it — the girl just describes what happened and what they did and how the neighbors helped. At four your child can follow the whole emotional arc. What stays with most kids isn't the fire or the hardship but the jar of coins. The concrete goal, the family working toward it together. If your move is hard-circumstances-driven — financial stress, divorce, downsizing — this is the book that says "you can build something real from a hard beginning."

## How to read these without it becoming A Big Production

Casual beats ceremonial every time. Don't sit your four-year-old down and say "this is a special book about our move and we need to talk about feelings." They'll shut down immediately. Slip it into the regular bedtime rotation. "Oh I got this one from the library, want to read it tonight?"

When they argue with a character — and they will — take their side for a second. "You're right, Alexander has a really good point. He does have the best dentist." Let them feel smart and right before you let the story make its point.

If they want to act it out, act it out. Four-year-olds who play "moving" after reading about it are processing. Give them the cardboard boxes. Let them pack their stuffed animals. It's not regression, it's rehearsal.

## The friends question

At four, the friends question is going to come up. "But what about [best friend's name]?"

Honest answer only: you'll visit, you might stay in touch, AND it's also true that it will be different. Don't promise it'll be the same. Don't promise you'll visit every month. Just hold the grief honestly and tell them it makes sense to miss people who matter.

The books that name this specific loss — especially Ira Says Goodbye and Mitchell Is Moving — are worth reading together when that conversation surfaces. Having the vocabulary from the book sometimes makes it easier to have the real conversation.

## When a book isn't enough

Some four-year-olds express the stress of a move in ways that surprise you — new fears, sleep problems, a sudden return to babyish behavior, or a real and sustained sadness that doesn't lift. None of that is a failure. All of it is a signal worth listening to. Talk to your pediatrician or an early childhood counselor if you're concerned. Books are conversation starters — they give you a shared vocabulary. But they're not a substitute for actual support when support is needed.

## Make a book made just for your child

At four, your child is old enough to know the book is about them. And that matters. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books — your child's name, their move, their specific situation. Not Alexander's house. Not Ira's street. Theirs. You describe it, we build it, in about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about learning a new language
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a new language. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is the age of questions. Not just "why" (though oh, yes, there's plenty of why) but real questions — curious, specific, sometimes uncomfortably direct. "Why does abuela talk different?" "Why does my name sound different at school?" "Is Spanish a baby language?" That last one, if you've heard it, probably stopped you cold.

Four-year-olds are starting to map the social world. They notice who fits where. They notice which words get laughed at and which ones get approved of. And if they're growing up between languages — speaking one thing at home, another at school, maybe a third with grandparents — they're doing something cognitively incredible while simultaneously being extremely vulnerable to the message that one of their languages is less than.

Books can't do everything. But a book that shows a kid who has two languages and is *fine* — actually more than fine, is interesting, is themselves — puts something in the room that stays there. And at four, they're old enough to start talking about what they read. That's the real opportunity. Not the books themselves but the conversations they unlock.

## What four-year-olds are ready for

At four: longer plots, more complex feelings, real moral questions. They can hold a character's experience for a whole book. They can remember what happened in the beginning when the end arrives. They'll tell you whether they think a character made the right choice.

For the language theme, this means books that don't shy away from the harder stuff — feeling caught between worlds, not understanding something, feeling ashamed and then reconsidering it. The resolution still needs to be meaningful, but it doesn't have to arrive in three pages. Four-year-olds can sit with some tension.

Also at four: they might be starting to have opinions about which language is "theirs." This can go either way — fierce pride or rejection of one language — and the books you put in front of them during this period do matter. Intentionally so.

## The picks

**"The Name Jar" by Yangsook Choi** — Still the best book for this specific situation at four. Unhei has just immigrated from Korea and is struggling with the decision of whether to adopt a more American-sounding name. The class nominates names for a jar she can choose from. She doesn't. She keeps her name. The emotional journey here — the desire to belong, the cost of erasing yourself to fit in, the moment of choosing yourself — is something a four-year-old can fully follow. Read it and then ask: "Why do you think Unhei kept her Korean name?" The answers you'll get will tell you something about where your kid is.

**"Dreamers" by Yuyi Morales** — Morales arrived in the US from Mexico with her infant son, speaking no English. This is that story, illustrated in the most gorgeous way possible. At four, they can track the emotional arc — the disorientation, the library as a place of safety, the power of stories in any language. The art is overwhelming in the best way — crowded with beautiful detail, a book you can return to a dozen times and still find things you missed. A book about the dignity of the immigrant experience, told without making that the *point* of every page.

**"Thunder Boy Jr." by Sherman Alexie, illustrated by Yuyi Morales** — Thunder Boy Jr. has his dad's name — Big Thunder — but he doesn't want to be a "junior." He wants his own name. The story is set in a Native American family, and the names he imagines for himself are rooted in his own life and culture ("Falls Down All the Time," "Almost Drowns in the River"). For a four-year-old navigating a name or language that feels like it doesn't quite fit the world they're in, this is a book about the power of naming yourself. The humor is genuinely funny — Alexie's voice, even in picture book form, is distinctive.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson, illustrated by E.B. Lewis** — This is not strictly a language book, but it's about a new girl (Maya) who seems different — she dresses differently, the other kids aren't sure about her — and the narrator, Chloe, repeatedly passes up the chance to include her. Then Maya moves away. The watercolor illustrations are aching. The message is about what you can't take back. For a four-year-old who is on either side of the outsider experience — the new kid or the one who didn't know how to include the new kid — this is the book. It doesn't flinch. Read it and hold the silence afterward.

**"Islandborn" by Junot Díaz, illustrated by Leo Espinosa** — Lola was too young to remember the island her family came from before they immigrated. Her teacher asks all the kids to draw their home countries. Lola can't remember hers, so she goes around her neighborhood asking her neighbors — who all came from the same island — to describe it. The result is a patchwork of memories: the beaches, the food, the flowers, *and* the monster (the Trujillo dictatorship, handled carefully for children). At four, your kid will be drawn into the collective memory-building. The illustrations by Leo Espinosa are vivid and Caribbean and joyful. The story takes heritage seriously without turning it into a museum exhibit.

**"Sona Sharma, Very Best Big Sister?" by Chitra Soundar, illustrated by Jen Khatun** — Not primarily a language book — it's about Sona waiting for a new baby sibling — but Sona's life is warmly, specifically South Asian: the chai, the cousins, the words from Tamil woven in naturally, the extended family. For kids from South Asian families, the representation here is genuinely specific rather than generic-multicultural. The language bits aren't the point; the full and particular life is the point. Which is what good representation does.

**"Round Is a Mooncake: A Book of Shapes" by Roseanne Thong, illustrated by Grace Lin** — A little girl finds shapes all around her world — and that world is Chinese-American: mooncakes, woks, dim sum, the shape of a compass. It's a concept book (shapes) that's also a culture book without being a lesson. At four, the concept level is exactly right, and the cultural specificity is refreshing — Chinese New Year and egg rolls and grandmother's apartment exist in the same visual world as the everyday. A gentle, warm book.

## Talking about it afterward

Four-year-olds are old enough for real conversation. After reading, try a few of these:

"Does [character] remind you of anyone you know?"

"What would you do if you had two names and had to pick one?"

"Is there a word you know that [the child's friend] doesn't know?"

That last question is a good one. It reframes the bilingual experience as *having something others don't* rather than *being different in a way that needs explanation*. Small shift. Matters.

## The code-switching conversation

Around four, you might be able to have a real (if simple) conversation about code-switching — the fact that people use different languages in different places with different people, and that's completely normal. "We speak Tagalog with Lola because that's the language of her heart. We speak English at school because that's what the other kids know. You know both, so you're actually doing more." That last line is important. You're doing *more*, not something weird.

## On one parent, one language

If you're doing the one-parent-one-language approach — one of you speaks only the heritage language with the child, the other only English (or whatever) — four is the age where this can get complicated. Kids at four start to test the rules. They know which parent is which and will deliberately speak the "wrong" language to see what happens. Don't panic. Don't turn it into a fight. Stay consistent with your language without making it a punishment situation. The data suggests consistent exposure matters more than strict enforcement. And consistent, joyful, warm exposure matters most of all.

## When a book isn't enough

If your four-year-old is actively rejecting one of their languages — refusing to speak it with grandparents, getting upset when you use it in public — this is worth addressing but not with force. Forcing a child to speak a language they're feeling shame about usually backfires. The goal is rebuilding the positive association with that language. More stories in that language. More music. More food. More time with people who make that language feel like belonging. Books are one piece of that. Community is a bigger piece.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what none of these books can give you: a story about *your* child, in *your* family's language situation, with their actual name and their actual world. Plubis generates custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — you describe the situation, we write and illustrate a book that puts your child at the center of the story. "Arjun knows that *namaste* means hello and *I love you* and *come eat your dinner* all at once" is a sentence that belongs in a book about your specific kid. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about getting a new pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a new pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds asking for a pet is a special kind of torture, honestly. They know exactly what they want. They have been very specific. They have informed you, repeatedly, in detail, that they want a dog (or a cat, or "a dinosaur, but a real one"), they have explained their whole plan for taking care of it, and the plan was surprisingly coherent — which made you nervous because you thought you'd be able to poke holes in it and end the conversation, but no, your four-year-old has thought this through more carefully than you have.

So you got the pet. Or you're about to. Or you inherited one from someone because life is what it is.

Here is what you're working with at four: a kid who has massive emotional range, a developing sense of responsibility, a passionate belief that the pet is THEIRS and THEIR JOB, and approximately zero follow-through on anything that isn't immediately rewarding. The feeding enthusiasm will last about four days. The "I want to walk the dog" phase will peak in week one and then quietly disappear like a misplaced sock.

This is normal. Completely normal. Four is not the age of sustained responsibility. It's the age of intense, beautiful bursts of care and ownership, followed by total amnesia. You'll be taking care of this pet yourself — you knew that, admit it, you always knew that — but the emotional connection your four-year-old is building with this animal is real and worth supporting.

Books are one of the tools. Here's how to use them.

## What actually works with four-year-olds and new pet books

At four, a kid's reading life has opened up considerably. They can handle actual narrative now — a beginning, middle, end with real stakes. They understand that characters have goals and run into problems. They feel suspense. They feel relief. They cry at sad parts in a way that is genuinely surprising if you weren't expecting it.

The best pet books for four-year-olds have:

**Actual story tension.** Will the pet be okay? Will the kid and the animal figure each other out? Is there a real problem to solve? Four-year-olds lean in when something is at stake.

**A child character doing something recognizable.** Reading about a kid their age navigating a new pet — the excitement, the mistakes, the figuring-it-out — is far more engaging than a story where the pet is just there being cute.

**Some humor.** The new pet situation is inherently funny. It's chaos with fur (or scales, or whatever you've brought home). Books that acknowledge this — that animals are ridiculous, that kids make mistakes, that everything is kind of a mess — land really well at four.

**Real feelings, not sanitized ones.** Four-year-olds are working through jealousy (why does the dog get more attention), disappointment (the rabbit doesn't want to be held), fear (the dog is bigger than expected), and guilt (I accidentally hurt it and now I feel terrible). The best books don't skip these.

## Book picks for 4-year-olds about getting a new pet

**"A Shelter Dog Named Bruno" by Cynthia Rylant** — Rylant's Cobble Street Cousins series has a quieter charm, but for pet books, her animal stories are the gold standard. This one is honest about what shelter dogs carry with them and what a family gives in return. It doesn't talk down to kids. It assumes they can handle emotional complexity. They can.

**"Because of Winn-Dixie" by Kate DiCamillo** — Too long for a single sitting at four, but you can read it chapter by chapter over a week. DiCamillo writes about what animals do for lonely people — how they become anchors, bridges, reasons to leave the house. Opal and Winn-Dixie are one of the great child-pet pairs in all of literature, and reading it alongside a newly acquired dog is something your four-year-old will not forget.

**"The One and Only Bob" by Katherine Applegate** — Sequel to The One and Only Ivan. Bob is a dog who survived on his own before landing somewhere good, and his voice is funny and tough and secretly tender. Four-year-olds who've been told their new pet "came from somewhere before us" will find something in Bob's story that helps them understand that.

**"Poppleton" by Cynthia Rylant** — Poppleton the pig lives his life with great seriousness and specific preferences. He has friends. He has routines. He is, in other words, not that different from your new cat, who also has opinions about when dinner should be served and where you're allowed to sit. Reading Poppleton helps kids understand that animals have inner lives. Also it's very, very funny.

**"Chester's Way" by Kevin Henkes** — Chester and Wilson have a very specific way of doing everything. Then Lilly moves in. The friendship adjustment — the way someone new disrupts established patterns and eventually makes everything richer — maps perfectly onto what happens when a new pet joins a household that had its own routines. Henkes sneaks big ideas into small books like a professional.

**"Fly Away Home" by Eve Bunting** — This one is harder. A homeless father and son live in an airport, and their small moments of hope are anchored by a bird trapped in the terminal. It's not specifically about getting a pet. But it is about what it means to care for something wild, to want it to be free, to understand that animals carry their own needs. If your four-year-old is ready for it (you'll know), it's stunning.

**"Rotten Ralph" by Jack Gantos** — Ralph is a terrible cat. Genuinely terrible. He ruins things, embarrasses his owner, causes chaos, and is still, somehow, loved completely. This book is hilarious and also very useful if your new pet has already destroyed something important (which, by the time you're reading this, it probably has).

**"How to Steal a Dog" by Barbara O'Connor** — A girl steals a dog in order to earn reward money. The moral complexity here is real. Four-year-olds who are deep in the "I want this pet so badly I'd do anything" stage will find the desperation immediately recognizable, even if they wouldn't actually steal anything. Probably.

## Reading together: the four-year-old edition

Four is a great age for reading because your kid is starting to anticipate. They remember what happened last time. They make predictions. "I think the dog is going to come back!" they'll announce, before you've gotten to that part. Let them be right. Let them be wrong. Ask them "what do you think will happen?" before you turn the page — not as a quiz, just as a shared game.

Four-year-olds are also starting to connect stories to their own lives in explicit, sometimes startling ways. They'll say things like "When we got our cat, I was scared too, like Chester." This is enormous. This is the whole point. Just nod and take it in. You don't have to turn it into a lesson. The connection itself is the work.

The best time to read about the new pet isn't the first chaotic day. Try after the initial excitement has settled but before the novelty has completely worn off — usually around day three through week two. When they're comfortable enough to think about the experience, but the experience is still fresh enough to be interesting.

Also: let them pick the book sometimes. Even if they pick the same one four days in a row. Repetition with a four-year-old is how meaning gets made.

## When the pet and your kid need a reset

Something goes wrong, usually in week two. Your four-year-old got too rough and the cat scratched them. Or they dropped the rabbit. Or the dog bit (not hard, but hard enough). Or they forgot to feed it and felt awful when you pointed it out, and now they're drowning in four-year-old guilt, which is enormous and disproportionate and real.

Books can help with this moment specifically. Not by lecturing. By normalizing — look, Rotten Ralph's owner still loves him even though he's terrible, and look, Chester's friend Lilly makes mistakes too, and everything still turns out okay.

You can also just say: "The cat scratched you because it was scared. It doesn't mean it doesn't like you. It just means you both need to learn each other's signals." That's more useful than any book. But the book is where you practiced saying it.

Some conversations worth having:

"Do you think our pet misses its mom?" (Yes, this might make them cry. That's okay.)

"What do you think our dog is thinking right now?"

"What's your favorite thing you've noticed about our pet this week?"

"What do you think we need to do differently to help them feel safe?"

That last one, said without blame, is surprisingly generative with four-year-olds. They have ideas. Sometimes genuinely good ones.

## Make a book made for your child

Your four-year-old has a specific story with this specific pet. The day you brought it home. What it was like. What they were afraid of. What they named it. The moment something clicked and it stopped being just an animal and started being THEIR animal.

That story exists. And a book built around that story — with your kid's name, your pet's name, the actual emotional arc of what happened — is going to mean something that no generic pet book can touch.

At four, kids are ready to see themselves as protagonists of real narratives. They want to be the hero of the story, and with a new pet, they kind of are.

Plubis makes that book in about five minutes. You give us your kid's details, your pet's name, the situation — and we build a custom illustrated story just for them. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about starting at a new school
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a new school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
My kid started a new preschool in March. Not September. Not the first day. March, when the class had been together since fall and had already sorted themselves into the pairs and trios that make up the social universe of four-year-olds.

The teacher was lovely. The school was fine. My kid stood at the edge of the playground for eight straight days before a girl named Priya asked her to play restaurant.

Eight days. I counted.

Mid-year is its own animal. First-day-of-school stuff, the books, the advice, all of it assumes a level playing field — everyone's nervous, everyone's new, someone will bond over shared confusion. That's not what this is. What this is: your four-year-old walking into a room where everyone already has a "person," and figuring out what to do with themselves in the meantime.

Four is old enough to feel that acutely. To come home and tell you, in very specific terms, that Maya and Jess played together all day and nobody asked them to play. That is a real sentence a four-year-old can say to you. You will feel it in your chest.

Books help. Not as answers. As company.

## What four-year-olds are ready to understand

Four is a big developmental leap from three. They have empathy now — actual perspective-taking, starting to wonder what other people feel. They understand time better (yesterday, tomorrow, "when I was little"). They can hold a narrative in their head and talk about it afterward.

This means you can read a book with a four-year-old about being the new kid and then have a real, if short, conversation about it. Not a therapy session. A five-minute conversation where they tell you something true about how they feel.

Four-year-olds also have strong opinions now. They will tell you if a book is boring. They will refuse the book you carefully selected because the cover doesn't interest them. This is fine. Let them pick which book first and then suggest the one you want to read. They'll often agree once they're already in reading mode.

The key things a four-year-old can take from a new-school book:
- That wanting a friend and not having one yet is a normal, known feeling that happens to characters in stories
- That things shift — not always fast, but they do
- That small moments (sitting next to someone, sharing something) are how friendships start
- That being the new kid doesn't mean something is wrong with you

That last one matters more than it sounds. Four-year-olds are starting to build self-concept. The experience of being left out at this age can start to become a story they tell themselves about themselves. Good books interrupt that story before it calcifies.

## The books

**"The Brand New Kid" by Katie Couric** — This is the most directly relevant book on this list. A boy named Lazlo starts mid-year. Not on the first day — mid-year. The other kids already have their routines and their friendships and nobody goes out of their way to include him. Until one girl does. It's simple, it's kind, and the specificity of it being mid-year rather than first-day makes it unusually useful for this exact situation. Read it before the school switch if you can.

**"Chrysanthemum" by Kevin Henkes** — Chrysanthemum loves her unusual name until she starts school and the other kids tease her about it. Then a beloved music teacher with an unusual name makes everything okay. This isn't specifically a new-school book but it's the most accurate picture book I know of what it feels like to be different in a classroom where everyone's already established their cool. The subtle mean-ness of the other kids is drawn without sugarcoating and the resolution is earned. Four-year-olds feel this one.

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Honestly, this might be the best book on this entire list for this specific situation. Woodson writes about children starting school who feel like they don't quite fit — different backgrounds, different experiences, different summers. The thread is: the moment you begin to share yourself, a door opens. It's lyrical and illustrated beautifully by Rafael López. The message for the new kid is this: your different-ness is not your weakness. Start somewhere.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — I mentioned this for three-year-olds with a caution. At four, I'd read it. It's the story of a new girl, Maya, who keeps offering friendship and keeps getting ignored — and then moves away, and the narrator has to live with what she didn't do. It does not have a happy ending. But it's a true, important ending, and four-year-olds can hold it. More importantly, if your kid is the one being ignored, hearing the perspective of the ignorer — that she felt bad about it, that she wished she'd been different — is quietly useful.

**"Spaghetti in a Hot Dog Bun" by Maria Dismondy** — Lucy is teased by Ralph about her different lunch and her different self. She responds with kindness. Ralph eventually comes around. The message is about holding onto who you are when a new place makes you feel like who you are is wrong. For a kid in a new school environment where the social culture is already established and they're an outsider, that message is specifically useful.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — A boy's best summer is ruined when someone new moves in and becomes his "enemy." His dad suggests Enemy Pie, but to make it work they have to spend a whole day together. By the end, the enemy has become a friend. This one works for both sides of the new-kid coin — if your four-year-old is the one arriving, and also if they're the one watching someone new arrive at their school and feeling weird about it. Read it and see which angle resonates.

**"Ish" by Peter H. Reynolds** — Not about school specifically. About making something and being afraid it's not good enough and then finding out that the trying is the whole point. I include it because for a four-year-old in a new school, there's a real fear of doing things wrong in front of kids who already know the rules. This book says: "ish" is enough. Trying is enough. You don't have to be perfect to belong.

## Reading together without forcing it

Four-year-olds can tell when you have an agenda. They're like tiny politicians — they know when someone wants something from them and they get cagey. So don't read these books *at* your kid. Read them *with* your kid.

Sit next to them, not across from them. Read in a voice that sounds like you're interested in the story too, not like you're delivering a message. If they want to comment mid-read, let them. If they lose interest, close the book. Come back tomorrow.

After you finish, the best question is often nothing. Just: "Huh. What do you think about that?" And then silence. Four-year-olds will often fill silences with the truest things they've been sitting on.

If they say something that surprises you — something real about feeling left out or scared — don't jump straight to solutions. Just say "yeah, that makes sense." Validation before problem-solving. Every time. Kids will keep talking if they feel heard and shut down if they feel managed.

## The bigger picture (in case you're up at midnight worrying)

Here's what I know and what I'm uncertain about, in the interest of being honest with you:

I know that kids who are given language for their emotions — by books, by parents, by teachers — handle transitions better. That's well-supported and it's why books matter here.

I'm less certain about timelines. Some kids settle into a new school in two weeks. Some take three months. I don't know your kid. What I can say is: if you're still seeing significant distress — not just sadness, but not eating, sleep problems, refusing to go — past six weeks, that's worth professional input. Talk to your pediatrician. Or ask for a referral to a child therapist who specializes in transitions. That's not failure. That's good parenting.

What you can control: the bedtime routine, the books you read, the conversations you open, the pick-up ritual that says *I will always come back.* Those matter more than the perfect book.

## Make a book made for your child

All the books above have characters in them. Good ones. But not your kid.

A four-year-old who hears their own name in a story — their actual name, their actual school, a character who looks like them — responds differently than they do to a story about Wemberly or Lazlo. There's a flicker of recognition that goes: *this one is about me.*

That's what we build at Plubis. Personalized picture books for the exact thing your kid is going through. New school, mid-year, all of it. You give us the details, we give you a story.

The first one is free.

situationLabel: a new baby brother or sister
title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about a new baby brother or sister
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through new sibling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds are in a specific kind of chaos when a new baby arrives: old enough to feel deeply displaced, articulate enough to narrate their grievances in real-time, and still young enough to need the reassurance delivered at about the level of a good picture book. Which is convenient, because that's exactly where we are.

## What makes a book land at this age

At four, kids have real interior lives. They understand fairness — or at least their version of it — and they will absolutely tell you that it is not fair that the baby gets carried everywhere while they have to use their legs. They're starting to understand time ("how long until the baby can play with me?") and they have enough language to ask genuinely hard questions.

So the best books for four-year-olds aren't just "here's a character who felt sad and then felt better." They need a character who *does something* — who makes a choice, tries something out, has a moment of real connection. Four-year-olds are building their sense of agency. The books that work are the ones where the older sibling finds their place, not just their peace.

Also: four-year-olds are starting to care about being read to versus reading along. Longer books work now. More text per page. A slightly slower build. That's actually a gift — it means the better books, the ones with real emotional arcs, are finally on the table.

## Eight books worth putting on the shelf

**"Julius, the Baby of the World" by Kevin Henkes**
Lilly cannot stand Julius. She makes this extremely clear throughout the entire book. She whispers mean things at his crib, she tells everyone who will listen, she is magnificently, unapologetically unhappy about this baby. And then her cousin says something rude about Julius and Lilly shuts it down instantly. The turn is so perfectly observed — that particular mix of "I hate him but he's *mine* to hate" — that it will resonate with your four-year-old even if they can't articulate why. Best on this list, hands down.

**"Peter's Chair" by Ezra Jack Keats**
Yes, this also appears in the three-year-old list. It belongs here too. Peter's internal shift — from outrage to something more like acceptance, arrived at through action rather than lecture — is exactly what four-year-olds can follow and feel. Read it slowly. The collage illustrations reward looking.

**"The Berenstain Bears' New Baby" by Stan and Jan Berenstain**
This one gets teased sometimes for being old-fashioned, but honestly it earns its place. Brother Bear gets a new baby sister and has to move out of his cozy room in the tree trunk. He is not pleased. There's a real log cabin building sequence that four-year-olds love. The resolution is earned. A little corny? Sure. Still works.

**"Siblings" by Elissa Haden Guest and Elise Primavera**
This is a book *about* what siblings actually are — the fighting, the sharing, the inside jokes, the loyalty. It's less about the new-baby adjustment and more about the long game. If the baby is already a few months old and your four-year-old is starting to understand that this person is here to stay, this book does something the crisis-moment books don't: it makes the sibling relationship sound worth having.

**"Oonga Boonga" by Frieda Wishinsky**
Baby Louise will not stop crying. The parents try everything. Big brother Daniel says "oonga boonga" and she stops. Now Daniel has something the parents don't. This is a short, funny, deeply satisfying book about the specific power older siblings have — the power to be the only one who can reach the baby. For four-year-olds who are wondering what their role even is, this is the answer.

**"Hello Baby!" by Mem Fox**
Mem Fox is reliable in the way that very few children's book authors are. This is a gentler book — a toddler narrating all the things a new baby will discover about the world. It's more poem than story. Some four-year-olds love it; some find it slow. Worth trying. The language is beautiful enough that it's worth reading aloud even if only you are charmed by it.

**"A Baby Sister for Frances" by Russell Hoban**
Frances runs away. To under the dining table. With crackers. This is the four-year-old's book more than any other age, because at four they're starting to understand "I have an idea, I'll make a plan, I'll execute the plan" — and Frances's plan is extremely four-year-old energy. The resolution is warm and real. A classic.

**"There's Going to Be a Baby" by John Burningham and Helen Oxenbury**
A mother and child have conversations about what the new baby will be like as it grows up — a farmer, a cook, a singer. The child's imagination runs wild. It's a gentler approach than most, leaning into curiosity rather than anxiety. Not every four-year-old is anxious; some are just full of questions. This one is for them.

## How to read these in a way that actually helps

Four-year-olds will project. A lot. When Frances runs away, your four-year-old will say "I want to run away too" and then look at you to see what you do with that. The answer is not to pivot into a feelings lesson. The answer is: "Where would you go?" Let them play it out in the safety of the story.

Read at a consistent time — bedtime works, but so does the quieter daytime nap window if the baby is asleep. Avoid reading new-sibling books right after an incident. You want the emotional temperature low enough that the book can warm things up, not fan something already burning.

Let them turn back pages. "Can we see the part where Lilly was mean again?" That re-reading of a particular scene is your kid processing. They're not being difficult. They're doing the work.

## When a book isn't enough

Four is an age where sibling-related anxiety can show up as sleep problems, clinginess, or sudden regression in skills they had mastered. If those patterns persist for more than a few weeks, it's worth talking to the pediatrician — these books are good conversation starters, but some kids need more than a conversation.

## Make a book made for your child

On Plubis, the book has your child's name in it — not "a child" or "a big brother," but their actual name. You pick the new-sibling situation, type their name in, and the story is generated around them in about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about being afraid of the dark
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through nightmares and the dark. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
## the nightmare phase, age 4 edition

Four is a particularly rough age for this stuff. And I don't mean rough in a "this is hard" way — I mean rough in a "what is even happening" way.

Four-year-olds have vivid imaginations, strong memories, and absolutely no off switch. They will lie in the dark and construct an extremely detailed monster from a bathrobe hanging on the door. They will remember a single scary image from a book they saw six weeks ago and deploy it at 11pm. They will ask follow-up questions about death while you're trying to leave the room.

Also, actual nightmares ramp up around four. The sleep architecture that produces vivid dreams is maturing. So some of what's happening isn't just bedtime anxiety — it's real nightmare content playing in there.

Here's something I think is true: books can't fix four-year-old nightmares. But they can build a vocabulary, a set of mental models, a collection of characters who have survived the dark. That's what you're doing. Building a library of "I know how this goes" before the lights go out.

## what works at this age

Four-year-olds are old enough for complexity. Their favorite thing right now is understanding how things work. So books that actually explain darkness — what it is, what lives in it, why it exists — land differently than pure comfort books.

They also respond to empowerment. Not "you're safe" (passive) but "you can handle it" (active). The books that do best at this age put the child character in charge somehow: they investigate, they confront, they make a decision, they have agency.

And they're ready for books where fear is taken seriously — not dismissed, not magicked away. Four-year-olds have excellent nonsense detectors. If a book tells them the dark is nothing to worry about, they know something's off. But a book that says "yes, this is scary, AND here's what happens when you go into it anyway"? That's speaking their language.

## book picks

**"There's a Nightmare in My Closet" by Mercer Mayer**

Still excellent at four. But now your kid is ready to absorb the deeper move: the boy doesn't win by defeating the nightmare. He wins by taking care of it. The nightmare climbs into bed with him, crying, and he pats it on the back. There's something in that which four-year-olds get almost philosophically — that the scary thing is also a scared thing. Some kids start applying this logic to their own monsters. "Maybe my nightmare is scared too." You can't teach that. The book has to surface it.

**"The Dark" by Lemony Snicket, illustrated by Jon Klassen**

This is the book for your four-year-old who wants to understand. The dark is a character — it has a voice, a location (the basement), and something it needs from Laszlo. The plot requires Laszlo to go down into the dark to get what the dark is offering him. He goes. He survives. The thing that was terrifying turns out to be manageable.

Klassen's illustration style deserves a separate paragraph. His use of negative space — the way darkness is represented as actual emptiness on the page — is genuinely beautiful and does something no other book on this list does: it shows four-year-olds that darkness isn't a thing, it's the absence of a thing. That's almost a science lesson. Highly, highly recommended. Published by Little, Brown.

**"Orion and the Dark" by Emma Yarlett**
