Orion gets taken on a tour of the dark by the Dark itself. The physical book is a whole experience — flaps, windows, textures. At four, your kid will want to work the interactive elements themselves. The cumulative nighttime details — stars requiring darkness, nocturnal animals, the quiet that the world needs — build into a genuinely convincing case that the dark is useful. Orion doesn't just stop being scared. He starts to understand. That's the move. Understanding replaces dread. This one often works when nothing else has. Templar/Candlewick.

**"Bedtime for Frances" by Russell Hoban, illustrated by Garth Williams**

Frances the badger cannot sleep. There's a tiger. There's a giant. There's a crack in the ceiling that might be a spider. Her parents, to their enormous credit, are patient but also clear: you need to stay in bed. Frances negotiates, stalls, notices things, gets scared again. Sound familiar? The humor here is for parents AND kids. Four-year-olds find Frances hilarious because she's doing all the things they do, described out loud with complete sincerity. But the book also, gently, normalizes going to sleep even when you're not sure. Frances does it. Every night. A classic from 1960 that reads as fresh as ever.

**"The Darkest Dark" by Chris Hadfield, illustrated by The Fan Brothers**

Chris — future astronaut, current small Canadian boy — is terrified of the dark. Then he watches the moon landing on TV in 1969 and realizes: space is the darkest dark there is. And people go there on purpose. That reframe — darkness as the thing astronauts explore — is oddly powerful for four-year-olds who are into space (many are). Even for those who aren't, the idea that the thing you're scared of might be the thing that's also amazing is a useful mental model. The Fan Brothers' illustrations are quietly spectacular. Little, Brown, 2016.

**"Go Away, Big Green Monster!" by Ed Emberley**

Your four-year-old might feel like they've graduated from this one. They haven't. The die-cut reveal and the empowerment of shouting "GO AWAY" at each feature of the monster still works. In fact, some four-year-olds find it cathartic in a new way — they can feel the power in their own voice when they tell the monster to go away. Consider reading it in full shout mode. Very satisfying. Little, Brown.

**"Where the Wild Things Are" by Maurice Sendak**

I'm going to make the case for this as a fear-of-the-dark book even though it's not sold as one. Max gets sent to his room — dark, alone — and the dark becomes an adventure that he controls. He's the king of the wild things. He decides when to leave. He comes home to his supper still warm. That arc — into the scary place, king of it, back home safe — is exactly what you want your four-year-old to internalize about darkness. It's one of the greatest picture books ever published. If they don't have it, fix that.

## bonus section: the bedtime routine as ritual

Here's something that works better than most books at four: the monster check.

Not dismissive: "There's no monster." Not anxious: frantically looking under everything.

A calm, official, slightly silly ritual: you and your child check together. Under the bed. In the closet. Behind the curtain. You make it the same every time — same order, same words, maybe a flashlight. "All clear." And they help. They hold the flashlight. They report back.

What you're doing is giving them ownership of the investigation. A four-year-old who's done the check and reported all clear carries that with them into the dark. They know. They looked.

Pair this with a book like "There's a Nightmare in My Closet" and you've got a complete system. Book: concept. Ritual: practice. Together they build something real.

## when a book isn't enough

Nightmares at four are often vivid and remembered. Your kid wakes up and tells you about them with specificity. A monster with orange eyes. A bad person who came in the window. Something chasing them on the stairs.

Don't dismiss the content. Ask about it. "What did it look like?" "Then what happened?" You're helping them process by narrating. The story only has power while it's trapped in their head. Get it out.

Then: "That was scary. And then you woke up. So you were okay." The ending matters. Make sure the story ends with them waking up okay, not with the scary thing.

Some families do a drawing. The kid draws the nightmare and then scribbles over it, or draws a happy ending. Works really well at four. Their visual imagination is powerful — use it both ways.

## make a book made for your child

Four-year-olds have specific fears. Not just "the dark" — but the particular corner, the specific sound, the exact shape of the shadow.

A book that knows about THAT — a story built around your child and the precise things that get them — hits differently than any shelf book can.

At Plubis, we make custom picture books. You tell us your child's name, what scares them, and a couple of details about what makes them feel brave. We write them a story where they're the hero.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about a parent being unwell
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a parent being sick. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds ask hard questions.

Not because they're trying to make things difficult. They ask because they're wired to, because they've recently figured out that asking "why" repeatedly is a route to understanding, and because they haven't learned yet that some questions don't have satisfying answers. So when you're sick — really sick, not just a cold — your four-year-old is going to ask you things you don't know how to answer. Why does the medicine make you tired? Why can't you come to my thing? Are you going to die?

That last one stops you cold every time.

Four is also the age where children start to develop a more solid sense of self, of other people as separate beings with their own inner lives. They're beginning to feel genuine empathy — not just "you're sad so I feel sad" but something more like "you're sick and that must be hard for you." They can hold the idea of your experience alongside their own. That's new. It's actually a gift in this situation, even when it's painful to witness.

But they still need scaffolding. They still need the abstract made concrete — a character going through something similar, a story with a shape, illustrations that show what words can't quite reach.

That's what good books do at four.

## What lands at this age

At four, your child can follow a more developed narrative. Books with a beginning, a middle, and a resolution. Books where the character starts in uncertainty and ends somewhere steadier — not fixed, not falsely happy, but steadier. Four-year-olds are sophisticated enough to recognise when an ending is a lie, and they won't trust a book that wraps everything up in a bow that doesn't match reality.

What they need from a story is: someone like me felt this way, and they came through. Not "everything got better." Just: they came through.

At four, it also helps to read the same book many times. Don't be surprised if they want the same one every night for two weeks. Repetition is how children this age process — each re-reading is another pass at the material, another chance to sit with the feeling and not be destroyed by it.

Let them ask questions while you read. "Why is the daddy in the hospital?" "Is that what chemo looks like?" "Will the character's mama get better?" Answer honestly in age-appropriate terms. "The doctors are trying really hard to make her better." That's enough. You don't need to give them a prognosis.

## Book picks

**"Nowhere Hair" by Sue Glader, illustrated by Edith Buenen** — This is one of the best books written specifically for the four-to-six age range about a parent with cancer. A child asks why Mama has no hair, and the book answers with warmth and honesty: the medicine that fights the sickness also takes the hair, and the hair will come back. The rhyme is accessible. The illustrations are bright and warm, not clinical or frightening. And the relationship between child and parent stays firmly at the centre. What I find most valuable about this book is that it doesn't ask the child to pretend everything is fine. It says: this is strange and different, and we're still us.

**"Cancer Hates Kisses" by Jessica Reid Sliwerski, illustrated by Mika Song** — Written by a mother who was diagnosed with breast cancer four months after giving birth, this book has an emotional specificity that comes through on every page. The device — cancer hates love, cancer hates laughter, cancer can't survive warmth — might sound simple, but for a four-year-old, it's genuinely powerful. It gives them agency. Their love is not passive. It is, in this framing, literally fighting the illness. Four is an age that responds to that kind of meaning-making.

**"When Mommy Is Sick" by Ferne Sherkin-Langer, illustrated by Kay Life** — I recommend this one across several age groups because it's rare to find a book that handles the *recurring* nature of illness honestly. This isn't a one-time hospital visit. It's a pattern: Mama goes in, comes out, goes back in. The child in the story waits, counts the days, visits when allowed. For families dealing with something long-term — cancer treatment, chronic illness, anything that doesn't resolve quickly — this book mirrors the actual experience. At four, your child is old enough to recognise that mirror and feel less alone in it.

**"You Are the Best Medicine" by Julie Aigner Clark** — Specifically for the parent who is the one who's sick: this book gives you something to say to your child that is true. You are not lying to them when you say their presence helps you. Julie Aigner Clark wrote this from inside her own cancer treatment, and the tenderness is real. At four, children need to know that loving you is not passive. This book tells them it isn't.

**"Mommy's Going to the Hospital" by Josie Leon** — Written specifically for children preparing for a parent's hospital stay, this one is practical and emotionally honest in equal measure. It goes through what a hospital is, what it looks like, why a parent might need to be there, and what happens while they're gone. Four-year-olds often fear what they don't know. This book helps fill in the picture. It's not dramatic or dark — it's matter-of-fact in a way that is genuinely reassuring.

**"What Happens When Someone I Love Has Cancer?" by Sara Olsher** — Sara Olsher is a mother who was diagnosed with stage 3 breast cancer while her daughter was young, and she's written a series of age-appropriate books because the ones that existed didn't satisfy her. This book explains what cancer is, what treatment involves, and what it means for family life — in language a four-year-old can absorb. It also consistently names the feelings: worried, confused, scared, angry, and reassures that all of those feelings are okay. I'd pair this one with a quieter, more narrative book. Use it to answer questions, use the others to feel things.

**"When Pete's Dad Got Sick" by Kathleen Long Bostrom, illustrated by Cheri Bladholm** — This one is about chronic illness specifically, and what I appreciate is that it doesn't name the illness. Pete's dad used to run. Now he can barely walk. Pete is angry about that, and hurt, and confused — and the book doesn't try to make those feelings go away. It just holds them alongside love. For families dealing with something other than cancer — MS, long-term injury, lupus, anything that changes a parent's physical capacity — this book is one of the most useful I've found.

## Reading when the story gets too real

Some nights the book will hit too close. Your four-year-old will start crying in the middle of a page, or go suddenly silent and pull the book out of your hands, or ask a question that you have to leave the room to answer.

Don't push through. Put the book down. Hold them. Come back to it another night.

This is not failure. A child who responds that strongly is processing something real. Let them process. Your job in that moment is not to finish the book. It's to be the steady presence that stays through the feeling.

## When the book becomes a conversation

After reading, try this: ask your four-year-old to draw what they're feeling. Not describe it — draw it. Four-year-olds often have an easier time externalising emotion through colour and image than through words. The drawing doesn't need to make sense. It doesn't need to be explained.

But sometimes they'll explain it. And what they say might surprise you. Or break your heart a little. Or both at once.

If your child has been struggling significantly — with sleep, behaviour, eating, mood, or intense anxiety — please don't rely on books alone. Ask your child's doctor or your own care team whether there's a child life specialist available. They're trained for exactly this.

## Make a book made just for your child

All the books above are worth reading. But here's what none of them can do: they can't put your child's name on the page. They can't show a parent who looks like you, or a house that looks like yours, or name the specific thing that's happening to your specific family.

At four, children are beginning to understand that books are made — that someone decided to write this story. A book made about *them* is something else entirely. It's the story of their life, made safe enough to hold.

At Plubis, you can create a personalised picture book for your child in five minutes. Tell us what's happening, how your child is feeling, and a few details about your family. We'll build a story that speaks directly to them — not a template, a real story.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about a parent going away
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through a parent traveling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is when the questions start. Not the crying-and-clinging of two and three, though sometimes that too — but actual questions. "Why do you have to go?" "What is a conference?" "Will your plane crash?" (That last one, delivered at 6am in complete seriousness, is a special kind of parenting experience.)

Four-year-olds are thinking. They're running scenarios. They're imagining all the possible things that could happen while the parent is away and approximately zero of those imagined scenarios are good. Books that enter that mental space with them — that take the worry seriously and follow it through to the okay ending — are doing real work.

## What changes at four

Four-year-olds can hold a whole story. Beginning, middle, end. Character who has a problem, tries something, something resolves. They're not just collecting images and feelings anymore — they're following narrative logic. Which means the books you pick can be more emotionally complex now. A character who's angry the parent is leaving, not just sad. A kid who acts out and then understands. A child who finds their own small bravery during the absence.

They also remember. Read a book at bedtime on Monday and by Wednesday they'll reference it unprompted. "Remember how the owl babies had to wait? I'm waiting like them." That kind of internal referencing is what you want — the book has become part of their vocabulary for the experience.

And four-year-olds have humor. Books that are a little funny about the hard stuff — not dismissive, but gently comic — can take some of the charge out of the fear in a way that purely solemn books can't.

## Books worth reading

**"Owl Babies" by Martin Waddell** — Still the most honest book about waiting for a parent to come back. Bill's line — "I want my mommy" — is the three-word distillation of the entire experience of having a parent away, and four-year-olds will say it back to you with real feeling. The mother's return is simple and complete. What I love about this book is that it doesn't explain the mother's absence or make it logical — it just holds the feeling of her being gone and coming back. That's actually what a four-year-old needs. Not the reason. The shape.

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Four-year-olds who've had this read to them since two know the ritual. If yours doesn't, start now. Chester's mother presses a kiss into his palm before he goes to school; you can use the exact same ritual before the traveling parent leaves. The specific magic at four: your kid can explain the ritual to other people. "Daddy pressed a kiss in my hand and it's there all the time." The articulating-it-to-others is how four-year-olds cement their understanding.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — The metaphor fully works at four. Love is a string between you and the people you love that doesn't break no matter how far apart you are. You can look at the night sky together before the parent leaves and say "the string goes all the way to where Mama is going" — point up, point to your chest, point to theirs. I know it sounds a bit much when I write it like that but in practice, at bedtime, four-year-olds receive this with complete seriousness. They want the metaphor to be literally true. Let it be.

**"Wherever You Are: My Love Will Find You" by Nancy Tillman** — Read this out loud and record it on your phone before the traveling parent leaves. The child can play it back at bedtime while looking at the book. There is something about hearing the gone parent's voice reading "wherever you are, my love will find you" that is worth more than any amount of reassurance during a 2am phone call. The book itself is breathtaking — Tillman's illustrations are the most emotionally specific images I've seen in a picture book. Highly recommend.

**"When You Are Brave" by Pat Zietlow Miller** — A child navigates hard things — being far from someone they love, doing scary new things — and the book names what bravery actually feels like: not comfortable, not easy, but possible. For the four-year-old who's working hard to hold it together while a parent is away, this gives a name to the thing they're already doing. That's a powerful gift. Read it on day one and day three and whenever they seem like they're struggling.

**"The Rabbit Listened" by Cori Doerrfeld** — This is not specifically about parent travel but I include it for the staying parent to read with the child during the trip. Taylor's tower falls and many animals come with loud suggestions for how to feel. Only the rabbit just sits quietly and listens. For a four-year-old who needs to cry about missing the absent parent and doesn't want to be cheered up or distracted, this book gives the staying parent a model: sometimes you just sit with it. Sometimes that's the whole job.

**"In My Heart: A Book of Feelings" by Jo Witek** — Each page is a different feeling — happy, sad, brave, angry, lonely — with a die-cut heart shape in the corner that changes as the feelings do. Four-year-olds can name their feelings but they don't always have a book that says "lonely is a feeling you have and here's what it looks like." That specific emotional labeling matters during a parent's trip. The feeling of loneliness isn't something to fix — it's something to name. This book names it beautifully.

## The conversation the books open (and the one to avoid)

Four-year-olds will want to talk during books. Let them. "Why does the mama owl have to go?" is an opening, not an interruption. You don't need a perfect answer. "Sometimes grown-ups have to go somewhere for work or to take care of something important, and they always, always come back" is enough. You can add specifics about your own situation — "Mama has meetings in another city" — but keep it short. The book is the container for the feeling. You're just supporting the container.

But here's the thing to avoid: don't use the books to prove the child should feel okay. Don't read Owl Babies and then say "see? everything is fine!" Let the story be the story. Four-year-olds are exquisitely sensitive to being managed. The book is most useful when you read it and just let it sit there.

## The night before and the morning of

Do the kissing hand ritual the night before, not the morning of. The morning of departure is a high-pressure, high-stimulus moment. Do the slow, intentional ritual when things are quiet.

Morning of: keep the goodbye short. I know that feels counterintuitive but drawn-out goodbyes give four-year-olds more time to escalate. A hug, the kiss in the palm, "I love you, I'll call you tonight, I'm coming back on [day]" — and then the leaving parent actually leaves. Clean and quick is kinder than long and drawn-out.

## A bonus section: when the parent comes HOME

This catches a lot of parents off guard. You'd expect the return to be pure celebration. Sometimes it is. But often four-year-olds greet the returning parent with a cold shoulder, a tantrum, or pointed ignoring. This is NOT rejection. It's a four-year-old's way of saying "I missed you so much that now that you're back I'm going to make you feel how scared I was." It passes. Don't take it personally. Give it an hour. Read a familiar book together. Let them be in charge of which one.

## When a book isn't enough

If every trip results in serious behavioral disruption — sleep falling apart for the whole absence, aggression, regression in development — it's worth a conversation with your pediatrician and possibly a few sessions with an early childhood therapist. Separation anxiety in four-year-olds is real and treatable and nothing to be embarrassed about. Books are a bridge; sometimes the bridge isn't enough on its own.

## Make a book made just for your child

Your four-year-old will know the book is about them. That distinction matters at this age — not a bear, not a raccoon, not a fictional child — them, by name, in their specific situation. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. Tell us your child's name and what's happening and we generate the whole story. "Leila's daddy went on a work trip" is a different reading experience than anything on this list. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about trying new foods
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through picky eating. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four-year-olds have opinions about food that are basically canonical. Set in stone. Non-negotiable. "I don't eat yellow foods." "I don't eat things that are mixed together." "I only eat pasta if it's not touching sauce." (The pasta touching the sauce debate is an eternal one in many households and I have no solutions for you.)

Here's the good news about four: they're actually starting to become more capable of reasoning about food. Not always. Not on command. But a four-year-old can understand "you don't have to eat it, you just have to touch it" in a way a two-year-old cannot. They can be curious about a new food without immediately needing to refuse it, sometimes. The fortress walls are still there, but there are occasionally windows.

Books at this age work differently than at two or three. Four-year-olds don't just absorb them — they engage with them. They have opinions about the characters. They'll tell you if they identify with the refuser or the brave taster. That conversation is the actual opportunity.

## What four-year-olds are ready for

They can now have a real conversation about food and feelings around food. "Why do you think Sam was so sure he'd hate it?" is a real question that gets a real answer at four.

They can sometimes apply book lessons in real life, if the transfer is explicit and done gently. "Remember how the dragon loved tacos? I wonder if this pasta is kind of like your tacos." This sounds ridiculous. It occasionally works.

They can be proud of bravery in a way that two-year-olds can't access. "That was so brave to try the bite" lands at four. At two it's just words.

## The books

**"Dragons Love Tacos" by Adam Rubin** — Possibly the best picky eating book for four-year-olds of all time, mostly because it has nothing to do with picky eating. It's about dragons and tacos and disastrous spicy salsa incidents. The food joy in this book is infectious. Read it without agenda. Let the joy happen. Then notice when your kid says "I want tacos like the dragons." Not directed. Just: exposure to food as fun.

**"Little Pea" by Amy Krouse Rosenthal** — Little Pea hates candy. He has to eat it every night because that's what pea parents feed their children. His real favorite thing is... spinach. The whole joke is the inversion, and four-year-olds are absolutely old enough to get the joke. It's very funny and kids who hate vegetables love it because the vegetable is the reward. Genuinely sneaky and sweet.

**"I Will Never Not Ever Eat a Tomato" by Lauren Child** — Best on this list for the actual technique. Charlie talks his sister Lola into eating foods by renaming them and telling stories about them. "Orange twiglets from Jupiter." "Cloud fluff." Four-year-olds love this and will sometimes actually try this approach themselves — renaming the food. Let them rename it. It's theirs to own.

**"Green Eggs and Ham" by Dr. Seuss** — Still works at four, with a different angle. Now you can ask "do you think Sam should have tried it earlier?" and get a real opinion. Four-year-olds will often say "yes" because they can now see Sam's stubbornness for what it is. That self-awareness about the refuser is progress.

**"The Seven Silly Eaters" by Mary Ann Hoberman** — Each of seven kids will only eat one specific thing, and the book is a comedy of accommodation. It's long-ish for a four-year-old but if yours has stamina, the humor of everyone having different food rules will land. The chaos feels familiar. The ending — everyone accidentally having the same meal — is satisfying.

**"Bee-bim Bop!" by Linda Sue Park** — A joyful, rhyming book about making bibimbap. It's food as cultural pride, as kitchen adventure, as family ritual. Four-year-olds who are picky sometimes aren't picky about foods they've helped prepare. This book makes food-making look like the most exciting thing ever. Companion activity: make something together, anything, and let them eat whatever part of it they want.

**"Stone Soup" (various adaptations, the Marcia Brown version is classic)** — Soldiers trick villagers into sharing food for a communal soup. The communal meal at the end is warm and full. For the picky eater whose pickiness is partly about control and autonomy, the idea that food is better when it's made together sometimes opens a door. Worth trying.

## Reading together

At four, make the conversation explicit. "So Sam really didn't want to try those green eggs. Why do you think he was so sure he'd hate them?" Wait for the answer. Four-year-olds' answers to this question are often surprisingly insightful.

You can also ask: "is there a food you'd be willing to try if someone renamed it something cool?" This is a game, not a lesson. Let them name it. Write the name down. Then offer that food with the official new name at some point in the next week. This works more often than it has any right to.

## The division of responsibility (DOR)

This is the framework that actually saves sanity at this age: you decide what food is offered and when and where. They decide whether and how much to eat. No forcing, no clean-plate-club, no one-more-bite negotiations. Your job is to offer. Their job is to decide. Books support this by building the internal motivation to try — because it comes from them, not from you.

## When to ask for professional help

If the list of accepted foods is under ten items, if new foods produce real anxiety (not just preference), if meals are consistently distressing for the whole family, consider talking to a feeding therapist. Occupational therapists who specialize in pediatric feeding are specifically trained for this. It's not a failure to ask for help with it.

## Make a book made for your child

At four they'll actually read the book with you and engage with it actively. A custom book where the character has their name, faces their specific food challenge, and comes out brave on the other side — that's a different experience than any off-the-shelf book.

Plubis makes it in five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about potty training
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through potty training. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Okay so your four-year-old is having accidents. Not "learning about the potty for the first time" accidents. Regression accidents. The kid who was fully trained, who bragged about it, who made a big show of washing their hands — that kid is now having multiple accidents a day and you're washing clothes and biting your tongue and trying to figure out what is happening.

Four is a very specific flavor of this problem. At four they have language. They can tell you things. But what they're usually telling you is "I didn't need to go" approximately thirty seconds after wetting themselves. Because at four, the executive function needed to feel the urge, stop what they're doing, decide to go, and actually go is still genuinely hard. The prefrontal cortex has not checked in yet. It won't for twenty more years, honestly. This doesn't mean nothing is wrong — something might be — but it also doesn't mean your child is broken or defiant.

Some of this is genuine physiology. Some of it is big feelings happening in the body. Some of it is that something changed and the stress is coming out at the lowest point of resistance, which is bladder control when you're absorbed in something else.

Books can help at four in a real way, because four-year-olds are solidly in story-processing mode. A character going through something hard and finding their way through it is genuinely useful to them. It gives them a script. A frame. Something to point to.

## What actually helps at this age

Four-year-olds hate being made to feel little. If they sense that a potty book is condescending — aimed at babies, full of pictures of potties with cartoon faces — they will reject it immediately and you will have made things worse. Go for books with real narrative, real characters, real stakes.

The best approach is oblique. Books that address the feelings underneath the accidents — anxiety, change, wanting to control something when everything feels out of control — often do more work than books explicitly about potty training. A four-year-old who is regressing because of a family move doesn't necessarily need a book about the potty. They might need a book about big feelings, or about change, that you can use as a bridge.

That said, there are some genuinely good direct books for this. Let your kid's emotional temperature guide which direction you go.

## The picks

**"Everyone Poops" by Taro Gomi** — The classic doesn't age out until at least five. At four, kids actually laugh at this one in a knowing way — they get that it's funny because it's true and nobody usually says it out loud. That shared laugh at dinner-table-topic territory is valuable. It destigmatizes without being heavy-handed about it. Gomi is quietly brilliant.

**"I Have to Go!" by Robert Munsch** — At four they fully understand the comedy of Andrew's situation. The urgency, the terrible timing, the adults being mildly exasperated. Munsch wrote this for kids to feel seen in their most relatable moments, and four-year-olds feel seen by it. If your kid is giggling, they're processing. The giggles are good.

**"Time to Pee!" by Mo Willems** — Mo Willems does something most children's book authors don't: he treats kids as intelligent people having a genuine experience. The pep squad mice feel like the reader's team, not a lecture. The book is specifically calibrated for kids who know what they're supposed to do and are having trouble doing it consistently — which is the regression situation exactly. The tone is warm and encouraging without being saccharine.

**"Danny Is Done with Diapers" by Rebecca O'Connell** — An ABC-structure potty book where every letter has a kid successfully doing something potty-related. It's cumulative and cheerful and four-year-olds who are regressing sometimes need a book that's just... entirely positive about the whole enterprise. No accidents, no shame, just kids using the bathroom and being proud. Sometimes pure positive modeling is the right move.

**"The Potty Train" by David Hochman and Ruth Kennison** — At four they can enjoy the worldbuilding in this. It's a whole train with passengers and destinations and a theme. Kids who like trains, who like pretend play, who like riding in vehicles — this one gives them something to imaginatively inhabit. Reading it and then playing Potty Train afterward is a completely legit way to spend twenty minutes, and the play reinforces the message better than the reading alone.

**"Bedwetting and Accidents Aren't Your Fault" by Steve Hodges and Suzanne Schlosberg** — This is the one you want if accidents have been going on for a while and your kid is starting to feel bad about themselves. Dr. Hodges is a pediatric urologist and the book's whole argument is that most accidents in older toddlers and preschoolers aren't behavioral — they're often physiological, usually related to constipation putting pressure on the bladder. This is genuinely useful information and the illustrated format makes it accessible to both you and your four-year-old. Warning: the explanations are frank and anatomical and totally age-appropriate, but worth knowing before reading aloud cold.

**"Big Girl Panties" by Fran Manushkin** — If your four-year-old is a girl and is regressing, this one's positivity about the "big kid" identity can help. Four is an age where identity matters — who you are, what big kids do — and framing underwear as a big-kid thing (which it objectively is) can resonate. The rhyming text makes it fast and bouncy enough to stay engaging.

**"Once Upon a Potty" by Alona Frankel** — Reading this one with a four-year-old is an interesting experience. They might find it too young for them — which can actually be useful. "You remember this? You used to read this when you were little." The conversation about how far they've come can be a re-motivation tool. You're not going backward, you're just having a harder week.

## The shame question

Here's the thing about four-year-olds and accidents: they are old enough to feel shame. Real shame. The kind that goes inward and makes things worse. If your child is reacting to accidents with big distress — hiding wet clothes, lying about it, extreme upset — the shame is the problem more than the accident itself. Books that say clearly and non-dramatically that accidents happen, that this is normal, that no one is angry, are doing real emotional first aid.

And your reaction when the accident happens matters enormously. You don't have to perform cheerfulness. But calm and matter-of-fact goes a long way. "Okay, let's change your clothes. Do you need to go now?" That's the whole script. The books help because they pre-load that this is a neutral, manageable thing before any accident occurs.

## A bonus section: when the issue is school

Four-year-olds who are dry at home and wet at school are telling you something specific. Preschool bathrooms are often unfamiliar, sometimes a little scary, and the cues to go are different — there's no parent reminding them, the bathroom is down a hall, they have to stop what they're doing and interrupt something fun. If school accidents are the main issue, have a specific conversation with the teacher about timed bathroom trips. The books help at home but the fix for school accidents is almost always structural, not emotional.

## When a book isn't enough

Four-year-olds who are having frequent accidents, especially if they're accompanied by constipation, pain, or urinary tract infection symptoms, need a pediatrician visit before anything else. The book from Dr. Hodges mentioned above is actually a good read for you — the parent — for understanding whether this might be a physiological issue that no amount of sticker-charting or story-reading will solve. Don't let the "it's behavioral" assumption delay getting a physical checkup.

## Make a book made for your child

Four-year-olds are old enough to be genuinely interested in a book about themselves. Not in an eerie way — in the way that kids this age love hearing their own name in songs, love when teachers remember what they said last week, love being the subject of the story. A custom book that says "Eli had some accidents this week, and he felt embarrassed, and it was okay because everyone has accidents" is doing something no off-the-shelf book can do. Plubis makes them in five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about learning to share
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through sharing. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Four is a genuinely interesting age. It's when children start to really care what their friends think. It's when the social world expands — preschool, playgrounds, birthday parties, the complicated dynamics of "who is whose best friend today." And with that expanding social world comes new sharing terrain: sharing attention, sharing space, sharing the good swing, sharing a parent when a new sibling shows up.

"Mine" is still happening. But at 4, something new appears alongside it: guilt. A 4-year-old often knows, in real time, that they're being unreasonable. They can feel the wrongness of grabbing. They just... can't stop themselves yet. Which is its own special kind of miserable for everyone involved — including them.

What 4-year-olds are ready for that 2- and 3-year-olds weren't: more complex stories, characters who reflect on their behavior, situations that don't resolve perfectly, and books that are genuinely funny without being preachy. Four is old enough to appreciate irony. Barely. But it's there.

These are the books I'd stack up for this age.


## The books

### *The Doorbell Rang* by Pat Hutchins

This 1986 classic is, I will argue, one of the most perfectly constructed picture books ever made, and it is almost entirely about sharing. Victoria and Sam are thrilled — Mom has made twelve chocolate chip cookies, and there are only two of them. Six each! Then the doorbell rings. More friends. Now four cookies each. The doorbell keeps ringing. More and more friends arrive. The cookie count dwindles. The children's faces do extraordinary work in the illustrations as the math gets bleaker.

What makes this book perfect for 4-year-olds specifically is that it lets them feel the math. They can count along. They can do the simple division and feel, viscerally, what it's like to want something badly and have to share it further and further. It makes sharing concrete in a way that "sharing is caring" never does. And the ending — I won't ruin it — is genuinely satisfying in the most cozy, warm way possible.

This is also one of the best books for introducing the concept that sharing isn't loss. That more people at the table doesn't mean less for you. That abundance has a different arithmetic than the one jealousy runs on. Your 4-year-old might not articulate that. But they'll feel it.


### *It's Mine!* by Leo Lionni

At 4, kids can finally engage with the full fable structure of this book, and it delivers. Milton, Rupert, and Lydia are three quarrelsome frogs on a small island who bicker endlessly about what belongs to whom. The pond is mine. The mud is mine. The air is mine. And then a storm comes, and they huddle together in fear on a small rock, and they realize — in one of children's literature's quieter and more powerful moments — that they were never really fighting over anything that mattered.

Lionel Lionni's torn-paper collage illustrations are something else entirely. They look like nothing else in children's books. The textures are rich and layered, and the colors have a depth that rewards slow looking. At 4, kids start to really engage with art, not just story, and Lionni rewards that engagement.

What I love about recommending this at 4 is that the lesson isn't delivered — it's shown. The frogs don't have a conversation where one of them says "I've realized I was wrong." They just... change. Quietly. Under pressure. That's how it actually works, and your 4-year-old is old enough to understand that.


### *Enemy Pie* by Derek Munson, illustrated by Tara Calahan King

This is the sweet spot age for this book. The premise — a boy tries to get rid of his enemy using a mysterious pie his dad concocts, only to discover the enemy was actually a friend he hadn't met yet — is perfectly calibrated for a 4-year-old's social world.

At 4, kids have started to have "enemies" — the kid who got their spot in line, the one who took the block they were using, the one who was mean that one time. This book speaks directly to that, without minimizing it. It doesn't say "you shouldn't have enemies" or "everyone is really nice if you try." It says: sometimes if you give it a day, and actually spend time with the person, things turn out different than you expected.

That's subtle. That's true. And your 4-year-old will absorb it better through story than through any conversation you could have with them about giving people chances.

Also the idea of an Enemy Pie is so deliciously weird that kids are immediately hooked on finding out what's actually in it. Good luck getting them to go to sleep before you finish reading this one.


### *One* by Kathryn Otoshi

This is not technically a "sharing" book. It's a book about bullying and courage. But I'm including it here because it's one of the most elegant picture books I've ever encountered about what it means to take up space, to let others take up space, and to stand up when someone is being excluded — which is, at its root, the social-emotional territory sharing lives in.

The book uses colors as characters. Blue is quiet and kind. Red is bold and bossy. And none of the other colors will stand up to Red — until One shows up.

For 4-year-olds, it works on multiple levels simultaneously: the counting and colors appeal to where they are developmentally, the emotional arc is clear and satisfying, and the message — that it only takes one voice to change the temperature of a room — is something they can genuinely act on in their own little social world.

Read this one slowly. Sit with each page. It earns the silence.


## The part where I tell you what doesn't work

There are dozens of books about sharing that are technically fine and practically useless. You know the type: a cheerful animal discovers that sharing makes everyone happy, the other animals clap, everyone smiles, the end. I am not recommending any of those.

The problem isn't that they're wrong. Sharing does make people happy. But they model the outcome of sharing without the struggle that precedes it. And the struggle is the whole thing. Your 4-year-old doesn't need to be told that sharing feels good afterward — they've experienced that. What they need is company in the part that comes before: the moment when they have the thing and someone else wants it and their whole body says NO.

The books on this list all honor that internal conflict. That's why they work.


## What you can do alongside the books

**Play games that require turn-taking.** Not as a lesson — just as fun. Simple card games, board games with a spinner, anything where waiting is built into the structure. Kids who play a lot of turn-taking games develop better turn-taking instincts in other areas. It's practice.

**Point out sharing in public when it's not about your child.** "Look, that kid is letting her brother try the scooter." No moralizing. Just observation. You're building a vocabulary of what sharing looks like in the wild.

**Notice and name it when your child shares without being asked.** Don't go overboard — "Oh my gosh that was AMAZING sharing!!" lands weirdly. Just a quiet "hey, that was really kind of you" goes further than you'd think. You're reinforcing what they already did, not praising them into performance.


## A bonus thing nobody says about 4-year-olds and sharing

Four-year-olds are incredibly susceptible to narrative identity. Meaning: if you start telling the story of your child as "a good sharer," they will often try to live up to that story. "You know, you're the kind of kid who notices when your friends need a turn." Not in a manipulative way — sincerely, when you see evidence of it. Children this age are actively constructing who they are. If you give them a piece of identity to grow into, they'll grow into it.

Books help with this too. When a 4-year-old deeply identifies with a character — decides they are basically Llama Llama or basically Gerald the elephant — they start testing the character's choices against their own. "What would Gerald do?" is not a silly question. It's how 4-year-olds build their moral imagination.

So read often. Read slowly. And let them tell you what they think the character should have done. They almost always have something worth hearing.


*Make a custom picture book with your child as the main character — name, face, favorite toy, real moment — about sharing in their own world. The first one is free.*

title: The best picture books to read to a 4-year-old about tantrums and meltdowns
metaDescription: Picture books for 4-year-olds going through tantrums. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
You thought you'd be past this by four. You were told, maybe, that the tantrums get better after three. And they do — in frequency, often. But a four-year-old having a meltdown hits different. Because now they have opinions. Elaborate, articulate, completely ironclad opinions. And when those opinions run into reality, the meltdown arrives wearing a TED talk.

"I don't WANT to go to Maya's birthday. I don't LIKE Maya's birthday. Actually I don't LIKE Maya." (Maya is her best friend. She will spend the birthday party attached to Maya's hip like a barnacle.) The feelings are genuine. The logic is impeccable from the inside. The prefrontal cortex is still, infuriatingly, not fully online.

Here's the thing about four: it's actually a good age for books about big feelings. Four-year-olds follow stories well. They have enough emotional vocabulary to engage with the material. They're old enough to recognize themselves in characters, laugh at themselves a little, and start — just start — to build some actual self-regulation. Books are one of the things that moves that process along.

## What four-year-olds can actually do now

They can sit through a longer book. They can answer "why did she feel that way?" with a real answer. They're starting to understand that feelings pass — that the terrible moment isn't permanent. They can sometimes catch themselves mid-meltdown, which looks like a tiny pause before continuing, which is actually enormous progress.

What they still can't do: regulate in real time without support. Apply the lesson from a book to the meltdown that's currently happening. Be rational about the wrapper being the wrong color.

So you're still reading these books in calm moments. You're still planting seeds. But now you can water them a little more directly.

## The books

**"Alexander and the Terrible, Horrible, No Good, Very Bad Day" by Judith Viorst** — This is the age. Four-year-olds can now hold the whole arc of Alexander's day, find it funny, and feel the solidarity of "some days are just like that." The humor is the thing. Laughing about a terrible day in retrospect is a coping skill. It's also just very funny. "Even in Australia." Read it like you mean it. Do the voice. They'll ask for it again.

**"Grumpy Monkey" by Suzanne Lang** — Still works. Maybe better at four than at two or three, because now you can actually ask "do you think Jim's friends were being helpful?" and they'll say NO with strong conviction and then explain, in detail, why unsolicited advice about feelings is annoying. Which is correct. They're right. Let that discussion happen.

**"The Bad Mood and the Stick" by Lemony Snicket** — This one is genuinely excellent and underappreciated. A bad mood moves through a town, sticking to different people, changing how everyone feels. It's strange and funny and a little philosophical. Four-year-olds are exactly old enough to get the absurdism and exactly young enough to find it completely delightful. Also opens up the conversation about how feelings are contagious — why mom's bad morning becomes everyone's bad morning. Worth owning.

**"When Sadness Is at Your Door" by Eva Eland** — Technically about sadness, not tantrums. But four-year-olds' meltdowns often have sadness underneath the rage, and this book handles that beautifully. Sadness arrives as a creature. You give it a name, you let it exist, you go for a walk together. The message is: feelings don't have to be fought. They can just be. A very different message from most parenting advice, and one of the better ones.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At four, separation-driven meltdowns are real — especially around preschool drop-off or when a parent travels. This book gives kids a physical metaphor for connection that they can actually use. "I'm holding the string" is something a four-year-old can say in a moment of anxiety instead of full collapse. The string is invisible and it stretches everywhere and it never breaks. A lot of four-year-olds genuinely believe in it, in the way they believe in other important invisible things.

**"Ish" by Peter H. Reynolds** — Not exactly a tantrum book but best on this list for the four-year-old whose meltdowns are about perfectionism and frustration. You know the one: they crumple their drawing because it's "wrong." They refuse to try the thing they haven't mastered yet. Reynolds's message — that ish-perfect is better than not at all — hits this demographic hard. Beautiful and gentle and genuinely useful for the performance-anxious four-year-old.

**"Breathe and Be" by Kate Coombs** — Beautiful poetry + illustrations pairing animals with mindfulness moments. More contemplative than instructional. Four-year-olds who are starting to develop a quieter, more internal way of handling big feelings respond to this really well. It's a different register from Grumpy Monkey — you'll want both kinds in the rotation.

## Having the conversation

At four, you can actually talk about feelings in a more real way. Not as a lesson. As a conversation. "I read this book where the bad mood traveled through the town. Does that ever happen in our house?" And then you listen. They have thoughts. Genuinely interesting thoughts. 

Four is also when you can start naming your OWN feelings out loud in front of them. "I'm feeling frustrated right now. I'm going to take a deep breath." And do the breath, visibly. You're modeling. They're watching. The modeling does more than the book, actually.

When a meltdown happens at four, there's sometimes a tiny window after the peak where they're ready to be held. Not talked at — held. That window is real and it closes fast. If you catch it, everything is easier.

## When tantrums at four are a flag

Tantrums at four are normal. But tantrums at four that are as frequent and intense as they were at two — with no pattern, no predictable trigger, no better days — are worth a conversation with your pediatrician. Not because there's definitely a diagnosis waiting, but because four is old enough that assessment tools exist if needed. Better to ask now than wonder for another year.

## Make a book made for your child

The thing about Alexander's terrible day is that it's Alexander's. Grumpy Jim's bad mood is Jim's. A book where the character having the terrible, no-good day is named the same thing your kid is named? That's different. That lands somewhere specific. Plubis builds it in five minutes.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about bedtime
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through bedtime routines. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Some five-year-olds go to sleep great and you have no idea why you're reading this. Good for you. Genuinely. Enjoy that and don't question it.

The rest of you: welcome. Your five-year-old has now added *logic* to their arsenal of bedtime resistance. They are in kindergarten or about to start. They have opinions about fairness — "why does the baby get to stay up later" (the baby is not staying up later, you just checked on the baby) — and they understand that you're tired, which they sometimes use strategically, and sometimes they're just processing everything that happened that day out loud in the dark and they can't actually stop.

Five is a fascinating, exhausting age for sleep. Old enough to know they're supposed to sleep. Old enough to resent being told what to do. Dealing with a social world — school — that's genuinely complicated and worth processing. Their brains are busy in a way two-year-old brains just aren't.

Picture books still work. In fact, they might work better at five than at any other age, because your kid can now enter a story completely. They can inhabit a character. They can feel what the character feels. That's the superpower you're working with.

## The honest truth about five-year-olds and sleep

Five is the age of the 6am wake-up and the 9pm refusal. You do the math on whether they're getting enough sleep. You know they need ten or eleven hours. You know that's not happening. You are doing your best.

The thing that actually helps most at five — besides a consistent bedtime — is connection. The day was big. School is socially intense. They've been managing themselves in a group setting for six hours. They need you. Not entertaining-you, not problem-solving-you, just *present-you* for twenty minutes. The book is a vehicle for that presence. You reading next to them, your voice, their body against yours — that's the real thing that winds them down. The story is the excuse.

So choose books you can read warmly. Slowly. Books that invite stillness rather than excitement. At five they can appreciate more complex storytelling, but bedtime is not the time for a page-turner. Save the plot twists for the afternoon.

## Books that do real work at five

**"Frederick" by Leo Lionni** — Frederick is a field mouse who, while all the other mice gather corn and berries for winter, collects words and colors and sun. The other mice think he's lazy. In the dark cold of winter, he shares his words and colors and the mice are warmed. It's about being a different kind of person, and about the value of imagination and inner life. Five-year-olds who feel like they live more inside their heads than other kids respond to this with something that looks like relief. It says: the things you hold in your mind matter. You're not lazy. You're Frederick.

**"The Owl Who Was Afraid of the Dark" by Jill Tomlinson** — Technically a transitional chapter book, but it reads as a multi-night bedtime book beautifully. Plop the barn owl is afraid of the dark despite being an owl who lives at night. He keeps meeting different people who tell him what the dark is for — darkness is exciting, darkness is kind, darkness is fun. By the end he's made his own list. For a five-year-old with night fears, this is the best single book I know. It doesn't dismiss the fear. It genuinely explores darkness from multiple angles. Each chapter a new encounter — perfect for multi-night reading that builds.

**"Bedtime for Frances" by Russell Hoban** — Still works at five, and now they might actually be a little smug about Frances. "She's just being dramatic." Maybe. Or maybe they're being diplomatic about recognizing themselves. Either way, reading it with a five-year-old who can think critically about Frances's tactics is a different and better experience. They can laugh at the pattern. That distance is genuinely useful.

**"Alexander and the Terrible, Horrible, No Good, Very Bad Day" by Judith Viorst** — Not a bedtime book. The perfect bedtime book. A day where everything goes wrong, perfectly catalogued, ending with the recognition that some days are just like that — even in Australia. Five-year-olds who've had a hard day at school need to see that named. Not fixed. Not resolved into a lesson. Just: yes, some days are terrible, that's real, and you go to sleep and tomorrow is something else. Viorst's refusal to wrap this up in a neat bow is the whole gift.

**"Night Noises" by Mem Fox** — Old Lily Laceby dozes by the fire on her birthday while her dog Butch watches over her and her family secretly gathers outside. The night sounds — knocks, footsteps, voices — all turn out to be something wonderful. For the child who finds night sounds frightening, this reframes them gently. Maybe the sounds outside are not threats. Maybe they're something coming. The warm resolution and the birthday twist make it deeply reassuring without being dismissive of the fear.

**"The Big Red Barn" by Margaret Wise Brown** — Same author as Goodnight Moon, same genius for slow peaceful prose. Animals in a barn going through their day and settling into sleep as the day ends. Simpler than you'd think a five-year-old would want, but that's the point — sometimes after a complex day, a slow, simple book is the thing. There's no problem to solve. There's no conflict. Just a red barn and animals going to sleep. Profoundly effective on overstimulated, overscheduled evenings.

**"Click, Clack, Moo: Cows That Type" by Doreen Cronin** — Completely different energy. The cows type demands to Farmer Brown. Hilarity follows. This is not a wind-down book — it's a funny book, and funny books belong in the rotation at five because laughter is a legitimate stress-release and some days your kid needs to laugh before they can sleep. You'll know the difference. On those days, put away the gentle ones and read the cows typing. A good hard laugh and then lights out.

**"Owl at Home" by Arnold Lobel** — Five short chapters about Owl, who befriends winter, tries to be in two places at once, and finds two bumps in his bed (which turn out to be his own feet). Gentle and funny and a little surreal. Owl is domestic and cozy and slightly confused about the world in a way that's entirely charming. Perfect for a five-year-old who needs company at bedtime — Owl is home, warm, figuring things out, not alone.

## Reading together when they're "too old for picture books"

They're not. Five-year-olds who are starting to read themselves sometimes develop the opinion that picture books are for babies. This is a phase, and it usually passes, but while it's happening: don't argue about it. Offer chapter books. Try *The Owl Who Was Afraid of the Dark* as a chapter book read-aloud. Try the first Magic Tree House. Let them pick sometimes.

But also: quietly keep the picture books. On hard nights. On sick days. After difficult social situations. Picture books aren't baby books — they're short-form emotional literature. Some of the best picture books ever written are being read by adults without apology. Hold your ground gently on this one.

## Bonus: the school-day debrief ritual

Five-year-olds are processing a lot of social information every day. Who said what. Who they sat with. Who they didn't sit with. That internal replaying keeps brains busy at bedtime. A simple ritual that helps: after the book, and before lights out, ask one question: "Tell me the best part of today." That's it. One question, not a full debrief. They talk, you listen, you say "I'm glad." The talking empties some of that processing and makes it easier to settle. It takes three minutes. It works.

If they want to tell you about a hard part too — let them. Don't fix it right there. "That sounds hard. We can think about it together tomorrow." Same move as with the big questions. Honor it, defer it, sleep.

## When a book isn't enough

At five, some sleep issues are starting to look like something specific — anxiety, sensory needs, gifted-kid overstimulation, early ADHD presentation. If your kid is consistently taking more than forty minutes to fall asleep, or waking regularly with fears that don't ease, or refusing bedtime so hard that it's affecting the family — it's worth a conversation with your pediatrician. Behavioral sleep help at five is effective and there's no reason to white-knuckle it alone.

## Make a book made just for your child

Five-year-olds know about stories being written. They know books are made by someone. The idea that someone made a book specifically about them — with their name, their situation, their world — is the most exciting thing. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. You put in your child's name and what they're going through, and we make them the hero of their own story. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about big feelings
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through big feelings. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Here is something nobody tells you before you have kids: five-year-olds are emotional philosophers. They will look you dead in the eyes at 8pm and ask "why do feelings hurt?" and you will stand there holding a dish towel and have absolutely nothing.

Five is sophisticated. They can describe what's happening inside them. They can listen to someone else's explanation and push back on it. They have opinions about whether a resolution in a story is realistic. They will tell you the bunny SHOULDN'T have done that and it STILL doesn't make sense that everything worked out fine. They are becoming critical readers of emotional narratives, which is delightful and occasionally maddening.

At the same time: five is still in the body. Feelings still explode before the thinking brain has a chance to catch up. They'll say something mean to a friend and genuinely not be sure why. They'll cry at something small and know even as they're crying that it doesn't warrant tears and cry harder because of that. The gap between emotional awareness and emotional regulation is real at five, same as it was at two, just the awareness side has gotten way ahead.

Books are starting to do something different now. They're not just introducing concepts — they're deepening them. A five-year-old who's been raised on feelings books is ready for books that have complicated feelings, feelings with no obvious resolution, characters who mess up and make things harder before they get better.

## What five opens up

At five, you can read a book where the character is wrong — not the outside character, but the main character — and your kid can sit with that. "He shouldn't have done that." "No, he shouldn't have. Why do you think he did?" That's a real conversation now. That's a conversation about the gap between feelings and actions and choices. Five-year-olds can be in that conversation.

They're also beginning to understand other people's perspectives. Not fully. But beginning. A character who does something unkind because they're sad, not because they're bad — that's accessible at five in a way it isn't at three. Books that work with this complexity are worth adding now.

And five-year-olds remember. They'll pull a book reference weeks later. They build internal libraries of story-based emotional models. Choose books with models you want in that library.

## The books (with strong opinions)

**"The Rabbit Listened" by Cori Doerrfeld** — Taylor builds something and it falls apart and is devastated. And then every animal comes with advice: the elephant wants to talk it out, the bear wants to roar about it, the snake says to pretend it never happened. None of it helps. The rabbit just comes and sits quietly. And eventually Taylor feels better. I cry at this book every single time. Five-year-olds who have been talked at when they were upset understand this book in their bones. The message — sometimes the best thing you can do for someone who is hurting is simply be present, without fixing — is one of the most important things a human can learn. Worth reading slowly. Worth talking about afterward: "What did you need from me last time you were really upset?"

**"Feelings" by Aliki** — An older book, from 1984, and still unmatched for sheer density of emotional vocabulary at the right age. Dozens of feelings shown through small vignette scenes: mixed feelings, surprising feelings, feelings about feelings. Five-year-olds who are starting to have layered emotions — embarrassed AND angry, excited AND scared — find this book a relief. The art is dated. Don't care. The emotional honesty is not.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — A boy has an enemy. His dad offers to make Enemy Pie but the boy has to spend a whole day being nice to the enemy first. The twist is what it is. This is the best book I know for a five-year-old who has social conflict — a kid at school they've decided is a permanent enemy, a friendship falling apart, that specific childhood hatred that feels total and absolute. The feelings in here are social feelings, which at five are enormous: jealousy, contempt, resentment, and then the much more uncomfortable thing that happens when you realize an enemy is just a person. Excellent dinner table conversation starter.

**"When Sophie Gets Angry — Really, Really Angry" by Molly Bang** — I'm putting this on every age list and I'm not sorry. At five it works because they can discuss the whole arc now. What made Sophie angry? Was the sister being unfair? Where did Sophie go? Why did the tree help? What happened in her body while she was out there? Sophie is a philosophy seminar for five-year-olds masquerading as a picture book.

**"A Little Spot of Anger" by Diane Alber** — The Spot books are aggressively cheerful in a way that you either love or find grating, but the information they contain is genuinely solid. Anger is a little red spot that gets bigger when you feed it with more angry thoughts. There are concrete strategies for shrinking the spot. Five-year-olds respond well to visual metaphors and to the idea that they have some control over how big the spot gets. Practical in a way the more literary books aren't. Worth having both.

**"Inside Out and Back Again" — no, that's a novel. Let me give you picture books.**

**"The Smallest Girl in the Smallest Grade" by Justin Roberts** — Sally notices everything. She's small and often overlooked, but she sees the kids who are sad, the lunch tables that are lonely, the things that aren't fair. The feelings in here are social justice feelings — empathy, outrage, courage — which five-year-olds are genuinely starting to develop. It's also a book about the power of one small person to change something. Very good for the kid who says "but I can't DO anything about it." You can. Sally proves it.

**"No, David!" by David Shannon** — I know, I know. But at five, David is funnier in a knowing way — your kid has now been David, knows they've been David, and can laugh at it. The last page — mama holding David and saying "I love you" — hits differently at five because they know what that love is carrying. Worth a re-read even if they had it at four.

**"Chrysanthemum" by Kevin Henkes** — Absolutely the right time for this. Chrysanthemum loves her name until school starts and kids make fun of it. The feelings — the creeping shame, the loss of certainty about something you loved, the discovery that the right person seeing you can restore it — are textured and real. For any five-year-old navigating being different in a social world, this one is worth reading every few months. It doesn't age out.

## Reading together at five — the part that actually matters

At five, read it once through without interruption. Then go back to one page and ask a single question. Not five questions. One. "What do you think Sophie was feeling right before she ran outside?" And then be quiet and wait for the answer. This age will surprise you with what they have if you make the space.

Also worth doing at five: occasionally ask them to tell you the story after you've read it. Not a quiz — not "what happened first and then what happened" — but "what was that story about to you?" The answer reveals what they took from it, which is often not what you thought they took from it, which is often more interesting and more useful.

Five-year-olds are also old enough to read books about characters who look and sound like them. Representation isn't just representation — it's the difference between "this is a story" and "this could be MY story." Keep it in mind when you're choosing.

## The question they'll ask that you won't expect

"Why does angry feel different from sad if they both feel bad?" I have been asked this by a five-year-old in the dark at bedtime. I did not have a great answer. We talked about it for a long time. It was one of the better bedtime conversations I've had. This age asks these questions. Have the books ready as a starting point, but know the books are just the opening act.

## Bonus: the co-regulation moment that actually works at five

When your five-year-old is in a big feeling and you're trying not to escalate it: get physically lower than them. Crouch or sit on the floor. Something about eye level matters enormously — being looked down at when you're already feeling overwhelmed is activating. Get low. Say one sentence. "That was really hard." Then be quiet. Wait. Let them come to you. It works more often than you'd think.

## When a book isn't enough

Five-year-olds who can articulate big feelings but consistently cannot act in accordance with what they know — who get overwhelmed, dysregulated, and have significant difficulty recovering — might benefit from working with a therapist who specializes in early childhood emotional development. Knowing about feelings and being able to regulate them are two different skills. Both can be built. The second one sometimes needs more support than books and patient parenting alone. There's no failure in that. The brain that needs more support is the brain you have. Work with it.

## Make a book made just for your child

At five, your kid can be an active collaborator in this. "I'm making a book about you — it's about that time you felt really angry and then found your way through. What should we call the main character?" They know their name. They will like this. Plubis generates a full custom illustrated picture book in five minutes — your child's name, their situation, a story built around them. It's not a generic insert-name-here book. It's a real story, illustrated, about someone with their name having feelings like theirs and finding a way through. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about building confidence
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through confidence and self-esteem. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is the year everything gets audited.

Up until now, your kid's confidence was mostly unexamined. They tried things, fell, tried again, and their self-concept was loosely assembled from these experiences without too much meta-analysis. But five is the year they start examining themselves like a report card. "Am I good at this? Am I fast? Am I smart? What do the other kids think of me?"

And five is also, very often, the year they start school. Real school. Which is an absolute confidence gauntlet.

You hand your child to a room full of strangers, a teacher they've never met, rules they haven't learned yet, tasks that expose gaps in real time in front of peers. A five-year-old who was totally confident in their home environment — who could tell you exactly what they thought about everything and do their morning routine and boss their younger sibling around with natural authority — suddenly freezes in the school hallway because they don't know where to put their backpack.

This is normal. This is the adjustment. But it's disorienting to watch, especially if you had a confident-seeming kid before kindergarten and now they're coming home quiet.

Books help here in a specific way: they give kids language for what they're experiencing, and they show them that the feeling is survivable, common, and not the same thing as being incapable.


## The confidence landscape at five: what you're actually seeing

Five-year-olds are exquisitely tuned to social judgment. They know when they've gotten something wrong in a way that others noticed. They feel the heat of that. A wrong answer in circle time — especially if it got a laugh — can change how a kid participates in circle time for weeks.

At the same time, fives still have a lot of pre-rational confidence available. They'll attempt things they have no business attempting and occasionally succeed through sheer nerve. The kid who gets up and dances at a school concert while every other kindergartener stands frozen is not more talented — they just haven't yet fully internalized the risk of being seen.

Your job, as the adult who loves them, is not to manufacture confidence by protecting them from every failure. It's to help them develop what psychologists call *self-efficacy* — a felt sense that "I have handled hard things before, and I can handle the next one." That sense comes from experience, not pep talks. But books can build the emotional vocabulary that makes the experience legible afterward.


## The books

### *Jabari Jumps* by Gaia Cornwall (2017)

This is still my first pick at five, for different reasons than it was at four.

At four, the book's power is mostly in validation — Jabari is scared, Jabari waits, that's okay. At five, especially at the start of a school year, the book's power shifts to the arc: he waits *and then he does it*. The doing-it part becomes more salient because five-year-olds are starting to see themselves as actors in their own story, not just passengers.

His father's patience is also worth pointing at explicitly at five. "Sometimes when you try something new, it's normal to feel nervous." Read that sentence again. It doesn't say the nervousness is wrong. It doesn't say to push through it or ignore it or that brave people don't feel it. It says: *this is normal*. Normalize the nervousness, don't fight it.

For a kid who is anxious about kindergarten or a new activity, knowing that the nervous feeling is expected and doesn't mean "don't do it" is genuinely useful information.


### *Chrysanthemum* by Kevin Henkes (1991)

This is the year *Chrysanthemum* is at its full power.

Henkes wrote a book about a kid starting school, loving herself, having her self-love systematically eroded by peer cruelty, and then reclaiming it. It's devastatingly accurate. If your five-year-old has started school, they have already encountered at least one Victoria — the kid who delivers perfectly calibrated little cuts with a smile.

"Your name is so long." "It barely fits on your name tag." "It's a *flower*."

These are not dramatic bullying incidents. These are the daily drip of social status-setting that happens in every kindergarten classroom, and Henkes captures them with clinical precision.

What makes this book great for confidence work is what it doesn't do. It doesn't tell Chrysanthemum to have thicker skin. It doesn't give her a speech about ignoring mean kids. What ultimately restores her is community — the music teacher, Mrs. Twinkle, who has a flower name too and wears it with joy. It's not about internal fortitude. It's about finding your people.

For a five-year-old navigating the social world of kindergarten, this is useful and honest: sometimes confidence comes from finding the person who reflects your worth back at you. That's not weakness. That's human.


### *Stand Tall, Molly Lou Melon* by Patty Lovell, illustrated by David Catrow (2001)

Oh, I have strong feelings about this book.

Molly Lou Melon is small. She has buck teeth. She has a voice that sounds like "a bullfrog being squeezed by a boa constrictor." Her grandmother has told her her whole life to stand tall, smile big, and march. So when Molly Lou moves and starts a new school and a bully named Ronald Durkin makes fun of every single thing about her, she doesn't crumble. She stands. She does her thing. And the whole class eventually shifts to her side.

Here's my opinion, clearly stated: this book is better for five-year-olds than for older kids, despite what the age range on the back says, because five is exactly when kids need to see a model of confident *weirdness* — of being entirely yourself in a new environment without apologizing for it.

But I'll also be honest about the limitation: this book makes it look easier than it is. Molly Lou never wavers. Her confidence is improbable. Real five-year-olds do not always have grandmothers who spent years arming them with the right phrases. So use it as an aspirational model, not a realistic one. Read it and ask: "What do you think helped Molly Lou feel okay when Ronald was mean to her?" Their answers will be interesting.


### *The Dot* by Peter H. Reynolds (2003)

Vashti refuses to draw. She says she can't. Her teacher asks her to just make a mark. She makes a dot — angry, tiny, dismissive. The teacher frames it. The following week, Vashti is making a hundred dots. Colored ones, large ones, dots made with splashes and swirls. And then she passes it on — when a younger boy says "I can't draw a straight line," she hands him a ruler.

This book is about something very specific: the difference between "I can't" and "I haven't yet." And it's about the role a trusted adult can play in cracking that door open — not by arguing, not by forcing, but by taking the first tiny reluctant effort and treating it as real.

For five-year-olds who are beginning formal learning and encountering the "I can't" feeling — which is going to happen constantly, around reading, numbers, cutting, writing, all of it — this book plants a useful idea. The mark you make when you're not trying is still a mark. You can work with it.

Candlewick Press published this in 2003. It spawned International Dot Day (September 15). It's been in print ever since, for good reason.


## The one teachers always recommend: *The Name Jar* by Yangsook Choi (2001)

Unhei is new to an American school. On the bus, kids try to pronounce her Korean name and make it into a joke. So she tells her new class her name isn't decided yet — and they put possible new names in a jar. But as the story unfolds, Unhei realizes she doesn't want a new name. She wants her own.

This is not quite the same as the other confidence books, but it deserves a place in this list because the confidence it addresses is identity-based. Not "can I do the thing" but "am I allowed to be fully myself here."

For five-year-olds in diverse classrooms, in new environments, in families where their name or appearance or language is sometimes pointed at — this book says directly: the thing that makes you different is not the problem. The people who don't know how to handle it yet are the ones who need to catch up.

That's a confidence message. A harder, deeper one.


## The bonus section: What to say when they come home defeated
