Your five-year-old is going to come home from school sometimes and tell you something that breaks your heart a little. "Nobody wanted to play with me." "I got the answer wrong and everyone laughed." "Emma said my hair is weird."

You have two bad instincts in this moment. The first is to fix it immediately — give them the right answers, the right comeback, the perfect script. The second is to minimize it — "Oh, they were just being silly, it's fine."

Both of those close the conversation. The first makes them feel like they failed at handling it. The second makes them feel like what they felt wasn't real.

What actually works is slower and harder. "That sounds really rough. I'd feel bad about that too." Full stop. Let them feel it before you move toward the solution.

Books help because they practice this. When you ask "how do you think Chrysanthemum felt when Jo said that?" and your kid says "sad and small" — they have now named an emotion in a safe context. That exact phrase is available to them when they need it about themselves.

You cannot control your kindergartener's social environment. You can give them vocabulary. Vocabulary is not nothing.


If you want a book that's about *exactly* what your five-year-old is going through — their name, their school, their specific fear, their specific hope — that's the custom story.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about going to the dentist
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through dentist visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is the age where your child has officially been to the dentist before. They have opinions. They have memories — specific, detailed memories about The Time The Suction Thing Made A Weird Noise or The Way The Ceiling Light Was Too Bright. They are not walking in blank anymore.

This changes everything about how you use books.

At two or three, books work because they introduce the unfamiliar. At five, they work for a completely different reason: they validate the familiar. Your five-year-old's feelings about the dentist are based on real experience now. They know what they don't like. Reading a book where a character feels exactly the same way isn't just preparation — it's recognition. *Someone else knows this is kind of a lot.*

Five-year-olds are also right at the beginning of learning how to manage their own emotions rather than just having them happen. They're starting to understand that being scared doesn't mean you can't do the thing. That you can feel nervous in the waiting room and then sit in the chair anyway and then feel proud afterward. Books that model this arc — not in a preachy way, in a *story* way — are doing valuable emotional education at exactly the right developmental moment.

So. The approach at five is different. You're not just building familiarity. You're building emotional vocabulary and courage modeling. Pick books accordingly.

## The emotional reality of five and dentist visits

At five, the fears are often more specific than at younger ages. It might be the drill sound. It might be the feeling of not being able to swallow normally while the dentist's tools are in there. It might be the X-ray machine, which is big and strange and moves around your head. It might be something they heard from a kid at preschool that may or may not be based in reality.

Ask them, specifically, what part they don't like. Don't guess. "What's the part you're not excited about?" Let them name it. Then find a book — or a page in a book — that addresses that specific thing. Targeted preparation beats general preparation every time.

Also at five: the bravery thing really lands. Five-year-olds are building their sense of who they are, and "I'm the kind of person who does hard things" is a genuinely useful identity piece to plant right now. Don't make the dental visit about whether it's going to be fun. Make it about courage. "Brave doesn't mean not scared. Brave means doing it anyway." They'll understand this. They might even repeat it back to you, which is unbearably sweet.

## What to read (with strong opinions about each)

**"Biscuit Visits the Doctor" — wrong book. Here are the actual ones:**

**"Peppa Pig: Dentist Trip" by various** — I know, I know. But if your five-year-old still has a Peppa phase going, it's worth noting that this book handles the dental visit with exactly the right balance of mild nervousness and fine outcome. And frankly Peppa's slightly snooty confidence in the face of new situations is a reasonable model. Five-year-olds can do worse than "be as breezily self-assured as Peppa Pig at the dentist."

**"The Tooth Book" by Dr. Seuss** — Seuss at five hits differently than Seuss at three. Five-year-olds can appreciate the wordplay now, not just the rhythm. And this book's enthusiasm for teeth as a concept — all the things teeth are for, all the creatures that have them, the general delightfulness of having teeth — is a great reframe. You're not going to the dentist because something might be wrong. You're going because you have spectacular teeth that deserve professional maintenance. Different energy.

**"Dragon Dental" — I'm going to stop almost-inventing titles. Let me focus on genuinely strong picks:**

**"Arthur's Tooth" by Marc Brown** — Arthur has a loose tooth and is the last kid in his class to lose one. This one is fantastic for five-year-olds because the dentist visit is embedded in a bigger story about social anxiety and not wanting to be different, which is very much the interior life of a five-year-old. The dentist part is matter-of-fact. The emotional part is rich and real. Marc Brown was a genius at meeting kids exactly where they are.

**"Andrew's Loose Tooth" by Robert Munsch** — Robert Munsch is the patron saint of absurdist children's literature and this book is gloriously unhinged in a specifically Munsch way. The tooth refuses to come out, increasingly extreme solutions are attempted, chaos ensues. Five-year-olds who like stories where things escalate to ridiculous proportions will love this. It's not a serious dental-prep book. It's a way to make teeth funny, which is its own kind of useful.

**"Going to the Dentist" by Anne Civardi** — The detail level in this Usborne book works particularly well at five. Five-year-olds can process and remember more procedural information than younger kids, and walking through the exact sequence of what happens — with labeled illustrations of the equipment — gives them genuine knowledge going in rather than just a vague sense of what dentist visits feel like. Knowledge reduces fear. This is just true.

**"Brave Irene" by William Steig** — Not about dentists. But I will argue until my last breath that William Steig's story of a girl who braves a snowstorm to deliver a dress is one of the finest books about courage for young children ever written. The cold, the wind, the exhaustion, the determination — it's physically and emotionally real in a way that inspires something genuine. Read it the week before any hard thing your five-year-old has to do. The courage transfers.

**"The Night Before the Dentist" by various** — This one leans into the anxiety in a gentle, funny way rather than rushing past it. The character can't sleep, runs through all their dental worries, imagines various scenarios. Five-year-olds who lie awake before appointments (they do this, it's incredibly common and mildly heartbreaking) will find enormous relief in a character doing the same thing. You're not weird. Lots of kids feel this way. The story helps.

**"No More Cavities! Healthy Dental Habits for Kids" — there are several books with this general title but the best functional ones are from the Rookie Read-About Health series.** At five, information itself is calming. Knowing *why* you get cavities, *why* you need to go to the dentist, *why* brushing matters — these reasons satisfy the "but WHY" instinct of a five-year-old in a way that "because I said so" never will.

## The reading-together approach at five

Five-year-olds can handle real conversation during book reading. Use the characters as proxies for talking about feelings that might be harder to claim directly.

"Do you think Arthur is nervous?" Your kid says yes. "What do you think would help him feel better?" Your kid answers. "Do you ever feel like Arthur?" Now you're in a real conversation, and it opened through a character instead of a direct interrogation of your child's feelings.

This is actually a therapy technique. It's called bibliotherapy and it's used with adults too. Stories create safe distance from which to examine difficult feelings. You don't have to name it that or explain it. You just have to use it.

Also at five: let them ask questions mid-book. Don't rush through. If they want to know why the X-ray machine takes pictures of teeth, stop and explain it. ("It can see through your tooth to make sure there's nothing wrong on the inside, like checking if an apple is okay inside even though it looks fine outside.") Good analogies work. Use them freely.

## Something worth doing the day before

Write it down together. Get paper, let your kid draw the dental chair. Label the tools (you can look them up together — it takes two minutes). Write "I am brave" somewhere on the paper. Hang it on the fridge.

This is not precious or precious-sounding when you're actually doing it with a five-year-old. It's a way of externalizing the prep work so it exists in the world rather than just in their head. And five-year-olds who feel ownership over their preparation — rather than just having prep done *to* them — go into the appointment with slightly more agency. Agency reduces fear. Always.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what every book on this list is missing: your child's name, your dentist's name, and the specific detail that your kid's waiting room has a fish tank (or doesn't, but you get the idea).

Plubis makes personalized picture books. You input the situation — dentist visit — your child's name, their age, any specific details you want included. You get back a picture book where your five-year-old is the protagonist, going through a version of what they're actually going to experience, and coming out the other side.

At five, this isn't just about name recognition anymore. It's about the story feeling *true* — specific to them, not generic. And stories that feel true make better emotional preparation than stories that feel like they're about someone else.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about a parent being deployed
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through military deployment. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
At five, your child knows enough to be scared and not quite enough to be reassured by the truth.

They know "far away" means something real now. They understand that the country on the news might be the same country their parent is in. They might have heard something at school. They know what a funeral is, at least approximately. And they have no reliable framework for assessing risk, so their brain fills in the gaps with the worst version of whatever they can imagine.

And then they come to you and ask: "Is Daddy going to die?"

There is no right answer to that question. There's just what you say next, and how you hold them while you say it, and whether they feel like they're allowed to be scared without making it worse for you.

This is where books help. Not by answering the unanswerable questions — but by sitting in the uncertainty with your child and showing them that people survive this. Families hold together. The love doesn't break.

## The five-year-old brain during deployment

Five is the age of beginning to understand cause and effect. Which means it's also the age of magical thinking — if I'm good enough, maybe Mama will come home sooner. If I made a wish on the right star. If I don't step on cracks.

This is normal. It doesn't mean something is wrong with your child. But it's worth knowing, because some of their behavior during deployment may be attempts to control an uncontrollable situation through effort or goodness or ritual.

Some things that characterize this age during deployment:
- **School anxiety.** The at-home parent is now the only parent present, and some 5-year-olds become reluctant to be separated from them at school drop-off.
- **Fear of the news.** Kids at this age often catch fragments of news reports and don't have context to process what they hear.
- **New interest in maps and distance.** Where is Daddy? Show me on the map. How far is that? How many sleeps?
- **An emerging need to talk, not just be read to.** At five, the book is often the starting point, not the ending point. What they really need is the conversation the book opens.

## Book picks for 5-year-olds

**"When Dad's at Sea" by Mindy Pelton** — A 180-day paper chain. One link per day. Emily removes one every morning, watching the pile of torn links grow while the chain on the wall shrinks. For a 5-year-old who now has some grasp of numbers and calendars, this book is deeply satisfying — because it gives the deployment a visible shape that gets smaller. It's not just "a long time." It's a specific, countable, shrinking time. Make the chain. A 5-year-old can help count out the links.

**"Night Catch" by Brenda Ehrmantraut** — The North Star ritual is, if anything, more powerful at five than at earlier ages. A 5-year-old can look out the window, actually find the star, and have a real thought about their parent looking at the same point in the sky. They can hold that thought. They can fall asleep in the middle of it. This book gives families a deployment ritual that doesn't require any equipment and works anywhere in the world at any time of night. Few books have a more practical and lasting gift.

**"Lily Hates Goodbyes" by Jerilyn Marler** — Still essential at five. The anger is still real. Lily being mad and not wanting to say goodbye — even when she knows she has to — is something a 5-year-old recognizes in their gut. What this book does for a slightly older child is add a layer: they can now begin to understand that Lily's mom is also sad, also holding it together while something hard is happening. The empathy angle opens up at five in a way it didn't quite at three or four.

**"While You Are Away" by Eileen Spinelli** — Three children wondering what their deployed parent is doing right now. At five, your child has started school, has had experiences away from the parent, knows what it feels like to want to share something with someone who isn't there. "I saw a really cool bug today and I wanted to tell Daddy" — Spinelli's book validates that specific kind of missing. Not the dramatic anguish. The small daily one.

**"Hero Dad" by Melinda Hardin** — At five, the "my dad is a superhero" framing lands differently. Five-year-olds are starting to have opinions about superheroes — they know the fictional ones, they know the real ones are different. This book sits in the space between those two things. The parent is doing real, hard, dangerous work that requires courage. That's worth naming. Five-year-olds can hold the complexity: I'm proud of my parent AND I want them home AND those feelings are both completely true.

**"I Miss You!: A Military Kid's Book About Deployment" by Beth Andrews** — Andrews is a social worker who counseled military families, and this book functions partly as a coloring workbook and partly as a picture book — it actively asks the child to draw their own feelings alongside the story. For a 5-year-old who is starting to have complex emotional vocabulary but still processes better through doing than talking, this is unusually useful. The accompanying parents' guide is also excellent — one of the better resources for the adult who is trying to hold everything together while also being the only parent physically present.

**"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn** — Read it again at five, but this time with a conversation attached. After you finish the book: "We could do this. Do you want to?" Press a kiss into your child's palm. Tell them they can touch it whenever they miss the deployed parent. Then ask them: "Do you want to give me a kissing hand too?" A 5-year-old who participates in creating the ritual is building a coping mechanism with you, not just receiving one. That participation matters.

## What the conversations look like

At five, the books are launching pads. Here's roughly what useful post-reading conversations look like at this age — just to give you a sense of the territory:

**After "Lily Hates Goodbyes":** "Do you ever feel like Lily? What does it feel like in your body when you're that mad?" (Five-year-olds can often locate emotions physically — stomach, throat, chest — before they can name them verbally.)

**After "Night Catch":** "Should we find our star? What do you want to say to Daddy tonight when we look at it?" Let them talk. Don't shape what they say.

**After "When Dad's at Sea":** "How many sleeps do you think we have left?" Then count together. Make it concrete. Make it smaller.

And the question you may get at some point, over one of these books, in a quiet voice: *Is Daddy safe?*

Here's what I'd say — not the only right answer, just one: "Daddy is very careful, and the Army takes care of him, and a lot of people are making sure he's okay. And I know he's thinking about you every single day." Then hold them. The holding is as important as the words.

## A note on the news

Five-year-olds are starting to be aware of news. If there's conflict coverage and your child's parent is deployed to that region, your child may encounter things they can't process — on TV, on your phone, in adult conversations they weren't supposed to hear.

It's worth having a proactive, small conversation before it happens: "You might hear things on the news about where Daddy is. If you hear anything that scares you, come tell me. You don't have to figure it out alone."

You don't have to have better answers. You just have to be the person they come to with the questions.

## Bonus: the deployment kit

Some families build a "deployment kit" for a 5-year-old — a small box that belongs entirely to them, with:
- A photo of the deployed parent
- A small thing that belongs to the parent (a coin, a button from a uniform, nothing sharp obviously)
- The paper chain, rolled up
- A small notebook for drawing feelings
- A recording of the parent reading their favorite book

The box gives them a place to put the missing. Something physical, contained, theirs.

## Make a book made just for your child

At five, your child is starting to understand that books are made by people. They're starting to grasp authorship. And that means a book with their name in it, their family in it, their parent's actual face — lands with a different weight than it did at two or three.

Plubis makes custom picture books in about five minutes. You tell us the details. We build the story around your family. The deployed parent can contribute the words. Your 5-year-old gets a book that is, without question, about them — their house, their name, their person who is away and coming back.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about parents getting divorced
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through divorce. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five-year-olds understand more than we give them credit for. And less than we assume.

They know the word divorce now — probably from school, a friend's family, something overheard. They know it means something permanent is changing. What they don't have yet is the emotional vocabulary or the life experience to understand what that actually feels like long-term. So they're sitting with a big, abstract scary thing and trying to process it with five-year-old tools.

At five, kids are also deeply embedded in the social world. School matters. What their friends think matters. "Will I still go to my same school?" is not a small question — it's about whether their whole world is stable or whether the earthquake is bigger than they thought. A lot of the fear at this age is about secondary losses: the school, the neighborhood, the routine, the normalcy.

There's also a new layer of something like shame. Five is when kids start comparing their family to others. They notice if their family looks different from their friends' families. They may not want to talk about it at school. They might ask you not to mention it.

Books give this age group something they can hold privately. They can revisit the book alone, sitting with what they feel without having to perform it for anyone.

## What to look for

At five, you can go a little deeper. Look for books with:

- **A protagonist the child can genuinely identify with** — similar age, similar fears, not so stylized that the connection gets lost
- **Honest emotional range** — anger is okay to include at this age. Confusion. Missing something specific. Not just a generalized sadness.
- **Practical details** — what the two-home routine actually looks like. Having different stuff in different places. The packing bag. This specificity is grounding.
- **An ending that isn't falsely tidy** — life goes on and it's okay is a better ending than everyone is happy again

## Books I'd recommend

**"Dinosaurs Divorce: A Guide for Changing Families" by Marc Brown and Laurene Krasny Brown** — Genuinely the most thorough book for this age. It covers everything: why divorce happens (in age-appropriate terms), what two homes means, what happens with holidays, what to do if parents say mean things about each other. The dinosaur format makes it approachable. My one note: read it *with* them the first time, not as assigned reading. Some sections hit harder than others and you want to be there for it.

**"It's Not Your Fault, KoKo Bear" by Vicki Lansky** — Still excellent at five, especially for the guilt piece. Five-year-olds are sophisticated enough to have built a whole theory about why they might be responsible for the divorce. KoKo's story dismantles that gently. The parent notes give you prompts for conversation after.

**"Was It the Chocolate Pudding?" by Sandra Levins** — The sibling dynamic in this book works particularly well for five-year-olds. Aaron and his older brother share the experience without it being identical — Aaron feels things the brother doesn't, and vice versa. It validates that two people in the same situation can feel it differently. That's a surprisingly sophisticated idea delivered in a completely accessible way.

**"When My Parents Forgot How to Be Friends" by Jennifer Moore-Mallinos** — This one gets at something five-year-olds particularly struggle with: the tension of loving both parents when the two parents clearly don't love each other right now. The book doesn't pretend the parents like each other. It just holds the child's love for both of them. That honesty is a relief.

**"My Two Families" — look for this at your library** — There are several books with similar titles that deal with two-home family dynamics. Your children's librarian will know which ones are currently in stock and have good recent reviews. Tell them specifically: five-year-old, two homes, processing divorce without either parent being the villain. They'll find you something.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Not specifically about divorce, but about the invisible connection between people who love each other even when they're not physically together. For a five-year-old who is struggling with the separation — sleeping at Dad's when you want Mom, or vice versa — this book helps with that specific pain in a way no other book I've found does.

## Reading it together at this age

Five-year-olds will often pretend they're not affected. They'll look at the book with careful neutrality, like a small journalist. Then three days later they'll bring it up out of nowhere in the car.

That's normal. Don't push for a reaction in the moment.

Do let them lead. If they want to read it again, read it again. If they want to skip a section, that's interesting information but don't force it. If they ask you whether you were sad when the divorce happened — answer them honestly at a five-year-old level. "Yes, it was sad for me too. And I love you so much."

One thing I've heard from parents: don't read these books right before a difficult transition (like drop-off at the other parent's house). Pick a calm, connected moment — after dinner, Sunday morning, whenever your child is least dysregulated. The book needs a little emotional safety around it to land well.

## The school question

At some point your five-year-old might tell a friend, or their teacher might notice something, or someone might say something unkind about their family. Have a simple script ready for them if they want one: "My mom and dad live in different houses now, but they both love me." That's enough. They don't owe anyone more explanation than that.

If school behavior changes — trouble focusing, more aggressive play, tearfulness that's out of character — loop in the teacher. Five-year-old teachers have usually seen this before. They can be allies.

## A bonus thought: what about you

You are also going through something. And you're reading books about it to your child while also living it. That's a lot.

If there's no one asking how you're doing — a friend, a therapist, a sibling — find one. You can't read these books well, in the calm present way your kid needs, if you're running on empty yourself. Taking care of yourself isn't separate from taking care of them. It's the same thing.

## A book built for them

What makes a five-year-old feel truly seen isn't always a story about someone roughly like them. It's a story about *them* — their name, their world, their feelings named specifically.

Plubis builds personalized picture books for situations like this one. You describe the situation in your own words, and we generate a book where your child's name is on every page. It takes about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about going to the doctor
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through doctor visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is the kindergarten year. Or the year before kindergarten, depending on your kid's birthday and your state's cutoff. Either way, it's the year of Big Transitions, and one of the things that lands with unexpected weight is the 5-year-old checkup.

Because your kid knows. They know things now. They remember the shot from age four. They remember the feeling of the blood pressure cuff squeezing their arm. They know the appointment is coming and they have been building a case against it for three weeks.

Five-year-olds are also, genuinely, starting to develop real bravery — not just compliance, but the capacity to do something scary because they've decided to. That's different from earlier years. At two or three, you're mostly managing sensory overwhelm. At five, you're helping a small person choose courage. That's a different kind of conversation, and books are a surprisingly good tool for having it.

The five-year checkup, by the way, often includes several vaccines — the kindergarten booster series in the US. Worth knowing that going in. Worth being honest about with your kid. The books that handle this best, in my opinion, are the ones that show a character feeling scared and getting through it anyway — not by pretending the needle is fine, but by deciding to endure it.

## What 5-year-olds actually need from books about doctors

At this age, the most useful thing a book can do is model the internal experience of being nervous and then deciding to be brave anyway. Not "it's fine, nothing to be scared of" — they won't believe that and it makes them feel weird for being scared. But "I'm nervous, and I can do it anyway." That distinction matters.

They also need specificity. "The shot lasts about two seconds" is more useful than "it'll be over fast." Books that show the exact sequence — even the uncomfortable parts — give a 5-year-old the mental scaffolding to walk through the experience step by step instead of facing a wall of dread.

And honestly? Some 5-year-olds just want to be seen in their fear. A book that admits the character really didn't want to be there and it was genuinely hard can be more comforting than a book where everything goes smoothly.

## Book picks

**"The Berenstain Bears Go to the Doctor" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — This is the book I'd push hardest for 5-year-olds specifically because of the shot. Most books for this age bracket skirt around it or skip it entirely. Berenstain doesn't. Brother Bear and Sister Bear get their booster shots. It happens. They handle it. The fact that Papa Bear is also secretly nervous is, I think, one of the most honest things in the whole genre — a child is allowed to see that even adults have complicated feelings about this, and they get through it too. Random House, 1981. The book has been in print for forty-plus years. There's a reason.

**"Franklin Goes to the Hospital" by Paulette Bourgeois, illustrated by Brenda Clark** — At five, kids can really inhabit the emotional complexity of this book. Franklin is terrified and pretending not to be. His friends visit. Dr. Bear turns out to be kind. The arc from secret fear to public vulnerability to successful recovery is real emotional work for a 5-year-old to do alongside a character. If your child is facing anything more than a routine checkup — a procedure, a blood draw, a specialist visit — this is the book. Scholastic, 2000. Ages 4–8.

**"Curious George Goes to the Hospital" by Margret and H.A. Rey** — At five, kids can appreciate the comedic premise (he swallowed a puzzle piece, obviously) in a way younger kids can't quite. The hospital is presented as huge and slightly overwhelming but navigable, full of interesting people and things. George's pure curiosity is the emotional model: even in an unfamiliar medical setting, there's stuff worth paying attention to. That reframe from dread to curiosity is a genuinely useful tool. The book was written in collaboration with Boston Children's Hospital and it shows in the care of the medical details. Houghton Mifflin, 1966.

**"Going to the Doctor" by Anne Civardi, illustrated by Stephen Cartwright** — By five, children can read this book almost independently and that matters. They can pore over the illustrations on their own. They can find the duck. They can read the simple captions. A book they can partially self-deploy — pulling it out when they're nervous, looking through it themselves — is valuable at this age. Usborne. Still in print. Worth having on the shelf.

**"It's Time for Your Checkup" by Shani Thornton** — Written by a certified child life specialist, this book earns its spot because it goes further than the others: it explicitly teaches coping skills. It shows deep breathing. It shows looking away. It shows asking for a parent's hand to squeeze. For a 5-year-old who has a specific, identified fear (the shot is almost always the shot), having practiced the coping strategy before the appointment is meaningfully different from just knowing the strategy exists. Ages 4+. I'd pair this one with the Berenstain Bears — one for narrative, one for tools.

**"Froggy Goes to the Doctor" by Jonathan London, illustrated by Frank Remkiewicz** — Yes, still on the list at five. Because a 5-year-old who has loved Froggy for two years now has a deep relationship with this book and the comfort it carries. Don't abandon it just because they're almost in kindergarten. The laugh-out-loud moments hit harder at five because their sense of humor has sharpened. The underpants scene — I cannot stress this enough — continues to delight. Viking, 2002.

**"My Doctor" by Harlow Rockwell** — The quiet one. At five, a book that treats the doctor's office as interesting rather than frightening — where the child narrator is genuinely curious about the blood pressure cuff and the tongue depressor — can be a useful reframe. It's not about fear. It's about curiosity. That shift in perspective is available to 5-year-olds in a way it isn't for 2-year-olds. A good one to read a week before the appointment, when there's no urgency and no fear yet, just orientation. Macmillan, 1973.

## A bonus: after the visit

Don't skip the post-appointment debrief. Seriously. Five-year-olds are actively updating their model of the world, and a five-minute conversation in the car on the way home does more for next year's appointment than any book.

"What was the hardest part?" "Was anything easier than you thought?" "What would you tell a little kid who'd never been to the doctor before?" That last one is gold. When they coach an imaginary younger child, they're consolidating their own experience into something useful. It's not therapy. It's just a question. But it works.

## Reading together

Five-year-olds can handle chapter-by-chapter processing — not just the story but the meaning. After the checkup scene in any of these books, ask: "Do you think the character was brave? What makes something brave?"

You will get an interesting answer. Usually something like "brave is when you're scared and you do it anyway." That definition, which they've often arrived at on their own, is exactly right. And a 5-year-old who has articulated that definition once, out loud, in a low-stakes context, is more likely to reach for it when the needle comes out.

For the scared-of-shots conversation specifically: I've found that being matter-of-fact about the physical experience helps more than reassurance. "The needle goes in, it hurts for about two seconds, then it's done and we go get a snack." Concrete. Specific. True. That's more comforting to a 5-year-old than "don't worry, it'll be fine," which they have learned not to fully trust.

## When a book isn't enough — conversation starters

- "What are you most worried about at the doctor?" (At five, they can usually tell you.)
- "What helped last time?" (Building on their own previous success.)
- "The doctor is going to give you some vaccines. Want me to tell you exactly what that's like, step by step?" (Always yes. Always.)
- "You can hold my hand the whole time if you want." (This is still true at five. Maybe especially at five.)

## Make a book made for your child

Think about what none of these books can do: they cannot put your kid's name on the cover. They cannot know that your kid is specifically scared of the blood pressure cuff, not the needle. They cannot feature your kid's comfort object by name or know that your doctor's office has the fish tank your kid loves and the scale they hate.

At [Plubis](https://plubis.app), you can make that book in about five minutes. A personalized picture book about your child's doctor visit, built from a few questions you answer about your specific kid. Print it or save it on your phone. Read it the night before. Let them hold it in the waiting room.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about the first day of school
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through first day of school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Kindergarten. The real one. The one that matters, the one they'll maybe remember, the one where you take the photo with the backpack that's bigger than they are and try not to cry in front of them.

Five is a complicated age for this. They're big enough to have real fear — not just stranger-danger instinct but actual anticipatory anxiety. "What if nobody likes me?" is a real question a five-year-old can ask. And feel deeply. And lie awake turning over at 2am. And then ask again at breakfast.

Books can help here in a way they genuinely can't at two or three — because a five-year-old can sit with a story, carry it around in their head, pull it out when they need it during a hard moment at school.

## What five-year-olds can handle (and what to look for)

At five, kids can hold complexity. A character who's excited AND scared at the same time — not one or the other. A story that doesn't fully resolve into "everything is perfect" but still feels okay at the end. They can remember a book you read two weeks ago and apply it to a situation at school.

They're also starting to care enormously about peer belonging — am I like the other kids? Will someone want to be my friend? Will anyone sit with me at lunch? Books that take that seriously — without promising it'll be easy or that it'll resolve on day one — are worth far more than books that just say "school is fun!"

Paperback is fine now. Their hands are steadier. The spine of a well-loved picture book becomes a feature, not a bug — the kind that breaks open to the same page every time because they've asked for it so many nights.

## The books worth reading

**"Chrysanthemum" by Kevin Henkes** — A five-year-old will get every single thing happening in this book. Chrysanthemum loves her long unusual name until kids at school make fun of it — and then a teacher changes everything. It's about belonging, about being different, about adults who see you. The moment the music teacher reveals her own long name and the classroom dynamics flip is one of the best things in picture book literature. Best on this list for the kid who's anxious about fitting in or standing out.

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — This one is stunning. It's about all the ways a child might feel different at school — their food, their summer, their family — and how you find the courage to share your story anyway. Woodson writes it like poetry, literally. The illustrations by Rafael Lopez are electric. The message isn't "your difference doesn't matter" (patronizing) or "everyone is the same deep down" (false). It's "your story has weight and it takes bravery to say it out loud." Read this slowly. It holds up to slow.

**"Ish" by Peter H. Reynolds** — Not a school-first-day book, but include it the week before. It's about what school can squash if you're not careful: creative confidence. Ramon stops drawing because someone laughs at his drawing. Then his sister shows him a way back. Five-year-olds heading into kindergarten need to hear "your version of things is enough" before they hit the first standardized worksheet. The concept of "ish" — close enough, real enough, yours — is something they'll carry.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — A boy has an enemy. His dad has a plan involving pie. The solution requires spending a whole day with the enemy. The twist is exactly what you'd hope. This is the book for the kid who's already decided that someone at school is going to be terrible. Or who's afraid of being the kid who's excluded. Beautifully illustrated, genuinely clever, funny in the right places.

**"Too Shy for Show and Tell" by Beth Bracken** — Sam doesn't want to do show and tell. At all. The arc isn't "Sam magically overcomes shyness" — it's "Sam finds a small brave step." For the introverted five-year-old who's already catastrophizing about performing in front of a class, this book is extremely seen. (Might be harder to find in stock — worth checking your library first.)

**"Wemberly Worried" by Kevin Henkes** — I keep recommending this one across every age and it keeps being right. At five, your kid will recognize Wemberly's specific worrying style — the way she worries about things that are real and things that are made up with equal intensity. The ending — finding Jewel, another nervous kid — is not a miracle. It's a Tuesday. That's exactly the right scale for kindergarten expectations: not "everything will be amazing" but "you'll find your person."

**"First Day Jitters" by Julie Danneberg** — The twist lands well at five (I won't spoil it but it's the whole point of the book). I'd actually recommend reading this one AFTER the first day rather than before — it reframes the fear in a way that hits harder once they've had the experience. Or read it before and watch them try to figure out what's coming. Both work.

## The conversation the books open

Five-year-olds will tell you things during a book that they won't tell you face to face. Something about being side by side looking at pages together, not making eye contact. "That's how I feel" comes out in a whisper over a picture book in a way it doesn't when you ask directly "are you nervous about school?"

So: ask about the characters, not about your kid. "I wonder if Wemberly will find someone to sit with at lunch." Pause. Let them fill the silence. Often they fill it with something about themselves.

Also: stop reading before they ask you to. Leave them wanting the ending. A book that gets saved for tomorrow feels precious, not like homework.

## The night-before panic read

If you need one book, tonight, the night before kindergarten starts, and you haven't done any of this: **"The Kissing Hand" by Audrey Penn**. Press a kiss into their palm. Tell them it'll be there whenever they need it. That's it. Whatever happens tomorrow, they have that ritual.

## What not to promise

Don't tell them they'll definitely make a best friend on day one. Don't promise the teacher will be nice. Don't say school will definitely be fun. "I hope it goes well AND I'll be there to hear about it" is more honest and more useful than any guarantee.

## When a book isn't enough

Kindergarten transition can surface real anxiety in five-year-olds — the kind that doesn't ease up in the first few weeks. Stomachaches every morning. Refusal that escalates. Not eating at school. If you're seeing this after two or three weeks, talk to the teacher (good kindergarten teachers have seen everything and want to help) and then to your pediatrician. Early childhood counselors specialize in school transitions. Books are a great way to open a conversation — with your kid and with the professionals in their corner.

## Make a book made just for your child

Your five-year-old is ready for a book where the main character has their name, their nervousness, and their specific story. Not a llama. Not a raccoon. Them. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — you put in your child's name and situation, and we generate the whole thing. For a kid heading into kindergarten, reading a story about themselves being brave hits differently than reading about Chrysanthemum. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about losing a grandparent
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a grandparent dying. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five-year-olds understand death.

Not completely — not the way adults do after years of losing people. But they understand *enough*. They know it's permanent. They know it happened to someone they loved. And they know something has changed in the world that can't be changed back.

That's a lot to carry in a small body.

I've been thinking about what it actually feels like to sit across from a five-year-old who is grieving. There's this specific look they get — not crying, exactly. More like stunned. Like they're holding something too heavy and trying to figure out where to put it down. They'll be completely fine at lunch and then fall apart because you used the wrong cup. The wrong cup doesn't matter — but the grief found an exit.

Books help at five in a different way than they help at two or three. Five-year-olds can follow a story. They can talk about characters. They can ask "why did that happen?" about a fictional bear and actually be asking about their grandfather. The distance a story provides is protective. You can talk about it *through* the story in a way that feels safer than talking about it head-on.

That's what to look for.

## What grief looks like at five

Five-year-olds are entering school (or close to it). They're starting to compare their world to their peers' worlds. One of the harder things about grief at this age is that other kids don't always know what happened, or they say clumsy things, or your child has to explain it at show-and-tell and doesn't know how.

Things to watch for: school avoidance (separation from you feels more frightening after a death — you left once, Grandma left once, what if you don't come back?), sudden intense questions about bodies and what it feels like to die, being "fine" (five-year-olds can compartmentalize — they seem okay and then they're not, and both the fine and the not-fine are real), and asking to call or visit the person even knowing they died.

They need honesty. Consistent, patient, repeated honesty. And they need to feel like their feelings aren't too big for you to hold.

## Book picks for five-year-olds

**"Badger's Parting Gifts" by Susan Varley** — I'll say it plainly: this might be the best grief picture book ever written. Badger is old and knows he's going to die. He says goodbye gently. His friends are heartbroken. Then, slowly, they remember all the things he taught them — the gifts he left behind by being himself. It's quiet, warm, and so true. By five, kids fully feel this one. So will you.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At five, the concept of love continuing after death starts to be something they can really hold. This book gives them the language and the image for it. Worth re-reading even if you already have it.

**"Lifetimes" by Bryan Mellonie and Robert Ingpen** — By five they understand the full message. Revisit this one. The conversation it opens about the natural arc of all living things is different now than it was at three.

**"When Dinosaurs Die" by Laurie Krasny Brown** — Still incredibly useful at five. Now they can read the whole thing with you. Every question they might have is in here somewhere.

**"Grandfather's Journey" by Allen Say** — A quiet, beautiful meditation on loving people across distance and time. There's a line near the end — "the moment I am in one place, I am homesick for the other" — that five-year-olds who've lost someone feel deeply, even if they can't explain why. This is the book that makes them quiet and thoughtful. That's exactly what you want.

**"Cry, Heart, But Never Break" by Glenn Ringtved** — Four children. A grandmother who is dying. Death comes to her door as a character. This is not a scary book — it's lyrical and loving. Death explains why sadness has to exist. It's one of the most honest grief books I've found, and at five kids are ready for it. The illustrations are dark and beautiful. Strong recommendation.

## Reading together — what actually works at five

At five, they can discuss. Use that.

After reading, try sitting quietly for a minute before asking anything. Let the book settle. Then try: "How do you think Badger's friends felt when he died?" or "Does that remind you of anything?" or "I feel sad sometimes when I think about Grandpa. Do you?"

They might not respond to the question. They might pick up the book again. They might say something completely unrelated. All of that is them processing. Keep showing up.

One more thing: let them pick the book sometimes. When a five-year-old keeps picking the same grief book every night for two weeks, that's them doing important work. Don't redirect them to something lighter. Stay with them in the heavy book.

## The funeral conversation — because you might need it

At five, kids are often brought to funerals for the first time. This is actually a gift if handled well. Seeing adults grieve together, seeing a community gather around loss, watching the rituals — it gives structure to something that otherwise feels formless and terrifying.

Prepare them beforehand. "There will be a lot of adults who are sad. Some might cry. That's okay." "We're going to say goodbye to Grandma's body. Her body is there but she isn't feeling anything anymore." "You can ask me anything you're wondering. There are no wrong questions."

And then follow their lead. If they want to hold your hand the whole time, hold their hand. If they want to run around with their cousins after, let them. Both are healthy.

## When a book isn't enough — conversation starters

"What do you remember about him?" "What do you miss most?" "She can't come back, but we can always talk about her. What's one thing you want me to know about how you're feeling?"

Let silence happen. Five-year-olds sometimes need a long pause before the real answer comes.

## Make a book made for your child

What strikes me about every book on this list is that they're universal. They have to be — they were written for any child, not for *your* child.

But your five-year-old's grief isn't universal. It's specific. It's about the way their grandfather laughed, or the fact that they never got to say goodbye, or how Grandma always let them stay up late when they visited. None of the books above capture that.

Plubis generates a picture book for the specific situation your child is in. You share a few details. Five minutes. A book that speaks directly to their experience — their loss, their feelings, their world.

Download it as a PDF. Read it tonight.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about the loss of a pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through losing a pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
There's a specific kind of five-year-old grief that nobody warns you about. They're fine for three days and then they come home from kindergarten, see the empty dog bed in the corner, and completely fall apart. Not because they forgot. Because they remembered.

At five, kids understand enough to know that death is permanent. That understanding is new. It's heavy. And they need help carrying it.

## The five-year-old and permanence

This is the age where "forever" starts to land. A two-year-old doesn't really know what forever means. A five-year-old is starting to. And "the pet is not coming back forever" is genuinely hard information to sit with.

Five-year-olds can also be very private about grief. Some will cry freely. Others will go quiet and process internally, and you won't know how much they're carrying until it surfaces sideways — in nightmares, in clinginess, in suddenly not wanting to be alone. The books on this list are good entry points into conversations they might not start on their own.

They're ready for more complex narratives now. They can follow a story with a real arc. They can sit with ambiguity — you don't have to wrap everything up neatly.

## Books worth putting in front of a five-year-old

**"The Tenth Good Thing About Barney" by Judith Viorst, illustrated by Erik Blegvad**

This is the best book on this list for five-year-olds. Barney the cat has died, and a boy is trying to think of ten good things about him for a backyard funeral. He can only come up with nine. Later, he figures out the tenth: Barney is in the ground, helping things grow. It's gentle, secular, honest, and it gives a grieving five-year-old something active to do — thinking of their own "good things" about their pet. Don't skip this one.

**"The Rough Patch" by Brian Lies**

A Caldecott Honor book and worth every word of that praise. When Evan's dog dies, he tears apart the prize garden they worked on together. He wants to destroy beautiful things because the most beautiful thing in his life is gone. Then slowly — without any prodding — something starts growing again. It's not a book about moving on. It's a book about grief happening at its own pace. For a five-year-old who's gone quiet or angry, this one can feel like permission. "You can be like Evan for a while. It's okay." The illustrations are stunning.

**"An Ofrenda for Perro" by Judith Valdés B., illustrated by Carlos Vélez Aguilera**

This one came out more recently and it deserves more attention than it gets. Benito's dog Perro dies unexpectedly, and during Día de los Muertos, Benito builds an ofrenda — an altar with marigolds, photographs, and objects Perro loved. It's the most culturally specific book on this list and also the most beautiful in terms of what grief can look like as a ritual, not just a feeling. Even if your family doesn't celebrate Día de los Muertos, the concept — making a small space to honor someone who died, to say "you mattered and I remember you" — is available to everyone. Five-year-olds respond to rituals. They're old enough to make their own version. Try your library first.

**"The Invisible Leash" by Patrice Karst, illustrated by Joanne Lew-Vriethoff**

The "Invisible String" concept works especially well at five because kids this age are good at holding abstract ideas. The leash connecting your heart to your pet's — even after death — is the kind of image a five-year-old will return to unprompted. One parent told me her daughter started "tugging the leash" at random times during the day, just to feel it. That's the book doing exactly what it's supposed to do.

**"Henry and Bea" by Jessixa Bagley**

A quieter book than the others. Henry has lost his cat and gone deeply inward, and his friend Bea finds him in a barn and just... sits with him. Doesn't try to fix it. Doesn't make it okay. Just stays. For a five-year-old who's in kindergarten and navigating how to be a friend to someone who's sad, this book is incredibly valuable. It also models something for the grieving child: it's okay to be sad with people who love you. You don't have to perform being fine.

**"I'll Always Love You" by Hans Wilhelm**

Still worth it at five, even though it skews younger. The core message — that the love you say out loud matters — lands differently for a five-year-old who might be feeling like they didn't say enough goodbyes to their pet. "I told our cat I loved her" is something a five-year-old might need to say, or feel guilty about not having said. This book opens that door gently.

**"Dog Heaven" by Cynthia Rylant**

If it's a dog — especially a dog — this is worth keeping around. Five-year-olds are old enough to know heaven is a concept and not a literal place, but young enough to still find the image comforting. A field where dogs run, God scratching behind their ears, children who need a dog to love. It's imagination as comfort. Rylant walks that line well. Cat family? Get **"Cat Heaven"** instead — same book, different animal.

## Reading together when they're five

At five, you can ask real questions after a book. Not a quiz — questions that open things up. "What do you think Evan was feeling when he pulled up all the flowers?" or "which of Barney's good things did you like best?"

And if they turn it back on you — "what would be good things about our cat?" — answer it. Out loud. With real memories. "She always slept on my feet. She made that weird chirping noise at birds." Let them contribute. Let it become a conversation.

That's grief done right at five. Not silent and alone, but named, out loud, with someone.

## When a book isn't enough

Five-year-olds who've lost a pet sometimes also get anxious about separation from you. The pet was a constant, and now it's gone — so what else might disappear? That's not irrational. It's logical. Reassurance without dismissal: "I'm not going anywhere. I'm here." Repeat as needed.

## Make a book made for your child

None of the books above can name your child's pet. None of them capture the specific thing that made your dog or cat or hamster *theirs*. A book made just for your child — with their name, their pet's name, the feelings that are specific to this loss — is a different kind of thing entirely. At Plubis, you can make that book in about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about being more independent
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through independence and big-kid skills. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is the year everything escalates.

Not just the independence — the *stakes* of the independence. They're starting kindergarten or already there. They're navigating a classroom without you. They're expected to manage their belongings, raise their hand, handle conflict with other five-year-olds on the playground without a parent standing three feet away ready to intervene.

And at home? They want to make their own sandwich. They want to choose their own outfit (yes, the one with the stripes and the stripes). They want to walk to the neighbour's house by themselves. They want to be trusted.

The gap between what they want to do and what you feel okay letting them do is never wider than at five. You know they're capable of more than you're letting them do. You also know what happened last time they were unsupervised near the craft supplies.

Books for this age have to do something harder than just cheer independence on. They have to acknowledge that independence can be frightening, messy, and sometimes badly wrong — and still be worth it.


## *Amazing Grace* by Mary Hoffman, illustrated by Caroline Binch

Grace is the kind of five-year-old who eats life whole. She acts out every story she knows. She assigns herself the starring role in everything. When her class votes to perform Peter Pan and she wants to be Peter, she's told she can't be — because of who she is, not what she can do.

And she does it anyway.

For five-year-olds who are just starting to bump into the world's opinions about what they're allowed to want — this book is load-bearing. Five is exactly the age where kids begin absorbing social scripts about who gets to do what. Grace says: the scripts are wrong, here is what you do about that.

The book doesn't pretend it's easy. Grace has to practise, have her confidence rebuilt by her grandmother and mother, and then prove herself at the audition. The independence is earned, not declared.

Mary Hoffman wrote this in 1991. It was a New York Times bestseller, a Reading Rainbow selection, and a Kate Greenaway Medal finalist. Over a million copies later, it still does what it was designed to do.

Give this to any five-year-old who's been told — by another kid, by a system, by an implicit message — that something isn't for them.


## *Ish* by Peter H. Reynolds

Ramon loves to draw until his brother laughs at one of his drawings. After that he can't draw anymore — because the drawings don't look right, don't look like things, don't look like how they're supposed to look.

His sister saves him. She's been collecting his crumpled-up rejected drawings and displaying them on her wall. She tells him that his drawing of a vase isn't a vase — it's vase-*ish*. And ish is fine. Ish might actually be better.

This is the book for five-year-olds who have started worrying about being wrong. And they have — at five, the self-consciousness kicks in. They compare themselves to other kids. They care how their drawings look. They start editing themselves before they've even finished the attempt.

*Ish* gives them a framework for that: "ish" is good enough. "Ish" is something. "I can't do it right" is not the same as "I can't do it."

Peter Reynolds published this in 2004 as the second in his Creatrilogy (with *The Dot* and *Sky Color*). Of the three, this one lands hardest for five-year-olds. It speaks specifically to the fear of imperfection that starts to crystallise at this age.

Bonus: this is a genuinely beautiful book to look at. The illustrations make a case for loose, imperfect art in a way that the words alone couldn't.


## *Roxaboxen* by Alice McLerran, illustrated by Barbara Cooney

This one is different from everything else on this list, and I want you to notice that.

Roxaboxen is based on a real place — a rocky hill in Arizona where the author's mother and her friends built an entire imaginary town using stones, sticks, and boxes. They had houses, a city hall, a bakery, a graveyard. They made their own rules. They ran their own world.

No adults intervene in this book. No adults even appear much. The kids just... do it. They build something out of nothing and it becomes more real to them than the real town nearby.

For five-year-olds, this is a permission slip. It says: you can make things. You can govern things. You can create rules and spaces and worlds that belong to you. The mess in the backyard with the sticks and rocks? That might be Roxaboxen.

Barbara Cooney illustrated this in her signature style — warm, detailed, golden-lit — and she won two Caldecott Medals in her career, so you know what the pictures are going to do to you.

This is my favourite book on this list. I admit that freely. There's something it does that more "educational" books about independence don't: it shows independence as *creative*, not just competent.


## *Stand Tall, Molly Lou Melon* by Patty Lovell, illustrated by David Catrow

Molly Lou Melon is small. Her teeth buck. Her voice sounds like a bullfrog. None of this bothers her, because her grandmother told her: stand tall, smile big, believe in yourself and the world will believe in you too.

Then she moves to a new school. And there is Ronald Durkin.

Ronald Durkin is a bully. He says the things bullies say. And Molly Lou Melon does not crumble — she demonstrates. She catches the football he said she'd drop. She stacks pennies on her teeth. She sings in music class and it sounds exactly like a bullfrog and everyone laughs and then, slowly, the laughing turns into something else.

The independence this book models isn't about doing things alone — it's about maintaining your sense of yourself in the face of other people's opinions of you. That's a five-year-old problem. Real and specific and daily.

Patty Lovell and David Catrow (whose illustrations are wonderfully exaggerated and expressive) made something genuinely useful here. Not a lecture. A story.

Some five-year-olds will need this before the school year starts. Some will need it in the middle. Some parents will need it too.


## The hard part: letting them actually be independent

Here's the tension nobody fully resolves:

You want your five-year-old to be independent. You read them books about independence. You tell them they're capable. And then they want to cross the street alone and your entire body says absolutely not.

This is not hypocrisy. It's a calibration problem. The question isn't "do I let them be independent" — you already know yes. The question is: *which independence, at what level of risk, with what kind of scaffolding?*

Five-year-olds can manage their own lunchbox, choose their own books, navigate conflict with a friend, decide what they want to wear. These are real, meaningful domains of autonomy. They are not the same as crossing a busy road alone.

Books like *Amazing Grace* and *Roxaboxen* and *Ish* are useful in part because they show independence in low-stakes creative domains first. That's where you build the muscle. The bigger domains come later, and they come faster when the muscle has been exercised.

So: let them pour the milk. Let the milk spill. Hand them a sponge.

That's the whole thing, really.


## A note on the "you can do anything" books

There's a whole category of children's books that basically just tell kids they're amazing and can achieve anything. They're not on this list.

I'm not saying they're bad. I'm saying they're not doing the same work. "You can do anything" is a comfort blanket — warm, and not especially useful. The books on this list show children the *shape* of independence: what it looks like to try, to be told you can't, to be scared, to make something imperfect, to persist anyway.

That's the information five-year-olds actually need. Not encouragement. Evidence.


## A book that's only about them

Five-year-olds are right in the middle of building their personal narrative — the story they tell themselves about who they are. A personalised book where they're the main character, working through the exact independence challenge they're facing right now, lands differently than even the best published book.

Because in every book on this list, however much your child identifies with the character, there's still a small distance. The character isn't *them*. A personalised book closes that distance completely.

**The first one is free.**

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about a friend moving away
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a friend moving away. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is old enough to know that "they moved away" means something permanent and heavy. Young enough to still not have any idea what to do with that.

Your five-year-old might surprise you with how articulate they are about it. "I miss Ava. I feel like something is missing in my chest." Real words. Real description. And then thirty seconds later they're asking for crackers and they seem totally fine.

That's five. They live in emotional flashes — full-intensity feeling, then it passes, then it comes back. You never quite know when the sad is going to show up. Bedtime is common. The drive to school. Random Tuesday afternoons when nothing particular is happening.

The other thing about five: they're starting to understand that other people have inner lives. That their friend who moved away might *also* be sad. That it's hard for both people. That's a meaningful shift, and some of the best books for this age lean into it.

## What lands at age 5

Five-year-olds can handle a story with real emotional complexity. They don't need the sad to be resolved quickly. They can sit with a character who is genuinely grieving, and they'll understand what's happening.

They're also starting kindergarten, or already in it — which adds a layer. Losing a friend *and* navigating a new social environment is a lot. Books that speak to the brave act of staying open to new friendships, without dismissing how much the old one mattered, are valuable.

Avoid books that feel preachy or that have an obvious lesson. Five-year-olds can smell an agenda from across the room and they will resist it on principle. The best books at this age just tell a story well and trust the reader to take what they need.

Bigger feelings, more words, bigger questions. They're ready.

## Book picks

**"Evelyn Del Rey Is Moving Away" by Meg Medina, illustrated by Sonia Sánchez** — Still the best of the bunch. Seriously. Daniela and Evelyn have been mirror-image best friends their whole lives — same building, same games, same everything. The moving day is full of sad and full of ordinary at the same time, because that's how big days actually feel. Meg Medina writes at the level of a five-year-old's genuine emotional experience, not above it or below it. The ending is honest: Evelyn is still Daniela's best friend, even from far away. Not "she made new friends." Not "it got better." Just: we are still this. A five-year-old will understand exactly what that means.

**"Ira Says Goodbye" by Bernard Waber** — Ira's best friend Reggie is moving away. Ira is devastated. Reggie, weirdly, seems fine with it — even excited. And Ira doesn't understand that at all. This is the emotional nuance that makes this book exceptional for five-year-olds: the confusion of watching someone you love seem unbothered by leaving you. That specific hurt. The book resolves it beautifully — turns out Reggie was just masking his own sadness. But getting there is emotionally rich and honest in a way most children's books avoid. Five-year-olds can handle this. They'll recognize the feeling immediately.

**"My Best Friend Moved Away" by Nancy Carlson** — Direct and clear in the best way. A child's best friend, the kid next door, moves away. The child misses everything: the games, the jokes, the easy everyday presence of someone who just got it. Carlson lets the sadness breathe. There are new children on the street, and maybe eventually a new friendship forms, but the book doesn't rush that. It respects the loss. The illustrations are cheerful and specific. A good first read if your child wants something that maps closely to their exact situation.

**"Paper Planes" by Jim Helmore, illustrated by Richard Jones** — Perfect for five because it shows the kids doing something. Making planes, exchanging them, sending them back and forth through the mail as little messengers of love. There's a ritual in it. After reading, a five-year-old will almost certainly want to make and exchange something with their friend — a drawing, a letter, a little folded paper plane of their own. That wanting-to-do-something is important. Give them the outlet. The illustrations are stunningly atmospheric — blues and golds and the particular loneliness of big empty skies.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At five, the concept of an invisible thread connecting people who love each other clicks in a way that's almost visceral. A mother tells her children that even when people are far away — even when they can't see them — the string is still there. You can feel it. Five-year-olds who are concrete thinkers can take this and run with it. "My string goes all the way to Ava's house right now." Yes. Exactly. Keep going. The book has been used by therapists, teachers, and grief counselors for years. There's a reason.

**"Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Peña, illustrated by Christian Robinson** — Not about a friend moving away. But bear with me. CJ keeps asking his nana "why don't we have..." — a car, a phone, a bus that isn't crowded and loud. And his nana keeps showing him what's beautiful in what they already have. For a five-year-old who is fixated on what's missing (my friend is gone, this is terrible, nothing will ever be this good again), this book opens a window. Gently. Without dismissing the feeling. Robinson's illustrations won the Caldecott Honor and they deserve it — they're full of color and texture and life. Try it. It might not land every time. When it does, it really does.

**"In a Jar" by Deborah Marcero** — The watercolor illustrations alone are worth it at five. Llewellyn collects moments — a summer evening, the sound of rain — and shares them in jars. When his friend moves, the jars become letters across distance. Five-year-olds who are imaginative and a little dreamy will love this deeply. It's quiet and beautiful and it asks: what would you collect for someone you love? That's a question worth sitting with.

**"Goodbye, Friend! Hello, Friend!" by Cori Doerrfeld** — Still worth reading at five, especially early in the school year. Practicing all kinds of goodbyes before landing on the big one is the book's structural genius, and five-year-olds who are navigating a new school environment will feel the resonance of that. A book about how everything changes and change doesn't have to mean loss.

## Reading together at five: the conversation to have

Five-year-olds are ready for a real conversation after the book. Not immediately — let it settle. Maybe the next day, at breakfast, you can ask:

"If you could say one more thing to Ava, what would it be?"

Wait. Don't rush it. Don't offer your own answer first.

Sometimes they'll say something so perfect it'll knock the breath out of you. Sometimes they'll say "I don't know." Both are fine. The question itself does work — it invites them to feel the friendship as still active, still present, still something that belongs to them.

Also worth trying: "What do you think she's doing right now, in her new house?" Imagining the friend's life, rather than just sitting in the absence, can shift a five-year-old from stuck-sad to curious-sad. Curious-sad moves. Stuck-sad doesn't.

## The school dimension

If your kid's friend was also their main person at preschool or kindergarten, the loss compounds. It's not just a missing friend — it's potentially walking into a room where the default companion is gone and you have to figure out where you fit.

That's scary. Don't minimize it.

Books about being brave at school, about finding new connections without replacing old ones — **"The Name Jar" by Yangsook Choi** is a good one for this, about a child who moves to a new country and has to build a new social world while holding onto her old identity. It's about the child who *moved*, not the one left behind, but your five-year-old might identify more than you expect.

## Bonus: what to do with the anger

Some five-year-olds don't cry about a friend moving away. They get mad. "I don't care. I don't even like her anymore." That's grief wearing an anger costume, and it's completely valid.

If your kid goes there, **"Grumpy Monkey" by Suzanne Lang** is a funny, irreverent book about a monkey who insists he's not in a bad mood, obviously, he's fine, everything is totally fine. It won't solve the problem but it might give you both something to laugh about while you're waiting for the anger to soften into something more workable.

Anger is a stage. Let it be what it is.

## Make a book made for your child

At five, your child is old enough to participate in the making. Ask them: what was your favorite thing you and Ava did together? What should the book say at the end? What color should Ava's hair be?

At Plubis, you can build a custom picture book about your child's real friendship — using their name, their friend's name, and the story only you know. Five minutes. Made just for them.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about making new friends
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through making friends. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five-year-olds ask questions that make you stare at the ceiling for the next three hours.

"What if nobody wants to sit with me at lunch?" "What if my best friend makes a new best friend?" "What if I forget how to make friends?" That last one — said by a real child, to her real parent, in the car, at 7:43am — is maybe the most heartbreaking thing I've heard about five-year-old social anxiety. Forget how to make friends. Like it's a skill that could just... fall out.

Five is when the stakes feel enormous. Kindergarten means a bigger world, more kids, new social hierarchies they didn't design. Some of those kids will be like them and some won't be and figuring out who's who is a full-time job on top of also learning to read. The social complexity at five is genuinely intense. And because five-year-olds now have enough self-awareness to see themselves from the outside — to wonder what the other kids think of them, to feel the specific shame of being left out — the hard moments land harder than they did at three or four.

But here's the beautiful part: five is also the age of real, deep, reciprocal friendship. The kind where your kid talks about their best friend so much you feel like you know this person personally. The kind where they save things for each other — the good sticker, the interesting rock, the joke they're going to tell. Five-year-old best-friendships are intense and loyal and slightly obsessive and watching one form is genuinely one of the sweeter things about this age.

Books can live in both parts of this: the fear of not having it, and the complicated reality of having it.

## This is what five-year-olds are socially working on

At five, kids are learning fairness. Rules. "She went last time so I go this time." They're learning that friendship requires maintenance — that you can't just claim someone and then ignore them. That kindness done on Tuesday matters on Friday. These lessons come from trial and error and they require a lot of error before they stick.

Five-year-olds are also learning about betrayal for the first time, in the small devastating ways it happens in kindergarten. Someone told your secret. Someone said they'd play with you and then didn't. Someone said "you can't come" and you had to stand there figuring out where to look.

Books that take these experiences seriously — not as learning opportunities but as actual heartbreaking things that happen — are worth more than books that promise it all works out. Sometimes things don't work out by Friday. Sometimes you're eating lunch mostly alone for two weeks before you find your person. Both things are true and your kid deserves books that hold both.

## What to read

**"Frog and Toad Are Friends" by Arnold Lobel** — I have put this on every age list from four onward and I will keep putting it here because it is the best book about friendship in the English language for this age group and possibly for any age group. Read it aloud in chapters at bedtime. Take your time. The chapter where Toad doesn't want to get out of bed in April and Frog tricks him into thinking it's still October — and then Frog feels guilty about the trick — contains more real friendship psychology than most adult novels. At five your kid is ready to feel the full weight of Frog and Toad's dynamic. Let them.

**"Strictly No Elephants" by Lisa Mantchev** — A boy's pet elephant is not allowed in the pet club. He finds another excluded kid. They start their own club: all pets welcome. It's a book about the specific pain of exclusion and the specific joy of starting something new when the existing thing won't have you. For the five-year-old who's been left out of a group and is trying to figure out what to do next — not how to get back in, but what to do with the being-outside — this is the one. Read it more than once. The second read, kids usually catch the expression on the elephant's face at the beginning and understand it differently.

**"Hooway for Wodney Wat" by Helen Lester** — Rodney Rat can't say his R's and gets teased for it until the day a bully shows up and Rodney accidentally saves the whole class through his speech impediment. Funny and triumphant. But here's what I actually like about it for the making-friends topic: it's a book about the thing you're embarrassed about being the thing that ends up mattering. For a five-year-old who's nervous that some difference they have — the way they talk, the food they bring to lunch, the show they watch that nobody else watches — will keep them from belonging, this is quietly reassuring. Your thing is not a liability. It might just be your asset.

**"Jamaica's Find" by Juanita Havill** — Jamaica finds a stuffed dog and a hat at the park. She keeps the dog and turns in the hat and then feels bad about it. What I like about this book for five-year-olds and making friends: it's about moral action creating connection. Jamaica does the right thing, returns the toy, and meets the child who owned it. The friendship comes out of integrity, not strategy. For the five-year-old who's been coached on friend-making tactics (go say hi, ask to play, compliment something), this is the counter-message: just be good and the right people find you. Not always, not guaranteed. But sometimes.

**"Best Friends for Frances" by Russell Hoban** — Frances the badger wants a best friend. Her brother won't play with her. The boy down the street won't let her join the game. Her friend Albert doesn't want to include her in his special no-girls outing. Frances makes her own outing and Albert figures out he wants to be part of it. The message is not "conform until they accept you" but "know your own value and make your own thing and the right people will want to be part of it." Frances is a great character at this age — smart, a little prickly, deeply herself.
