Five-year-olds who have strong personalities and don't understand why that's sometimes a social obstacle will find her.

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — It's about the moment when you realize you might be different from the other kids in your class — your lunch looks different, your summer was different, your family is different — and the bravery it takes to say something about yourself anyway. Woodson doesn't promise it'll go well. She just says: your story is worth telling. The way it's written — more like poetry than narrative — gives it a different texture than other picture books. Read it slowly. Don't rush to the end. Five-year-olds can sit in a beautifully written sentence if you let them.

**"Those Shoes" by Maribeth Boelts** — Jeremy desperately wants the shoes all the cool kids have. He can't afford them. He finds a pair in his size at a thrift store that don't quite fit, forces himself into them, and then ends up giving them to a classmate who needs them more. It's not specifically about making friends but it's about the social economy of belonging — the ways kids try to buy their way in — and the kid who earns real connection not through having the right things but through seeing someone else. Five-year-olds in kindergarten environments where social status starts to attach to objects will recognize this story in their bones.

## The lunch table question

At some point this year your five-year-old is going to tell you something about the lunch table. Where they sat. Who was there. Whether anyone saved them a seat. It's going to feel enormous — to them and to you.

Here's what to ask instead of "did you sit with friends?": "What did you eat? What did the kid next to you eat? What was the funniest thing anyone said?" Get the texture of the experience. The friendship status will come through in the details — you'll know if they were connected or alone from the way they describe the forty-five minutes. You don't have to ask directly. They'll tell you more if you don't.

## Reading together at five: the thing they'll ask you

Five-year-olds are at the exact age where they start asking personal questions during books. "Did you have a best friend when you were little?" "Were you ever left out?" "Did kids ever be mean to you?"

Answer honestly. Not with every detail — but honestly. "Yes. Yes I was. It felt really bad for a while and then things got better." That "I've been there" does something that no reassurance does. It makes them believe the better-things-got part, because they can see that you actually got there.

## Bonus: the friendship that's going to hurt them

At five, some kids form a very close best-friendship that then ends. Suddenly, dramatically, over something that seems tiny from the outside. Your kid might be the one who ends it or the one who gets ended. Either way it's going to feel like a breakup, because it is one.

Frog and Toad is good for the aftermath of this too. The chapter where Frog and Toad have a disagreement — a small one, over nothing — and then Toad sends Frog a letter just to say he values their friendship: that chapter teaches something about repair. About how the small gesture after the fight is the friendship itself. Five-year-olds who have just been through a friend-breakup and are now tentatively back together will feel that chapter in a specific way.

## A book made just for your child

Your five-year-old is old enough to fully understand that a book is about them. Not just identify with a character — actually understand that this story was made for them, specifically. That changes what a book can do. Plubis builds custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. You put in your child's name and situation and we build a story with them at the center — the one who's nervous about lunch, the one who finds their person, the one who does the brave thing. For the kid who asked "what if I forget how to make friends" at 7:43am: a book with their name in it that says you haven't forgotten, and here's what it looks like when you find your person. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about moving to a new home
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through moving house. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Moving at five. It's a lot.

Five-year-olds are right at the edge of really understanding what "moving" means in a way that makes it genuinely hard. They have best friends. Real ones. The kind they've built over years of preschool and playdates and knowing each other's lunch preferences. They know their neighborhood. They have a tree they climb, a park they go to, maybe a corner store where the person knows their name.

And now you're telling them all of that is ending.

The grief that happens at five around a move is real and it deserves to be taken seriously. Not catastrophized, not dismissed. Taken seriously. The books that work at this age are the ones that say "yeah, this is a genuine loss" and then sit with that for a while before moving toward "and you'll also find new things." Not one or the other. Both, in that order.

Five-year-olds are also sophisticated enough to pick up on your stress, your ambivalence, your "I know this is hard" face. They're reading you as much as they're reading the book. So one job the book does is give you both a shared object to look at instead of looking at each other, which is sometimes easier when the feelings are big.

## What five-year-olds can actually handle

At five they can hold real complexity. They understand that two things can be true: sad about leaving AND curious about the new place. They can track a character's emotions through a full arc. They'll remember what you read last week and bring it up this week in a completely different context — mid-dinner, in the car, right before they fall asleep.

They're also in a period of intense social development right now — who is my friend, am I likeable, will I fit in — so the social dimensions of moving hit hard. Not just "I'll miss my friend" but "what if I can't make friends at the new place?" That's a different fear, and a bigger one, and it deserves specific attention.

Longer picture books are fine now. Books that take their time with complexity. Books that trust a five-year-old to hold bittersweet without needing to resolve it immediately.

## Books worth reading before, during, and after

**"Ira Says Goodbye" by Bernard Waber** — This is my top pick for five. Ira's best friend Reggie is moving away. The whole book is their last day together — the mix of wanting to be sad, wanting to be brave, wanting it to not be over yet. It captures the specific feeling of a friendship ending or changing in a way that is almost disarmingly accurate. Five-year-olds who are leaving a best friend, or whose best friend is leaving them, will recognize everything in this book. The ending doesn't tie it up neatly. It says goodbye honestly. But it also leaves room for something — for the friendship to continue in some form, for letters, for visits. That's the right level of honesty for five. Don't rush through the quiet pages near the end.

**"Alexander, Who's Not (Do You Hear Me? I Mean It!) Going to Move" by Judith Viorst** — Still great at five, maybe even better than at four because they can appreciate more of the jokes. Alexander's indignant resistance is funny — deliberately, satirically funny — and a five-year-old can be in on the joke while also completely meaning it. Reading this with a kid who is furious about moving and then both of you laughing at Alexander's list of objections is one of those small parenting wins. The humor doesn't minimize the feeling. It holds it while making it a little more survivable. Read it loud, with full theatrical Alexander energy.

**"The Leaving Morning" by Angela Johnson** — Best on this list for the quiet, emotionally perceptive five-year-old. The morning before the move, captured in this slow, sensory way — sticky fingerprints on the window, the sound of the empty apartment, the weight of the last breakfast in a place. At five they can sit with this kind of beauty-mixed-with-grief and recognize it. If they've been living somewhere long enough to have sensory memories of it — the way the light comes in at a certain time, the smell of the backyard in summer — this book names that specific kind of loss with real precision. Read it before the move. Slowly. More than once.

**"Henry and Mudge and Annie's Good Move" by Cynthia Rylant** — Annie is moving to a new neighborhood and she's nervous. She meets Henry and his very large dog Mudge. A friendship starts. This is one of the Henry and Mudge books, and if your five-year-old already loves the series, this is an easy entry point. Even if they don't know Henry and Mudge yet, the story is warm and accessible. What I like about it for five-year-olds specifically: it's about the other side of the move. Not what you're leaving. What can actually be good in the new place. Not in a fake "everything is great!" way but in a real "here's a specific good thing that happened" way. A dog. A kind neighbor kid. An afternoon that turned out okay. Sometimes you need that story too.

**"The Berenstain Bears' Moving Day" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — Five-year-olds are a little past the prime Berenstain Bears age for some, but this one holds up because the emotional content is exactly right. Small Bear has real doubts. He misses his friends. He's skeptical about the new place. There's a slow adjustment period shown, not a magical fix. If your child read this at three or four and loved it, reading it again at five with more understanding is a genuinely different experience — they'll notice the nuances they didn't before.

**"Goodbye House" by Frank Asch** — The goodbye-to-every-room ritual is even more powerful at five because they can lead it themselves now. Give them the book the night before, take the moving-day walk through the house together, let them decide which rooms to say goodbye to and what they want to say. You might be surprised what comes out. Five-year-olds have a lot to say when they're given a framework for it.

**"A Chair for My Mother" by Vera B. Williams** — At five they can catch everything in this book. The fire, the loss, the jar of coins, the community of neighbors who bring pots and blankets to help start over. It's a story about what you build on the other side of a hard thing, told from a child's perspective with the child's own sense of what mattered and what was beautiful. If your move is tinged with hardship, this book sits with that honestly, without drama or false comfort.

## Reading together at five — what actually works

Five-year-olds will tell you things during books they won't tell you otherwise. Side by side, looking at pages, not making eye contact — that's when "what if I don't make any friends" comes out. Let it. Don't jump immediately to reassurance. Sit in it for a second first. "Yeah. That would feel really hard." Let them say more before you fix anything.

If they ask "did you ever have to leave somewhere you loved?" — and they might — answer honestly. Whatever the honest answer is. They're not asking for a lecture. They're asking if you understand.

Stop reading before they're done if you can — leave a page for tomorrow. Books that feel scarce and precious are ones they'll ask to return to. A five-year-old who asks for the same book three nights in a row is telling you something about where they are emotionally.

## The map project

This isn't a book, but it lives alongside the books. Before you move: draw a map with your child of the neighborhood you're leaving. Label the friends' houses, the park, the corner store, the place they had their birthday party. Make it silly and specific and theirs. Take it with you to the new house. Put it on the wall there.

It's a grief object. It says: this was real, it mattered, and we carry it with us.

Some books help most alongside something concrete like that.

## When a book isn't enough

A five-year-old who's really struggling — persistent sadness that doesn't lift, withdrawal, night fears, real distress about starting school somewhere new — is worth a conversation with your pediatrician. Not because something is broken. Because moves at this age can be genuinely hard and some kids need more than books and reassurance. A play therapist who works with early childhood transitions can make a real difference. Books are a way into the conversation, not the whole conversation.

## Make a book made just for your child

Five-year-olds are old enough to completely understand that a book is about them — and to have that feel like a big deal. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. Your child's name. Their best friend's name. Their specific street, their actual pet, the park they're leaving. Not a story about a bear in the valley. A story about them. Reading something like that the night before a move is different from anything else on this list. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about learning a new language
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a new language. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is a loaded age for language. This is when school gets real — kindergarten, structured reading, phonics, spelling tests eventually — and suddenly the way your child uses language is being evaluated in a formal way for the first time. For kids in multilingual homes, this can be a confusing period. The language of instruction is probably not the language of home. They might be academically ahead in some ways (bilingual children often have strong executive function) but feel behind in vocabulary in the majority language. Other kids might notice the differences.

And then there's the heritage language situation. Five is the age when some kids start to let it slip. The majority language is everywhere — school, TV, friends — and the heritage language starts to feel like effort. If you've watched a five-year-old gradually switch to English-only even at home, you know the specific, low-grade grief that comes with it. You don't want to force it. You also don't want to just watch it disappear.

Books can't stop that process by themselves. But books about language, about having two worlds, about the specific texture of a bilingual life, can make the heritage language feel like something worth keeping. Not as duty. As identity.

## Why this age is actually a great time for these books

Five-year-olds can fully engage with picture books as literature. They'll ask questions that go beyond the plot — "Why did she feel that way?" "Was that fair?" They have opinions. They'll argue with you about a book's ending. They can hold the emotional complexity of a character who feels two contradictory things at once.

For the language topic, that means you can read books that don't wrap everything up neatly. Books where the immigrant experience is genuinely hard. Where language barriers have real costs. Where a character chooses to hold onto something that makes them different, and that choice isn't portrayed as a fairy-tale resolution but just... as a real choice a person makes. Five-year-olds are ready for that kind of honesty.

Also: five is the age of best friends. Who your child's best friend is this year matters a lot to how they see themselves. The communities in the books you read can quietly expand their sense of who counts as a peer, who counts as someone to admire. That's not nothing.

## The picks

**"Dreamers" by Yuyi Morales** — I keep putting this one on these lists and I will keep doing it because at five the full arc of this book becomes available. Morales arrives with her infant son. She speaks no English. The library gives her books in both languages, in all languages, and she learns and then her son grows and the books they loved together became the building blocks of their lives. The ending, where adult Morales and adult son look back at baby Morales and baby Yuyi Jr., is one of the most quietly devastating things in children's literature. At five, your kid will ask you why you're crying. Just say the book is really good.

**"The Name Jar" by Yangsook Choi** — At five, Unhei's choice to keep her Korean name lands completely. They understand the social pressure she's under. They understand what it cost her. And the moment when she chooses herself — when her new friend reveals that he kept her name card the whole time — is satisfying in the way that good picture book endings are satisfying: earned, not cheated. Read it and talk about names. Ask your child what their name means. Tell them where it comes from. That conversation is worth more than the book.

**"Amina's Voice" by Hena Khan** — Amina is Pakistani-American, musical, and navigating a best friendship that's changing as her friend becomes more "American." The language isn't the central theme — it's more about identity and belonging — but the cultural specificity here is wonderful. This is a chapter book, not a picture book, so it's at the outer edge of what a five-year-old can handle as a read-aloud. But for kids who are ready for longer stories, and whose family background is South Asian or Muslim, the representation is rare and real.

**"The Proudest Blue: A Story of Hijab and Family" by Ibtihaj Muhammad and S.K. Ali, illustrated by Hatem Aly** — Faizah's older sister Asiya is wearing hijab for the first time on the first day of school. Some kids are rude. Faizah wants to protect her sister. The story is about the family's culture and faith and how Faizah holds onto her sister's peace rather than taking on other people's smallness. For any five-year-old whose family has a visible cultural or religious difference from the mainstream, this book is practically medicinal. The illustrations are quietly beautiful.

**"Inside Out and Back Again" by Thanhha Lai** — This is a verse novel, not a picture book, but I'm including it because for five-year-olds who are strong listeners, read-aloud novels in verse are surprisingly accessible. Hà is a Vietnamese refugee resettling in Alabama. The language is spare and poetic and her experience of learning English — the humiliations and triumphs — is rendered with such precision. "Nobody here speaks my mother tongue" is a sentence from this book that I haven't been able to forget. For families who have actually gone through immigration and language loss, this one lands differently than almost anything else.

**"Separate Is Never Equal: Sylvia Mendez and Her Family's Fight for Desegregation" by Duncan Tonatiuh** — In 1940s California, Sylvia Mendez was denied admission to a white school because of her Mexican heritage — even though she was a US citizen who spoke perfect English. Her father sued and won, desegregating California schools years before Brown v. Board of Education. At five, kids can fully grasp the injustice here. They can understand why language and culture became tools of exclusion. And they can understand what it looked like when a family refused to accept that. A picture book that reads like a document of something that actually happened, because it did.

**"Mango, Abuela, and Me" by Meg Medina, illustrated by Angela Dominguez** — Abuela comes from Cuba to live with Mia's family. She and Mia don't share a language — Abuela speaks Spanish, Mia speaks English. They teach each other. Mango the parrot helps. This is a gentle, funny, warm book about the specific joy and awkwardness of learning a language with someone you love. For five-year-olds who have grandparents who speak a different language — and who feel that distance as both magical and frustrating — this one is tender and real.

## How to use these conversations well

Five-year-olds are starting to have a sense of their own story. "This is who I am" is forming. So the conversations after these books are worth taking seriously.

Try: "Does anyone in our family feel like Unhei sometimes?" Or: "What language does your heart speak?" (I know that sounds cheesy. Try it anyway. You might be surprised.)

Avoid: "Aren't you lucky to know two languages!" — it shuts down the conversation before it starts. It also doesn't acknowledge the work. Being bilingual isn't only luck and privilege, it's also confusion and in-betweenness and sometimes exhaustion. Acknowledging all of it is more useful.

## The heritage language maintenance question

At five, if you've been doing heritage language at home and it's starting to slip, here's what the research loosely suggests:

Children who feel *warmth* and *identity* attached to their heritage language maintain it better than children who feel *obligation* attached to it. You cannot grammar-police a child into keeping a language. You can invite them into a world where that language is beautiful and alive. Stories, songs, TV, visits, food — the more the language is associated with pleasure and love and real communication (not drills), the better the long-term outcome.

These books are one small part of that. Not the whole strategy. But a real part.

## When a book isn't enough

If your five-year-old is in active distress about their language situation — refusing to go to school because of language barriers, being bullied because of accent or vocabulary, struggling academically in ways that seem related to English proficiency rather than ability — please involve the school's ESL or language support team. Schools have resources for this and they're underused. Push for them.

And if the heritage language is disappearing faster than you're comfortable with: Saturday language schools, heritage language playgroups, summer visits to family — those interventions, even imperfect ones, make a measurable difference. The research on this is consistent. Don't wait for the language to be gone before acting.

## Make a book made for your child

None of these books features your child's name, your family's specific two languages, or the exact version of this that is happening in your house. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books for exactly that — five minutes, your child at the center, the first one free. A book where Nadia is the one who knows how to say "bedtime" in two different ways and both of them mean love — that's a different thing than a book about a fictional child. It's specific. And specific is what sticks.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about getting a new pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a new pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
## Start here: the books that actually land

Five is a good age for this. I'm going to say that clearly before I get into all the chaos, because it's easy to miss: five-year-olds and new pets have a real shot at genuine relationship-building, and it's genuinely wonderful to watch.

But you didn't come here for encouragement. You came here because your kid is oscillating between intense love and total overwhelm, the pet has already done something unexpected and upsetting, and you need books that can do some emotional heavy lifting. Here they are.

**"Because of Winn-Dixie" by Kate DiCamillo** — The gold standard. Opal finds a dog in a grocery store and through that dog, slowly builds a whole life in a new town. DiCamillo writes about loneliness and connection with a precision that five-year-olds feel even before they can name it. If your kid has just gotten a dog, or if a dog has come into your lives unexpectedly, read this together. It will take a few sessions. Do it anyway.

**"The Puppy Place" series by Ellen Miles** — Not literary, not trying to be. These are warm, fast-reading chapter books about a family that fosters dogs temporarily, and each book follows one specific dog with one specific need. Your five-year-old will absolutely get through these on their own with some support, and the series teaches, quietly, that different animals have different histories and different needs. Really useful if your pet came from a shelter.

**"Shiloh" by Phyllis Reynolds Naylor** — This one is heavy. A boy finds an abused dog, hides it, lies about it, does morally questionable things to protect it. The ethical complexity is real. I'm not suggesting you hand this to a five-year-old unprepared. But if you read it together, pausing to talk — why did Marty lie? was it right? what else could he have done? — you get one of the best conversations about loyalty, honesty, and what we owe animals that I've ever seen packaged in a children's book. For a confident reader-listener at five, this is extraordinary.

**"Harriet's Halloween Candy" by Nancy Carlson** — About a girl who eats all her Halloween candy and feels terrible. Wait, wrong book. But actually — the emotional arc is identical to what happens when your five-year-old does something wrong with the new pet and has to sit with the guilt of it. Nancy Carlson's books are underrated.

**"Mr. Popper's Penguins" by Richard and Florence Atwater** — A man receives a penguin, then another penguin, then several penguin eggs, and his entire household becomes a penguin-management operation. It is completely chaotic and completely joyful and five-year-olds find it hilarious. Also a gentle reminder that pets have needs beyond what we imagined when we wanted them. The Poppers did not plan for this. Nobody plans for any of it.

**"Stuart Little" by E.B. White** — Stuart is a mouse who is also somehow a person, and White doesn't apologize for the absurdity. The relationship between Stuart and Snowbell the cat is complicated and real and funny. For a five-year-old with a new cat or small animal, reading about a household navigating a creature that doesn't fit the expected categories is resonant in odd, good ways.

**"The Cricket in Times Square" by George Selden** — Tucker Mouse and Harry Cat befriend a cricket who finds himself in New York. The friendships are strange and tender, the city is alive, and the cricket's eventual choice — to return to what he came from — is genuinely moving without being manipulative. Five-year-olds who are starting to wonder what their pet's life was like before their house will find something here.

**"Charlotte's Web" by E.B. White** — Obviously. But read it now, with a new pet in the house, and the way it lands will surprise you. Wilbur's total trust in Charlotte, the daily intimacy of barn life, the irreversibility of loss — five is the youngest I'd try this one, and only with a kid who can handle hard feelings. But if they can, it's the best thing you'll ever read together.

## What five-year-olds actually need from a new-pet book

Before we go any further, it's worth being clear about what's happening developmentally at five, because it changes what you're looking for.

Five-year-olds have genuine empathy now. Not perfectly deployed, not consistent, but real. They can understand that the dog had a life before them. They can feel sorry for an animal that's scared. They can worry about whether the pet is happy. This is new — at three and four, the concern was mostly "is this fun for ME" — and it opens up an entirely different category of books.

They're also starting to feel responsibility as a real thing. Not just as a chore imposed by adults, but as something they can take pride in. "I'm the one who feeds her" said with genuine ownership. A five-year-old can build an actual caretaking routine if you build it with them, and books that show kid characters doing exactly that are motivating in a way they weren't a year ago.

And they're starting to sit with emotional complexity. They can hold "I love this pet" and "this pet is hard sometimes" at the same time. They can understand that the cat scratching them wasn't mean — it was scared. That's a sophisticated read of a situation, and five-year-olds are capable of it.

So the books you want aren't just "dog comes, kid is happy." They're books with real stakes, real mistakes, real repair.

## Reading together at five: what actually works

Five is the age where reading together can become genuinely collaborative in a way that's new. Your kid is probably reading some words themselves, or close to it. Let them try. If they skip a word or guess wrong, don't correct immediately — wait, see if they self-correct, let them stay in the story.

But the bigger shift at five is in conversation. After you finish a chapter (or a book, depending on length), ask one question. Just one. Not a quiz — a real question you're actually curious about. "Why do you think Marty decided to hide Shiloh instead of telling the truth?" or "What would you do if our dog got lost?" or "Do you think Charlotte knew what was going to happen at the end?"

Five-year-olds will surprise you. They've been paying attention in ways you didn't know. They have opinions. They'll tell you things about the book you didn't notice yourself.

Also: read outside, or in weird places. New pets change the texture of home. Sit on the floor near where the pet sleeps, with the book, and read there. Let the pet be part of the reading environment. It sounds small. It's not.

## The part where things get hard

New pets bring up things you don't always anticipate.

For some five-year-olds, a new pet brings up the concept of death for the first time in a way that feels real. "Will our dog die someday?" asked at 8pm after a week of having a puppy is a question you weren't ready for. (If this happens: be honest, be simple, be present. Don't promise anything you can't keep. This is one of those moments.)

For some kids, a new pet brings up jealousy — the pet is getting attention, and it's hard, and the five-year-old doesn't know how to name that. They might act out near the pet, or become suddenly needy with you. This is not a character flaw. It's a five-year-old experiencing something real without the tools to explain it.

For some, the pet being scared or shy is confusing and upsetting — "why doesn't it like me yet?" is a question worth taking seriously. Books like "Charlotte's Web" and "The Cricket in Times Square" are actually useful here because they show friendship between unlikely parties forming slowly, with patience, over time.

Here are some conversation starters that work:

"I know it's weird when the pet doesn't want to be held. I feel a bit sad about it too sometimes. What do you think would help it trust us?"

"You've been taking really good care of the pet this week. What's been the hardest part?"

"What do you think our pet dreams about?"

That last one will generate content. I promise.

## The thing nobody says about five-year-olds and pets

Your five-year-old is going to love this animal in a way that becomes foundational. Not every kid-pet relationship hits this depth, but many do, and five is prime territory for it. The pet you bring home this year might be the thing your child talks about when they're thirty. The first creature that was theirs. The companion through whatever's coming.

Books are part of how that relationship gets language. How your kid learns to see the animal as a subject with an inner life, not just an object of affection. That's a big thing to build. Worth doing carefully.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what I know about five-year-olds: they want to be the protagonist. They want the story to be about THEM. And right now, they have one of the best stories of their young lives happening in real time — the arrival of a new animal into their world.

A custom book that puts your five-year-old by name into a story about their specific pet, their specific feelings, their specific situation? That's a different category of reading experience. Five-year-olds at this age are starting to understand that stories can be true, and a story that's true about them lands with a weight that fairy tales can't match.

Plubis builds that book in about five minutes. Tell us about your kid, your new pet, what the experience has been like — and we generate a custom illustrated story that makes your child the hero of their own animal adventure. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about starting at a new school
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a new school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five years old is when the social stuff starts to actually hurt.

Not just "I feel weird" but real, articulable pain: "Nobody talked to me at lunch." "They said I couldn't play because it's only for two people." "Olivia has a best friend already and I don't have one."

You thought you were past the hard part — they survived toddler years, they can dress themselves, they sleep mostly. And then you move, or your situation changes, and they have to start a new school in February, and you watch them become five again in the worst way. Clingy, tearful, uncertain.

The mid-year part is what makes this hard in a specific way. Starting school in September, everyone has jitters, the teacher is expecting to do connection work, there are rituals built for this. Starting in February? The class has a culture. There are inside jokes. There are friendships that are seven months deep. Walking into that as the new person is genuinely hard regardless of your age. It's just that at five, you don't have the emotional toolkit to manage it and you also can't describe why it hurts.

Books don't solve this. But they are one of the best tools you have for opening the door to the conversation, and at five, the conversation is where the real help happens.

## Why picture books still work at five

Five-year-olds sometimes act like picture books are beneath them because they have an older sibling or they're "almost in kindergarten" or whatever developmental posturing is happening this week. Ignore this completely. Picture books are not beneath any human at any age. The art form is legitimately good.

At five, your kid can:
- Follow a narrative arc and remember it afterward
- Identify with a character going through something similar to them
- Ask questions about motivation ("why did she do that?")
- Connect what happens in a book to what's happening in their own life
- Hold complexity — they can feel two things at once

That last one is important. A five-year-old can feel excited about the new school AND sad about the old one. They can like their new teacher AND miss their old one. That's not contradiction. That's actually emotionally sophisticated. Books that hold both feelings simultaneously are better for this age than books that tidily resolve everything into happiness.

## The best books for this situation

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Start here. If you buy one book, this is it. Woodson writes about children who start school feeling different — different backgrounds, different experiences, things that didn't happen the same way for them as for everyone else. The invitation the book makes is: share the different thing. Start there. That's how you begin to belong.

What I love about this for the mid-year situation specifically: it doesn't pretend being new is easy. It acknowledges that walking into a room where everyone else already knows each other is hard. And it doesn't give you a magic fix. It gives you a direction: toward honesty, toward sharing yourself, toward beginning. Rafael López's illustrations are extraordinary. Read this one slowly.

**"Stand Tall, Molly Lou Melon" by Patty Lovell** — Molly Lou Melon is small, has buck teeth, has a funny voice, and has always been weird. When she moves to a new town and starts a new school, a bully named Ronald Durkin makes fun of everything about her. She handles it with total self-possession, because her grandmother told her she was wonderful and she believed it.

This book is about what happens when external circumstances (new school, mean kids) meet internal groundedness. Your five-year-old probably doesn't have Molly Lou levels of self-possession — most adults don't — but the book models it so clearly that it's aspirational without being preachy. Also, it's funny. Molly Lou's confidence is genuinely funny to read aloud. Your kid will laugh.

**"The Brand New Kid" by Katie Couric** — Lazlo starts mid-year. This detail matters. The other kids ignore him not out of cruelty exactly but out of not knowing what to do. One girl decides to reach out. The book is told partly from Lazlo's perspective and partly from the class's, which makes it good for discussing both sides: how it feels to be new, and how it might feel to the kid who eventually does say hi.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Two Woodson books on this list. Worth it. "Each Kindness" is about what happens when we don't reach out — the new girl is never fully welcomed, and then she's gone. For a five-year-old who is the new kid, this is useful in a non-obvious way: it shows them they're not invisible. Their presence registered. The other kids notice a new kid even when they don't show it. That's a small but real piece of comfort.

**"Making Friends Is an Art!" by Julia Cook** — More instructional than the others. The main character, Eli, genuinely doesn't know how to make friends and has to learn. Julia Cook's books are more didactic than literary — they're explicit about the lesson rather than embedding it in narrative. Some kids at five really respond to this directness. If your kid is less "read me a story" and more "explain things to me," this one is good.

**"Spaghetti in a Hot Dog Bun" by Maria Dismondy** — Lucy is teased for being different. She doesn't fight back, she doesn't change herself, she stays kind. This book is particularly good if your five-year-old has come home with stories of social friction at the new school — kids who were unkind, exclusion that felt deliberate. The message is: you don't have to become someone else to fit in. You can stay exactly who you are.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — I've listed this for younger ages too, but it belongs here as well because five is the age when they start to really feel the loss of the old school. Old friends. The teacher they loved. For kids who are mourning the switch rather than just navigating the new place, this book about connection across distance lands differently than at three.

## A bonus section: books about being brave that aren't school books

Sometimes the most useful framing for a five-year-old in a new school isn't "this is about making friends" but "this is about doing something hard." Brave-themed books can work as a different angle when the direct approach gets resistance.

**"When You Are Brave" by Pat Zietlow Miller** — Beautiful, simple, true. Brave doesn't mean fearless. It means going anyway. For a five-year-old who has to walk into that classroom tomorrow morning, this matters.

**"Ish" by Peter H. Reynolds** — About trying things and not needing to be perfect. If your kid is freezing at the new school because they don't want to do anything wrong in front of kids who already know the rules, this is the book for them.

## The reading conversation that actually helps

At five, there's a specific conversation that works better than most. After you finish a book like "The Day You Begin" or "The Brand New Kid," you ask:

"What would you want the new kid to know?"

Not "how do you feel" — that question often bounces off. But third-person, talking about a character, giving advice: five-year-olds are surprisingly eloquent here. They'll say things like "I'd want them to know that it gets better" or "I would want someone to just talk to them." And then, gently, you can turn it: "Is there anything you wish someone knew about you at your new school?"

Let silence happen. Don't rush. This is the conversation that matters.

The other thing that helps: asking about the smallest things, not the biggest. Not "do you have friends yet" but "did anyone sit near you today?" Not "do you like it?" but "what was the most interesting thing you saw?" Small-aperture questions get real answers. Big-aperture questions get "fine."

## When it's been long enough to worry

I don't want to tell you what the cutoff is for worry, because honestly I don't know your kid. What I'd say: if after a month you're still seeing significant distress that doesn't ebb and flow but is just consistently there — sleep disruption, refusal to go, crying every morning, regressive behavior — that's worth talking to someone about. Your pediatrician, the school counselor, a child therapist.

Five is old enough that the social anxiety of a mid-year switch can start to entrench. It doesn't always. But if it does, catching it early matters.

Books help. Conversations help. But some kids need more than that and there's no shame in it.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's the thing about five-year-olds: they're old enough to be genuinely flattered. Genuinely moved. A personalized picture book with their name in it, their school, their teacher's name, about starting exactly this kind of new school — they'll read it five times the first night.

And at five, they might want to read it to you. That's the good version. That's when you know the message landed.

We build these at Plubis. You give us the details. We give you a real illustrated story starring your kid.

The first one is free.

situationLabel: a new baby brother or sister
title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about a new baby brother or sister
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through new sibling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five years old, and suddenly not the baby anymore — or maybe just barely not the baby anymore, or the baby for a few years and now there's a new one. However you got here, there's a five-year-old in the house with complicated feelings and probably a few theories about why this is unfair.

## The five-year-old emotional landscape

Five is a real pivot point. Kids this age are often in or about to start kindergarten, which means they're already navigating huge identity questions: Am I a big kid now? What does that mean? And then: there's a baby, and suddenly the answer to "big kid" feels less like a promotion and more like something that was decided for them without asking.

The gift of five is emotional sophistication. Your kid can feel jealous AND excited AND proud AND left out, sometimes in the same afternoon. They can name those feelings if you give them the vocabulary. And they can follow a real story arc — a character who struggles, chooses, fails a little, and finds something true on the other side. That's what the best books for this age do.

The trap is books that are too tidy. Five-year-olds have a well-developed radar for when they're being managed. If the emotional resolution happens too fast or feels too convenient, they'll tune out or push back. Look for books where the complexity is real.

## Books for a five-year-old dealing with a new sibling

**"Julius, the Baby of the World" by Kevin Henkes**
Still the best. Lilly's hostility is detailed and committed and funny, and the reversal at the end isn't a conversion — she doesn't suddenly love her brother. She just defends him. That's such a true note. Five-year-olds feel that distinction even if they can't articulate it. This is the book I'd start with, full stop.

**"Harriet's Halloween Candy" by Nancy Carlson**
This one's not strictly about a new sibling, but it's about the specific feeling of not wanting to share something that feels like yours. Harriet eats all her Halloween candy so she doesn't have to share with her brother. And then she feels terrible. For a five-year-old in the middle of having to share everything — parental attention, space, the best toys — this book does something useful: it names the greediness without shaming it, and shows the natural consequence without lecturing. Try your library first on this one.

**"A Baby Sister for Frances" by Russell Hoban**
Frances is a badger. An extremely self-possessed badger who runs away under the dining room table with crackers and a doll. Five-year-olds are old enough to find Frances's logic funny AND empathize with it at the same time. The parental response in this book is pitch-perfect — they don't chase her, they just talk about how much they'll miss her. The whole arc happens in about ten pages and it's completely satisfying.

**"Noisy Nora" by Rosemary Wells**
Nora is the middle child. She bangs and crashes and makes noise, trying to get her parents' attention while they're busy with the baby and the older sister. She finally "disappears" and the family notices. Short, rhyming, a little old-fashioned, but the emotional truth is exact. For the five-year-old who's been making a lot of noise lately — not because they're misbehaving, but because they need to be seen. This book sees them.

**"The New Small Person" by Lauren Child**
Elmore Green has a perfect life until "the new small person" arrives. He is not consulted. He is not pleased. The humor is dry and the art is collage-style and somewhat chaotic — which suits the content. Five-year-olds who feel the injustice of not being asked will recognize Elmore immediately. This one's been out a few years so check your library first.

**"Siblings" by Elissa Haden Guest and Elise Primavera**
A book about what siblings are — the bickering, the loyalty, the you-can-never-get-rid-of-them permanence of it. More of a long-view book than a crisis book. For the five-year-old who's past the initial shock and starting to understand that this sibling thing is actually a relationship they're building. Pair it with one of the more emotionally immediate books above for the full picture.

## How to actually use these books

At five, reading is more of a conversation than a performance. Stop mid-story and ask "what do you think she should do?" Your five-year-old will tell you. That answer is useful data. It tells you something about what they're working through themselves.

Let them read to the baby. Underrated. A five-year-old sitting next to a newborn or young infant, reading a picture book out loud — even just narrating the pictures — does three things at once: it gives them a role (expert reader), creates a bonding moment, and positions them as the knowledgeable older sibling rather than the displaced younger child. It sounds small. It isn't.

Don't only read new-sibling books. Read their favorites too. The message that reading time with you is still theirs, still special, not just a vehicle for processing the baby transition — that matters. One new-sibling book, one of their current favorites. Rotate.

And if the book opens something up — a "I don't like the baby sometimes" or a "I wish it was just us again" — don't rush to fix it. "I know. Tell me more." That's the whole response. You don't need to solve it tonight.

## A note on timing

Five-year-olds often hit a second wave of hard feelings around the 3-4 month mark, when the newborn phase is ending and the baby is becoming a more present, demanding little person. The first wave can seem to pass, and then this second one catches parents off guard. That's a good time to bring out books again — not when it's a crisis, but when the low-grade friction is becoming a pattern.

## When a book isn't enough

If a five-year-old is showing consistent school avoidance, long stretches of sadness, or acting out that's escalating rather than cycling through normally, it's worth a conversation with the pediatrician. Books are wonderful conversation starters, but some kids need a professional ear — not a sign of failure, just a sign that the feelings are bigger than books can hold.

## Make a book made for your child

Plubis generates a custom picture book with your five-year-old's name woven into the story — about their new sibling, their specific feelings, their family. You pick the situation, add their name, and it's done in about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about being afraid of the dark
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through nightmares and the dark. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
My kid is five and afraid of the dark and I genuinely thought we'd be past this by now.

We're not past this. Nobody warned me that five-year-olds' newly expanded brains — the ones that can do math now, the ones that understand time, the ones that have OPINIONS about everything — will apply all of that capacity directly to constructing elaborate nighttime threat scenarios. Turns out knowing more doesn't automatically mean being less scared. Who knew.

Here's what I've learned: books still work at five. Actually, in some ways they work better than at younger ages because now your kid can really sit with a story, hold the arc, discuss it afterward. They're ready for books that take the fear seriously — not cute, not dismissive, not "the dark is actually your friend!" (said no five-year-old ever). Books that say: yes, this is real, and here is someone who went through it.

## what works at this age

Five-year-olds are building their identity. They're asking: what kind of person am I? Am I brave? Am I capable? The best nighttime-fear books at this age tie directly into that project.

Books where the character goes from scared to brave — where something shifts internally, not just in the external situation — land the hardest at five. Your kid is doing that same internal work. Seeing it mirrored in a character gives them language for it and, crucially, evidence that it's possible.

They're also old enough for books with real prose. Not just simple sentences and single concepts per page, but books with paragraph-sized chunks of text, nuanced emotion, characters who feel complicated things simultaneously. That range exists in picture books — it's not just middle grade. The best ones at this age read almost like short stories with pictures.

And five-year-olds respond well to information. Why does the dark happen? What IS night scientifically? What do animals do when the lights go out? Knowledge is armor. Any book that gives them real, interesting facts about darkness alongside emotional reassurance is doing double duty.

## book picks

**"The Dark" by Lemony Snicket, illustrated by Jon Klassen**

If you haven't tried this one yet, five is absolutely the right age. Laszlo is afraid of the dark. The dark lives in his house — in the basement, mostly, but it moves around. One night the dark calls him down. And Laszlo goes. What he finds is not a defeat of the dark, not a reveal that darkness is friendly, but something stranger and more satisfying: a negotiation. He gets what he needs. The dark gets to stay. Snicket writes for the part of the five-year-old brain that knows the world is actually a little complicated, and Klassen's illustrations do things with shadow and negative space that most adult art doesn't do. One of the best picture books of the last twenty years, full stop. Little, Brown.

**"The Darkest Dark" by Chris Hadfield, illustrated by The Fan Brothers**

Chris Hadfield grew up terrified of the dark. He also became a Canadian astronaut and commander of the International Space Station. "The Darkest Dark" is the story of how those two things connect. Eight-year-old Chris watches the 1969 moon landing and realizes that outer space — the actual physical void, the most dark thing there is — is where he wants to go. Not despite the dark. Because of it.

This is a genuinely useful reframe for five-year-olds. The dark isn't the enemy of adventure. It IS the adventure. The Fan Brothers' illustrations are stunning, soft and luminous and strange. And the glow-in-the-dark cover edition is worth getting if your kid loves it — they will hold it in their dark room and watch the stars appear. Little, Brown, 2016.

**"Where the Wild Things Are" by Maurice Sendak**

Max goes to his room. His room becomes a jungle, then an ocean, then the place where the wild things are. He's the king. He decides to leave. He comes home. His supper is still warm.

This is a perfect book. I know you know it. But specifically for nighttime fear at five: the whole arc is about a child entering the dark (his room, his imagination, the wild world) and not only surviving it but ruling it, and then choosing to come home. Five-year-olds who feel overwhelmed by nighttime fear often need to be reminded that they have agency in it. They can decide. They can leave the scary dream. They can come back to safety. Sendak wrote that without a single didactic word. Read it for the arc, not the monsters.

**"Orion and the Dark" by Emma Yarlett**

Orion is convinced he's the most scared child on earth and he has a detailed list to prove it. The Dark itself shows up and offers to take him on a tour. The book then becomes a guided explanation of what the dark actually does, with gorgeous physical elements — flaps, windows, hidden images. At five, your kid can work these independently. Let them drive the reading. They can find the hidden details, operate the flaps, discover things you "missed." The interactivity gives them ownership of a book that's doing some serious emotional work in the background. Templar/Candlewick.

**"Bedtime for Frances" by Russell Hoban, illustrated by Garth Williams**

Frances finds approximately nine things to be scared of before bed. A tiger. A giant. A strange noise. The crack in the ceiling. Her father, beautifully, does not dismiss any of this — but he also doesn't indulge it indefinitely. Eventually Frances runs out of fears and falls asleep.

At five, kids find this book funny in a way they couldn't at three. They can see Frances doing the exact thing they do, described with complete earnestness, and they laugh. Laughter at your own fear is sophisticated emotional processing. This book enables it. Also: Francis's parents are warm and present and don't lose their patience, which is aspirational content for the adults in the room too. Published originally in 1960, still utterly relevant.

**"Scaredy Squirrel at Night" by Mélanie Watt**

Scaredy Squirrel has a plan for every nightmare scenario. He's documented them. He has exit strategies. He has a kit. The comedy is that his planning is so detailed, so sincere, so elaborate — and totally unnecessary, because when he finally sleeps, it's fine. Five-year-olds who are anxious by temperament often relate to Scaredy Squirrel with an almost painful recognition. This is me. And then they laugh at it. That's the gift: the book creates enough distance to see your own anxiety pattern and find it a little funny. Mélanie Watt is wonderful.

**"What a Bad Dream" by Mercer Mayer**

Little Critter has a dream where a magic potion turns him into something scary, and everyone treats him differently, and he wakes up relieved it wasn't real. This is Mercer Mayer at his most direct about nightmare content — and at five, your kid is having real nightmares with plot. This book gives them language for that experience: it felt real, then you woke up, and it was a dream. Simple. But naming that cycle matters. Available as a board book and picture book. Try your library.

## bonus section: make them the expert

Five-year-olds love being the expert. They love having information nobody else has.

Try this: after reading one of these books, ask them to explain the dark to a stuffed animal. "Owl doesn't know about darkness. Can you tell him what you know now?" Let them teach. Let them be the authority.

You'll hear their own fears reflected back in what they say, but you'll also hear them processing. "The dark just means there's no light. But you can still be there." Five-year-olds who can say that out loud are doing important work. They're becoming the narrator of their own experience instead of the victim of it.

## when a book isn't enough

By five, some kids have full nightmare narratives that they remember and worry about. They wake up convinced the dream was real, or convinced it'll happen again the next night.

The conversation that helps:

"Tell me about it." Let them narrate. Don't fix it yet.

"That sounds really scary in the dream." Validate the experience, not the reality of the threat.

"What woke you up?" This is the key move. Something woke them up — their own brain, protecting them. "Your brain knew it wasn't okay and it woke you up. Your brain is good at that."

Give them agency over the ending: "What do you wish had happened?" Let them rewrite it. The brain can work with alternative endings. This is actually a therapeutic technique — it's called Imagery Rehearsal — and you can do a version of it with a five-year-old at 3am. Draw the new ending tomorrow morning. Close the loop.

## make a book made for your child

At five, your child is old enough to co-create. Ask them: who's the hero in a story about the dark? What do they find down there? What helps them?

Then take those answers to Plubis. We'll write a book around the story your child basically told you — their name, their specific fear, their invented solution. Five-year-olds who help make their book and then read it are doing something genuinely powerful: they're authoring their own courage.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about a parent being unwell
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a parent being sick. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Somewhere in the last few months, your five-year-old started understanding things you didn't tell them.

That's the hard thing about five. They're not babies anymore. They overhear. They watch your face when you're on the phone. They notice when you don't finish your food, when you wince getting up from a chair, when the grown-ups go quiet when they walk into the room. Five-year-olds are sharp in a way that catches you off guard — they'll look up from a puzzle and say something that shows they've understood far more than you thought they had.

And then they go back to the puzzle, and you stand there trying to figure out what just happened.

At five, the gap between what a child understands and what they can articulate is enormous. They have more questions than words. They feel things deeply — worry especially — but they don't always know how to say "I am scared that you are going to die," so it comes out as aggression, or clinginess, or a complete refusal to get in the bath. Or all three on the same evening.

Books help at five for a reason that might seem obvious but is actually worth naming: they create a shared object. You're not talking about your illness, which is terrifying. You're talking about this character's parent, who is sick. The distance is protective. It lets both of you approach the fear at an angle instead of head-on.

That angle is where the real conversation lives.

## What works at five

Five-year-olds can handle books with more complexity — longer text, more developed characters, situations that don't resolve neatly. They're also starting to understand cause and effect in more sophisticated ways, which means they can begin to hold the idea that sickness happens for reasons that have nothing to do with them or their behaviour.

That last part is important. Magic thinking doesn't disappear at five. "I wished Daddy would stop being so tired and then he got really sick" — that is a thought a five-year-old can have and carry for years without ever saying it out loud. Books that explicitly or implicitly address the "it's not your fault" question are valuable at this age.

Five-year-olds also benefit from books that name specific emotions — not just "sad" and "scared" but the more nuanced ones: the anger that comes from helplessness, the confusion of loving someone who looks different now, the embarrassment of not knowing what to say when a friend asks about your parent. These are real feelings at five. Books that reflect them back give children the words they need to use them.

Let your child ask questions during and after reading. Take your time. An incomplete answer is better than no answer — "I don't know exactly, but the doctors are doing everything they can" is not a failure. It's the truth.

## Book picks

**"Nowhere Hair" by Sue Glader, illustrated by Edith Buenen** — Even though this sits comfortably in the four-to-eight range, it works especially well at five because children this age have a new relationship with physical appearance and difference. Hair loss is visible. Other children might ask about it. Friends might stare. "Nowhere Hair" addresses the strangeness of a parent who looks different with warmth and a lightness that doesn't minimise. The rhyme is still accessible, and five-year-olds who've been reading for a year or two will feel the satisfaction of following it. The message — Mama is still Mama, even bald, even sick — is one that bears repeating at this age.

**"When Pete's Dad Got Sick" by Kathleen Long Bostrom, illustrated by Cheri Bladholm** — Pete is angry. That's the emotional truth at the heart of this book, and I think it's one of the most honest depictions of what a child really feels when a parent's illness takes something away. The book is about chronic illness — Pete's dad used to run and play, now he can barely walk — and it doesn't pretend that's fine. Pete's anger is allowed to exist in the story. It's not pathologised, not fixed quickly. It just exists alongside love. At five, when a child might be feeling a version of that anger and not understanding why, seeing it named in a story is enormously relieving. *Oh. That's allowed.*

**"What Happens When Someone I Love Has Cancer?" by Sara Olsher** — Sara Olsher wrote this book for her own daughter, and it shows. It's specific without being clinical, honest without being terrifying. It explains what cancer is, what treatment involves (including why a parent might be tired or have no hair), and what a child can and cannot do about it. At five, children are beginning to develop a real sense of wanting to be useful — they don't want to just be helpless bystanders. This book gives them real answers about what's happening and real, age-appropriate ways to still be part of things. That matters.

**"I Wonder What It's Like When a Parent Has Cancer: Max's Story" by Jacquelyn Rebecek, illustrated by Anna Boxall** — This is the first in the Wonders & Worries series, and it was written by a certified child life specialist, which shows in the emotional intelligence of the storytelling. Max is a boy whose dad has cancer, and the book follows him through the feelings: the confusion, the worry, the wondering what to tell his friends, the fear of the unknown. Wonders & Worries is a nonprofit with twenty-five years of experience supporting children through parental illness, and that expertise is visible here. For a five-year-old, seeing Max's specific worries named and validated is powerful.

**"Cancer Hates Kisses" by Jessica Reid Sliwerski, illustrated by Mika Song** — By five, children can engage with the idea in this book more fully. It's not just a comforting story — it's a genuine framework. The illness has a weakness. Love and warmth and laughter are that weakness. Five-year-olds can grab onto that and carry it. They can say to themselves: *I am doing something. My love is not useless.* In a situation that is largely out of their control, that is a meaningful thing to have.

**"Our Mom Has Cancer" by Abigail and Adrienne Ackermann** — This book was written by two sisters — who were nine and eleven when their mother was treated for breast cancer — because they couldn't find anything that matched their experience. The illustrations are their own drawings. There is something about that origin story that makes the book different from anything a professional author and illustrator team might produce. It's unpolished in a way that is deeply honest. At five, children are beginning to read other children's perspectives, and seeing two kids who went through this and came out the other side — who could even make something out of it — is genuinely hopeful.

**"Butterfly Kisses and Wishes on Wings" by Ellen McVicker** — A child's parent has cancer, and the book moves through the treatment period with a focus on the emotional experience of the child: the worry, the love, the small rituals that hold things together. At five, children are beginning to be sentimental in a new way — they understand that rituals matter, that doing the same thing every day means something. This book speaks to that. It's a gentler, more lyrical choice alongside the more explanatory books in this list.

## Sitting with the fear together

Here's the thing about five-year-olds and fear: they know when you're not being straight with them. They don't always have the evidence, but they sense it. And when they sense that the adults are managing them rather than telling them the truth, they get more anxious, not less.

So when you read these books together, let the hard parts be hard. Don't rush to reassure. Sit with the difficult page for a moment. Let your child feel it. Then turn the page together.

That act — staying, turning the page, finding what comes next — is itself a lesson. Hard things can be survived. We go through them together. What's on the other side of the feeling is not nothing.

## The conversation that starts after the book closes

The most useful conversation often starts after the book is put away. You're both lying there in the dark, or you're at the kitchen table with the book closed between you, and your five-year-old says something you weren't expecting.

*"If you die, who will put me to bed?"*

Hold that question. Don't deflect it. "That is such an important question. Here's what I've arranged: [name the person]. You will always have someone who loves you to put you to bed." Your child is asking whether they will be okay. Tell them yes. Give them the specific, true answer.

If your five-year-old is struggling significantly — anxiety, sleep disruption, behaviour changes, sadness that doesn't lift — please reach out to a professional. Your treatment team may have a child life specialist. Your child's school may have a counsellor. These are real options, not last resorts.

## Make a book made just for your child

There's a version of this support that's more precise than anything on a bookshelf: a story about *your* child, *your* family, *your* specific situation. A story where the character has your child's name and something of your child's face, and where the parent who's sick looks something like you.

At five, children are starting to understand their own story. They're starting to know that their life is a narrative, that it has a past and a present and a future. A book that puts their experience into that narrative — that says, in the gentlest way, this is part of your story and you can hold it — is different from anything generic.

At Plubis, you can create that book in five minutes. Tell us what's happening, how your child is feeling, and a few things about your family. We'll build a story that's made for them.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about a parent going away
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through a parent traveling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five-year-olds ask questions that stop you cold. "If your plane goes down, does that mean I get a different daddy?" Real sentence, real breakfast table, real fork paused in midair.

Five is old enough to have a concept of danger and not old enough to have realistic risk assessment. They know planes crash because they've heard adults mention it, seen it in a movie, overheard something at school. They don't know it's statistically rarer than the drive to the airport. So the fear can be very specific and very concrete in a way that the younger years' separation anxiety isn't — and it needs to be met differently.

But underneath the scary questions is the same basic thing it's always been: I love you and I don't want you to go and I need to know you're coming back.

## What five-year-olds are actually ready for

Five-year-olds can hold complex emotions at the same time. Excited that the parent is going somewhere cool. Scared they won't come back. Proud they're being brave. Angry the trip is even happening. All of that in the same Tuesday morning.

They can also read subtext now — they know when a book is trying to teach them something versus just telling a story. Books that feel like lessons get an eye-roll even at five. Books that feel like an honest story, with a real character who feels real things, land. The difference is usually whether the character's emotions are specific and somewhat inconvenient versus clean and tidy.

Also: five-year-olds are starting to write and draw. They can make things in response to hard feelings. Involve them in making something — a card for the traveling parent, a drawing of what they want to do when the parent gets back, a countdown with stickers. The books are one input; making things is another.

## The books worth reading

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At five, this metaphor is fully operational. Kids this age can extend it: "does the string work at night?" "does it go through walls?" "what if we're on different sides of the earth?" Yes. Yes. Yes. Play the game with them. Let them draw the string on a piece of paper — from them, through their house, across the ocean, to wherever the parent is going. That drawing, taped to the wall at their height, does real work every night the parent is away.

**"Owl Babies" by Martin Waddell** — I know five might seem old for this book but I'd push back. The specific accuracy of Bill's repeated "I want my mommy" is not childish — it's the exact sentence inside every age. And the mother's return, landing on the branch, gathering the babies — that image is doing something that longer, more sophisticated books sometimes miss. There's a reason this book has been in print for over thirty years. Read it slowly. Sit in the waiting part.

**"When You Are Brave" by Pat Zietlow Miller** — This is the best book on this list for a five-year-old who's working hard to be okay. It doesn't say bravery means not being scared — it says bravery is doing the hard thing even when your heart is hammering. For the kid who is holding it together at school and then falling apart at home, this gives a name and a frame for what they're doing. "You're being brave right now" is so much more honest than "it's okay" that it almost isn't in the same category.

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — This is a kindergarten book but it works for any five-year-old processing a disruption to their normal world. The central question is whether you're brave enough to share your story — your specific, different, strange, real story — in a room full of people who don't know it. For a kid navigating a parent's absence at school, the resonance is real: you have something happening at home that the other kids don't. This book makes carrying that feel less lonely. Woodson writes with extraordinary care. The illustrations by Rafael Lopez are extraordinary.

**"I Love You to the Moon and Back" by Amelia Hepworth** — Simple, warm, cumulative. A bear parent and cub go through the day together; the love is measured in big distances. Less emotionally complex than some others on this list but at five it can be exactly the right before-bed book during a parent's trip: not asking anything hard, just restating the fact of the love. Good for the nights when your kid is just sad and tired and doesn't have questions — they just need the feeling stated plainly.

**"Lifetimes" by Bryan Mellonie and Robert Ingpen** — I'm putting this one here carefully, because it depends completely on your child. If the fear around the parent traveling has any connection to death — "what if you die?" asked genuinely and seriously — this book handles mortality in the most honest and gentle way I've seen in children's literature. It says living things die, it says that's real, it says the time in between is a lifetime and every lifetime is different. Some five-year-olds need this. Others absolutely do not need it brought up right before the parent's trip. Know your kid. If you're not sure, don't.

**"A Bike Like Sergio's" by Maribeth Boelts** — Not about parent travel, but it's the book I'd put in the back pocket for a five-year-old who's been behaving terribly since the parent left. It's about a kid who wants something badly, makes a choice he knows is wrong, and has to figure out how to make it right. The feelings — wanting, doing the wrong thing, shame, repair — are all handled with extraordinary delicacy. If your kid has been acting out since the parent left, reading this together gives a frame for "I don't feel like myself when I miss someone, and that's real, and I can repair things." One of the best picture books published in the last decade.

## The conversation a five-year-old actually needs

Don't deflect the scary questions. "What if your plane crashes?" deserves an honest answer, not a cheerful redirect. Something like: "Planes are actually very safe — much safer than cars. But I know it feels scary when someone you love gets on a plane. That makes sense. I always come back." Short. Honest. Confident, not dismissive.

Five-year-olds are building their model of how the world works. When adults give obviously false reassurance — "nothing bad will ever happen" — they sense the lie and trust decreases. When adults say "that's a scary thought AND here's why I feel okay" — they learn how to hold fear without being ruled by it. That's the whole job.

## The ritual matters as much as the book

The kissing hand. The invisible string check-in before bed. A video of the traveling parent reading a book, to be played at bedtime. A photo taped at eye level. A paper chain countdown, one loop per day, ripped off every morning.

Five-year-olds are old enough to be given a small job during the parent's absence. Water one plant. Feed the cat. Keep the parent's pillow smooth. The job gives them agency — they are taking care of something in the world while they wait. That agency is not nothing.

## A bonus section: what the traveling parent can do from far away

Send a voice note just to them, not a family group call. Something specific: "I saw a bird here that looked exactly like the one in your book and I thought of you." Read them a story over video call. Send a postcard — actual paper in the mail — with their name on it. Mail has a physical weight and a specific arrival that a text message doesn't. A five-year-old who has received a postcard from the parent's trip will carry it to school and show every single person.

Be careful with promises of "I'll call every night" if your schedule is uncertain. Better: "I'll call when I can, and every time I do I'll tell you one weird thing I saw that day." Lower the commitment, make the calls feel like gifts rather than obligations you might fail.

## When a book isn't enough

Some five-year-olds have anxieties about parent travel that go beyond the ordinary — persistent nightmares, school refusal, somatic complaints that reliably appear before every trip. If the pattern is disrupting their daily functioning, an early childhood therapist can help. Cognitive-behavioral approaches work well at this age for anxiety. The books are a bridge, not a ceiling.

## Make a book made just for your child

At five, your kid is old enough to know the book is about them and to feel the difference that makes. Not a bear, not a raccoon, not a fictional "Lily" — your actual child, in their actual situation. Plubis generates a full illustrated picture book in five minutes with your child's name, their specific situation, their story. Reading "Riya's mama went to London for a work trip" before bed is a different experience than anything on this list. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about trying new foods
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through picky eating. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is the age where picky eating starts to have social consequences. Not just at home — at birthday parties, at school lunch, at the cafeteria where everyone can see what's in your lunchbox. A five-year-old who only eats plain pasta is going to notice that they're the only one who didn't eat the pizza at the party. That's new territory.

This matters because the motivation to try new things can, for some kids, start to come from wanting to participate in the social world. "The other kids had pizza" is a more compelling argument to a five-year-old than "pizza has nutrition in it." You're not above using that. It's a real motivator.

But five is also an age where anxiety about food can calcify if handled badly. The kid who is embarrassed about their restrictions, who feels shame around what they eat, who hears "just try it, what's wrong with you" at every family dinner — that kid is not developing a healthier relationship with food. They're just developing a more private one. So the social piece is complicated.

Books at five can handle some of this complexity. Not all of it. But they can model a child navigating food in a social world without shame, which is exactly the reference you want them to have.

## What works at five

Five-year-olds can engage with full narrative complexity. They can feel the social awkwardness Yoko feels at school. They can understand that the food isn't just food — it's identity, pride, belonging. They can also now ask for something from themselves: "I want to be the kind of person who tries new things." That self-concept is the key to the whole lock.

You want to build toward that self-concept. Not by telling them they should try things. By offering stories where the trying feels brave and worthwhile and normal.

## The books

**"Yoko" by Rosemary Wells** — Best on this list for the social dimension of food. Yoko brings sushi to school. Everyone makes fun of it. Then one classmate tries it. Then another. Yoko's food is validated not as exotic but as delicious. For the five-year-old navigating what it means to eat differently — or for any kid navigating cultural difference in a social context — this book is exactly right. Wells's illustrations are warm and old-fashioned and the story doesn't flinch from the hard social moment.

**"Little Pea" by Amy Krouse Rosenthal** — Still great at five because now they really get the joke. Candy as the terrible forced food. Spinach as the reward. Ask them afterward: "what would be your spinach?" This often produces a genuinely surprising answer.

**"Dragons Love Tacos" and "Dragons Love Tacos 2" by Adam Rubin** — The sequel exists and is equally good. Five-year-olds can now handle two books in a series and will love both. Food as fun, food as adventure, food as community. No preachy messaging. Just joy.

**"What's In Your Lunchbox" style books or "Lunch" by Denise Fleming** — At five, books that show what people eat in different places and cultures do something useful: they expand the category of "normal." A five-year-old who knows that kids in Japan eat rice for breakfast, that kids in India eat dal and roti, that kids in Mexico eat tamales — that kid has a wider map of what food is. Which makes trying something new feel less like a leap into the unknown and more like joining a bigger world.
