**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — Not a food book per se, but the meal in this book — the shared pie — is central to the transformation. Eating together as the vehicle for connection. This is a longer read and five-year-olds handle it beautifully. Use it to open up conversation about meals and sharing and what happens when you try the thing you're afraid of.

**"The Ugly Vegetables" by Grace Lin** — The ugly vegetables that everyone ignores turn out to make the best soup. Best on this list for the five-year-old who has written off an entire category of food because it "looks wrong." The look is not the whole story. The soup brings the whole street to your door. Grace Lin's illustrations are beautiful and the message is gentle.

**"Eat Your Peas" by Kes Gray** — Molly won't eat her peas. Mom offers increasingly elaborate bribes (the whole world, outer space, every chocolate factory). Molly still won't eat her peas. Then Molly notices Mom isn't finishing her peas either. This is the funniest picky eating book on this list and five-year-olds will absolutely catch the joke. Also: it validates that sometimes people just don't like things. Even grown-ups. That's allowed.

## What's worth trying at five

The exposure model: keep offering the refused food in small amounts alongside accepted foods. Say nothing about it. Don't track it visibly. Over weeks and months, familiarity builds.

The kitchen helper model: kids who help prepare food are more likely to try it. Even washing vegetables. Even tearing lettuce. Ownership changes the relationship to the food.

The brave bite model: agree together, outside the pressure of mealtime, on a "brave bite" — one small taste, no judgment, nothing required after. Some families use a special plate or cup for brave bites. The ritual helps. The five-year-old who controls the terms of the try is more likely to actually try.

## When picky eating deserves more attention

At five, if your child is still eating fewer than fifteen foods, or if the restriction is getting tighter rather than broader, or if there's real anxiety around food — mention it to your pediatrician. Feeding therapy (often with an OT) is effective and non-stigmatizing. Many kids need more than time and strategy, and there's no shame in that.

## Make a book made for your child

At five, a child reading a book that's explicitly about them — their name, their specific food battle, their particular bravery — is having a real experience. Plubis builds the book in five minutes. The character with their name tries the thing and it goes okay.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about potty training
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through potty training. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five-year-olds having accidents is genuinely one of the more stressful parenting situations nobody talks about. Because by five, people expect this to be done. Kindergarten expects this to be done. Your mother-in-law thinks it should have been done years ago. And your kid — your smart, verbal, capable five-year-old — is still having accidents at school or wetting the bed or just... not making it in time, consistently.

And you are exhausted in a very specific way. You're managing the logistics (spare clothes, overnight protectors, conversation with the teacher) and also the emotional side (your kid is embarrassed, possibly already hiding it from friends, maybe starting to refuse activities because of anxiety about accidents) and also the judgment from the outside (from other parents, from well-meaning relatives who suggest things you've already tried).

Here's what I want to say before we get to the books: accidents at five are more common than you've been led to believe. Daytime accidents happen in roughly 5-10% of children this age. Nighttime wetting is even more common. This is not a failure of your parenting. This is not a failure of your child. For many five-year-olds, the physiological and neurological readiness just isn't fully there yet, and no amount of reminding or sticker-charting changes that timeline.

Books help differently at five than they did at two or three. A five-year-old doesn't just absorb mood — they process ideas. They can have a real conversation about a story. They can connect what happened to a character to what's happening to them, if you give them the space to do it.

## The approach at five

Skip the toddler potty books. Seriously. Showing a five-year-old a board book with a cartoon baby on a potty communicates exactly one thing: that you think this is a baby problem. It's not a baby problem. It's a five-year-old problem, and five-year-olds have big feelings about being treated like babies.

What works at five is a combination of: books that speak to the feelings underneath the accidents (anxiety, overwhelm, change, not being able to control the body), books that normalize the experience without infantilizing it, and honest conversation.

For some kids, it also helps to read books that are only loosely about the potty at all — books about being embarrassed and surviving, about having a body that does unexpected things, about the gap between wanting to do something and being able to do it. Those books do the emotional scaffolding that makes the harder conversations easier.

## The picks

**"Bedwetting and Accidents Aren't Your Fault" by Steve Hodges and Suzanne Schlosberg** — This is the most important book on this list by a significant margin. Dr. Hodges is a pediatric urologist who has spent his career treating kids with accidents. His central argument — supported by clinical research — is that the vast majority of accidents in children ages 4-7 are caused by constipation pressing on the bladder, not behavior, not attitude, not parenting. The illustrated children's version makes this accessible to you and your five-year-old together. Reading this changed how I thought about the problem. If accidents have been happening for months, start here.

**"I Have to Go!" by Robert Munsch** — Your five-year-old is officially old enough to appreciate Munsch's comedy fully. Andrew's terrible-timing accidents are played for laughs and the whole family gets pulled into the chaos. The absurdity gives your kid permission to laugh at something they might otherwise feel only shame about. Munsch's books are prescription-grade for shame reduction. The humor is the medicine.

**"Everyone Poops" by Taro Gomi** — I know. It's a board book. But at five, if your kid is dealing with accidents in a shame spiral, sometimes going back to the very beginning — this is a body thing, all bodies do it, no one is exempt — resets something. Read it in a deliberately silly voice. Make it clearly a joke. Let them feel the absurdity of a book that is explaining something this basic. Sometimes the joke is the point.

**"Time to Pee!" by Mo Willems** — At five they're solidly old enough to appreciate Mo Willems' specific brand of dry, knowing humor. The pep squad mice are funny to them now in a meta way — they know they're a cheering section, they know what the book is doing, and it's still charming. For kids who know what to do and are struggling with the doing of it, this book says: you have a team. You're not alone in this.

**"Danny Is Done with Diapers" by Rebecca O'Connell** — The alphabet structure here means it moves fast and feels achievable to read. The cumulative positivity — kid after kid after kid successfully doing the thing — can be surprisingly useful for a five-year-old who's gotten trapped in a shame loop. Sometimes you need to see the thing done successfully, over and over, to rebuild the mental model that it's possible.

**"No More Diapers for Ducky!" by Bernette Ford** — At five this might read as very young, and you can use that. Read it with them and let them notice it's for little kids. Let them be the expert. "That was YOUR book when you were little." The trip back to a book they've graduated from, framed that way, can rebuild the identity of "I am the kid who does this, I've always been the kid who does this, this is a temporary hard patch."

**"The Potty Train" by David Hochman and Ruth Kennison** — For five-year-olds who are anxious about accidents away from home — at school, at friends' houses — the Potty Train's cheerful framing of the whole enterprise as an adventure can help. All aboard. Everyone's going. You're going too. The book's energy is so unconcerned about the whole thing that it lowers the emotional temperature just by existing.

## The embarrassment problem

Five is the age where social awareness kicks in hard. Your five-year-old knows that other kids don't have accidents. They may have already had an incident at school. They may be managing this privately with a kind of exhausting vigilance — never raising their hand to go, sitting through discomfort, avoiding anything that takes them too far from a bathroom.

The books matter here, but the conversation matters more. What five-year-olds need to hear — clearly, calmly, repeatedly — is that this is a body thing, it's not their fault, lots of kids deal with it (more than they'd ever know because nobody talks about it), and you're not upset with them. They need to hear this from you more than from a book. But a book gives you an opening.

When you read together and the character has an accident, or deals with a body that won't cooperate, that's the moment to say it. Not a speech. Just: "that happens. Bodies are hard sometimes. You're okay."

## See the doctor first

I want to say this plainly: if your five-year-old has been having regular daytime accidents for more than a month, or regular bedwetting, they need a pediatric checkup before anything else. Check for UTI. Ask about constipation. Rule out the physical stuff. No book recommendation fixes a bladder infection. The Steve Hodges book will help you understand the questions to ask. Read it yourself first.

## When books genuinely aren't enough

If your five-year-old is in genuine distress about accidents — avoiding school, avoiding friends, having anxiety spikes around the issue — that's a pediatric urology referral conversation. It's also potentially a therapy conversation. Not because anything is terribly wrong, but because a child this age who has developed real anxiety around body function sometimes needs someone other than a parent to help them work through it. Books are a bridge. Sometimes you need a longer bridge.

## Make a book made for your child

At five, kids are conscious enough of themselves that a custom book hits differently. "This book is about you" is not abstract to them — they will pay close attention in a way they might not with a stranger's character. A book that says "Zara sometimes had accidents, and she felt embarrassed, but she learned it wasn't her fault, and things got better" is a different reading experience than any general-audience title. Plubis makes these in five minutes. You tell us the child's name and situation, and we generate an illustrated book built around them. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about learning to share
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through sharing. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is when sharing gets genuinely complicated. Not toddler-complicated, where the whole problem is "this is mine and I don't want it to be not mine" — but actually socially complicated. At 5, your child is navigating friendships that have history. They know who their best friend is and who is trying to edge in. They understand the difference between sharing with someone they like and sharing with someone they don't. They are starting to figure out fairness, and fairness and sharing are not always the same thing.

"But I already shared yesterday" is a real sentence that will come out of a 5-year-old's mouth with complete sincerity. Because they're keeping track now. They are running an internal ledger — who gave what, who got what, whether the exchange was balanced. This is actually cognitive progress. It's also exhausting to be on the receiving end of.

At 5, kids can handle books with more nuance. They can follow a character through multiple scenes, understand backstory, and sit with an ambiguous ending. They can begin to understand that other people's inner lives are as real and complex as their own — the technical term is theory of mind, and it's clicking into gear around this age. The best sharing books for 5-year-olds take advantage of all of that.


## The books

### *Enemy Pie* by Derek Munson, illustrated by Tara Calahan King

If you haven't read this one yet, this is the age it really sings. A boy spends the whole summer avoiding his enemy Jeremy Ross, who moved in down the street and ruined everything. His dad makes Enemy Pie — supposedly a foolproof way to get rid of enemies — but the catch is that the boy has to spend the whole day with Jeremy first.

So he does. And Jeremy turns out to be kind of fun. And by the end of the day, the boy has to grapple with something uncomfortable: he was wrong about Jeremy. And if he was wrong about Jeremy, what does that mean?

For 5-year-olds who are increasingly cliquey and increasingly quick to form "we don't like so-and-so" alliances, this book is genuinely useful. Not because it tells them to be nice to everyone — it doesn't — but because it shows, with warmth and humor, that proximity and a little bit of time can completely change how you see a person. The pie itself is a wonderful MacGuffin. Kids are desperate to find out what's in it, and it keeps them hooked through the whole emotional arc.

The ending is sweet without being saccharine. Read it more than once.


### *The Doorbell Rang* by Pat Hutchins

Yes, this was on the 4-year-old list too. But at 5, kids are doing more with numbers and the cookie math hits differently — they can now do the simple division themselves, which makes the whole experience more interactive and a bit more intense. "Wait, if there are twelve cookies and now there are six kids, that's only TWO each." The horror on their faces is real and deeply relatable.

What I want to flag specifically for 5-year-olds: the character work in the illustrations is extraordinary. Look at the children's faces as more and more friends arrive. There's no dialogue about how they feel — it's all in the drawings. Five-year-olds are good enough now at reading faces to catch it. Ask them what Victoria is thinking on each page. You'll get some very honest answers about resource scarcity.

This book also has great reread value at this age because kids start using it as a vehicle to talk about fairness. "But if there are enough for everyone to have two, that IS fair." Versus: "But they had it first, shouldn't they get more?" These are real philosophical questions that come up organically from this story, and 5-year-olds love arguing about them.


### *The Rainbow Fish* by Marcus Pfister

At 5, your child is old enough for the conversation that this book quietly invites and most adults avoid. Because here's the thing about the Rainbow Fish: the other fish won't be friends with him until he gives away what makes him beautiful. And that is, at minimum, worth talking about.

I still recommend this book. The illustrations are spectacular, the story moves well, and the core emotional experience — being lonely, taking a risk, finding connection — is completely real. But at 5, don't just close the book and move on. Ask: "Do you think the Rainbow Fish had to give away his scales to make friends? Is that what friends are supposed to want?"

You might be surprised by what your 5-year-old says. This is an age where kids are starting to figure out what good friendships look like versus friendships that feel kind of bad. Giving this book a second layer of conversation turns it from a gentle sharing lesson into something genuinely useful for navigating their actual social world.

The sparkly pages are also still very compelling at 5. That has not changed.


### *Last Stop on Market Street* by Matt de la Peña, illustrated by Christian Robinson

This one is not directly about sharing in the preschool sense — no one fights over a toy. But it is about one of the deepest forms of sharing: sharing your time, your attention, and your resources with people who need them. CJ and his grandma ride the bus across their city after church, and CJ asks her a series of questions — why don't we have a car, why don't we have this thing other kids have? — and she answers him with a particular kind of wisdom that is neither dismissive nor saccharine.

At the end, they arrive at a soup kitchen where they volunteer every week. And CJ finally sees the beauty his grandma has been pointing toward all along.

This book has won every major children's book award that exists, and it deserves all of them. For 5-year-olds who are starting to notice what other kids have and compare it to what they have, this book quietly shifts the frame. It's not about having less. It's about where you put your attention and what you choose to do with what you've got.

It will make you cry. Possibly starting on the bus scene. Be ready.


## What changes at 5 that you should know about

**Fairness is now the lens through which everything is evaluated.** "That's not fair" will be said at approximately every meal. This is actually developmentally healthy — a sense of fairness is the precursor to a sense of justice. But it means sharing conversations at 5 need to address fairness, not just kindness. "Sharing doesn't mean equal pieces every time — it means everyone gets what they need" is a concept 5-year-olds can begin to understand, but it takes work.

**Exclusion is a form of not-sharing.** At 5, kids start to understand that leaving someone out is a choice, not just an accident. The social dynamics at preschool and kindergarten involve a lot of "you can't play with us" — which is its own version of not sharing space, attention, and belonging. Books like *One* by Kathryn Otoshi (mentioned in the 4-year-old list, still excellent here) speak directly to this.

**They can now understand your inner life too.** This is new. At 5, your child can start to understand that *you* have feelings — that when they grab something from you or don't say thank you, that affects you. This makes conversations about sharing real in a new way. "When you grab without asking, I feel frustrated" lands differently at 5 than it did at 3.


## A section on what five-year-olds actually worry about with sharing

Here's something parents often miss: 5-year-olds don't always refuse to share because they're being selfish. Sometimes they refuse because they're afraid. Afraid the thing won't come back. Afraid they'll lose it. Afraid that once it's out of their hands they won't have control over what happens to it.

This is especially true of special objects — beloved stuffed animals, favorite blankets, things that carry emotional weight. And it's worth distinguishing that from standard "I don't want to take turns with the trucks" reluctance. The fear-based refusal needs a different response. It needs reassurance, not redirecting. "I'll make sure you get it back" goes further than any book or lesson.

Read the room. Know your kid. The books help with the general case. You handle the specific one.


## One last thing

At 5, your child is about to enter — or is already in — kindergarten. And kindergarten is basically an extended daily experiment in sharing: sharing a teacher's attention, sharing space with 20 other kids, sharing the limited number of minutes in a school day. That's a lot.

The best thing you can do to prepare them isn't to drill sharing. It's to make sure they feel secure. Kids who feel like their needs are seen and met at home are consistently better at sharing at school. The emptier the cup, the harder the sharing. Fill the cup.

Read with them. Give them your undivided attention at bedtime. Sit with the books. That is not unrelated to sharing. It is the foundation of it.


*Your child as the main character — their name, their world, their sharing moment — in a custom picture book made in five minutes. The first one is free.*

title: The best picture books to read to a 5-year-old about tantrums and meltdowns
metaDescription: Picture books for 5-year-olds going through tantrums. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Five is the age where you start to feel like: okay, we're past the worst of it. And then your kid loses it completely because you cut their sandwich diagonally instead of vertically and you realize you're still in it.

But here's what's different at five. A five-year-old can sometimes catch themselves. Mid-meltdown, there's a tiny flicker of awareness — they know this is happening, they hate that it's happening, they can't stop it. That awareness is not nothing. That's the beginning of real self-regulation, and it's worth naming and celebrating when you see it.

Five is also kindergarten age, which means the social stakes just went up dramatically. At home, a meltdown is embarrassing in front of the family. At school, it's in front of twenty other five-year-olds who are very, very interested in it. The motivation to manage feelings in public is suddenly real for a lot of kids this age. Books that validate feelings AND offer concrete strategies land differently at five than they did at three.

## What five-year-olds are working with

They can follow a full story arc. They can understand cause-and-effect emotionally — "she was mad because he took her thing without asking." They can discuss feelings in the abstract. They're starting to understand that other people have internal states too (theory of mind is solidifying around this age). They can, sometimes, use a calming strategy on purpose.

What they still can't do: regulate in the actual moment without support, most of the time. Apply wisdom under pressure. Be patient about food that touches other food.

## The books

**"Alexander and the Terrible, Horrible, No Good, Very Bad Day" by Judith Viorst** — Still the gold standard. At five they'll get every joke, they'll feel the solidarity, and they can identify with Alexander's frustration that nobody else seems to understand that today is terrible. Read it on a bad day, read it on a good day. It holds up.

**"The Bad Mood and the Stick" by Lemony Snicket** — Best on this list for opening up the conversation about how feelings travel. Five-year-olds can engage with the idea that they "caught" a bad mood, or that their bad mood "gave" something to someone else. This is sophisticated emotional insight but they're developmentally ready for it. Worth the conversation.

**"What If Everybody Did That?" by Ellen Javernick** — A bit preachy, honestly. Fine for classrooms. At home you might get an eye-roll from a five-year-old who has developed opinions about being lectured. Use your judgment. If your kid responds to clear rule-setting, it works. If they're sensitive to feeling taught at, skip it.

**"Wemberly Worried" by Kevin Henkes** — Worry is often under the meltdown. Wemberly is a worrier; the book takes her anxiety seriously and follows her through starting school without dismissing how hard it is. Five-year-olds who are anxious in ways that look like defiance or meltdowns often respond deeply to Wemberly. "Are you more like Wemberly than like a grumpy monkey?" is worth asking.

**"In My Heart: A Book of Feelings" by Jo Witek** — Covers the full spectrum: happiness, sadness, bravery, anger, shyness, worry, and others. Each feeling gets a spread with beautiful die-cut hearts that physically change size. At five, kids can page through this and pick the feeling they're having, which is a self-awareness tool more than just a reading experience. Genuinely useful for the child who says "I don't know" when asked how they're feeling.

**"Spaghetti in a Hot Dog Bun" by Maria Dismondy** — About being different and being targeted for it. Best on this list for the five-year-old whose meltdowns are connected to social difficulty — kids being mean, feeling excluded, not fitting in. The message — you don't have to be mean back, and your kindness is yours to keep — is one kids this age are ready to hear. Not in a preachy way. In a warm, specific way.

**"Breathe Like a Bear" by Kira Willey** — Breathing exercises for kids. At five, these actually work sometimes, which feels miraculous when it happens the first time. Practice the exercises at calm times. Make them silly. "Lion breath" is the best one — they will use this in real moments of stress if you've practiced it enough.

## Having the conversation at five

Five is the best age so far for real conversation about feelings, because they can track and reason but the stakes feel lower than they will at ten. Ask questions that don't have right answers: "if you were Wemberly, what would you do?" "Why do you think the stick made everyone feel worse?"

And when a meltdown happens — after, not during — you can actually do a quick debrief. Not as punishment, not as analysis. Just: "that was really hard. What was the hardest part?" And then you listen, without jumping to solutions. The listening is the thing.

Five-year-olds who know they can come to you with the hard thing without being immediately fixed or corrected develop the emotional vocabulary that makes meltdowns less necessary over time. The meltdown is partly a communication failure. You're building the communication.

## When to ask for backup

Tantrums at five are less common than at two or three but still happen. If they're frequent, intense, and seem to be getting worse rather than better — especially if they're creating real problems at school — it's worth talking to the pediatrician. Not as an emergency. Just as a check-in. Five is a good age to catch things if there's something that needs support.

## Make a book made for your child

A five-year-old is old enough to have the book be explicitly about them. Not just "a character like them" — them. Their name, their specific thing that sets them off, their classroom or house. Plubis builds it in five minutes and they will read it and say "that's me" and you will both feel something.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about bedtime
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through bedtime routines. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds have discovered that reading themselves is a power. Your child might be sounding out words at bedtime, hiding a flashlight under the blanket, reading the same Captain Underpants book for the sixth time because they can and they know you're downstairs. This is good. This is great, actually — you've built a reader. It's also its own challenge because now they're using their newfound literacy as a reason why bedtime rules don't apply to them specifically.

"I'm reading, I'm learning." Yes. It's also 10pm on a school night. These things can both be true.

But here's what hasn't changed: six-year-olds still love being read to. They may not admit it with the same enthusiasm as a four-year-old who climbs into your lap, but sit next to them with a good book and start reading and they will lean in. They will stop pretending to be indifferent. They will ask you to keep going.

This is a gift. Use it.

## What bedtime actually looks like at six

Six is the age of school friendships, playground politics, and a steady growing understanding that the world is complex and sometimes unfair. Your kid is navigating real social dynamics — who's in whose group, who said something mean, whether the teacher noticed their drawing. These things live in their head at night.

They're also starting to have a real inner life — rich imaginative play, private thoughts, preferences they guard. The bedtime resistance at six isn't usually about not being sleepy. It's about not wanting to be alone with all of that. The ritual of reading together, your voice, the warmth — it's a bridge. It says: you're not alone in the nighttime. The day can end safely.

The other thing at six: they will argue you into the ground if they sense weakness. "All my friends get to stay up until 9." "I'm not even tired." "One more chapter" when you've already done two. Hold the container. Warmth inside it, firmness about its edges.

## The books — but different at six

Six can handle more. Longer sentences, longer books, more ambiguity. They can tolerate an ending that isn't completely resolved, if the emotional arc felt true. They're starting to notice craft — you'll catch them asking why an illustration is all one color, why the words are big on some pages and small on others.

So the books for six include some proper chapter-book-length picture books, some sophisticated emotionally complex ones, and a few where the point is beauty rather than lesson.

**"Where the Wild Things Are" by Maurice Sendak** — Still. I will put this on every list because it ages up forever. A six-year-old reads it completely differently from how a four-year-old does. At six, they feel the specific ache of wanting to be somewhere wilder than your bedroom and also knowing home is the safest place. Max's journey is recognizable in a deeper way. The line "and Max the king of all wild things was lonely and wanted to be where someone loved him best of all" will register with a six-year-old differently than it ever did before. Read it slowly. Pause on that line.

**"Stellaluna" by Janell Cannon** — A baby fruit bat falls from her mother and is raised by a family of birds. She learns to act like a bird — sleeping at night, eating bugs, sitting right-side-up on branches. Eventually she finds her mother and her bat family. The emotional core is about being a different kind of creature, adapting, not losing yourself, and finding your people. Six-year-olds who feel different from their classmates in any way find this one intensely. It's also genuinely beautiful — Cannon's illustrations are detailed and warm and treat the natural world with real respect.

**"The Polar Express" by Chris Van Allsburg** — Not a summer book. A winter book. But if you have a six-year-old who's starting to grow out of magic — starting to ask whether Santa is real, whether things can actually be this good — this is the book. A boy who wants to believe takes a train to the North Pole at midnight. The ending, when only those who believe can hear the bell ring, is one of the most quietly devastating pages in all of picture books. No resolution. Just: the question of belief is the question. Six is exactly the age for that. And it reads beautifully aloud by lamplight in winter.

**"The Gardener" by Sarah Stewart** — Lydia Grace goes to live with her uncle in the city during the Depression. She brings seeds. She makes a secret garden on the rooftop. Uncle Jim almost never smiles. The ending is tender and earned. This is the book for a six-year-old who carries a lot inside and expresses it indirectly — who does things rather than saying things. Lydia Grace doesn't explain herself. She plants. The book honors that. And the illustrations are gorgeous, warm period-piece watercolors that are perfect for slowing down into before sleep.

**"Weslandia" by Paul Fleischman** — Wesley is a misfit. Over the summer he grows a civilization from a single plant — his own ecosystem, his own language, his own games. By fall the kids who teased him want in. It's about the joy of building your own world when the given world doesn't fit you. For the six-year-old who feels outside of things at school — or who just has an incredibly rich inner life — this is affirmation that that's exactly right. Also: the concept of making your own civilization appeals to the six-year-old imagination in a very specific, very powerful way.

**"Amos & Boris" by William Steig** — Amos the mouse and Boris the whale become friends across completely incompatible worlds. They save each other's lives. Years later they meet again and Boris is dying on the beach and Amos gets two elephants to push him back into the sea. The last scene — Boris sinking beneath the waves, Amos watching from shore, knowing they'll never see each other again — is devastating and true. Six-year-olds can handle this. Six-year-olds who've experienced loss, or who love someone deeply, need this. It says: some connections cross every difference and matter forever and also end. Steig trusted his readers with it.

**"Flotsam" by David Wiesner** — Completely wordless. A boy on a beach finds a camera washed up from the ocean. The photographs inside show the ocean's strange secret world. And within one photograph is another photograph of a child holding a photograph of another child — a chain stretching back decades. No words. Just images. Six-year-olds will stare at every page. This is the book for the night when you want to read together without talking — or when you want *them* to narrate and you to listen. Their imagination does the work. That's a different kind of wind-down but a real one.

**"The Other Side" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Clover is Black. Annie is white. They live on opposite sides of a fence in a segregated town. They spend the summer sitting on top of it together, between sides. It's understated and beautiful and trusts the child completely. Six-year-olds are starting to notice difference and fairness and who's allowed where. This book offers no tidy resolution — just two kids deciding the fence is a strange place to put a border between friends. The conversation that follows might be the most important one of the week.

## What to do when they're "too old for picture books"

The same thing you do at five, but more so: don't fight it. Offer chapter books. *Charlotte's Web* at bedtime is wonderful and will make you both cry and is worth it. *The BFG* by Roald Dahl. *My Father's Dragon*. The first Harry Potter if they're ready.

But also: keep a few picture books available for the bad days. After a hard day at school. When they're sick. When they need to be small for a bit. Those are the days a picture book works better than a chapter book. The story is shorter, the world more contained, the ending cleaner. There's a reason adults reread children's books sometimes. The form is not beneath anyone.

## The decompression conversation

Six-year-olds who've had full school days often need to talk before they can sleep. Not a long debrief — just acknowledgment. Two questions max: "What was the best part of today?" Then: "Was there anything hard?" Listen to both. Don't fix the second one immediately — just say "that sounds hard" and move on. You can problem-solve tomorrow.

The goal is that they don't go to sleep carrying something unacknowledged. Kids who feel heard settle faster. Not always. But often enough that it's worth the five minutes.

## Bonus: when the resistance is really about something else

Six is a classic age for unspoken anxiety showing up as bedtime drama. If your child is suddenly, newly terrible about going to sleep — especially if it correlates with a school transition, a friendship issue, a family change — the bedtime battle is probably not actually about bedtime. It's about the thing they haven't told you yet.

The book is a way in. Reading something that touches on fear, or change, or being different, and then sitting with the silence after — that's when they sometimes tell you. Be unhurried. Don't push. Just be there.

If the anxiety is bigger than a hard week — nightmares, physical complaints every morning, persistent dread rather than normal resistance — it's worth professional support. Child therapists who specialize in anxiety are genuinely helpful at this age, and the earlier the better. Books are the start of the conversation, not the whole conversation.

## When a book isn't enough

If nightmares are frequent, if sleep refusal is affecting school mornings, if your child is genuinely suffering and not just stalling — please talk to a pediatrician or child psychologist. There's real help available. You're not failing by needing it. The books on this list are excellent. They are not a treatment plan.

## Make a book made just for your child

Six-year-olds understand completely that books are made by people. That someone thought up the story, made the illustrations, sent it out into the world. The idea that someone made a book specifically for them — not just a name-personalization but an actual story about their life, their situation, their struggle — lands with real weight at six. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. You put in your child's name and what they're going through, and we build them their own book. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about big feelings
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through big feelings. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the age when some kids start performing being fine.

Not actually being fine. Performing it. Because school happens now, and school is full of social observation, and somewhere between preschool and first grade a child learns that crying about something can make other kids stare. So they hold it together all day and then they come home and lose it completely over homework, or over dinner being wrong, or over nothing — just a long, slow release of everything that got compressed since morning.

You know that 4pm-to-dinner stretch. Yeah. That.

Big feelings at six are in some ways harder to handle than big feelings at two, because at two you had permission to be therapeutic and narrating and present in a way that now, at six, can feel condescending. "Sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed" lands differently when addressed to a kindergartner who was just trying to do their reading. They know they're overwhelmed. They don't want you to label it. They want you to... actually, they're not sure what they want, which is part of the problem.

Picture books are still useful here. But you have to be canny about how you offer them. Don't present them as "this is a book about feelings" — that's a flag that a lesson is incoming and your six-year-old has become capable of disliking lessons. Just read them. Put them in the bedtime rotation. Let the work happen quietly.

## What's different at six

Six-year-olds have genuine social emotions now. Shame. Embarrassment. The feeling of being left out, specifically and painfully. The feeling of being seen doing something wrong in front of people. The fear that someone they liked doesn't like them anymore. These are adult-weight feelings in a kid who doesn't have adult-weight coping infrastructure. It's a lot.

They're also beginning to compare. Not just "I want what she has" but "why am I like this when other kids aren't" — comparisons that land somewhere that's not quite jealousy and not quite inadequacy but somewhere in between. Books that deal honestly with being different, or feeling wrong, or having feelings that seem bigger than everyone else's are doing something important.

And at six, a book that treats them as capable of complexity is a gift. They can tell when they're being talked down to. They don't want the toddler version of the emotion conversation anymore. Give them something with some actual weight in it.

## Books that hold up at six

**"The Rabbit Listened" by Cori Doerrfeld** — Yes, I'm starting with a picture book often shelved in the toddler section. Read it anyway. At six, your kid will understand every piece of it — the well-meaning friends who want to fix things, the rabbit who just stays, the specific relief of not being handled. If your six-year-old has had the experience of telling an adult about something hard and having the adult immediately try to make it better rather than just listen — which, if they've attended school for more than a week, they have — this book will name something real for them. It might name something for you too.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — One of the best books ever written about the specific childhood social experience of having an enemy. The feelings are social and petty and fully real — the hatred that feels righteous and total, and then the slow, uncomfortable dissolution of that certainty when you actually spend time with the person. For a six-year-old navigating complicated friendships (a friend who sometimes includes them, a classmate they've decided is their nemesis), this book is exactly right. Read it together. Don't moralize at the end. Just let it sit.

**"Chrysanthemum" by Kevin Henkes** — The definitive book about shame and belonging at this age. Chrysanthemum's name, which she loved, is mocked at school. The feeling of something about yourself being targeted — your name, your lunch, your shoes, your laugh — is a feeling six-year-olds have now. Keenly. Henkes handles it without the saccharine resolution most books reach for. It doesn't end with "and then everyone was nice and she was totally fine." It ends with belonging that came from an unexpected place. That's more true.

**"The Most Magnificent Thing" by Ashley Spires** — A girl is making the most magnificent thing. It keeps going wrong. She gets furious. She quits. She goes for a walk. She comes back and sees it differently. This book is specifically about the feelings around failure and persistence — frustration, rage, the urge to abandon a project, and what happens when you step away and come back. Six-year-olds starting to work on things — drawings, block structures, their first attempts at writing — are beginning to encounter failure and the feelings it brings. This book gives them a map.

**"Have You Filled a Bucket Today?" by Carol McCloud** — The bucket-filling metaphor is pervasive now, partly because schools have adopted it, but there's a reason: it works. Kindness fills buckets, cruelty dips from them. What's underappreciated about this book is that it also gives six-year-olds a framework for understanding why being mean doesn't actually help — the bucket dipper's bucket doesn't get fuller, it just empties others. For a kid who lashes out when feeling low, that's a concept worth sitting with. Read it without connecting it too directly to any recent incident. Let them make the connection themselves.

**"Beautiful Oops!" by Barney Saltzberg** — A spill, a tear, a bent corner. Every "mistake" becomes something else. Bright, tactile, interactive. More of an art/craft book than an emotional narrative, but the emotional message — mistakes are material, not verdict — is exactly what six-year-olds perfectionists need. (And there is a lot of six-year-old perfectionism. A lot. It shows up as crying over the wrong crayon, as refusing to attempt things, as erasing until there's a hole in the paper. This book helps.)

**"Strictly No Elephants" by Lisa Mantchev** — A boy has a pet elephant who isn't welcome at the neighborhood pet club. They find their own club. A book about rejection and belonging and building community when the existing one has locked you out. Very quietly devastating in a very useful way. Six-year-olds who feel excluded — from a friend group, from a club, from an activity — will recognize this without it being named. Read it slowly.

**"Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Peña** — CJ's grandma shows him that what looks bleak can be beautiful. The feelings in this book are quieter than some on the list — not anger or tantrums but something more like disappointment, wonder, the beginning of perspective. For a six-year-old who has been asking why things aren't fair, this book offers an answer that isn't "life isn't fair, deal with it" but also isn't "it's actually all fine." It's more like: beauty is in here too, if you look for it alongside the hard. Beautiful Newbery Medal winner. Read it after a hard day.

## How to offer books to a six-year-old who is suspicious of lessons

Timing is everything. Not right after the incident. Not as a preemptive intervention. Late in the evening, low-key, in a moment when the day has settled. "I found this book, want to read it?" — not "I found this book because of what happened at school today."

If they resist: "Okay, you don't have to." And then you read it yourself while they're nearby. Six-year-olds will eavesdrop on stories even when they're supposedly not listening. That's fine. Let them come to it.

If they engage: let them be the smart one. Ask them to explain things to you. "Wait, why do you think the girl ran away? What was that feeling?" They love being the expert. They'll engage harder when they get to lead.

## The specific thing six-year-olds need that two-year-olds didn't

Validation that big feelings are not embarrassing. Because somewhere around six the social surveillance kicks in and kids start getting a message — subtle, relentless — that big feelings are babyish. Only babies cry. Only babies get upset. Which is not true and is actively harmful but is also culturally ubiquitous. Books that show characters who have big feelings and are not shameful for it — characters who are treated with dignity in their hard moments — are doing anti-shame work. Prioritize those books.

Six is also old enough to start talking about what happens in the body during big feelings. Tight chest, hot face, stomach gone strange. Starting to name the physical signals of an emotion is the beginning of noticing the feeling before it peaks — which is the first, hard step toward actually interrupting the pattern. Even a five-minute conversation about "what does angry feel like in your body, specifically" is worth having. The books are the door opener.

## Bonus: the thing to say when nothing works

For the times when your six-year-old is fully underwater and no book and no strategy and no magic sentence is helping: "You don't have to talk about it. I'm just going to sit here." And then sit there. Don't scroll. Don't start another conversation. Just be a human presence. Six-year-olds regulate by proximity to a calm adult. You being physically present and not agitated is itself the intervention. It's boring and uncomfortable and it works.

## When a book isn't enough

Six-year-olds who consistently struggle with emotional intensity, who are having daily meltdowns that are disrupting school or family life, who seem to be carrying chronic anxiety or sadness rather than just situational big feelings — these kids deserve professional support. School counselors are a great first stop; they see a lot of children and can usually give you an accurate read on whether what you're seeing is typical or worth a referral. There's no age too young to benefit from working with a skilled child therapist. Early is better. The brain at six is still enormously plastic. That's good news.

## Make a book made just for your child

At six, a custom picture book is something your kid can have opinions about. "What should happen to the main character?" "What does she look like?" They're old enough to be delighted by the specificity of their own name in a story, and old enough to take it seriously. Plubis makes a custom illustrated picture book in five minutes — your child as the main character, their specific big feeling, a story built around them. Not a fill-in-the-blank. A real story. For the kid who has been told their feelings are too big: here is a book where someone with their name had those feelings and was seen, and loved, and made it through. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about building confidence
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through confidence and self-esteem. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Let's talk about what happens to a confident kid the second they start actually learning to read.

Before learning to read, confidence is relatively protected. Kids run and climb and imagine and paint and none of it has a test at the end. But reading is different. Reading is *graded*. Reading has a right answer. And the kid who was utterly fearless on the playground at five — the one who jumped from the highest thing and came home with scrapes they were proud of — can suddenly be sitting in a reading group at six, watching another kid decode a word they can't, and filing it in the permanent folder: *I'm not good at reading.*

Six is when the self-concept starts to calcify in ways that matter more. Not permanently — there's still enormous room for revision. But the self-stories are getting told more often, more confidently. "I'm bad at math" at six is different from "I'm bad at math" at four. At four it's a passing observation. At six it's starting to feel like a fact.

Picture books for six-year-olds about confidence have a harder job than picture books for younger kids. Six-year-olds are skeptical. They can smell a moral coming from across the room. They know when they're being managed. The books that work at this age earn the lesson — they don't announce it.


## The confidence issues that actually come up at six

Just so we're concrete about this:

**Academic self-concept is forming.** The kid who is a slower reader than their peers is already noticing. The kid who struggles with handwriting is already aware. These aren't catastrophic — kids catch up, skills vary, pace is not destiny — but they are real confidence challenges and they are happening right now in your first-grader's mind.

**Peer comparison is sophisticated.** Six-year-olds don't just notice who is better at something. They make inferences. "Mia is better at drawing than me, so she's better than me" (wrong inference, extremely common). The jump from "better at X" to "better as a person" happens constantly at this age.

**They know what "cool" means.** Something happens between five and six where kids become aware of social hierarchy in a much more explicit way. Who is popular, who is funny, who is ignored. It's not full middle-school social navigation yet, but the architecture is going up.

**Perfectionism is at peak.** The six-year-old who crumples a drawing because it isn't right, who won't start a task they might not finish perfectly, who cries in frustration when something is hard the first time — this is a real pattern and it needs real acknowledgment.


## The books

### *The Dot* by Peter H. Reynolds (2003)

I'm putting this first for six specifically, and here's why: six-year-olds are in formal learning environments where they are expected to produce things that are evaluated. The "I can't" feeling is now attached to real stakes. And Reynolds wrote a book specifically about that moment.

Vashti refuses to draw. Her staring-at-the-paper is not laziness — it's the paralysis of a kid who is scared of making the wrong mark. Her teacher doesn't argue. Doesn't force. Just asks for *any* mark, signs it, frames it.

For a six-year-old who has started resisting tasks they feel they can't do perfectly, this story does something specific: it shows that the small starting thing — the dot — is enough to begin. You don't have to do the whole thing. You have to make one mark.

This is a legitimate strategy for perfectionism. Start small. Sign it. Keep going.

Also worth noting: at the end of the book, Vashti passes the lesson on. She becomes the one who tells a younger kid he can make a mark. That intergenerational confidence transmission is a lovely thing for six-year-olds to see. Confidence can be given, not just received.


### *Stand Tall, Molly Lou Melon* by Patty Lovell, illustrated by David Catrow (2001)

At six, this book earns its place more fully than it does at five.

Molly Lou Melon is new. She is visibly different — small, buck-toothed, unusual voice. She has a bully. And she has a grandmother who, before the move, filled her up with specific, absurd, brilliant confidence. "Walk as proudly as you please and the world will look up to you." "Smile big, big, big and the world will smile back."

The bully, Ronald Durkin, is one of the better-rendered six-year-old bullies in children's literature. He's not a cartoon villain. He's just a kid who makes the easy jokes at the new kid's expense, as kids do. His meanness is mundane, which is what makes it land.

What Molly Lou does — without flinching, without crumbling — is demonstrate her thing. She's small but she catches a football. She has big teeth but that's amazing for a beaver scene in the school play. Her weird voice wins the music recital. She doesn't defeat Ronald by being better at the things he's better at. She defeats him by being excellent at being herself.

I have one caution, and I said it for the five-year-old section too: Molly Lou's confidence is a little too perfect for some kids to relate to. She never actually wobbles. Six-year-olds know that wobbling is real. So after reading this one, consider asking: "Do you think Molly Lou ever felt nervous inside even when she looked brave outside?" That conversation is worth having.


### *Chrysanthemum* by Kevin Henkes (1991)

If you haven't read this one yet, six is your last perfect window. After this, kids start to find it slightly young.

Chrysanthemum's social suffering at the hands of the three girls is depicted with Henkes' characteristic accuracy — the subtle deflations, the way she makes herself small in class, the way her parents try to restore her every evening and it only partially works. It's an honest portrait of how other kids' words can burrow in even when they're obviously wrong.

For a six-year-old in a real social environment, this book's honesty is what makes it valuable. It doesn't say "just ignore mean people." It doesn't say confidence means being immune to cruelty. It says: other people's words can get to you even when they shouldn't. That is a real experience. And here's what helps.

Henkes' solution — Mrs. Twinkle, the music teacher, who also has a flower name and wears it magnificently — is not "believe in yourself more." It's *someone in your life who reflects your worth back to you*. That is a different lesson. A more complicated one. And six-year-olds are fully ready for the complexity.


### *Emmanuel's Dream: The True Story of Emmanuel Ofosu Yeboah* by Laurie Ann Thompson, illustrated by Sean Qualls (2015)

This is the book that doesn't look like the others on this list, and that's exactly why it should be here.

Emmanuel Ofosu Yeboah was born in Ghana with a deformed right leg. His mother told him he could do anything. He went to school. He played soccer. He got himself across Ghana by bicycle — 400 miles — to show his country and the world that physical disability is not inability. His story changed Ghana's laws on the rights of disabled people.

This is a picture book biography for ages five and up, illustrated by Sean Qualls with gorgeous, warm, movement-filled art.

Why does it belong in a confidence list for six-year-olds? Because at six, kids are starting to understand that confidence isn't just a feeling — it's demonstrated through action, through persistence, through refusing a story someone else has written about you. Emmanuel didn't have a book to read or a perfect environment. He had a mother who said "you can" and a deep refusal to accept a smaller life.

This is a different register of confidence story. It's not about peer acceptance or artistic bravery or jumping off a diving board. It's about the decision to live fully against significant odds. That's worth introducing at six, gently. The world is bigger than the classroom.


## The bonus section: when your six-year-old says "I'm just not good at that"

This will happen. Maybe it already has. And it deserves a real answer, not a pep talk.

"I'm just not good at reading" from a six-year-old is not a fact about the future. It's a report about how reading feels *right now*. Those are different things. But you can't get away with just saying "yes you are!" because they have evidence they'll throw back at you.

Here's what tends to work better:

**Separate the feeling from the verdict.** "Reading feels hard right now. That makes sense — it's new." This validates the experience without endorsing the permanent conclusion.

**Name a thing they couldn't do that they now can.** This is specific and concrete. "You couldn't ride your bike last year. Now you can. Your brain learns." They like evidence.

**Make the hard thing small.** This is Vashti's dot. Not "read the page" — "read this one word." Not "do the whole thing" — "try just the start."

**Let them see you struggle.** At six, kids notice when adults pretend everything is easy. If they see you try something, mess up, adjust, try again — and survive it — they learn more than from any book. You are their most important model.


## The piece most parents miss

Here's a thing I think doesn't get said enough: confidence and self-esteem are not the same thing.

Self-esteem is the global feeling of "I am okay as a person." Confidence is the domain-specific feeling of "I can handle this particular thing."

Most of what goes wrong at six is confidence, not self-esteem. A kid who says "I'm bad at math" usually doesn't mean "I am worthless." They mean "I don't trust myself to handle this math problem."

The books here address both. But the work at home, the real work, is mostly on confidence — giving your six-year-old repeated experience of trying things and finding out that trying is survivable. That failure doesn't end them. That they can start again.

Books show them the arc. You live through it with them.


If you want to give your six-year-old a story where the main character is specifically, recognizably *them* — their name, their school, their exact stuck place — that's what a custom storybook does.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about going to the dentist
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through dentist visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the age of loose teeth. That first wobbly tooth is either thrilling or terrifying depending on your kid, and usually both in rapid succession. It's also the age when dental appointments start to involve new things — maybe a first X-ray, maybe a first cavity, maybe a first real conversation with the dentist about orthodontic stuff way down the road.

The stakes feel higher at six than they did at three or four, and your kid knows it. They're not worried about the dentist abstractly anymore. They're worried about specific outcomes. "What if I have a cavity?" "What if it hurts?" "What if they have to take my tooth out?" These are concrete, logical fears, and they deserve honest, concrete answers.

Here's the thing about six-year-olds and dental anxiety: they're often scared of the cavity conversation more than the cleaning itself. Getting told you have a cavity feels like getting in trouble. It feels like evidence that you've been doing something wrong. If your kid comes out of an appointment with news of a cavity and feels shame about it — that's the thing that will make them dread future appointments more than any drill noise ever could.

So a big part of prep at six is destigmatizing cavities. Lots of kids get them. Lots of adults get them. Getting a cavity doesn't mean you're bad at brushing (though it might mean we tweak something), it just means this is what teeth sometimes do and the dentist helps fix it. Books that normalize the cavity experience — not celebrate it, just normalize it — are worth having on your shelf.

## What makes this age different

Six-year-olds are readers, or almost-readers. They'll notice the words on the page, not just the pictures. They'll want to follow along. They'll ask what specific words mean. This means the writing quality in the books you choose matters more than it did when your kid was three — and it means early readers about dental visits are now an option in addition to picture books.

They're also old enough to have real intellectual curiosity about how things work. "How does the X-ray machine work?" is a question a six-year-old might genuinely want answered with a real explanation, not a toddler-sized deflection. Lean into this. The dentist's office is actually kind of fascinating if you stop treating it like a medical gauntlet and start treating it like a science museum visit.

And: six-year-olds have friends. They share information about the dentist with each other. Some of this information is wrong. ("My cousin said it definitely hurts, definitely." Great. Thank you, cousin.) Be ready to gently counter misinformation without dismissing it — "that might have been what it was like for your cousin, and everyone's experience is a little different" — and use the books as a check on dental folklore.

## The books that actually hold up at six

**"Arthur's Tooth" by Marc Brown** — Still my top recommendation at six, possibly even more so than at five. Arthur's social anxiety about being the last one to lose a tooth is *peak* six-year-old content. The dental visit is handled with realism and the emotional undercurrent about not wanting to be different from your friends is handled with enormous warmth. Marc Brown understood six-year-olds at a cellular level.

**"Andrew's Loose Tooth" by Robert Munsch** — The absurdism reads even better at six because they're old enough to find it genuinely funny rather than just confusing. Munsch's wild escalation — the dentist gets called in, a lumberjack appears, things get increasingly unhinged — is comedy that lands with first-graders. And a child laughing about teeth is a child who's slightly less scared of them.

**"Throw Your Tooth on the Roof: Tooth Traditions from Around the World" by Selby Beeler** — This one is not about dental anxiety at all. It's about what different cultures do when kids lose teeth — throw them on the roof (many cultures!), bury them, give them to a mouse, leave them for a fairy. It's brilliant because it makes the whole loose-tooth experience feel like something celebrated globally rather than something endured. Six-year-olds find the variety genuinely delightful. And it opens up conversations about teeth that aren't about fear.

**"Chez Bob" by Bob Shea** — Not about dentists, but Bob Shea is one of the sharpest comic writers in children's picture books right now and his books — this one about a crocodile who runs a restaurant — do something important: they make teeth funny. A kid who thinks of teeth as something funny and interesting rather than something that gets examined by a stranger with rubber gloves is in a better headspace going into an appointment.

**"The Tooth Book" by Dr. Seuss** — Seuss at six can be read with real attention to language, rhythm, and wordplay. It's worth revisiting specifically at this age. You'll find your six-year-old anticipating the rhymes now, finishing sentences, noticing patterns. That level of active engagement turns reading into play, and reading-as-play before a stressful event is calming in a way that reading-as-instruction isn't.

**"Behold the Dentist" — I want to be clear this is a placeholder. Let me give you a real one:**

**"Doctor De Soto" by William Steig** — This is a legitimate children's classic about a mouse dentist who treats a fox patient while navigating the obvious peril of being eaten. It's brilliant. It's funny. It approaches the dentist from the *dentist's* perspective for once, which completely changes how six-year-olds think about the relationship. Also it's Steig, so the writing is beautiful and the pictures are warm and strange in the best way. Read this one regardless of what's going on with dental appointments.

**"Biscuit Goes to the Vet" — wrong book. But the structure works:**

**"Going to the Dentist" by Anne Civardi (Usborne First Experiences)** — The detail-heavy approach really works at six, even though this book is written for younger kids. Six-year-olds will read along and correct you if you mispronounce a dental tool name they've decided they know. They'll want to explain to younger siblings what everything does. Knowledge-building at this age is an active process, not a passive one, and a book with labeled equipment turns into a reference they'll consult.

**"Just Me and My Dad" — not dental, but:**

**"Franklin and the Tooth Fairy" by Paulette Bourgeois** — Franklin's gentle anxiety about losing his first tooth and the whole tooth-fairy mythology wrapping around it is very true to the six-year-old experience. The Franklin books handle ordinary childhood milestones with a warmth that doesn't feel condescending, which is a harder trick than it sounds. And the tooth-fairy angle is useful because it reframes tooth loss as magic rather than loss.

## Bonus section: when your six-year-old has been before and it went badly

This is the part most dental prep articles skip. But some kids have had genuinely hard appointments — a shot that hurt, a procedure that was longer and more uncomfortable than expected, a dentist who wasn't great with anxious kids. Those experiences are real and they leave marks.

If your six-year-old had a bad experience, books that minimize dental visits or make them sound easy are actually counterproductive. They create a discrepancy between what the book promises and what your kid's memory knows to be true. That discrepancy breeds distrust — of the books, of you, of the premise that this time will be different.

For kids with dental trauma (even mild trauma — a hard cleaning, a scary X-ray machine moment), the approach should be: validate first, prepare second. "I know last time felt really hard. That makes total sense. We're going to talk to the dentist about what made it hard and see if we can do anything differently this time." Then the books. Not instead of the conversation — after it.

And if your child has significant dental anxiety that makes appointments genuinely very difficult, that's worth mentioning to your pediatric dentist. Good pediatric dentists have many tools for anxious kids — different pacing, nitrous oxide, special glasses that show movies during cleanings, sedation for more complex procedures. You're not asking for special treatment. You're asking for appropriate care.

## How to read together at six

Six-year-olds can read along. Let them. Even if their reading is slow, let them take a page. Take turns. Make it collaborative.

Ask harder questions than you did at five. "Why do you think the character was more scared before the appointment than during it?" "What do you think would have happened if they hadn't gone?" "If you were the dentist, what would you do to make it less scary?" These aren't rhetorical questions — your six-year-old has real answers. Listen to them. You might learn something about specifically what's worrying your kid that you didn't know.

Also at six: let them tell *you* about the book. Have them narrate a page to you as if you've never heard the story. Retelling a story requires integrating it, and integrating it is what makes it stick. The kid who can tell you the story of a character going bravely to the dentist has done more preparation work than the kid who passively listened to it.

## Make a book made for your child

At six, your kid is reading. They know their name starts with the right letter. They'll notice immediately — and point it out with glee — when a book has their name in it.

Plubis makes personalized picture books for situations exactly like this. You put in your child's name, their age, the situation (dentist visit), and a few details you want included. It generates a picture book where your six-year-old is the main character — going to the dentist, having their specific feelings about it, getting through it.

For a six-year-old who might have had a hard experience before, there's something uniquely powerful about a story where *they* are the protagonist and *they* make it through. Not a bear. Not Arthur. You. Going to the dentist. And coming out with your teeth intact and your pride intact and a sticker.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about a parent being deployed
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through military deployment. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the age where they start watching you to see if you're okay.

They're in school now. They have a world outside the house. They come home with information from other kids — some of it useful, most of it wrong. "My friend Tyler said soldiers always come back." "Jayden's dad got hurt." They're assembling a picture of deployment from fragments, and they're trying hard not to scare you with what they've put together.

Six-year-olds are, in other words, already protecting you. And they're doing it while still needing to be held.

This is the age where the books matter in a different way. Not because a 6-year-old can't handle the truth — they can handle more than you might expect — but because they need to see, in story form, that it's okay to have their own feelings. That their grief is allowed to exist next to your grief. That they don't have to be the brave one.

## What's specific about six

School is where a lot of it shows up. Teachers will sometimes be the first to tell you something's changed. Your child might be distracted, or unusually well-behaved (which is sometimes its own sign), or picking fights, or withdrawing from friends.

Military kids at this age are also often the only military kid in their class. The deployment experience is invisible to their friends. Nobody else's parent has to say goodbye like that. That isolation is its own specific weight.

Some things that are true about 6-year-olds during deployment:
- **They can read, or are learning to.** Some of these books they may start wanting to read themselves, not just be read to.
- **They understand time.** Six months is long. They know this. The "when is Daddy coming home" question now has more weight behind it because they have a felt sense of how long six months really is.
- **They need peer community.** If there's a school counselor, a military family support group, or another military kid in the area — make that connection. Books are good. Other kids who get it are better.
- **They're processing through narrative.** Six-year-olds tell stories to make sense of events. Pay attention to how they describe deployment to their friends. That's how they're making sense of it.

## Book picks for 6-year-olds

**"I Miss You!: A Military Kid's Book About Deployment" by Beth Andrews** — The interactive, workbook-style format is actually ideal at six, when kids are starting to take some ownership of their inner lives through writing and drawing. A 6-year-old who can write a few words can fill in the prompts. The act of writing "I miss my dad when ___" on a page in a book that is designed to hold that sentence is more powerful than it sounds. Beth Andrews was a social worker for military families, and every page reflects that training — the questions are exactly the right kind, specific enough to be useful, open enough not to box anyone in.

**"When Dad's at Sea" by Mindy Pelton** — A 6-year-old can actually count out 180 links and understand what that number means. They can mark dates on a calendar. The paper chain becomes a project, not just a symbol — and the project gives them something to do with the waiting. Emily's experience in this book is close to what a 6-year-old actually lives: school, friends, family dinners with a gap where the deployed parent usually sat, and the strange dual reality of life continuing while something profound is missing.

**"Night Catch" by Brenda Ehrmantraut** — The North Star ritual still works at six — maybe better, because the child can articulate why it works. "We're both looking at the same star." A 6-year-old can hold that thought. They can also write a message on a piece of paper, fold it up, and "send it to the star" at night. They can draw a picture and tape it to the window. The ritual is expandable. Let them expand it.

**"Lily Hates Goodbyes" by Jerilyn Marler** — At six, reading this book might lead to a very different conversation than it does at four. Your child may want to discuss it: "Why was Lily so mad?" "Why didn't her mom just make it not happen?" "Is it okay to be mad at the Army?" (Yes. It's okay. You can be mad at the thing without being mad at the person.) The anger question — can I be angry about this without betraying my parent's service — is one that military kids carry for years. Lily Hates Goodbyes opens that door.

**"While You Are Away" by Eileen Spinelli** — At six, your child may notice that the book has three different families, three different branches. They might have questions about why: why is one dad on a ship and one mom in a plane and one parent somewhere else? That's a real conversation about the military you can have, at a 6-year-old level. The book becomes a springboard. And the central emotional truth — I miss you and I'm keeping track of ordinary days until you come back — is exactly the register that a 6-year-old lives in.

**"Hero Dad" by Melinda Hardin** (or **"Hero Mom" by Melinda Hardin** if the deployed parent is the mother) — At six, the pride aspect of this book hits differently. Your child is at school, maybe showing this book to a teacher, maybe doing show-and-tell about a deployed parent. The dignity in this book — the way it says yes, your parent is doing something important and brave — matters for a child navigating what to say when classmates ask where their dad is. It gives them language: my dad is a hero. That's enough of an answer. They don't owe anyone more than that.

**"A Yellow Ribbon for Daddy" by Anissa Mersiowsky** — The question at the center of this book — why does my parent have to protect other people's families instead of just staying here with ours — is one that a 6-year-old is absolutely capable of sitting with. They can start to hold the complexity: what my parent is doing has a point beyond our family. That point doesn't make the missing hurt less, but it gives it a shape. Mersiowsky writes in rhyme, which at six is satisfying in a way that plain prose sometimes isn't — the rhythm gives the hard content somewhere to land.

**"My Mommy Wears Combat Boots" by Sharon G. McBride** — If the deployed parent is the mother, this book is important enough that I'd call it necessary. The default of so many deployment books is a deployed father, which is a real representation gap. A 6-year-old girl with a deployed mom who doesn't see herself in any of the books on her shelf is getting a small but persistent message that her family's version of this is unusual, different, worth less page space. It isn't. McBride's book is a real counter to that.

## Reading together when they're old enough to read themselves

At six, something shifts. Some nights, your child might want to read the book to you rather than the other way around. Let them. This is big.

A child who reads aloud a book about deployment is processing it in their body and voice in a completely different way than a child being read to. The act of giving voice to the character's feelings means inhabiting those feelings, briefly, safely, with distance.

If they stumble over a word — "What does 'deployed' mean?" — answer it plainly. "It means gone for a while to do their job. Like how you go to school to do yours, but much farther and for much longer."

You can also take turns. You read one page, they read one page. It becomes something you're building together.

## Talking to the school

This is worth doing. Tell your child's teacher that a parent is deployed. You don't have to go into detail. A simple note — "My child's father/mother is currently deployed with the military. Wanted you to know in case you notice anything." A good teacher will watch, will give your child some extra grace, might check in quietly.

Some schools have counselors with training in military family issues. Ask.

And if your child's school happens to have other military kids, ask whether they can be connected. Even one friend who understands makes a difference.

## When books are no longer enough — and what to add

At six, books are still useful, but they're one tool among several that are becoming available:

**Journaling.** A 6-year-old who is beginning to write can keep a deployment journal — what they did today, what they want to tell the deployed parent, a drawing of something funny that happened. Writing it down externalizes it. The deployed parent can do the same and share it on calls.

**Video recordings.** Have the deployed parent record themselves telling the child something specific — a favorite memory, a silly joke, a promise. Play it on hard nights. The voice is irreplaceable.

**Community.** Military family support groups, school counselors, other military families. Humans who have been through it. This matters more and more as the child gets older.

**Letting them feel it without fixing it.** This is the hard one. Sitting with a crying 6-year-old and not trying to cheer them up, not offering distractions, just saying "I know. I miss them too." That permission is its own kind of medicine.

## A book made just for your child

At six, your child may have started to distinguish between books made for everyone and books made for them specifically. A book with their name in it, their family in it, their parent's face in the illustrations — that's a different object than a library book. It belongs to them in a way that no shared story does.

Plubis builds custom picture books in about five minutes. Your family's details, your child's name, the deployed parent as a real character in their own story. The deployed parent can contribute the words before they leave — so your child has something their parent made for them, specifically, while they're apart.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about parents getting divorced
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through divorce. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds can hold a grudge.

They're in school now. They understand cause and effect. They've started to have opinions about fairness that they defend loudly. And when something hurts them — something big, like a family breaking apart — they sometimes find one person to be angry at. Maybe a parent. Maybe themselves. Maybe the situation itself, which they've decided to personalize.

Six is also the age where kids will sometimes perform okayness at school, hold it together all day, and then fall apart the moment they're home safe. That's not a sign they're damaged — it's actually a sign of healthy attachment. But it's exhausting to be the place where the falling apart happens.

Picture books still work at six, even though they might resist them as "babyish." The trick is not to frame it as a feelings lesson. Frame it as "I found a book I wanted to read with you." Let them decide how seriously to take it. They usually take it pretty seriously.

## What's different at six

By six, kids are building a clear internal narrative about the divorce. Who did what. Who left. Whose fault it was. That narrative is almost certainly incomplete and probably wrong in at least one direction — but it's theirs and they're attached to it.

Books can gently offer alternative framings. Not to tell them they're wrong, but to widen what they think is possible. Maybe it's not anyone's fault in the way they've decided. Maybe both parents really do still love them. Maybe other kids feel exactly this.
