The social piece is much bigger at six than at five. Does their best friend's family know? Did anyone at school figure it out? Is their life going to look different from their friends' lives? These anxieties are real and they don't always show up as sad — they show up as embarrassed, or prickly, or bizarrely focused on something like whether they can have the same lunchbox at both houses.

## Books that work at six

**"Dinosaurs Divorce" by Marc Brown and Laurene Krasny Brown** — I've recommended this across older ages and I'll recommend it here too, because it handles something no other book on this list does: the chapter on what happens when parents start dating again, and what it feels like when a parent introduces someone new. At six, that's a live possibility. Better to have a book that addresses it calmly than to have it ambush everyone.

**"Was It the Chocolate Pudding?" by Sandra Levins** — The older sibling dynamic in this book is genuinely useful for a six-year-old. Aaron's brother is further along in processing and has slightly different feelings — which models the idea that different family members can be at different places with the same thing. That's a lesson a six-year-old can actually use when they feel like no one else in the family is as sad as they are, or more sad.

**"Two Homes" by Claire Masurel** — I know. It's in every single one of these lists. But here's why: a six-year-old going through the acute phase of divorce, in the middle of the instability, sometimes needs the simplest possible version of "both places are home and you are loved." Not a complex narrative. Just that. Some nights that's the right book.

**"Standing on My Own Two Feet" by Tamara Schmitz** — The protagonist in this book feels slightly older than the characters in most of these, which lands differently at six. She's figuring out that having two homes doesn't mean she's broken. The rhyming text makes it easier to read multiple times without it feeling relentlessly heavy.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — At six, kids can be philosophical about this one in a way younger kids can't quite reach. The idea that love is a connection that doesn't require physical proximity — that you can miss someone and still be connected to them — lands with real force at this age. It won't answer the "why" questions, but it addresses the loss-of-access grief in a way that's surprisingly helpful.

**"When My Parents Forgot How to Be Friends" by Jennifer Moore-Mallinos** — Worth revisiting at six. The child in the book is navigating the tension of loving both parents without having to choose sides. That tension is very real at this age, and seeing it named in a book — not resolved, just named and held — gives kids language they didn't have before.

## A note on anger

Six-year-olds can be angry. Specifically, targeted, sustained angry. They might be angry at the parent they perceive as having caused the divorce, or at both parents, or at the situation in a generalized way that gets aimed at whoever is closest.

If the anger is showing up around the books — refusing to read them, getting hostile when you try — that's useful information, not a failure. It means there's something that needs more space than a book can give it.

A play therapist, a school counselor, even a structured conversation where you ask "what are you most angry about?" and then just listen — these help. Don't try to talk your child out of their anger. Just let it be heard.

## Reading together at this age — honestly

Some nights they'll lean in and want to talk through every page. Some nights they'll shrug and ask to watch something instead. Let both be okay.

The fact that you're reaching for books at all — that you're trying to find the right words even when you're exhausted and overwhelmed yourself — that matters more than whether any single reading session goes well. Your kid sees the effort.

You don't have to be perfectly calm. You don't have to have all the answers. You just have to keep showing up. That's what the whole book-at-bedtime thing is really about.

## Something built just for them

At six, a child can tell the difference between a book that sort of describes their situation and a book that's about *them* — their name, their dog, their actual bedroom at both houses.

If you want that level of specificity, Plubis can build it. You describe the situation and your child's details, we generate a picture book in about five minutes. It's personalized, warm, and honest — the same things you're looking for in any of the books above, just built for one specific child.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about going to the doctor
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through doctor visits. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds are in kindergarten or just out of it, and they've absorbed a lot of information about how the world works. They know about germs now, probably. They may have heard things on the playground about shots. They might have an older sibling whose medical anxiety has been accidentally transferred to them, or a friend who made the whole thing sound like a war zone.

Six is also the age when kids sometimes start to think picture books are "for babies." You've maybe already hit that. The eye roll. "That's a little kid book."

But here's the thing: they still listen when you read to them. Even the ones who've decided they're too old. Reading aloud together is different from reading alone — it's something you're doing together, and that togetherness is what makes it work. A 6-year-old won't sit down and read a picture book about doctors by themselves and feel better about their appointment. But they'll lie next to you on the couch and listen, and they will absorb it in ways they don't know they're absorbing it.

So yes, picture books still. Different ones, though. Six-year-olds can handle more narrative complexity, more emotional ambiguity, more honest acknowledgment of the uncomfortable parts. They're also old enough to find genuine humor in the absurd. Lean into all of that.

Also worth knowing: the 6-year-old checkup is usually lighter on vaccines than the 5-year one, depending on what was given at 4 or 5 and whether your child's practice did the kindergarten vaccines early. But there may still be a booster, or a flu shot, or a blood draw if anything came up flagged at the 5-year visit. Check with your pediatrician before the appointment so you're not caught off guard and neither is your kid.

## What's different at 6

At six, the emotional task is less about fear of the unknown and more about fear of losing control. Six-year-olds know what happens at the doctor. They know there might be a shot. And what they're actually afraid of is not having any say in the matter — being a passive recipient of something uncomfortable.

The best books for this age address that feeling, directly or indirectly. Stories where the child character actively participates, asks questions, uses coping tools, or gets some small form of agency in the process are more useful than stories where everything just goes fine because the doctor was nice.

Agency at the doctor's office is real, by the way. A 6-year-old can choose which arm to use. Can choose to count to ten. Can ask the nurse what they're doing before they do it. These are real choices and they matter enormously. Books that show characters exercising that kind of small agency plant the idea. You can build on it.

## Book picks

**"Franklin Goes to the Hospital" by Paulette Bourgeois, illustrated by Brenda Clark** — This is the strongest book for 6-year-olds in the whole genre, in my opinion. Franklin is facing something legitimately scary — surgery — and he's ashamed of being scared when everyone expects him to be brave. That tension between the expected performance of bravery and the actual experience of fear is something 6-year-olds understand acutely. They've been told to be brave. They've tried. It's not always possible. Franklin's doctor says something like "it's okay to be scared and brave at the same time" and that's a true thing that six-year-olds need permission to believe. Scholastic, 2000. Ages 4–8.

**"Curious George Goes to the Hospital" by Margret and H.A. Rey** — At six, kids can appreciate this at something close to its full value. George's approach — boundless curiosity, no self-pity, genuine interest in every person and machine in the hospital — is a model worth having. The other patients on the ward become friends. The nurses have personalities. The hospital is strange but navigable. For a 6-year-old who is facing something bigger than a routine checkup, or who has a sibling or parent with a medical situation, this book offers a compassionate portrait of what hospitals actually feel like from the inside. 1966, Houghton Mifflin.

**"The Berenstain Bears Go to the Doctor" by Stan and Jan Berenstain** — Even at six, this one is worth reading because of the shot. I will say this for the sixth time: the honesty about the needle, the honesty about Papa Bear's nervousness, the honest depiction of discomfort followed by getting through it — that sequence is a template for actual emotional courage. And at six, a child can watch Papa Bear be nervous and say "I get that" in a way they couldn't at three. The meta-point that even adults find this hard is available to them now. Random House, 1981. Yes, still.

**"It's Time for Your Checkup" by Shani Thornton** — At six, the coping strategies in this book become things your child can actually own. They can practice the deep breathing on their own. They can decide ahead of time which hand to squeeze. The book is shorter and less literary than the others, but the specificity of the coping tools is worth the trade-off for a kid with real appointment anxiety. Ages 4+. Pair it with something more narrative.

**"Going to the Doctor" by Anne Civardi, illustrated by Stephen Cartwright** — Six-year-olds can read most of this themselves. I mean that literally — the text is simple enough for a newly reading child to work through, and doing so gives them a sense of ownership over the preparation. Let them read it to you. They'll correct your mistakes. It's charming. And a child who has "read" the doctor visit already is measurably less anxious walking into the real one. Usborne, current editions.

**"Maisy Goes to the Eye Doctor" by Lucy Cousins** — I'm adding this one because 6-year-olds often have vision screening at this age and eye doctor anxiety is weirdly specific and underserved by books. Maisy needs glasses (spoiler) and the eye doctor visit is gentle and thorough and ultimately exciting — new glasses! Lucy Cousins' clean bold illustrations remain effective even at six, and the message that a medical visit can result in something that actually helps you see better is relevant and reassuring. Candlewick, 2022. Recent and recommended.

**"Froggy Goes to the Doctor" by Jonathan London, illustrated by Frank Remkiewicz** — For 6-year-olds who have grown up with Froggy, this book is comfort. For those who haven't met him, it's an introduction worth making. The humor at six is more sophisticated — they get why the underpants moment is specifically funny, rather than just generically surprising. And the structure of the whole book — chaotic approach, slightly absurdist checkup, triumphant exit with lollipop — mirrors the actual emotional arc of a doctor visit in a way that's almost therapeutic. Viking, 2002.

## A bonus section: when your 6-year-old insists picture books are for babies

This happens. Here's what I do: I read it to myself, out loud, while they're in the same room. I don't invite them to watch. I comment occasionally — "oh, this part is good" — and I laugh at the funny parts.

Nine times out of ten they wander over.

Because they're still six. The "too old" declaration is real but it's fragile. You don't fight it — you make the book interesting enough that they abandon the declaration on their own. Then you just... read together, without making a thing of it.

Alternately: you read it to a stuffed animal while your child watches. "Okay, Elephant, listen carefully because we're going to the doctor soon." Your kid will absolutely take over.

## Reading together

At six, the richest conversations happen around the complicated moments — the ones where the character is scared and trying not to be. "Why do you think Franklin didn't want to tell anyone he was scared?" "Have you ever felt like that?" You might get a real answer. You might not. Either way the door is open.

The post-read role-play is still powerful at this age. Let them be the doctor. Ask them what they're checking for. Ask them to explain what the stethoscope does. Explaining something — even explaining it slightly wrong — consolidates understanding in a way listening can't.

For the shot specifically, at this age I recommend the blunt approach: "You've had shots before. What's the hardest part for you?" Let them name it — the anticipation, the actual moment, the fact that you can't make it stop. Then you can actually address what they said. Generic reassurance doesn't touch any of those specific fears. Named responses to named fears do.

## When a book isn't enough — conversation starters

- "What do you already know about what happens at the doctor?" (Finding out what they've heard, including the playground mythology, is crucial.)
- "Is there anything you want to ask the doctor yourself?" (Six is old enough for a 30-second direct conversation with the pediatrician, and most pediatricians love when kids ask questions.)
- "What's the thing you're dreading most? How long do you think it actually takes?" (Timing reality-checks are powerful. The actual shot is two seconds. Even kids who know this haven't always applied it.)
- "You've done this before. What did you do last time that helped?" (Their own history of success is more convincing than anything external.)

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what I keep coming back to. Franklin is a turtle. George is a monkey. Froggy is a frog. Even the most scared 6-year-old knows, on some level, that they are not any of those things.

A book where the main character is named what your kid is named, is afraid of exactly what your kid is afraid of, has a doctor's office that sounds like their actual doctor's office, and gets through it — that book does something different. It's not a story about a character. It's a rehearsal.

At [Plubis](https://plubis.app), you answer a few questions about your child — name, worry, comfort object, what helps them feel brave — and we build that book. It takes about five minutes. You can have it before the appointment. Print it or read it on your phone in the waiting room.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about the first day of school
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through first day of school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the age where kids will sometimes tell you picture books are "for babies." And then ask you to read them one at bedtime anyway. Both things are true simultaneously and you just have to go with it.

First grade is a different kind of first day than kindergarten. The novelty is gone — they know what a classroom is, they know what a teacher is, they know roughly how the day goes. And somehow that makes it harder for some kids, not easier. It's not the unknown this time. It's the returning-to-something that was complicated last year. Or the fear of a new teacher who might not be as good as the last one. Or the best friend who ended up in a different class.

Six-year-olds need books that take them seriously.

## What's actually going on at six

The first day fear at six is usually more specific than it was at five. It's not "what is school?" It's "what if my best friend is in a different class" or "what if I can't read as fast as the other kids" or "what if the teacher is strict" or simply "last year was hard and I don't want it to be hard again."

Books that address vague anxiety are less useful here. Books that capture specific social dynamics — friendship, exclusion, the courage to try again, what happens when an adult actually sees you — land much better at this age.

Six-year-olds can also handle bittersweet. They don't need every story to end triumphantly. Sometimes a book that says "hard things are hard AND you make it through" is more honest and more useful than a tidy happy ending. They're ready for that.

## What to read

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — The best book for this age on this topic, full stop. It's about the moment you realize you're different from the other kids in your class — your lunch is different, your summer was different, your family looks different — and the bravery it takes to share your story anyway. Woodson writes it as poetry. The message isn't "your difference doesn't matter" (which is patronizing and also false) but "your story has weight, and saying it out loud takes courage." Six-year-olds understand that instinctively. Read this one slowly. Put it down at the end and don't immediately explain it.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Harder than "The Day You Begin." A new girl named Maya arrives at school and the narrator, Chloe, keeps refusing to be kind to her. Then Maya is gone. Chloe has to live with that. It doesn't end happily. There's no redemption arc. Chloe just has to carry it. Six-year-olds can handle that kind of weight — and they should encounter it, in the safe space of a story, before they encounter it in real life. The lesson about what you can't take back sticks in a way that no "be kind" poster ever will.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — The premise: a kid has an enemy and his dad has a recipe for Enemy Pie. The recipe requires spending a whole day with the enemy. What happens next is exactly what you'd hope. This is the book for the six-year-old who has already decided there's a kid they don't like, or who's afraid of becoming someone else's enemy. It's funny, it's specific, and the social insight is real. Works before AND after the first day.

**"The Recess Queen" by Alexis O'Neill** — Mean Jean rules the playground. Nobody questions it. Then a small new kid arrives who doesn't know the rules and invites Jean to jump rope. This is absolutely the book for the six-year-old who's anxious about the social hierarchy of recess — who's in charge, where you fit, what it costs to be kind to the wrong person. The resolution is satisfying. The big-font bold text is fun to read aloud. One of the most underrated books on this whole list.

**"Chrysanthemum" by Kevin Henkes** — Still works at six, though they might roll their eyes at the mouse thing first. Read it anyway. The moment where the music teacher reveals her own long, unusual name and the whole classroom flips is one of the best moments in picture book literature. A six-year-old who's been on either side of social cruelty will recognize it. Sometimes a mouse in a picture book says what you can't say to each other directly.

**"Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Pena** — Doesn't mention school at all. Doesn't matter. It's about finding beauty in an ordinary uncomfortable situation, and a grandmother who helps a kid see what he's missing by wishing for something different. First grade is often ordinary and uncomfortable. This book reframes that. The illustrations by Christian Robinson are the best in any book on this list — the kind you sit with on a page before turning.

**"Ish" by Peter H. Reynolds** — For the six-year-old who's already been told by another kid (or a teacher, which is worse) that their drawing or their story or their answer wasn't good enough. Ramon stops making art because his brother laughs. His sister shows him a way back in. The concept of "ish" — close enough, real enough, fully yours — is something to carry into a classroom where things start to be graded and compared. Read this before the first day.

## Reading with a six-year-old (they'll argue with the book)

Good. Let them. A six-year-old who says "I wouldn't have done that, that's dumb" is engaging. Ask them what they would have done instead. Let the character be wrong. Let the disagreement be the conversation. You don't need them to agree with the book's moral — you need them to think about it.

At six they'll sometimes start reading ahead of you. Let them. Then go back and read the pages they skipped slowly. You'll often find the thing that made them rush past was exactly the thing worth sitting with.

Don't make it homework. Don't quiz them at the end. Don't say "and what does that teach us?" Just read it. Let it land. The lesson is already in the story and they already absorbed it — they just don't need to perform that for you.

## The books that probably won't work at six

The separation-from-parent books are less useful now. "The Kissing Hand" is beautiful but a six-year-old often finds it babyish, which is its own kind of useful information. Their fear has shifted. The books that work now are about peers and teachers and whether you belong — not about whether you'll miss your mom.

## An honest note on first-day nerves at this age

Some six-year-olds just don't want to talk about how they're feeling about school. The book might go sideways — they'll roll their eyes, say it's boring, ask for something else. That's fine. You can say "okay, different book" and just let them have heard it anyway. They did. They just don't want to discuss it and that's a legitimate position.

And honestly? That's also okay. Not every kid processes through conversation. Some of them need to go through the first day and come home and eat a snack and then, three days later, tell you something they were nervous about. That's fine. The book you read planted something. You don't need to harvest it in real time.

## When a book isn't enough

If your six-year-old has persistent school refusal — anxiety that's escalating rather than fading week by week — or if they're describing specific social difficulties (bullying, exclusion, a teacher they're genuinely afraid of), that needs real attention beyond a picture book. Talk to the teacher first. Then to your pediatrician. An early childhood therapist who specializes in school anxiety can make a real difference at this age, before the patterns get more entrenched. Books are a tool for connection and conversation, not a replacement for professional support.

## Make a book made just for your child

Six-year-olds know when a book is about them. That's different, and it's powerful. Plubis creates custom illustrated picture books — your child's name, their situation, them as the main character. Not Chrysanthemum. Not Ramon. Your kid. You can build one in five minutes, and it might be the one book on this whole list they actually ask for again on day twenty, when the first-day magic has worn off and school is just hard and regular. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about losing a grandparent
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a grandparent dying. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the age when they start to understand that this will happen to everyone they love.

I don't know how to make that sentence lighter because it isn't lighter. That's what grief at six is. The six-year-old who loses a grandparent isn't just grieving one person — they're, for the first time, building a mental model of loss itself. *People die. The people I love will die. Someday I will die.* That whole architecture goes up in a matter of days, sometimes hours.

And they need you to be honest with them. Not clinical. Not distant. Just honest.

Here's what I've noticed talking to parents of six-year-olds going through this: the parents who tried to protect their kids from the truth ended up with more questions, more anxiety, more sleepless nights than the ones who just sat down and said "yes, Grandpa died, I miss him too, do you want to talk about it?" Protection, at six, can accidentally communicate that death is too terrible to discuss. And then they carry it alone.

You don't want that.

## What grief looks like at six

Six is first grade territory. They're in school. They have classmates, teachers, a social world that exists outside your line of sight. Grief at six leaks into all of it in ways you might not immediately see.

Things that show up: school-reported changes (teachers see kids who are quieter than usual, distracted, who burst into tears over something small), anger (the grief-anger connection is strong at six — they don't know how to say "I'm heartbroken" so it comes out as kicking a chair or screaming at a sibling), persistent questions about your mortality (more sophisticated than at four — not just "will you die" but "how old will you be when you die" and "will you die before I grow up"), dreams about the person who died (this can go on for months, it's healthy not pathological), and a new relationship with their own body (they might start noticing when they feel sick and worrying that they're "going to die like Grandma").

All of this is grief doing its work. Your job is to stay present and keep the channels open.

## Book picks for six-year-olds

**"Cry, Heart, But Never Break" by Glenn Ringtved** — I'd put this first on every list for every age from five up. Death comes as a character. He's not scary — he's kind and sad and wise. He tells the children that grief exists because love exists. The ending leaves room for both sadness and peace. The illustrations are haunting in the best possible way. Six-year-olds are fully ready for this one. Read it out loud slowly.

**"The Next Place" by Warren Hanson** — Gentle, lyrical, about what might come after death. Good if your family holds spiritual beliefs and your six-year-old wants to know "where did Grandma go." It doesn't impose a single belief system — it creates space. Beautiful watercolor illustrations. Your library almost certainly has it.

**"Charlotte's Web" by E.B. White** — Yes, I know. It's long. It's a chapter book. But six-year-olds can follow it as a read-aloud, and there is no better prepared experience for grief than having loved Charlotte before she dies. If you haven't read it together yet, now is the time. The death scene is one of the most honest things ever written for children. Let them cry. Cry with them.

**"Badger's Parting Gifts" by Susan Varley** — Still perfect at six. The concept of the gifts someone leaves behind in us — the things they taught us, the ways they shaped us — lands with particular weight now that they're old enough to really reflect on what the grandparent gave them.

**"The Memory Box: A Book About Grief" by Joanna Rowland** — A child creates a box of memories about someone who died — drawings, photos, objects, written memories. This one pairs beautifully with actually making a memory box together. Helps children understand that remembering is an action they can take.

**"Lifetimes" by Bryan Mellonie and Robert Ingpen** — Read it again. At six, the conversation it opens is different again. The natural world framing lands philosophically now. Worth revisiting.

**"Grandfather's Journey" by Allen Say** — The longing in this book — for the person, for the place, for the time you can't get back — hits hard at six. A grandparent who taught things, traveled, lived a full life before your child knew them. The pictures feel like memory. Read this one slow.

## How to use books at six — the discussion layer

Six-year-olds can have real conversations about books. Use that.

After reading, try: "If you could have put something in Badger's parting gifts — something Grandpa taught you — what would it be?" or "The book says love goes on even after someone dies. Do you believe that?" or "What do you miss most about her?"

Let their answers surprise you. Don't correct or redirect. If they say "I miss her kitchen" or "I miss her remote control" — that's specific and real and important. Let it be important.

And tell them yours. What you miss. What the person meant to you. Six-year-olds are starting to understand that their parent is also a person who loved this grandparent, who is also grieving, who doesn't have all the answers. That shared humanity is not a burden on them — it's a gift. It shows them that grief is part of love, and love is the point.

## The memory project — a bonus section

One of the best things you can do for a grieving six-year-old has nothing to do with books at all.

Make something.

A memory box (like "The Memory Box" suggests). A drawing book about the person. A playlist of songs they liked. A recipe written down with their details. A video where the child talks about what they remember — ten minutes of footage, saved, for when they're older.

Six-year-olds are just old enough to participate meaningfully in this kind of project. They can draw. They can dictate memories for you to write down. They can hold the photos and tell you which ones to include.

And in twenty years, they'll be grateful you did it.

Don't wait until the grief fades. It won't fade — it'll transform. Start the project now, while the memories are fresh and the love is sharp.

## When a book isn't enough

If your six-year-old is struggling for more than a few weeks — not sleeping, not engaging with school, expressing guilt ("it's my fault Grandpa died") or persistent fear — please reach out to your pediatrician. Childhood grief counselors exist and they're good at this. There is no weakness in asking for help. There's only love.

## Make a book made for your child

Every book on this list is about someone else's grandparent. They're wise and warm and true — but they don't know your child's specific grief. They don't know it's the grandfather who taught them to fish, or the grandmother they only got to see twice a year, or the grandparent who was sick for a long time and now your child feels guilty for also feeling relieved.

Those specifics matter. They're where the real healing lives.

Plubis generates a picture book about your child's actual situation. You tell us a few things about your child and the grandparent they lost. We build a book that speaks to them directly — their name, their feelings, their story — in five minutes.

You get a PDF. Read it tonight.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about the loss of a pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through losing a pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
A six-year-old in first grade told his class that his dog died. His teacher said he was "very brave." He came home furious. "I wasn't being brave," he said. "I was just sad."

That's six. They have opinions about how their grief gets described. They're entering a world that doesn't always know how to hold it for them. The right books help.

## What grief looks like at six

Six-year-olds understand death — the permanence of it, the finality. But big feelings still come out sideways, in tantrums or regression or just a week of being quietly off. They might also be managing grief at school, which is its own thing. You can't cry about your cat in the middle of circle time.

At six, kids often compartmentalize. They can seem totally fine all day and then break down at bedtime. That's not fake during the day — that's them doing what they can to hold it together, and bedtime being the first safe moment to let it out. Books at bedtime create a container for the feeling.

Six-year-olds can handle complexity. Real ambiguity. They don't need a bow on the ending. They'll reject books that feel falsely resolved — they're just starting to be sophisticated readers.

## Books that work for a six-year-old

**"The Tenth Good Thing About Barney" by Judith Viorst, illustrated by Erik Blegvad**

The best entry point. A boy's cat dies and he tries to think of ten good things about Barney for a backyard funeral. The book takes death seriously — Barney is really gone, the boy really cries, his friend says the wrong thing ("he's in cat heaven now" — the boy isn't sure he believes that). The resolution is secular and grounded: Barney is in the earth, helping things grow. That's a real idea a six-year-old can hold. The book also models something important: having your own feelings about death, separate from what other people tell you to feel. For a six-year-old starting to form their own inner world, that matters.

**"The Rough Patch" by Brian Lies**

This is the one I keep coming back to for this age. Caldecott Honor, and it earned it. Evan and his dog tend a beautiful garden together. When the dog dies, Evan destroys every last thing in it. A pumpkin vine creeps in on its own, and after fighting it for a while, Evan lets it grow. It's about grief turning into destruction and destruction eventually giving way to something new — not "healed," not "over," just open again. The art carries the emotional weight. Six-year-olds can read this and not fully articulate what it did to them, but they'll feel it. And sometimes that's what they need.

**"An Ofrenda for Perro" by Judith Valdés B., illustrated by Carlos Vélez Aguilera**

One of the most recent books on this list and one of the most beautiful. When Benito's dog dies, he builds an ofrenda — a Día de los Muertos altar — with photographs, marigolds, and things Perro loved. The book captures grief in the everyday details: the way the house feels different, the specific things he misses. Six-year-olds are ready to engage with the idea that different families honor death differently, and that making something physical — an altar, a box, a drawing — is a real way to grieve. Even if your family doesn't celebrate Día de los Muertos, the ritual is entirely adaptable. Try your library.

So. "The Rough Patch" and "An Ofrenda for Perro" together are a strong combination if you're looking for two books to alternate across a hard week. Different emotions, different cultures, both honest.

**"Henry and Bea" by Jessixa Bagley**

Slightly underrated. Henry has lost his cat and checked out. His friend Bea finds him, doesn't push, doesn't fix, just shows up and sits. For a six-year-old navigating school where friends might not know what to say — or might say the wrong things — this book opens a real conversation: what does a good friend do when someone is sad? The answer here isn't cheerful distraction. It's presence. One of the better lessons in friendship I've seen in a picture book.

**"The Invisible Leash" by Patrice Karst, illustrated by Joanne Lew-Vriethoff**

Some six-year-olds will find this too abstract. Others will completely love it. The idea — that even after a pet dies, an invisible leash connects your heart to theirs — either clicks immediately or it doesn't. If it clicks, it becomes a real comfort in concept form, something your child can return to privately when they miss their pet. Parents of kids who've taken to this book say they mention the leash unprompted for months.

**"The Goodbye Book" by Todd Parr**

Yes, Todd Parr usually skews younger, but this book earns its place at six. The fish who loses a companion goes through the full arc: denial, anger, not wanting to eat, pretending everything is fine. For a six-year-old who's watched themselves do some of those things after their pet died, there's real comfort in "oh, even a fish does that." Short enough for a night when neither of you has much left.

## A note on what not to do

Don't pick a book that rushes to the happy ending. Six-year-olds will notice. They'll feel that the book is trying to make them feel better too fast, and it'll feel like you're in a hurry for them to be done grieving.

Also — not every reading session has to lead to a conversation. Sometimes you just read it and go to sleep. The book did its work.

## When a book isn't enough

Six-year-olds may start asking larger questions: do all animals die? do all people die? what happens after? These are good questions. You don't have to have perfect answers. "I don't know exactly, but I believe..." is an honest starting point. Sit with them in the uncertainty. That's more useful than false certainty.

If they're having trouble sleeping, if school is suffering, if it's been more than two or three weeks and the grief seems to be intensifying rather than easing — trust your instincts and talk to someone. Their teacher. Their pediatrician. A school counselor. You know your kid.

## Make a book made for your child

Every book on this list is about a fictional pet. A fictional child. None of them know that your kid's hamster was named after a cartoon character, or that your dog used to steal socks specifically from the laundry basket. The specifics are what make a loss real — and a book that holds those specifics can be something your child keeps for a long time. At Plubis, you can make a picture book about your child and their actual pet in about five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about being more independent
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through independence and big-kid skills. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Something shifts at six that nobody warns you about.

At six, the independence stops being a battle you're managing and starts being a negotiation you're losing. They're in school full-time. They have opinions about homework. They have social dynamics you're not fully aware of. They know things — little things, things they learned on the playground or from a friend's older sibling — and they deploy this knowledge with the quiet confidence of someone who believes they have significantly outgrown your guidance.

You have not been fired. You have been demoted.

And that's actually how it's supposed to go. At six, kids are building a self that can function without you nearby. That's healthy. That's the goal. The fact that it feels like a small, sock-wearing person questioning your authority every 45 minutes is just the packaging.

Books for six-year-olds have to meet them where they are: smarter than they look, aware of what other people think, carrying a growing internal life that doesn't always show up on the surface. The best ones don't talk down to them. They trust them.


## *A Bad Case of Stripes* by David Shannon

Camilla Cream loves lima beans. She doesn't eat them because everyone at school thinks they're disgusting. On the first day of school, she wakes up covered in stripes. As the days go on, she transforms to match whatever anyone says — polka dots, the American flag, a Christmas tree. Doctors can't help her. Specialists can't help her. She gets worse and worse, more and more a reflection of what everyone around her expects, until an old woman comes to the door and offers her lima beans.

The cure is the thing she was hiding.

David Shannon published this in 1998 and it won the Montana Treasure State Picture Book Award and made the National Education Association's Teachers' Top 100 Books for Children. It's frequently used in classrooms, but it belongs at home too — especially the home of a six-year-old who is starting to understand, in a new way, that what other people think of them is a thing that exists and has weight.

Because here's what happens at six: the peer group becomes real. Real in the sense that it produces actual social pressure, real shame, real desire to belong. Camilla's stripes are an exaggerated version of something your six-year-old is already doing — editing themselves to fit what other people expect.

This book is about independence from other people's opinions of you. That's a more sophisticated version of independence than "I can pour my own milk," and your six-year-old is ready for it.

Read this one together and then sit with whatever comes up. Something will.


## *Ish* by Peter H. Reynolds

At five, this book is about coping with imperfection. At six, it does something different: it becomes about creative autonomy.

Ramon's journey — from drawing freely to being laughed at to being unable to draw to being freed by his sister's ish-collection — maps onto something that happens to a lot of six-year-olds. They start receiving systematic feedback from the world (grades, corrections, "do it this way") and their natural creative instinct starts to go quiet. It gets replaced by correctness.

*Ish* argues against that. Loudly and beautifully.

For a six-year-old who used to draw for hours and has started hesitating, who used to tell sprawling stories and has started worrying whether they're right — give them this. Read it yourself first, if you want. It's also a book for adults who stopped doing the thing they loved because they weren't good enough at it.

Peter Reynolds intended this as a companion to *The Dot*, but I think *Ish* is sharper. The stakes are higher. Ramon doesn't just need a push to start — he needs rescue from what the world did to him after he started. Six-year-olds know that feeling already.


## *Roxaboxen* by Alice McLerran, illustrated by Barbara Cooney

This is the book on this list I'd give to every parent of a six-year-old, separate from whether their child is ready for it.

Roxaboxen is the story of real children who built a real imaginary town in the desert — complete with governance, economy, and property laws. All child-made, all child-governed. Marian is the mayor. They have a bakery and a city hall and a cemetery with a popcorn-tin headstone.

At six, kids are capable of exactly this kind of world-building, and they're doing it constantly — in backyards, in cushion forts, in the corner of the playground. Roxaboxen sees that, takes it seriously, and gives it grandeur.

What I want parents to notice: Roxaboxen isn't just about children being imaginative. It's about children exercising actual governance and decision-making skills without adult mediation. They make rules. They solve disputes. They decide what's a bakery and what's a house. These are independence skills. Real ones.

Barbara Cooney's illustrations are perfect — warm and sun-soaked and unhurried. They make the place feel genuinely real. Which, for the children who played there, it was.

Read this when your six-year-old's play is getting crowded out by screens or structured activities. It's a gentle argument for the value of unstructured, self-directed time.


## *Stand Tall, Molly Lou Melon* by Patty Lovell, illustrated by David Catrow

At five, kids respond to this book with something like awe — "she just DID it." At six, they understand it more politically. There's a bully. The bully has social power. Molly Lou doesn't fight the power structure, she sidesteps it by being so completely herself that the structure becomes irrelevant.

That's a move six-year-olds need to see modelled, because they're starting to understand social hierarchies on the playground. Who's "popular." Who gets to decide what the group does. And the temptation to edit yourself to fit in — to be less, to be quiet, to not sing in the frog voice — is real and starts early.

Molly Lou Melon sings in the frog voice.

This book is one of the only picture books I'm aware of that presents a complete anti-conformity arc for a young child in a school context, with realistic stakes (this is a real bully at a real school, not a cartoon) and a payoff that isn't "the bully learned their lesson." Ronald Durkin doesn't reform. He just... stops. Because Molly Lou doesn't give his opinion any space to land.

That's the more honest ending, and six-year-olds are old enough to appreciate the honesty.


## *Amazing Grace* by Mary Hoffman, illustrated by Caroline Binch

At six, kids are fully in school and beginning to understand that the world has ideas about who they are and what they get to do. Amazing Grace is the picture book that handles this most directly, most beautifully, and most usefully.

Grace gets told no. By other kids, not adults. That's important — it's not a teacher or a parent limiting her, it's the casual social enforcement of a classroom. And it stings more than adult prohibition because it comes from peers.

Her grandmother takes her to see a ballet. She sees someone who looks like her doing the thing she was told was impossible. She goes to the audition. She prepares. She wins.

The preparation detail matters. Mary Hoffman doesn't let Grace just believe harder and win — she has to work. She has to earn it. The independence is built, not wished into existence.

For any six-year-old navigating identity, belonging, and what they're allowed to aspire to: this is the book.


## What six-year-old independence actually looks like on a bad day

It looks like this: they refuse to put their shoes on. You give them space and they still won't. You offer help and they reject it. You tell them you'll leave without them and they call your bluff. You are now ten minutes late and standing in a hallway negotiating with a person one-quarter your size who has decided that this is the hill.

This is not independence as a noble developmental journey. This is independence as a Tuesday morning power struggle, and no picture book fully prepares you for it.

But here's what I actually believe: the same kid who is refusing the shoes because they need to feel like they're in charge of something — that kid is also the one who will, in a few years, be capable of real self-direction. The stubbornness is the seed. It's just currently expressed in the least convenient possible form.

The books help with the bigger frame. They show your six-year-old a version of themselves that uses the stubbornness for something. Grace uses it to audition. Molly Lou uses it to keep singing. Ramon's sister uses it to save her brother's discarded art.

You're planting seeds. You won't see them sprout this week.


## A section about rereading old books

Six-year-olds are at the exact age where returning to books from earlier childhood is suddenly interesting to them. Not because they need the comfort of the familiar (though there's some of that) — but because they're old enough to notice things they missed.

If you have *No David!* or *Owl Babies* or *Froggy Gets Dressed* still on a shelf: pull them out. Let your six-year-old read them to you. Watch what they notice. The book hasn't changed. Their reading of it will have changed completely.

This is one of the pleasures of having young children and being paying attention: you get to watch the same story become a different story as the reader grows.


## One more thing

Six-year-olds are starting to understand that stories can be *about them*. Not in an abstract "I relate to this character" way — in a specific, delighted, "why does this book know about the thing that happened to me" way.

A personalised picture book, made specifically for them, with their name and face and their exact current situation woven into the story, does something different from any published book. It says: your experience is worth a story. Your specific version of this thing matters.

At six, that lands.

**The first one is free.**

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about a friend moving away
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a friend moving away. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds know exactly what happened.

That's both the hard part and the useful part. They know their friend moved far away. They understand distance now. They understand that you can't just pop over anymore, can't just knock on that door after school. They understand — really understand — that things are different and will stay different.

And they're feeling it in ways that map quite closely to how adults feel it. The weird emptiness of a friendship suddenly gone. The specific grief of losing your *person* — the one who got your jokes, the one you always sat next to, the one whose house was the house where things just worked. That's a real loss. Six-year-olds deserve to have it taken seriously.

But they're still six. Books still work. Reading together still works. In fact, at six, you have something you didn't have at younger ages: the ability to have a real conversation alongside the book. Not therapy. Just two people (one very small) sitting with a story and talking about what it means.

Let's get into it.

## What actually lands at age 6

Six-year-olds are done with books that sugarcoat. Instantly done. They've seen through the too-fast happy ending and they find it insulting. A book that rushes to "and then she made a new best friend and it was even better" will lose them. Not because they don't want hope — they do — but because they need the sad to be real first.

They're also reading (or starting to read), which changes the relationship with books. They're looking at the text. They might read some of it themselves. That means words matter even more than they did at four.

The best books at six:
— Let the main character really miss their friend, without fixing it immediately
— Show some concrete action the character takes (writes, creates, reaches out)
— Are honest about the complexity of emotions (sad and angry and scared and hopeful all at once)
— Have illustrations that reward lingering

You can also start pulling from early chapter books and longer picture books now. Six is a wide range.

## Book picks

**"Ira Says Goodbye" by Bernard Waber** — This is the best book on this list for a six-year-old, full stop. Ira's devastated that Reggie is moving. Reggie seems fine — bafflingly, hurtfully fine. Ira doesn't understand why his best friend isn't as sad as he is. The emotional truth of this is painful and specific: when you love someone and they seem unbothered by leaving, it compounds the grief with confusion and a kind of self-doubt. Am I overreacting? Did this friendship mean as much to him as it did to me? Six-year-olds have these thoughts. They just don't have words for them yet. Waber gives them the words. The resolution — that Reggie was masking enormous sadness — is earned and warm. Long. Slow. Worth every page.

**"Evelyn Del Rey Is Moving Away" by Meg Medina** — Still perfect at six, maybe especially at six because the emotional register of the book matches exactly where they are. Two best friends. Last day together. Moving boxes as playground equipment. Sadness and ordinary childhood existing right next to each other. Newbery Medalist Meg Medina writes at the level of a child's actual heart, not what adults think children's hearts look like. If your six-year-old is a feeler — if they process things hard and deep — this book will mean something to them for years.

**"My Best Friend Moved Away" by Nancy Carlson** — Shorter and more direct, good for a child who wants simple and honest over lyrical. A best friend next door moves away. The child misses all of it — the proximity, the easiness, the shared history. There's gentle hope at the end without any false promise. Carlson's illustrations are expressive and warm. Good first read if your child is newly in the thick of it and you want to start simply before going deeper.

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst, illustrated by Joanne Lew-Vriethoff** — Six-year-olds who are skeptical, logical, not-buying-your-magic children might resist this one initially. "There's no real string, Mom." To which the honest answer is: no, not a literal one. But the feeling it describes — of love persisting across distance, of connection that doesn't require physical presence — is real, and six is old enough to sit with that idea without needing it to be literally true. Therapists use this book for exactly this age for a reason. Give it a chance. If they resist the magic, talk about what the string represents. That conversation is worth having.

**"Paper Planes" by Jim Helmore, illustrated by Richard Jones** — At six, this book comes alive in a practical way. Mia and Ben exchange paper planes when Ben has to move — folded, mailed, physical objects that travel between them as messengers of love and connection. Your six-year-old can actually do this. They can write a letter. They can fold a plane. They can mail something. Six-year-olds who feel helpless about a friend moving away often feel better when there's something concrete they can do. This book gives them the idea. Let them run with it.

**"Charlotte's Web" by E.B. White** — I know. It's not a picture book. And it's not about a friend moving away — it's about something much harder. But if your six-year-old is a reader, or if you're ready for a longer read-aloud project together, this book teaches everything about loving someone fiercely, about the fear of losing them, about the way love persists even after someone is gone. The friendship between Wilbur and Charlotte is one of the most fully realized friendships in children's literature. The grief in it is handled with such care and such honesty that children can feel it without being overwhelmed by it. A friend moving away is not the same as what happens to Charlotte — but the feelings of attachment and loss and the question of how you carry someone with you? All there. Worth starting. Try your library.

**"Each Little Bird That Sings" by Deborah Wiles** — A longer picture book / early chapter for strong six-year-old readers or as a read-aloud over several nights. Comfort Snowberger's family runs a funeral home in Mississippi, and the book is full of grief and loss and community and humor and love in equal measure. It's about how we hold losses in our bodies and what we do with them. Heavy, but handled with extraordinary lightness and warmth. For the six-year-old who is processing something bigger — not just a friend who moved, but a friend who was their person — this one goes deep. Try your library.

**"The Name Jar" by Yangsook Choi** — Unhei is a Korean girl who has just moved to America and is deciding whether to keep her Korean name or choose an American one. It's told from the perspective of the child who moved, which is a useful flip for the six-year-old who keeps thinking about where their friend went. "What is it like to be the one who moved?" This book answers that with specificity and warmth. It can open a conversation about what your child's friend might be experiencing right now — which is a profoundly empathy-building thing to do at six.

## Reading together at six: go deeper

Six is old enough for the real conversation. Not right after the book — maybe the next morning, or on a walk. The question that works best at this age:

"What's something you think your friend might miss about you?"

This is a different question from "what do you miss about them." It flips the perspective. It invites your child to see themselves as someone worth missing. That's important. Sometimes in the grief of losing a friendship, a kid can spiral into "maybe they don't even care" or "maybe I wasn't that important." This question is the antidote to that spiral.

Also worth doing after reading: make something together. A letter. A drawing. A little package. The act of making something for an absent friend is a way of loving them from a distance, and six-year-olds can fully understand and feel that.

## What the grief looks like at this age

At six, grief over a friend moving can look like:

— Social withdrawal at school (not wanting to risk a new friendship that might also end)
— Talking about the friend constantly, or not at all — both extremes
— Declining invitations to play with other kids ("they're not as good")
— Difficulty sleeping, with a vague "I'm sad" that they can't explain further
— Clinging harder to you

If you're seeing these things: they're normal. They're the signs of a child who loved someone and is working out what to do with that love now.

Books help. Conversations help. Time helps. Staying close helps most.

## Bonus section: what to do about the "will I ever have a friend like that again" question

Your six-year-old might ask this. Maybe not in those exact words. Maybe as "what if nobody ever likes me as much as Ella did?" Or just a lot of reluctance around new friendships.

Honest answer, which you can adapt: yes, you will. It won't be the same — it'll be different. But you have room for more than one important friendship in your life. Ella isn't replaced. She just has a place in you that no one else is going to take. And there's room for someone new too.

That's hard to communicate. Books help carry it. So does watching you maintain your own long-distance friendships — calling, texting, talking about people you've loved for years who live far away. They're watching how you do it.

## Make a book made for your child

At six, a child can read parts of their own custom book. They can point to their name and their friend's name and know: this story is about us. This friendship was real enough to be written down.

At Plubis, you can make a custom picture book about your child's specific friendship — not a generic story about a child who loses a friend, but a story with their name in it, their friend's name in it, the details only your family knows. Five minutes to build. A thing they'll keep.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about making new friends
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through making friends. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds already know that making friends is hard. They've been trying for years. They've had the success of it and the failure of it and the complicated middle of it where you think you have a friend and then something shifts and you're not sure anymore.

This is the age where the social stakes feel genuinely high — not because they're overreacting, but because the world is actually getting bigger and harder. First grade means more kids, older kids, a more complex social hierarchy, the beginning of real exclusion dynamics. The casual cruelty of six is more sophisticated than the casual cruelty of four. Kids this age know what they're doing, at least partially. They're experimenting with social power and they're doing it on each other.

A six-year-old who's lonely at school is carrying something real and heavy. And they often don't say it directly. They say "I don't want to go." They say "nobody likes me" in a tone that's trying to seem casual. They come home and go straight to the iPad and you can feel something in them that's pulled tight.

Books matter at this age for a different reason than they do at two or three. At six, your kid can take something from a story and actually apply it. They can meet a character who did the brave thing — walked over to someone eating alone, said "I know that's your seat but can I sit here" — and think: I could do that. They're capable of using fiction as a rehearsal space for real life. That's genuinely powerful.

## What's different about making friends at six

The social world at six has rules. Written and unwritten. Explicit and totally opaque. Your kid is trying to read a system they can only partially see, and some of the rules contradict each other (be yourself, but also be like everyone else; be confident, but don't brag; ask to play, but not too eagerly).

Exclusion becomes conscious at this age. Kids know when they're leaving someone out. That's new. And it means both that the cruelty is worse, and also that the kindness is more chosen — a six-year-old who includes someone when they didn't have to is performing an act of moral courage that's genuinely significant.

Six-year-olds are also capable of real loyalty. The best-friendship at six can be the one they talk about at thirty. And books about deep, complicated, weathered friendship — not brand-new-friend books but old-friend books — are worth reading now, because your kid is starting to understand what that could mean.

## The books

**"Frog and Toad Together" by Arnold Lobel** — The sequel to Frog and Toad Are Friends and in some ways the better book. Toad makes a list of things to do today and loses it. Frog and Toad try to grow a garden and have a long conversation about the seeds needing courage. They try to resist a plate of cookies and fail spectacularly. These are small domestic stories between two very old friends who take each other completely for granted in the best possible way. At six, your kid understands what it means to have someone who just KNOWS you — who doesn't need explanation. Frog and Toad is the book about that kind of friendship. Read both books back to back if you can.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — This one hurts in exactly the right way. A new girl named Maya comes to school. The narrator, Chloe, keeps refusing to be kind to her. When given chances to include Maya, Chloe chooses not to. Then Maya moves away. And Chloe has to live with what she didn't do. The book ends there. No redemption. No second chance. Chloe is left throwing stones into a pond thinking about what her teacher said about kindness — each kindness makes a ripple, and you can't get those moments back once they're gone.

Six-year-olds can hold that ending. They should encounter it before they have to live it. The book doesn't teach through instruction — it teaches through consequence, through the specific weight of an opportunity missed. I've seen kids go very quiet after this one and stay quiet for a while. That quiet is the whole point.

**"The Hundred Dresses" by Eleanor Estes** — This is a short chapter book from 1944 and it still lands harder than almost anything published since. Wanda Petronski wears the same blue dress every day and claims she has a hundred dresses at home in a row. The other girls laugh at her. When Wanda disappears from school, the truth about the hundred dresses comes out, and the girls who laughed at her have to live with that too. For the six-year-old who has ever been Maddie — the bystander who watched someone get excluded and didn't stop it — this book is a specific kind of reckoning. Not accusatory. Just honest about how it feels to have seen something and done nothing.

**"The Recess Queen" by Alexis O'Neill** — Mean Jean rules the playground. Then a small new kid named Katie Sue arrives who doesn't know the rules. She asks Jean if she wants to jump rope. Jean doesn't know what to do with kindness aimed at her directly. The story is funny and satisfying and also weirdly true about how bullying dynamics actually shift: not through confrontation, but through someone just... not participating in the dynamic at all. For six-year-olds navigating social hierarchies at recess — who's in charge, where you stand, what it costs to be kind to the person at the bottom — this is the one.

**"Strictly No Elephants" by Lisa Mantchev** — I keep recommending this book to every age because it keeps being right. At six, kids can feel the full sharpness of the "no elephants" rule — the specific injustice of being excluded for something that isn't a flaw, just a difference. The ending, where the boy and his excluded friends start their own club for all pets, resonates differently at six than it does at four. At six, your kid understands that starting your own thing when the existing thing won't have you is not a consolation prize. It's actually the more interesting move.

**"Those Shoes" by Maribeth Boelts** — Jeremy wants the shoes. Everyone has the shoes. He can't afford the shoes. He finds them secondhand in his size but they're too small. He wears them anyway, painfully, trying to belong. Then he sees a classmate who really needs them and gives them away. What I love about this for the making-friends topic: it captures the exhausting way that social belonging at six is tangled up with objects, with having the right things, and it also shows a different path — the kid who earns connection not by fitting the mold but by seeing someone else's need. Six-year-olds are starting to navigate this exact economy. This book names it.

**"Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Peña** — CJ asks his grandma why they don't have things other people have. Why they always take the bus instead of a car. His grandma keeps turning the question back — she shows him the beauty in what they're passing, the value in what they have, the people around them who are worth knowing. It's not strictly a making-friends book but it is a book about how you relate to the world — open or closed, curious or resentful. For a six-year-old who's struggling to belong, sometimes the shift is internal, not external. This book teaches that shift gently.

## Reading with a six-year-old: the argument is the point

Six-year-olds will fight with these books. They'll say Chloe was wrong, or they'll defend Chloe, or they'll say Katie Sue should have just stood up to Mean Jean directly. Let them argue. Don't steer them toward the correct moral response.

Ask open questions: "What do you think Maddie should have done?" "What would you have done?" "Has anything like this ever happened to you?" And then stop talking. Just wait. Let them fill the silence. Six-year-olds often say the most true things about their own social lives in the moment after a long pause following a big question.

Also: if they fall silent at a specific page, don't turn it. Wait to see what they're looking at. What they're sitting with. The illustration they're staring at is usually the one that's met them somewhere specific.

## The part you're dreading

Somewhere this year your six-year-old is probably going to come home and tell you something that breaks your heart. Maybe just a fact delivered flatly: "I ate lunch alone today." Maybe more: "Everybody has a best friend except me." Maybe even flatter, harder to read: "I don't care about friends." (That last one is often the worst, actually. The caring while claiming not to.)

You can't fix it from the outside. You know that. What you can do is take it seriously without catastrophizing, find out more, and then go find them Frog and Toad and read it like it's just another night.

Because here's the thing about these books: they were written by people who were six once too, and they remembered the specific texture of it. They wrote it down so other kids — your kids — would know it's survivable. That there are Frogs out there for every Toad. That Chloe's regret is not the end of the story, just part of one.

## Bonus: ask your six-year-old who they see eating alone

Not who they want to be friends with. Not who's popular or nice. Who they see sitting alone. That question unlocks something different — it makes them the agent instead of the subject. And occasionally the answer leads to a real conversation about what it would take to do something about it. Six-year-olds are capable of that kind of courage. Sometimes they just need someone to ask.

## A book made just for your child

Your six-year-old knows when a book is about them. That awareness is new and it matters. Plubis builds custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — your child's name, their situation, their specific fear about making friends or keeping them. Not Chloe or Jean or Frog or Toad. Your kid. The one who came home and said "everybody has a best friend except me" and then ate their snack and pretended they were fine. A book made for them, specifically, saying: you are the kind of person someone would be lucky to call their best friend. Sometimes that's the thing they need to hear in exactly that form. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about moving to a new home
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through moving house. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Here's what nobody warns you about moving with a six-year-old: they will argue with you about it.

Not just cry. Argue. With evidence. "But you said this was our forever home" is a sentence an actual six-year-old might produce, calmly, over breakfast, and then look at you waiting for a response. They know things now. They have memory. They have a sense of fairness. And they're very aware that they did not vote on this decision.

Six is also, genuinely, one of the harder ages to move at. The social stakes are higher. First-grade friendships have more history, more meaning. There are best friends who've become part of the daily furniture of life. There are teachers who finally feel safe, classrooms that have started to feel like theirs. And now you're dismantling all of it.

The books that work at six are the ones that treat this loss as real. That don't try to logic them out of the grief. That say: it makes sense to feel this way, and it will also be okay, but we're not going to skip past the first part to get to the second.

## What's going on developmentally at six

Six-year-olds are in a full-on social expansion. Who is my best friend? Am I in the right group? What will the kids at the new school think of me? Moving disrupts all of this at the moment when it matters most to them — and they know it.

They can also handle emotional complexity in a way younger kids can't. Bittersweet isn't confusing at six — it's something they've felt. Missing something that also made them uncomfortable. Being sad and excited at the same time. Books that honor that complexity land hard at this age, in a good way.

They'll remember a book for weeks. Not just vaguely — they'll quote it. They'll bring it up at a completely different moment and you'll realize it's been sitting in them doing its quiet work. Choose books with lines worth carrying.

They might also push back on picture books. "That's for little kids." Fine. Acknowledge it. Read it anyway with a "this one's actually pretty good." Most six-year-olds who say picture books are babyish still want to be read to at bedtime. Both things are true simultaneously.

## The books

**"Alexander, Who's Not (Do You Hear Me? I Mean It!) Going to Move" by Judith Viorst** — Still the best book at any age about a kid who is furious about moving, and at six they can appreciate the full comedy of it. Alexander is not just sad — he's making a case. He has documentation. He has specific grievances. And there's something deeply satisfying about a book that takes a child's objections that seriously, gives them room to be elaborate and particular and a little absurd, and then lets the story move forward without any of those objections being resolved. Alexander doesn't stop being upset. He just goes. And for a six-year-old who is also upset about going, that's the most honest thing a book can do. Read this one loud, with full theatrical Alexander indignation. Encourage them to add their own items to his list. It helps.

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Not specifically about moving, but it's the best book about being new somewhere and not fitting in yet, which is exactly what six-year-olds face in a new school. The characters are different from their classmates in all kinds of ways — their lunch, their summer, their family. And the book sits with that discomfort honestly before arriving at the radical small act of telling your story anyway. The illustrations by Rafael Lopez are among the best in any picture book published in the last decade. Read this before the first day at the new school. Slowly. Leave space after the ending.

**"Ira Says Goodbye" by Bernard Waber** — At six this hits with full force. Ira's best friend is moving. The whole book is the texture of their last day together — the wanting to freeze it, the trying to be normal while knowing it isn't. A six-year-old who has a best friend they're leaving will recognize every page. The not-tidy ending — the goodbye that's real and the hope that something continues — is the right kind of honesty for this age. Don't rush past the quiet pages.

**"Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Peña, illustrated by Christian Robinson** — No moving in this book at all. CJ is on the bus with his grandmother, asking why they don't have things other kids have. His grandmother answers every complaint with something worth seeing. This is the book for the six-year-old who's arrived somewhere new and can only see what they've lost. It doesn't tell them to feel grateful — that's patronizing. It opens up the question of what you might be missing by looking only at the gap. The illustrations by Christian Robinson are extraordinary. I'd read this one for the art alone. Thankfully the writing is as good as the pictures.

**"The Recess Queen" by Alexis O'Neill** — Mean Jean rules the playground. Nobody argues with that. Then a small new kid named Katie Sue arrives and doesn't know the rules and just invites Jean to jump rope. This is the book for the six-year-old who's terrified of starting at a new school and landing at the bottom of the social hierarchy. It's not naive about how social hierarchies work. But it's honest about how they can shift. And it's funny and bouncy and satisfying in a way that makes the social insight land without feeling like a lecture. Best on this list for the kid who's anxious about the playground specifically.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — This one is hard. A new girl named Maya arrives and the narrator Chloe keeps choosing not to be kind to her. Then Maya is gone. There's no redemption. Chloe has to sit with what she didn't do. At six they can hold this — and they should, in the safe container of a story. If your child is anxious about being treated the way Maya was treated, this book names that fear directly. Read it slowly. Don't moralize after. Let them sit in the discomfort for a moment before the conversation happens.

**"Mitchell Is Moving" by Marjorie Weinman Sharmat** — Mitchell the dinosaur moves away from his best friend Margo. Both of them deal with it differently. It's funny in places and genuinely wistful in others, and it's one of the most honest depictions of what happens to a close friendship when one person moves — the ambiguity, the missing, the not-quite-knowing-how-it-ends. For a six-year-old facing that specific situation, this book doesn't promise anything false. Worth hunting down at your library.

## A bonus section: the new school email

If you know the name of the new teacher before school starts, consider sending them a short email. Not dramatic — just: "We're moving, our child is a little nervous, they love dogs and dinosaurs and being read to." A good teacher will use that. Your child walks in on day one and the teacher says their name, knows they love dogs. That's not nothing. That's a lot, actually.

Books can prepare a child for a new school. But a teacher who's been briefed and is actively looking out for them is the best preparation of all.

## How to read with a six-year-old who thinks books are for babies

Let them hold the book. Let them turn the pages. Ask what they notice in the illustrations before you read the words. Let them read some of the text themselves if they can — a six-year-old who is working through emergent reading will often engage more deeply with a book they're reading alongside you than one they're being read to passively.

And if they say "this is boring" — pause, look at what page you're on, and ask one specific question about the picture. "What do you think Alexander is about to say next?" Curiosity is almost always findable even when they're resistant.

## The conversations books open at this age

At six, a book about moving can open conversations you couldn't have directly. "Are you worried about finding a new friend?" said face-to-face to a six-year-old often gets a shrug. Said while you're both looking at Ira on the page, wondering if he'll miss Reggie — you might get something real.

The side-by-side structure of reading together is one of the most underrated things available to parents. Not eye contact. Just two people looking at the same thing.

## When a book isn't enough

Six-year-olds who are really struggling with a move — serious resistance at the new school, persistent withdrawal, expressions of wanting to go back that become consuming — deserve more than books and time. Talk to the new teacher first; good ones have helped kids through this before and they want to know. Then your pediatrician. A child therapist who works with school-age kids can make a real difference at this age, before patterns get harder to shift. Picture books are wonderful. They're also not a treatment plan.

## Make a book made just for your child

Six-year-olds are old enough to find it meaningful that a book is literally about them. Not a kid who's vaguely similar to them. Them — their name, their best friend's name, their specific street, the park they're leaving, the pet that's coming with them. Plubis generates custom illustrated picture books in five minutes. For a six-year-old heading into a move, a book that says their name and tells their story is different from anything else you can give them right now. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about learning a new language
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a new language. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds read. Or they're starting to, haltingly, with enormous concentration, sounding out words like they're defusing a bomb. And if your six-year-old is reading in one language while trying to hold onto a second language that nobody at school uses — or if they're in a new country and have just discovered that reading in their new language is a different kind of hard from reading in their first — then this particular developmental moment is a lot to be carrying.

Six-year-olds also have a clear sense of fairness and a developing sense of self. They know when something feels unfair. They know when they feel invisible. A kid who comes home from school having been laughed at for an accent, or who sat silent in a classroom because the words weren't there yet, has had a real experience of exclusion — and that experience needs to be named and honored, not just fixed.

Picture books might seem babyish to a six-year-old who is starting to read chapter books. But picture books about language, about immigration, about being between worlds — these are not baby books. The good ones are dense and emotional and literary. You can read them together and have a real conversation. That conversation might be more valuable than the book.

## The books that actually hold up at six

**"Dreamers" by Yuyi Morales** — A few years into reading this one, it hits differently. At six, your child can follow the full emotional arc: the disorientation of arriving somewhere new without language, the library as a place of refuge and building, the years of work, the son growing up. The illustrations by Morales (she also illustrated it) are layered enough that a six-year-old who has now read it multiple times will still find things they haven't noticed. A book that grows with the reader. The dedication — "For Yuyi and Kelly and all the dreamers who guided us along the way" — is a small thing worth pointing out to a six-year-old who needs to know they aren't alone in a big change.

**"Islandborn" by Junot Díaz, illustrated by Leo Espinosa** — At six, the Trujillo subplot of this book (handled carefully — referred to as "the Monster") is something they can start to sit with. This isn't just a charming book about a little girl asking her neighbors about the island. It's also a book about how a community holds difficult history together through stories.
