Six-year-olds can engage with both layers. The illustrations are exploding with color and life — the island exists on the page as a real, specific, beautiful place, not a generic tropical backdrop.

**"Front Desk" by Kelly Yang** — This is a middle-grade novel, not a picture book, but it's worth knowing about because at six (especially if you have a strong reader) it's a legitimate read-aloud. Mia Tang's family runs a motel in California after immigrating from China. Mia hides illegal immigrants in the rooms, helps her parents manage terrible working conditions, and fights to be seen. The language stuff — Mia's English getting better, her parents' English being weaponized against them — is handled with honesty and no apology. If your six-year-old is ready for a longer book and their family has an immigration story, this one is worth it.

**"Separate Is Never Equal" by Duncan Tonatiuh** — At six, the full legal and historical weight of this story is available. Sylvia Mendez, Mexican-American, was denied access to a school for white children in Orange County, California in 1944. Her father fought in court and won. The case contributed directly to Brown v. Board of Education. Tonatiuh's art style (Pre-Columbian inspired, flat figures) is striking and a little solemn in a way that suits the material. For a six-year-old who is already picking up on the ways language and culture mark you as belonging somewhere or not, this is history that explains something real.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson, illustrated by E.B. Lewis** — At six, this book lands with more weight than at four. A new girl, Maya, comes to school and is never included — and then she's gone. The narrator, Chloe, has to live with having passed up every chance. The final image — ripples on a pond, things you can't take back — is one of the most quietly devastating endings in picture book history. For a six-year-old who is either the new kid navigating a language or culture gap, or the kid who had a chance to include someone and didn't — this book creates a very useful discomfort. Don't rush past it.

**"The Arabic Quilt: An Immigrant Story" by Aya Khalil, illustrated by Anait Semirdzhyan** — Kanzi has just moved from Egypt to the United States. Her hijab and her Arabic words make her a target for teasing at school. Her teacher turns the Arabic words Kanzi has brought from home into an embroidered quilt that the whole class makes together. Based on the author's real childhood experience. At six, kids can feel the specific texture of being mocked for words that are yours, and the specific repair that happens when an adult takes those words and treats them as something worth honoring. The quilt metaphor works on multiple levels. The illustrations are soft and warm.

**"Amina's Song" by Hena Khan** — The sequel to "Amina's Voice," following Amina back to Pakistan to visit her family. She has to negotiate two worlds — American Amina, Pakistani Amina — and figure out that she doesn't have to pick. For six-year-olds (especially girls) from South Asian Muslim families, this series is one of the few that represents their specific life with warmth and real detail. The language isn't the explicit theme; the identity is. Which is exactly right.

## The bonus section: when they're reading in two languages and it's hard

If your six-year-old is doing formal literacy instruction in the majority language while also being expected to maintain literacy in the heritage language — I see you. This is genuinely hard. It's a lot of reading work. A lot of homework. A lot of mental-register switching.

A few things that actually help:

Keep heritage-language books in the house that are genuinely pleasurable, not just educational. Comics, funny picture books, simple chapter books — whatever is joyful. The minute heritage-language reading becomes only homework, kids associate it with effort. Keep a pile of books in that language that are just for fun, just for when they want to, with no quiz at the end.

Let them read to you in either language. Make being read to in the heritage language a moment of pride — "I love when you read to me in Mandarin" — rather than just a literacy exercise.

And if they're struggling with reading in the majority language at school, do not pull heritage-language resources to give them "more time" for English practice. The research here is counterintuitive but consistent: strong literacy in the home language *supports* literacy in the second language. These are not competing resources. Adding more English practice at the expense of the heritage language is not the shortcut it seems.

## Talking about it at six (for real this time)

Six-year-olds can have actual discussions. Not just answering questions but pushing back, adding their perspective, making connections. After any of these books, try:

"If Kanzi's teacher hadn't done anything, what do you think would have happened to Kanzi?" — opens a conversation about the responsibility of adults in language situations.

"Mia Tang in Front Desk is embarrassed about her parents' English sometimes. Do you ever feel something like that?" — this one requires trust. But if you have it, the answer tells you a lot.

"Yuyi Morales says books saved her. What has a book ever saved you from?" — a genuinely good question for a six-year-old. They'll have an answer.

## A word about accent

Accent is where language shame gets specific and real at six. Children are starting to be teased about accents — their own, their parents', their grandparents'. If this is happening, or if you're worried about it, one of the most direct tools you have is modeling how to respond. "My family speaks this way because we grew up somewhere with a different music to the words. That's worth knowing, not making fun of." Give them the script. Six-year-olds can use a script. They just need to know one exists.

## When a book isn't enough

If your six-year-old is significantly behind grade level in the majority language at school, ask for a formal language assessment before assuming anything. Schools sometimes misidentify language learners as having learning disabilities, and sometimes miss actual learning disabilities because they're attributed to language acquisition. You want both questions answered clearly, separately.

And if they're refusing the heritage language entirely — won't speak it, won't engage with books in it, might be feeling shame — this is a real thing and it's worth gentle, consistent effort to address. Not argument. Not enforcement. But the kind of joyful, low-pressure, warm immersion that makes a language feel like home again.

## Make a book made for your child

At six, a custom book has a different power than at two or three. Six-year-olds can read their own name in a book. They can trace the sentences. They can show it to their friends. Plubis generates custom illustrated picture books in five minutes — tell us your child's name, their language situation, and we write a full book that puts them at the center of the story. A book that says "Lucas knows that some words only exist in one of his languages, and that makes those words more precious, not less" — that's a sentence worth reading with your six-year-old, because it's true, and because they need to hear it. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about getting a new pet
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a new pet. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the age where "reading to your kid about the new pet" starts to feel slightly redundant, because your six-year-old has things to say about this. They've been watching you manage the situation. They've noticed the dog tracks mud. They've observed that the cat ignores their dad but comes to them, which they're very smug about. They have opinions, and they're going to share them, and the books you read together are going to get interrupted every four sentences with commentary.

This is good. This is six.

But here's the thing about six: the emotional complexity of a new pet is actually higher than it was at four or five, not lower. A six-year-old has real attachment, real fear of loss, a developing moral sense that makes them worry about whether they're doing the pet thing "right," and a social life where the pet becomes part of their identity. They tell their friends about it. They bring it up at school. They've already decided this pet is the best pet anyone has ever had, and this conviction is precious and should be respected.

Books that do justice to this age take the kid seriously. They don't simplify. They don't guarantee happy endings. They don't pretend animals are just cute props in a child's emotional development. The best books for six-year-olds about new pets treat the relationship as real — which, for your kid, it absolutely is.

## What works at six (and what stops working)

At six, your kid has mostly outgrown pure picture books as the primary text. (Though there are picture books with enough visual density and thematic weight that they still work — we'll get to those.) What they're ready for is:

**Short chapter books they can partially read themselves.** The act of reading about a pet, independently, is its own form of processing. Your six-year-old can sit with a book about an animal and think their own thoughts about it. That's new, and it's worth encouraging.

**Books with moral ambiguity.** Shiloh. The One and Only Bob. Stories where characters do complicated things for understandable reasons and have to live with the consequences. Six-year-olds are working out ethics in real time, and stories that give them cases to think through are genuinely useful.

**Books where the animal is a full character.** Not a prop. Not a reward for the child protagonist. A creature with its own agenda, its own fears, its own story. Six-year-olds are ready for this and it teaches something essential: that what we love has its own inner life that exists independently of our love.

**Books that include loss.** Six-year-olds often start asking about what happens if the pet dies. Not as a fear, exactly — more as a thing they're aware of that they want to understand. Books that handle animal death with honesty (not trauma, but honesty) are incredibly valuable at this age.

## Book picks for 6-year-olds about getting a new pet

**"Shiloh" by Phyllis Reynolds Naylor** — The best children's book about a kid and a dog, and I'll stand by that in most arguments. Marty finds an abused beagle and hides it from his parents and from the abusive owner, lying repeatedly, because the alternative is worse. The ethical tension — lying to protect something you love, vs. honesty as an absolute value — is handled with rare intelligence. Six-year-olds can handle this. They should read it. It will start conversations you didn't know you needed to have.

**"Charlotte's Web" by E.B. White** — The obvious pick and the correct one. But reading it now, with a new animal in the house, lands differently than reading it in school. The daily rhythms of Wilbur's life, the way Charlotte's affection is expressed through action rather than words, the way loss is woven in from the beginning — your six-year-old with a brand new pet is going to feel this book with their whole body.

**"The One and Only Ivan" by Katherine Applegate** — Ivan is a gorilla in a mall. He's been there for years. He makes art and remembers his life before captivity and, eventually, does something enormous to protect a friend. The novel is about what animals owe each other and what humans owe animals, and Applegate earns every feeling in it. Not a cozy reading experience but an important one.

**"A Dog Called Homeless" by Sarah lean** — Cally sees her dead mother's ghost — only Cally sees it, always near a large dog. The book is about grief and presence and what dogs sense that we can't explain, and it is written with tremendous gentleness. For a six-year-old whose family has recently lost something, and who now has a pet, this book does something unusual: it ties the new animal to something larger and older. I can't fully explain why it works. It just does.

**"Sounder" by William H. Armstrong** — This one is the hardest on this list. A family's dog is wounded by sheriff's deputies and disappears, and a boy's grief and search for him runs alongside his father's imprisonment. It's a Newbery Medal winner and it is not light reading. But it is about what a dog means to a family, what loyalty looks like in the hardest circumstances, and what love costs. Some six-year-olds are ready for it. You know yours.

**"My Father's Dragon" by Ruth Stiles Gannett** — Much lighter. A boy rescues a baby dragon from captivity on Wild Island, using improbable supplies (chewing gum, hairpins, lollipops) to outsmart various obstacles. The premise is absurd and delightful and the dragon is wonderful. Six-year-olds who wanted an unusual pet (and there are always a few who wanted a lizard or a snake or something genuinely strange) will find this satisfying.

**"The Incredible Journey" by Sheila Burnford** — Two dogs and a cat travel 250 miles through the Canadian wilderness to get back to their family. It is relentless and moving and occasionally very intense. But the depiction of each animal's specific personality — the old Lab, the young Bull Terrier, the Siamese cat — is so precise that reading it gives kids a new way to see their own pet. What's going on inside there? What does your pet do for you that you've been taking for granted?

**"Henry and Mudge" series by Cynthia Rylant** — The easiest on this list. Henry is lonely until his parents get him a dog named Mudge. The books are short and warm and full of specific sensory detail — Mudge's enormous wet nose, the smell of his fur, the weight of his head on Henry's lap. Six-year-olds can read these mostly independently and they're quietly doing emotional work that matters. Rylant knows what she's doing.

## Reading together at six: letting them lead

At six, the dynamic shifts. You're not just reading to your kid anymore — you're reading with them. They're tracking the story. They're predicting. They're noticing things in the text you glazed over.

Let them read pages aloud when they want to. Even if it's slow. Even if they sound out words wrong. The act of saying the story out loud, in their own voice, makes it different — it becomes theirs in a new way.

The best reading you'll do together at this age is what happens after the book. Lying in bed, lights out or almost out, "what did you think about when Marty hid Shiloh?" or "if we had to leave our dog like in The Incredible Journey, do you think they'd find their way back to us?" Six-year-olds in the dark will tell you things they don't say in daylight. The books are the permission slip.

## When the novelty wears off

Here's a section no one writes but everyone needs.

Around week three or four, the novelty of the new pet peaks and starts to descend. Your six-year-old is still asking to walk the dog, but less often. The cat that was fascinating is now just... the cat. The responsibility of feeding it, which they begged to take on, is starting to feel like an annoying thing that interrupts Minecraft.

This is completely normal and also a genuinely important parenting moment. Not because you need to lecture them about commitment (please don't — it doesn't work and it creates shame spirals), but because this is the first major experience many kids have with the difference between wanting something and maintaining something.

Books can help here not by moralizing but by modeling. Henry still loves Mudge in book 28. Opal and Winn-Dixie's relationship deepens, it doesn't peak and plateau. The relationship in Charlotte's Web isn't exciting — it's daily and mundane and enormous.

You might say, casually, after reading: "I think the best part of having a pet is kind of after the exciting part settles down. When it's just... normal. When you know each other." That's it. That's the whole thing. You don't need a lesson.

Some useful conversation starters for the novelty-drop phase:

"What's something you've noticed about our pet this week that you hadn't noticed before?"

"What do you think our pet's favorite thing about our house is?"

"If you could ask our pet one question, and it could answer, what would you ask?"

That last one. Trust me. Save it for the right moment.

## Make a book made for your child

A six-year-old understands stories about themselves in a specific way: they know it's real. A custom book where they're the main character isn't magic or pretend — it's documentation. It's proof that their experience with this new animal was real enough to become a story.

Six-year-olds also love to share. A book about them and their pet? They will show every person who enters the house. They will bring it to school. They will read it to their grandparents on a video call.

Plubis builds that book for you in about five minutes. Tell us your kid's name, your pet's name, what the situation's been like — we generate a custom illustrated story that puts your six-year-old at the center of their own adventure. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about starting at a new school
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a new school. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds know they're the new kid.

Not in the vague toddler way. In the fully aware, watching the room, cataloguing every interaction kind of way. They notice who sits together. Who gets picked first. Whose joke lands and whose doesn't. And they notice, with horrible clarity, where they stand in all of it: outside.

Mid-year is the worst time to be new. I'm just going to say that plainly. There's no spin. The class has had seven or eight months together. Friendships are formed. Rivalries are formed. There are jokes you have to have been there for. And then your kid walks in on a Tuesday in January and everyone already knows everyone and nobody has any particular reason to rearrange their social world to accommodate one more person.

That's the situation. It's hard. Your kid knows it's hard. The question is what you do with that knowledge.

Picture books are not going to make your six-year-old popular. (Sorry.) But they can do something that's actually more useful at this moment: they can give your child the language to think about what they're feeling, models for how to handle it, and the visceral knowledge that someone, somewhere, has felt exactly this and survived it and maybe even found their person on the other side.

## What's different about reading with a six-year-old

At six, kids can read. Or they're learning. This changes the dynamic — you're not just reading *to* them, you're sometimes reading *alongside* them. They can handle more text per page. They're starting to think about authorial intent ("why did she make the character feel that way?").

But — and this is important — picture books are not below six-year-olds. Do not let them convince you otherwise. The format is perfect for emotional content because the illustrations do a layer of work that text alone can't. A child can look at an illustration of a character standing alone on a playground and feel understood in a way that three paragraphs of prose couldn't accomplish.

At six, you can have real conversations after reading. Not long ones — ten minutes, tops, before they're done. But real ones. They can tell you nuanced things about how the character felt and connect it to their own life if you give them space to.

The trick at six is not to push. Six-year-olds have strong self-protective instincts. If you're too obvious about the agenda — "I got you this book BECAUSE of your new school" — they'll resist. Be casual. "I found this one at the library, want to try it?" Then see what happens.

## The books — and my honest opinions about each one

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — This is the book I'd hand to every parent of every new kid at every age. At six, it hits differently than it does at four or five. Your six-year-old can follow Woodson's prose at the level it's written, can understand the emotional specificity of the characters (a girl who spent her summer differently than everyone else, a boy who eats alone), and can sit with the book's quietly radical idea: the thing that makes you feel alone might be the thing that makes you interesting, if you let someone see it.

Read this one when things are hard. Not as a fix. As company.

**"The Name Jar" by Yangsook Choi** — Unhei moves from Korea to America and starts a new school where no one can pronounce her name. She considers changing it to fit in. Her new classmates, meanwhile, start a jar full of name suggestions. It ends with Unhei deciding to keep her own name.

For a six-year-old at a new school, this book is about identity under social pressure. The new school is asking, implicitly, that you become a different version of yourself to fit in. Unhei doesn't. This is a good book to read and then ask: "Is there anything about yourself you feel like you have to hide or change at your new school?" That question opens more than you'd think.

**"Stand Tall, Molly Lou Melon" by Patty Lovell** — At six, Molly Lou Melon's unapologetic weirdness is either inspiring or alienating, depending on where your kid is in their social anxiety. If they're in a "I just want to fit in" headspace, Molly Lou can feel unrealistic and even slightly annoying. If they're in a "forget these people, I'm going to be myself" headspace, she's a hero.

Read it and watch your kid's reaction. That reaction tells you something.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — At six, this book is fully appropriate and I recommend it without reservation. A new girl, Maya, arrives at school. The narrator and her friends don't include her. Maya moves away. The teacher does a kindness lesson. The narrator throws a stone in the water and watches the ripples and understands — too late — what she didn't do.

This is the most emotionally honest book about the new-kid experience I know of, told from the perspective of the kids who were already there. For a six-year-old who is the new kid, it says: you're not invisible. The other kids notice you even when they don't show it. Some of them feel guilty about not saying hi. That's not nothing.

**"Sofia Valdez, Future Prez" by Andrea Beaty** — Sofia arrives at a new school and wants to fix a problem she sees, but no one listens to her because she's new. She figures out how to make herself heard anyway. It's a book about agency and persistence in new social environments, which is exactly the kind of framing a six-year-old who's frustrated by their powerlessness in a new social situation can use.

**"Spaghetti in a Hot Dog Bun" by Maria Dismondy** — Still works at six, maybe better. Lucy is teased for being different. She responds with grace and self-possession. She doesn't become the popular kid by the end — she stays herself, and that's the point. A six-year-old in a new school where the social hierarchy is already set can feel like the only options are "become someone else" or "be a loser." This book offers a third option: be exactly yourself and let that be enough.

**"Making Friends Is an Art!" by Julia Cook** — More instructional than literary, but at six, some kids genuinely want to know: okay, but HOW do you make friends? The book treats this as a learnable skill. The protagonist, Eli, gets explicit instruction. It's not the most nuanced book on the list, but it fills a real gap. If your kid is asking "what do I even say to people?" — this one helps.

**"I Am Enough" by Grace Byers** — Lush, beautiful, lyrical. Every page is a statement of self-worth. Not problem-solving, not social instruction. Just: you are enough exactly as you are. For a kid whose confidence is getting sandpapered down by weeks of feeling left out, this book is specifically useful. Read it at bedtime when they're soft and open.

## A different kind of reading session — let them pick the character

Here's something that works with six-year-olds that doesn't work as well at younger ages: after reading one of these books, hand them the book and ask them to show you which character they feel most like right now.

Not "who's your favorite" — that's asking them to perform preference. "Which character feels most like you right now?" That's asking them to identify.

If they pick Wemberly, they're telling you they're anxious. If they pick Maya (from Each Kindness), they're telling you they feel unseen. If they pick Molly Lou, they're telling you they're trying to stay themselves under pressure. Each one is a different opening for a different conversation.

Six-year-olds have enough language and enough trust in metaphor to use a fictional character as a proxy for a real feeling. Let them. Don't dissolve the proxy too fast by saying "is this about your school?" Just stay in the character world a little longer and see where they take it.

## What's actually true about this (honest version)

Here's what I believe and what I don't know:

I believe that six is old enough for the social dynamics of a new school to do real damage to self-concept if they go on long enough without intervention. The "new kid" identity can calcify. Kids start to believe stories about themselves: I'm the one nobody picks. I'm not good at making friends. That's who I am.

Books and conversations interrupt that calcification. They introduce alternative stories. They say: this is a temporary situation, not a permanent truth.

But I also don't know your kid or your school or how long this has been going on. What I'd watch for at six — more than younger ages — is: are they starting to refuse to talk about school entirely? Are they becoming resigned rather than sad? Resignation is the thing to catch early. Sad kids are still hoping. Resigned kids have decided.

If you're seeing resignation, that's worth professional support. School counselor, child therapist, pediatrician conversation. Not dramatic. Just a check-in with someone qualified to assess where they actually are.

Books are good. Books alone are not always enough. Knowing the difference is good parenting.

## Make a book made for your child

At six, something interesting happens with personalized books: kids can actually read pieces of them. Their name on the page isn't just heard — it's seen. That's a different experience and a more powerful one.

A story about a six-year-old with their exact name, starting a new school mid-year, navigating exactly what they're navigating — that's not a generic school story. That's a mirror.

We make these at Plubis. Your kid's name, their details, a real illustrated picture book about their exact situation. You give us the details in five minutes.

The first one is free.

situationLabel: a new baby brother or sister
title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about a new baby brother or sister
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through new sibling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Here's the thing about six-year-olds and new siblings: they're old enough to be self-conscious about having big feelings, which makes the feelings worse. They've absorbed the message that they should be excited, that they're lucky, that being a big sibling is wonderful — and when what they actually feel is jealous, or annoyed, or invisible, they often don't say so directly. They go sideways. More attitude. More meltdowns about unrelated things. More "you love the baby more than me" said once, loudly, and then denied.

## What six-year-olds need from a book about this

Six is old enough for real moral complexity in a story. A character who isn't purely good or bad, who makes a questionable choice and faces a real consequence, who finds their way to something more true than "and then she was happy." Six-year-olds are in school. They're building friendships. They have a whole world outside the family now — and a new sibling can feel like the family world is contracting just as their outside world is expanding.

The books that work at this age are the ones with real inner life. Characters who wonder about themselves, not just act. Characters who notice contradictions in their own feelings. Your six-year-old might be quietly furious at the baby and simultaneously be the most tender big sibling you've ever seen. A good book holds both of those things without making it weird.

Also: six-year-olds are often early readers. Reading *together* is still valuable — maybe more valuable — but they may want to revisit books independently. Pick ones with illustrations worth returning to.

## The books

**"Julius, the Baby of the World" by Kevin Henkes**
This book appears in every age guide for a reason. It keeps appearing because it keeps being right. At six, kids can read the ironies that younger kids only feel. They notice that Lilly's behavior is over the top. They find it funny in a way four-year-olds don't. And the ending — Lilly defending Julius to her cousin — is a genuine character moment, not a lesson. Read this one. If you haven't already, start here.

**"The New Small Person" by Lauren Child**
Elmore Green's life is perfect until "the new small person" appears. He is not consulted. He is not thrilled. Child's dry humor and collage art are a perfect match for a six-year-old's sense of injustice — the illustrations look chaotic in a way that captures what upheaval actually feels like. The ending doesn't rush to sentimentality, which six-year-olds appreciate. Skip this for toddlers; it's written for this age.

**"A Baby Sister for Frances" by Russell Hoban**
Frances runs away (under the dining room table, with crackers) and her parents respond in the most psychologically elegant way possible. At six, kids are old enough to see both perspectives at once — they identify with Frances AND see through the parents' strategy. That double vision is actually valuable. It helps them understand that the adults in their lives are working on their behalf even when they can't see it directly.

**"Siblings" by Elissa Haden Guest and Elise Primavera**
The long-view book. What siblings are, across years and arguments and loyalty and weird in-jokes. For a six-year-old who's been living with the baby for a while and starting to form something that looks like an actual relationship — this one meets them there. Not the crisis book. The "this is who we are to each other" book.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson**
Not about siblings at all. It's about a new girl at school who gets excluded. The narrator regrets her unkindness and by the time she's ready to make it right, the new girl is gone. It doesn't end happily. Six-year-olds can handle that. The reason to read it in the context of a new sibling: it quietly asks what kind of person you want to be toward someone smaller and more vulnerable than you. That question is live when there's a new baby in the house.

**"Noisy Nora" by Rosemary Wells**
Nora is the middle child who makes more and more noise trying to get noticed, and eventually "disappears" to see if they'll miss her. They do. Wells is unshowy but precise — the feelings are exactly right. At six, kids can really sit with the wanting-to-be-seen feeling this book describes, and the relief when it turns out you are seen. Good for the six-year-old who's been acting out since the baby arrived and doesn't quite know why.

**"Over the Moon: An Adoption Tale" by Karen Katz**
If the new sibling in your family came through adoption, this is the book. Warm, specific, illustrated beautifully. A family waiting for their child, and the joy when they arrive. Not relevant for every family, but for those it is, nothing else comes close.

## Reading with a six-year-old who thinks they're too old for this

Some six-year-olds have decided picture books are for babies. This is a phase. It usually passes.

The workaround: don't frame it as "I want to read a book about feelings with you." That's the kiss of death. Frame it as "I found this one that made me laugh" or "I wanted to show you the illustrations in this, they're really something." Let them come to the feelings through the side door.

Alternatively, lean into the reading-to-the-baby angle. Suggest they read to the baby — not as a chore, but as something the baby literally cannot experience any other way. Your six-year-old is the one who can hold a book and read words. The baby can't. That framing shifts the dynamic completely.

And if the book does open something up — a rare, honest "I miss when it was just me" — don't jump to reassurance. Sit in it with them for a minute. "Yeah. That makes sense." Then reassurance. The sequence matters. Feeling heard first, then okay.

## One thing NOT to do

Don't use the book as a segue to a debrief. Six-year-olds will smell the agenda. Read the book for its own sake. If conversation happens, great. If it doesn't, the book still worked — it just worked quietly, over time, the way good books do.

## When a book isn't enough

A six-year-old having a genuinely hard time with a new sibling — persistent sadness, school issues, real behavioral changes that don't improve after a few weeks — might benefit from talking to someone outside the family. A pediatrician can recommend a counselor who specializes in family transitions. These books are good conversation starters; some conversations need more than a parent to hold them.

## Make a book made for your child

On Plubis, you choose the new-sibling situation, type in your child's name, and get a custom picture book in about five minutes — a story specifically about them, their feelings, their family. Not a generic kid. Them. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about being afraid of the dark
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through nightmares and the dark. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Your six-year-old knows monsters aren't real.

They will tell you this. Clearly. With confidence. Sometimes while climbing into your bed at 2am because of the monster.

This is not hypocrisy. This is a six-year-old brain doing something completely normal: it knows facts, and it also has feelings, and those two systems don't communicate as smoothly as we'd like. Knowing there's no monster doesn't stop the amygdala from firing when it sees a shadow. You can't think your way out of a fear response. Adults can't either, honestly.

So when your kid insists they're too old for nighttime-fear books: they're not. They might need a different KIND of book — one that doesn't talk down to them, one that acknowledges their intelligence while also doing the emotional work. Those books exist. Here they are.

## what works at this age

Six is a big developmental year. Your kid is probably in school, getting exposed to new stories and ideas from other kids, developing a sense of what's "babyish" and what's not. They may actively resist books that feel too young.

So: lean into books that are sophisticated. Books with real literary quality. Books where the pictures are art and the text actually has sentences in it. Six-year-olds can hold complex emotional states — proud AND scared, brave AND wanting their parent. Books that reflect that complexity feel like respect.

They're also at an age where information is genuinely empowering. Not just "the dark is okay" but actual explanations: what IS the dark, what do scientists say about dreams, what happens in the brain during nightmares. You can go there a little. Not a textbook — still a story. But a story that treats them as capable of understanding real things.

And humor. God, humor. Six-year-olds have developed a sophisticated enough sense of irony that they can find their own fears funny when they're put at the right distance. A book that makes them laugh ABOUT the nightmare is doing more therapeutic work than one that just reassures.

## book picks

**"Where the Wild Things Are" by Maurice Sendak**

Yes, I know. You've been reading this one for years. But at six, your kid is ready to get what it's actually about.

Max doesn't have a nightmare. He has a sendoff: banished to his room, his anger and wildness sent to a dark place where it becomes an adventure, then a kingdom, then something he's ready to leave. The real subject is the inner life — what happens in the dark of your own room, in the theater of your own imagination, when nobody else is watching. Six-year-olds are starting to develop that interior life consciously. They know they have thoughts nobody else can see. This book speaks to that directly. Read it slowly. Ask what they notice. You'll get answers that surprise you.

**"The Dark" by Lemony Snicket, illustrated by Jon Klassen**

At six, this one hits with full force. Laszlo's fear is specific and articulate — this resonates with six-year-olds who can now name their fears with precision. The resolution doesn't defeat the dark or befriend it; it reaches an accommodation. The dark shows Laszlo something he needs. He gets it. The dark stays. That's genuinely sophisticated — the thing you're afraid of isn't evil, it's just there, and you can have a relationship with it that works for both of you. This is adult-level emotional reasoning delivered in a picture book. Klassen's art is everything. Little, Brown.

**"The Berenstain Bears and the Bad Dream" by Stan and Jan Berenstain**

Brother and Sister Bear watch a scary movie. Brother Bear has nightmares. The family talks about it. That's the whole book — and it's extremely useful at six because it handles something that will happen: your kid encounters something scary (a movie, a story from school, something on a screen), and it follows them into their sleep.

The Berenstain Bears' solution is talking — telling Mama and Papa what the nightmare was, having it heard and normalized. For six-year-olds who wake up from vivid dreams and don't want to tell you about them because they think it'll make it worse, this book is gentle evidence that telling actually helps. Warm, accessible, not condescending. Random House.

**"Bedtime for Frances" by Russell Hoban, illustrated by Garth Williams**

Six-year-olds reading this have a completely different experience than younger kids. At six, they laugh at Frances. They see her working every angle — the tiger, the giant, the noise — with what they now recognize as bad faith stalling tactics. They are Frances. And seeing that clearly — recognizing themselves as the one making up reasons not to sleep — can be genuinely useful. Not shaming. Funny. There's a difference. Hoban's dialogue is so perfectly observed that parents often laugh out loud too. The child seeing the parent laugh at Frances is seeing something true: this is a human thing, not a shameful thing.

**"The Darkest Dark" by Chris Hadfield, illustrated by The Fan Brothers**

At six, kids can fully absorb the astronomy in this one. Space is dark. Genuinely, completely, profoundly dark. And Chris Hadfield decided to go there. The sequence where Hadfield describes outer space — the silence, the blackness, the vastness — is presented as awe-inspiring rather than terrifying. Six-year-olds who are interested in science, space, or exploration get a real conceptual shift from this book: darkness is where the interesting things are. Little, Brown, 2016. The glow-in-the-dark cover edition is worth every penny.

**"There's Something in My Attic" by Mercer Mayer**

A girl is convinced there's something in the attic. So she goes up there to catch it. She misses, it escapes, but she tried. And then — with her parents back in bed upstairs — she decides she'll try again tomorrow. That persistence, that decided courage in the face of failure, is pitched exactly right for six. She didn't win. She's not scared anymore. She just has a plan. Mayer's art in this one is slightly creepy in a way that six-year-olds find delicious. It honors the feeling that the scary thing might actually be there, which is more honest than books that dismiss the fear. Dial Books.

**"Scaredy Squirrel at Night" by Mélanie Watt**

At six, Scaredy Squirrel becomes a comedy about anxiety itself. Kids this age who are slightly anxious by nature can see their own behavior patterns reflected back in the most affectionate, absurd way. Scaredy's lists, his emergency kits, his elaborate scenarios — all to avoid the one thing that turns out to be totally fine. Six-year-olds laugh hard at this one, and the laugh is healing. It's okay to be the squirrel. The squirrel figures it out.

## bonus section: the nightmare journal

Six-year-olds can write. Not well. But they can.

A nightmare journal is a small notebook they keep by the bed. When they have a bad dream, they wake up and — either immediately or in the morning — they write or draw what happened.

What this does: it externalizes the dream. It gets it out of their head and onto paper where it's smaller. Many kids find that drawing the nightmare makes it funny somehow — monsters look less scary when you're the one drawing their stubby arms. And the act of writing it down tells the brain: this is information, not an emergency. We're documenting it.

You can combine this with a book ritual. Read a nighttime book, do the journal if they have something to record, lights out. The journal is the part that gives them control.

Some kids want to share the journal. Some don't. Both are fine. Don't push for sharing — let them own it.

## when a book isn't enough

At six, some kids have been afraid of the dark for a while and the fear has calcified. It's not just a phase that passed — it's a habit, a pattern, a thing their nervous system does automatically at lights-out.

If that's happening, books alone won't fix it. But here's what helps alongside books:

Gradual exposure. Start with the door slightly more closed than usual. Then a little more. Over weeks, not days. The nervous system needs small wins, not big confrontations.

Clear language about what fear is. "Your brain is trying to protect you. That's its job. But the dark isn't actually dangerous, and we can teach your brain that." Six-year-olds can hold this concept. They've heard about how the brain works. Make it concrete.

And if the fear is significantly disrupting sleep — yours or theirs — for months on end, talk to their pediatrician. There are people who specialize in exactly this. No shame in it. Sleep is non-negotiable for a six-year-old's developing brain.

## make a book made for your child

Here's something that works differently at six: ask them what they wish they could do if they weren't scared. What adventure would they go on? What would they find in the dark if they weren't afraid of it?

Let them answer. Write it down.

That's the story you're going to make them.

At Plubis, we build custom picture books where your child is the hero of their own dark-night adventure. Give us their name, their fear, their imagined courage, and we'll write them the book that's been waiting to be made.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about a parent being unwell
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a parent being sick. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is the year the world gets bigger.

School is fully real now. Friends matter in a new way. Your child is measuring themselves against other children constantly — what other families have, what other parents do, what other people's lives look like from the outside. And if your child has a parent who is sick, school is also the place where they have to decide: do I tell people? What do I say? What happens if I cry in front of my friends?

That's a lot to carry. And the adults around them are often so consumed by the illness itself — the logistics, the appointments, the exhaustion — that nobody quite notices the weight of that particular thing.

But it's there. Ask any adult who had a seriously ill parent in childhood and they'll often tell you: the hardest part wasn't home. It was the in-between places. The school bus. The classroom. The birthday party where everything felt normal except you.

At six, picture books are still the right tool — I'd push back hard on anyone who says they've outgrown them. Six-year-olds who are dealing with something this big need the simplified emotional world of a picture book, need the images that show rather than tell, need the short manageable arc that they can sit with in one sitting. The fact that they can also now read many of these books themselves is a bonus. A child who can return to a book alone, at midnight, when the worry gets bad — that's a child who has a resource.

## What six-year-olds are actually dealing with

At six, children are developing a more concrete understanding of illness and death. They know that sick doesn't always mean "gets better." They've probably heard adults talk in ways that scared them. They might have Googled something on a tablet when no one was watching and come across information that terrified them with no context.

They also have a new social awareness that makes everything more complicated. They worry about being different. They worry about being the kid whose parent is sick. They've started to develop a real sense of privacy — some things feel too big or too tender to share — and so they might not be coming to you with their fears. They might be sitting with them alone.

What books do at this age is give them a mirror. A character who is a kid like them, going through a thing like their thing, and showing them that it's survivable. That other children have lived inside this exact fear and come out the other side.

That's not nothing. That's actually everything.

## Book picks

**"I Wonder What It's Like When a Parent Has Cancer: Max's Story" by Jacquelyn Rebecek, illustrated by Anna Boxall** — Max is exactly the right age for a six-year-old reader to identify with. He wonders about what to say to friends. He feels the strangeness of life continuing normally at school when everything at home is changed. He has questions he doesn't know how to ask. Written by a certified child life specialist and published by Wonders & Worries — a nonprofit that has been doing this work for twenty-five years — this book is exceptional in the precision of its emotional understanding. It doesn't condescend to children, and it doesn't pretend that everything is fine. It holds the complexity honestly.

**"Nowhere Hair" by Sue Glader, illustrated by Edith Buenen** — Don't be put off by the fact that this reads as younger. At six, especially if the parent's cancer has caused visible changes like hair loss, this book serves a very specific purpose: it gives a child language for what to say to friends. "My mom's hair went away because of the medicine. It's coming back." That's something a six-year-old can actually use in the real world. That's the test of a genuinely good children's book — does it give the child something they can carry into Monday morning?

**"Our Mom Has Cancer" by Abigail and Adrienne Ackermann** — I love this book for six-year-olds because it was written by children, and six-year-olds know the difference. When you read a book written by a grown-up trying to imagine what a child feels, versus a book written by actual children who lived through something — even if those children were older at the time — the authenticity is different. The Ackermann sisters wrote this because nothing else existed that matched their experience. At six, your child can feel the realness of that. They can read these pages and think: someone my age went through this and made something out of it. That possibility is powerful.

**"When Pete's Dad Got Sick" by Kathleen Long Bostrom, illustrated by Cheri Bladholm** — The thing I want to say about this book at six specifically: this is the age where children start to grieve the version of the parent who existed before the illness. The parent who used to run with them, who used to carry them, who used to have energy in the evenings. Pete's dad used to swing Pete around. He can't anymore. That loss — not of the parent, but of a version of the parent, a way of being together — is real and rarely named. This book names it. For a six-year-old dealing with a parent whose capacity has changed, that naming is a relief.

**"What Happens When Someone I Love Has Cancer?" by Sara Olsher** — Six is an age of wanting to understand the mechanism. How does the medicine work? Why does it make you sick if it's supposed to help? What's actually happening inside your body? Sara Olsher's book answers these questions at exactly the right level — honest, specific enough to be satisfying, not so clinical as to be frightening. And it consistently returns to the emotional experience of the child: worried is allowed, angry is allowed, confused is allowed. All of it is allowed. For a six-year-old who is holding a lot of complicated feelings, that permission matters.

**"You Are the Best Medicine" by Julie Aigner Clark** — A six-year-old can receive this book's message with more sophistication than a younger child. They're old enough to understand that adults can be comforted by their children — not just the other way around. Julie Aigner Clark wrote this from inside cancer treatment. The tenderness is real and earned. At six, your child might read this and understand for the first time that their love is genuinely doing something. That they are not just a bystander in this. That the ordinary acts of being a child — bringing a drawing, making someone laugh, climbing into bed for a cuddle — are actually significant in the midst of something terrible.

**"Hair for Momma" by Kelly A. Tinkham** — A child learns that her mother's hair has been lost to cancer treatment and begins to try to find a solution. The premise is sweet and slightly comic in the way only small children's determined logic can be, but the emotional core is serious: a child trying to find a way to help, to fix something, to do something when everything feels out of their hands. Six-year-olds will recognise that impulse completely. And the book honours it, while also gently teaching that there are ways to help that don't involve solving the unsolvable.

## The social dimension: what to say at school

This is the thing that other books often miss for this age. Let me say it plainly: your six-year-old will, at some point, have to decide what to tell their friends about what's happening at home. That is a real, specific, stressful problem.

You can help them with this before it happens. Read together, and then talk about what the character does at school. Ask: "What do you think Max said when his friend asked why his dad couldn't come to the game?" Let your child think through the scenario.

And then — gently, not pressuring — talk about their own situation. Do they want to tell their best friend? Do they want the teacher to know? They get to make some of these choices. Giving a six-year-old agency over what to share and with whom is not just kind — it's good strategy. They're more likely to seek help if they feel they've had some control over the process.

If their teacher doesn't know, consider telling them quietly. A teacher who knows can do a hundred small invisible things that make school safer for a child carrying something heavy.

## A bonus note: books can be revisited

Something I want to say about six-year-olds specifically: this is the age where they start to have a relationship with books that extends beyond being read to. They might come back to a book from the shelf on their own, in a quiet moment. They might reread a page that meant something to them.

Leave the books where they can be reached. Don't put them away after one reading session. Let them live on the nightstand or the low bookshelf. Let your child be the one to decide when they need to return to them.

That's a form of agency too.

## When the weight is too much

If your six-year-old is struggling to concentrate at school, is suddenly performing worse than usual, is having persistent nightmares, is expressing hopelessness or persistent sadness, or is showing signs of extreme anxiety about separation from you — please reach out to a professional. A school counsellor, a child therapist, a child life specialist attached to your care team.

Books are real tools. But they're not therapy. And a six-year-old carrying serious grief or anxiety about a parent's illness deserves more than books. They deserve an adult who is trained to hold that with them.

You are already doing so much. Asking for help for your child is part of that.

## Make a book made just for your child

Here's a question worth sitting with: what would it mean to your six-year-old to have a book about them? Their name on the page. A character who goes through what they're going through, in a house that feels like their house, with a parent who looks something like you.

At six, children understand that stories matter. They're starting to understand that the books on the shelf were made by real people who decided to write that story. A book made specifically for your child says something that no published book can say: *this story was made because your experience matters enough to be written down.*

At Plubis, you can create that book in five minutes. Tell us what's happening, tell us your child's name, give us a few details about your family. We'll build a story that's made for them — not a template, a real story, for your specific child.

The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about a parent going away
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through a parent traveling. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Here's a thing nobody tells you: six-year-olds sometimes seem totally fine when a parent travels and then make your life absolutely miserable about something unrelated — dinner, homework, the wrong color cup — for the entire week. That's not a coincidence. That's a kid who's learned enough social regulation to hold it together during the day and is downloading the emotional backlog onto whoever is safe when they get home.

Six is first grade. They have school, friends, structure. They can manage the public face of "my dad is on a trip" with some competence now. But inside, the same wire is live.

## What's different at six

Six-year-olds are readers. Or they're learning to read. Either way, books have a different weight now — they know something goes into making them, and a book chosen specifically for them signals: this matters, you matter, I thought about this.

They're also at the age of fairness and reasons. "Why do you have to go" is not just emotion anymore — it's a genuine request for logic. They want to understand how the world works. Giving them a real explanation ("I have to meet with people I work with who live far away") respects that growing need. And then the book can handle the feelings the logic doesn't reach.

Socially: peer relationships are becoming central at six. A parent's trip intersects with that in interesting ways — the child might feel embarrassed that they miss their parent (their friends seem fine), or weirdly proud that something special is happening in their family. Both are worth knowing.

And six-year-olds can do things during the absence that feel like genuine connection: write a letter, draw pictures, save things to tell the parent. Give them the agency.

## Books that actually work at six

**"The Invisible String" by Patrice Karst** — Still. I've recommended this across every age and at six it can become almost philosophical. Six-year-olds will ask: is the string literal? Is it metaphor? Does it stretch or is it always the same length? Those are real questions worth sitting with. The book doesn't answer them — it holds the metaphor open. At six, you can draw the string together on paper, stretch it across a map from your house to wherever the parent is going. That map, on the fridge, does something.

**"When You Are Brave" by Pat Zietlow Miller** — The best book on this list for a six-year-old who's quietly struggling but not showing it. Bravery isn't the absence of fear — it's doing the hard thing anyway. For a kid who's holding it together at school and then melting at home, naming what they're doing as bravery is genuinely reframing. You're not failing. You're brave. There's a difference and this book makes it clear.

**"A Bike Like Sergio's" by Maribeth Boelts** — Not about parent travel but exactly right for a six-year-old who's been terrible since the parent left. A kid wants a bike. He finds money that isn't his. He keeps it. He can't let it go. The whole cycle of wanting-guilt-repair is handled with such specificity that it doesn't feel didactic — it feels like it really happened. If your kid has been acting out, stealing, lying, pushing buttons since the parent left, reading this together says: when we're off-balance, we sometimes do things that aren't us. And we can make it right. Six-year-olds respond to that framing.

**"The Day You Begin" by Jacqueline Woodson** — This is officially a school book but I'd offer it during a parent's trip because it's really about having something private and significant happening in your life while the rest of the world goes about its normal business. That's exactly what it feels like to be six and navigating a parent's absence at school. Nobody asks. You're just doing it. This book says that your private, specific thing gives you a story that's worth something. Woodson at her best.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — A harder book — it's about regret, about a kindness you didn't offer when you could have. But at six, if your child is dealing with some guilt (I was mean to Daddy the morning he left, I didn't give a good hug, I said I wished he wasn't going and then he went) this book meets that with extraordinary grace. It doesn't let the main character off the hook — it just holds the feeling of "I wish I'd done differently" without punishment. Remarkable. Use with care but don't be afraid of it.

**"Owl Babies" by Martin Waddell** — I know. Six seems too old. But here's the thing: six-year-olds often want to return to books they loved when they were smaller, especially during emotionally difficult moments. Asking for Owl Babies at six is not regression — it's comfort-seeking, which is healthy and good. If your kid was read this at three and four and they ask for it now, read it without comment. Bill still wants his mommy. That's allowed.

**"Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Peña** — Not about parent travel at all — it's about a grandmother and grandson riding the bus through a city, the boy full of questions about why they don't have what other people have. The grandmother's responses are perfect acts of reframing. I include it here because six-year-olds grappling with a parent's absence sometimes need someone to help them see the richness of what's present rather than the gap of what's missing. CJ's grandmother does that without dismissing the hard questions. Best read the week before the trip, not during.

## Talking about it without making it A Thing

Six-year-olds sense when a conversation is being managed. Asking "are you okay?" every morning while the parent is away starts to feel like surveillance. What works better is creating low-key openings — a puzzle together at the table, a walk, bedtime when the lights are off — and then saying nothing. Waiting. Letting the silence do its job.

Or: read the book and put it down without comment. If something needs to come out, the book will have created the channel for it. If not, you've just had a nice bedtime.

Also: tell them true things about where the parent is and what they're doing. Not vague "work things" — specific things. "Mama is at a conference in Chicago, in a hotel room, and she's probably eating a sad room service salad and missing us." The specificity makes the parent real and present even while absent. It's funny, slightly. And it's accurate. Both matter.

## What the traveling parent can do from away

Send something physical. A postcard with their name on it is more powerful than a hundred video calls. A small flat thing in an envelope — a drawing, a sticker, a leaf from somewhere interesting — arrives in the mailbox with their name on it and that is an event. Kids at six are starting to get real mail addressed to them approximately never. Make it happen.

If calls are happening, resist the urge to ask "are you okay?" every thirty seconds. Instead: tell them one specific weird thing about where you are. Ask about one specific thing in their day. Keep it short, keep it warm, end it before it gets sad. Six-year-olds have a limit on how long a phone call can hold their attention before it starts to feel bad instead of good.

## A bonus section: the return

Six-year-olds sometimes punish the returning parent. Cold shoulder. Elaborate indifference. Loud demonstrations of being very busy with something that doesn't include you. This is not rejection. It's a small person saying "you left and I had to manage that and I'm not just going to pretend that didn't happen." Respect it. Give them an hour. Don't force the reunion. Make their favorite dinner. Read a book together that night like nothing happened, because they'll have already moved on.

What makes the return easier: make it a small celebration, not a guilt trip. "I missed you" said simply, without making them responsible for managing your feelings about having been gone. And then: normal life. Routine is the gift.

## When a book isn't enough

Six-year-olds who consistently struggle with a parent's travel — sleep disruption, behavior regression, school performance dropping, persistent anxiety — are telling you something worth listening to. A few sessions with a child therapist can make an enormous difference at this age. Six is young enough that anxiety patterns are still quite malleable. Books and rituals are excellent supports; professional help is available and works. These two things are not in competition.

## Make a book made just for your child

A six-year-old who can read will read their own name in a book with a specific kind of thrill that doesn't wear off. Plubis makes custom illustrated picture books with your child as the main character — their name, their situation, their story. "Zara's mom went to a conference in London and Zara had to be brave for three whole days" is a story that belongs to Zara and only Zara. It takes five minutes to make. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about trying new foods
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through picky eating. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six-year-olds who are picky eaters have usually been picky eaters for a while. They've survived toddlerhood on a small rotation of acceptable foods, they've made it through preschool, and now they're in first grade where the cafeteria is loud and chaotic and someone is always looking at what's in their lunchbox.

The good news: most kids, most of the time, start to naturally broaden their food range between ages five and seven. The brain is changing. The sensory sensitivities that drove a lot of the early pickiness are often softening. The social motivation — wanting to eat what the other kids eat — is real and useful.

The trickier news: some six-year-olds have developed a genuinely anxious relationship with food. Not just preference — anxiety. The refused food is associated with gagging, with distress, with real fear. That's different from pickiness and deserves different support.

For the typically picky six-year-old — preferences, routines, stubbornness — books are still useful. They're useful in the same way they've always been: as a low-pressure place to meet the concept of trying something new, when nobody is watching your plate.

## What six-year-olds can do

They're fully capable of choosing to be brave. They understand the concept. They know that trying something is a choice. They can be proud of themselves for making it. That self-awareness is the engine — you just have to not undermine it.

They can also have a real conversation about what's hard about trying new foods. Not "just try it," but "what is it about the texture?" or "is it the smell?" Six-year-olds can often tell you, and knowing what the obstacle is makes it workable.

## The books

**"Eat Your Peas" by Kes Gray** — The joke fully lands at six. Molly holds the line against peas. Mom offers everything. Mom doesn't finish her peas either. Six-year-olds reading this will feel seen as the stubborn hold-out AND they'll notice the hypocrisy of adult food rules. That's actually useful — it levels the playing field a bit. Food stops being a power struggle when both sides admit they have preferences.

**"Dragons Love Tacos" by Adam Rubin** — At six, this is still in rotation. Food as pure joy and adventure. No lessons. Just a world where food is great and funny and communal. That's the culture you're trying to build.

**"The Seven Silly Eaters" by Mary Ann Hoberman** — Seven kids, seven specific food obsessions, one exhausted mom, and a surprise ending. Six-year-olds will laugh at the absurdity and recognize something in the specificity of the kids' rules. The book doesn't judge the picky kids — it finds them funny and human. That's the tone you want.

**"Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson** — Best on this list for the meta lesson: trying something (a day with your enemy, a slice of strange pie) is almost always worth it. The connection between being willing to try and getting something good out of it — not lecture, just story — is the message six-year-olds are ready to take in.

**"Yoko" by Rosemary Wells** — Six-year-olds in first grade are living this story. The social dimension of food — what it means to eat something different in front of other kids — is real and current for them. Yoko handles it beautifully: the food is good, the friendship is built through the sharing. Not a lesson. A narrative they can find themselves in.

**"How to Babysit a Grandpa" by Jean Reagan** — Not a food book, but it has a wonderful shared-meal scene and the relationship between generations built over food. For the six-year-old who eats differently at grandma's house (where different rules apply and somehow they try things they'd never try at home), this one resonates.

**"Each Kindness" by Jacqueline Woodson** — Not about food at all. Best on this list for the six-year-old whose pickiness is partly social anxiety — fear of standing out, fear of being judged. This book is about not getting a second chance, about the small moments of exclusion that ripple forward. It's heavy and beautiful and six-year-olds handle it. It opens up a conversation about what it means to include people, which isn't far from what it means to try their food.

## What actually helps at six

The pressure has to come off. Completely. Any energy at the dinner table around what they're eating or not eating makes it worse. The division of responsibility model (you provide, they decide) is worth adopting fully if you haven't.

Find out what they're curious about. Not what you want them to eat. What they've noticed. "I saw someone eating that thing at school" is a door. Walk through it gently.

Let them have control over a food experiment. "You pick one food to try this week that you've never tried before. We won't tell you it's good. We won't pressure you. We'll just try it together." Some six-year-olds will surprise you completely.

## When it's more than pickiness

At six, if the restriction is severe (fewer than fifteen foods), if there's real anxiety or gagging with textures that used to be tolerated, if mealtimes are consistently distressing — this is the time to bring in a professional. A feeding therapist or pediatric OT who specializes in selective eating can do things in a few sessions that years of home strategies haven't. And they're not judgmental about it. This is their specialty.

## Make a book made for your child

At six, a personalized book where the character shares their exact name and faces their exact challenge is a different experience than any published book. They know it's about them. They decide what they think about it. That agency matters.

Plubis builds it in five minutes. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about potty training
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through potty training. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
Six is a hard age for this. Six is first grade. Six is sleepovers starting to be a thing. Six is your kid being fully aware that their peers don't have accidents, that accidents are not normal in first grade, and feeling that awareness in a really acute way.

If your six-year-old is having daytime accidents or wetting the bed regularly, you are dealing with two separate problems. There's the physical problem — whatever is causing the accidents — and there's the emotional problem, which is the shame and anxiety that have built up around it. Both are real. Neither is your child's fault. And here's the part people don't say out loud: neither one is your fault either.

Bedwetting at six happens in about 10-15% of children. Daytime accidents are less common but not rare. The research on this is clear and it is not taught to parents clearly enough: most childhood accidents in this age range are physiological. Not a behavioral issue. Not a discipline issue. Often a constipation issue, because the colon and the bladder are neighbors and a full colon puts pressure on a bladder that's working hard to hold on. Dr. Steve Hodges has spent his career making this case and he's right.

So. Books. What can books do at six?

At six, books can normalize. They can open conversations that are otherwise awkward to start. They can give your child the experience of seeing something like their own situation in print — which for a kid who feels utterly alone in this is enormous. And a book you read together creates a shared reference point: "like the book said" becomes a shortcut you can both use.

But be thoughtful about which books. A six-year-old will not engage with a board book about potty training. A six-year-old who is sensitive about this might not engage with any book that leads with the topic directly. Sometimes you approach sideways.

## The approach at six

Two tracks. Track one: books that are directly useful, age-appropriate, and non-infantilizing. Track two: books that address the feelings underneath without leading with the potty situation — books about embarrassment, about bodies doing things we can't control, about the gap between wanting to and being able to.

You know your kid. Some six-year-olds want to read the book that names the thing directly. Others will shut down if the book announces its topic. Go with your gut on which track to start with.

Also: at six, reading together is still normal. Don't worry that suggesting a bedtime book is weird. Six-year-olds still like being read to, even first graders. Even kids who can read themselves. The ritual of reading with a parent is still a comfort at this age.

## The picks

**"Bedwetting and Accidents Aren't Your Fault" by Steve Hodges and Suzanne Schlosberg** — I'm going to keep saying this is the most important book until I am convinced otherwise. For a six-year-old who has been having accidents for a while, the explanation matters enormously. Your body is doing something your body is doing, not something you are doing. The plumbing has a kink. That's it. The illustrated version is genuinely written for children this age and older — the tone respects their intelligence and doesn't condescend. Read it yourself first, then with your child.

**"I Have to Go!" by Robert Munsch** — Munsch has been doing this for decades and he does not miss. The situation in this book — the child who insists he doesn't need to go and then very urgently does — is funny precisely because it's true, and six-year-olds who experience urgency accidents will recognize the pattern. The humor creates distance. Distance creates the ability to look at the thing without it being unbearable. Read it in silly voices. Let them laugh. The laugh is the therapy.

**"Everyone Poops" by Taro Gomi** — Here's my honest opinion: at six, this book is most useful if you lean into the comedy of reading a board book together. Say "I know this is a baby book but it says something important" and then read it like it's the most serious document ever written. The absurdity of the framing combined with the earnestness of the content usually gets a laugh, and the laugh breaks some tension. Sometimes the destigmatization just needs to happen at the absurd level before it can happen at the real level.

**"Time to Pee!" by Mo Willems** — The pep squad energy in this book is what six-year-olds need if they've gotten into a shame spiral. The message — you have people rooting for you, this is achievable, there's no judgment here — lands differently when your kid is six and in their head about it. Willems always writes with so much fundamental warmth. The book is short enough to read in five minutes and it always leaves the right emotional residue.

**"The Potty Train" by David Hochman and Ruth Kennison** — At six this one might feel a bit young, and that's okay. You can use it as a conversation starter about when they were little, about how things have changed, about how the body is still figuring stuff out even now. The train metaphor — everyone's on their way, everyone gets there eventually — is worth making explicit with a six-year-old. You're still on the train. The train keeps moving.

**"Danny Is Done with Diapers" by Rebecca O'Connell** — Potentially very young for a six-year-old who's sensitive, but genuinely useful for kids who are motivated by identity and achievement. The ABC structure and the gallery of successful kids using the bathroom can be a low-key visualization tool. I'm hesitant to recommend this one strongly at six — it depends entirely on your kid's emotional temperature around the topic. If they're already in a shame spiral, skip it. If they're more matter-of-fact about it, it works.

**"No More Diapers for Ducky!" by Bernette Ford** — Same caveat as above. Very young in feel. But sometimes a six-year-old who has built up enormous anxiety around something benefits from approaching it at a much earlier developmental level — it removes the stakes. There's no judgment in Ducky's world. Nobody is embarrassed. It might be worth trying if other approaches have created more anxiety.

## The conversation that matters most

Books are tools for opening conversations, not replacements for them. What your six-year-old needs from you is some version of this conversation, repeated in different ways, over time:

"This happens to more kids than you'd ever guess. Bodies are complicated. Yours is still figuring out some things. You are not in trouble. You are not doing anything wrong. We're going to figure this out together."

That's it. That's the whole thing. The books give you a runway into that conversation. They give you something to point to, a character to talk about, a page to pause on. But the conversation is what your six-year-old needs.

## The school anxiety piece

Six-year-olds in first grade who are having accidents are managing a logistical and social situation that is genuinely stressful. They're tracking when they last went. They're worried about making it through math class. They're calculating whether they can make it to the bathroom down the hall before something happens. That level of vigilance is exhausting and it interferes with learning.

If school is where the accidents are happening, a direct conversation with the teacher is worth having. Not to share more than your kid is comfortable with, but to set up a system — bathroom breaks at certain times, a low-key signal your kid can use, no fuss made if they need to leave suddenly. Teachers have seen this. Good teachers want to help. The structural fix at school often does more than any book.

## A bonus section: the bedwetting question specifically

Bedwetting at six is extremely common and almost never behavioral. If the accidents are happening at night, know that most kids who wet the bed at six simply haven't developed the combination of deep-sleep arousal + bladder capacity + hormonal signals that makes staying dry overnight reliable. That combination usually comes in on its own timeline. A waterproof mattress protector and a low-key attitude go a very long way. Some pediatricians recommend a bedwetting alarm (it wakes the child when moisture is detected to train the association); some kids respond well to this at six, some don't.

What six-year-olds need around bedwetting is to know that it's not something they're doing wrong in their sleep. They can't control it. Shame about something you can't control is genuinely harmful. The Hodges book addresses this directly and compassionately.

## When books genuinely aren't the right next step

If your six-year-old has been having regular accidents for more than a month, the next step is not a new book. It's a pediatrician visit. Rule out UTI. Discuss constipation. Ask for a referral to a pediatric urologist if daytime accidents are frequent and unexplained. None of what I've listed above is a substitute for that. The books are for the emotional side of the experience. The physical side needs a doctor.

## Make a book made for your child

Here's what's true about six-year-olds: they are old enough to know that a book was made specifically for them, and that knowledge changes how they receive it. Not in a creepy way — in the way that your name in a story makes the story feel like it's for you, which is the whole point of stories anyway. Plubis builds illustrated picture books around your child's name and specific situation in five minutes. A book that says "Noah had a hard time with accidents, and he felt embarrassed, but it wasn't his fault, and things got better" does something different when Noah is the actual kid reading it. The first one is free.

title: The best picture books to read to a 6-year-old about learning to share
metaDescription: Picture books for 6-year-olds going through sharing. Our picks plus how to make a custom one for your child in five minutes.
body:
At 6, sharing has officially left the "but it's MINE" stage and entered the "it's complicated" stage. Which is, honestly, harder. Because the black-and-white stuff was annoying but manageable. The complicated stuff is — well. Ask any adult how they're doing with sharing attention, credit, resources, or emotional bandwidth, and you'll understand where this is headed.

A 6-year-old knows how to share. They've been hearing about it for four years. They can recite the rule. They just don't always do it, because sharing at 6 is tangled up in things like: who their best friend is this week, whether they feel like it was fair last time, whether the other kid even said thank you, and whether the thing being shared is the special thing or just a regular thing. Six-year-olds have developed extremely sophisticated internal hierarchies for what they share, with whom, under what conditions. It would be impressive if it weren't also occasionally infuriating.
